**Step 1 — Substitute into momentum equations:**

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\lambda\theta_t$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta v_{t+1}$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $v$.** Combine both equations:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\bigl[\beta v_t + (1-\beta)\lambda\theta_t\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Express $v_t$ using prior step.** From the second equation:

$$v_t = \frac{\theta_t - \theta_{t-1}}{-\eta} = \frac{\theta_{t-1}-\theta_t}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\beta\left(\frac{\theta_{t-1}-\theta_t}{\eta}\right) - \eta(1-\beta)\lambda\theta_t$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \beta(\theta_t - \theta_{t-1}) - \eta(1-\beta)\lambda\theta_t$$

**Step 5 — Group terms:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \bigl[1 + \beta - \eta(1-\beta)\lambda\bigr]\theta_t - \beta\,\theta_{t-1}$$

**Step 6 — Characteristic equation.** Assume solution of form $\theta_t = c\,r^t$:

$$r^{t+1} = [1+\beta - \eta(1-\beta)\lambda]\,r^t - \beta\,r^{t-1}$$

Dividing by $r^{t-1}$:

$$r^2 - [1+\beta-\eta(1-\beta)\lambda]\,r + \beta = 0$$

**Step 7 — Stability condition.** Convergence requires both roots to lie inside the unit disk ($\vert r \vert < 1$). This yields the practical constraint:

$$0 < \eta < \frac{2(1+\beta)}{\lambda(1-\beta)}$$

For $\beta = 0$, this reduces to $\eta < 2/\lambda$, which is the classical GD stability bound. For $\beta > 0$, the allowable $\eta$ range **expands**, demonstrating that momentum permits **larger learning rates** without divergence.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Vanilla GD ($\beta=0$) | Heavy-Ball Momentum | Nesterov |
|---|---|---|---|
| Convergence rate (convex) | $O(1/t)$ | $O(1/t)$ | $O(1/t^2)$ |
| Convergence rate (strongly convex) | Linear | Linear (faster const.) | Linear (better const.) |
| Hyperparameters | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| Robustness to noise | Low | Medium-High | High |
| Computational overhead | Baseline | Negligible ($\mathcal{O}(d)$ extra) | Negligible |
| Recommended $\beta$ | N/A | $0.9$ | $0.9$ |
| Standard use-case | Teaching baselines | Production CNN/MLP | Production + RNN/LSTM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The characteristic-equation derivation (Section 3.2) is a **frequently asked 14-mark question**. Master both the algebra and the physical interpretation (larger permissible $\eta$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Momentum Optimization Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start t equals 0] --> initB[Initialize theta_0 randomly and v_0 equals zero]
    initB --> pickHP[Choose hyperparameters eta and beta]
    pickHP --> loopCheck{Iteration t less than T_max?}
    loopCheck -- No --> stopZ[Stop and return theta_t]
    loopCheck -- Yes --> compG[Compute gradient g_t equals grad J at theta_t]
    compG --> updV[Update velocity v_t+1 equals beta v_t plus 1 minus beta times g_t]
    updV --> updTheta[Update parameters theta_t+1 equals theta_t minus eta v_t+1]
    updTheta --> convChk{Convergence criteria met?}
    convChk -- No --> incT[Increment t then t plus 1] --> loopCheck
    convChk -- Yes --> stopZ
```

## 4.2 Side-by-Side Algorithmic Logic: Vanilla GD vs. Momentum GD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Vanilla_GD
        v1[Input theta_t] --> v2[Compute gradient g_t]
        v2 --> v3[Update theta_t+1 equals theta_t minus eta g_t]
        v3 --> v4[Output theta_t+1]
    end
    subgraph Momentum_GD
        m1[Input theta_t and v_t] --> m2[Compute gradient g_t]
        m2 --> m3[Velocity update v_t+1 equals beta v_t plus 1 minus beta g_t]
        m3 --> m4[Parameter update theta_t+1 equals theta_t minus eta v_t+1]
        m4 --> m5[Output theta_t+1 and v_t+1]
    end
```

## 4.3 Loss-Surface Trajectory Diagram (Ravine Convergence Behaviour)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at hilltop with random init] --> B[Ravine with steep walls and shallow floor]
    B --> C1[Vanilla GD path zigzags across the ravine taking many small noisy steps]
    B --> C2[Momentum GD path damps zigzagging and accelerates along the ravine floor reaching the minimum faster]
    C1 --> D1[Final point slow convergence]
    C2 --> D2[Final point fast convergence at valley bottom]
```

## 4.4 Nesterov Lookahead Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current theta_t] --> B[Compute lookahead theta_tilde equals theta_t minus eta beta v_t]
    B --> C[Evaluate gradient at lookahead grad J at theta_tilde]
    C --> D[Update velocity v_t+1 equals beta v_t plus 1 minus beta grad J at theta_tilde]
    D --> E[Update parameters theta_t+1 equals theta_t minus eta v_t+1]
```

> **Reading Guide:** In the classical momentum block diagram (Section 4.1), note the feedback loop where $v_t$ is reused at every iteration — this is the literal embodiment of the "memory" property. In the Nesterov block diagram (Section 4.4), the gradient is evaluated at a **hypothetical future position**, which is the key innovation enabling accelerated convergence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**State the update rules for Gradient Descent with Momentum. Explain the role of the momentum coefficient $\beta$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The update rules are:

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\nabla J(\theta_t)$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, v_{t+1}$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate. The coefficient $\beta \in [0,1)$ controls how much of the past gradient history is retained. A larger $\beta$ (e.g., $0.9$) gives longer memory and smoother averaging over roughly $1/(1-\beta) = 10$ iterations, while a smaller $\beta$ behaves more like vanilla GD. If $\beta = 0$, the algorithm reduces exactly to standard gradient descent.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Gradient Descent with Momentum and Nesterov Accelerated Gradient.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Momentum | Nesterov |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient evaluation point | Current $\theta_t$ | Lookahead $\theta_t - \eta\beta v_t$ |
| Mathematical convergence (strongly convex) | Linear | Linear (improved constant) |
| Intuition | "Roll then correct" | "Peek ahead, then roll" |

In Nesterov, the gradient is computed at the **anticipated next position**, providing a correction that counteracts momentum-induced overshooting, yielding faster practical convergence on deep networks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the update equations for Gradient Descent with Momentum and show that the velocity term represents an exponentially weighted moving average of past gradients. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the recurrence.** Initialization: $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$. At each iteration $t$:

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\, g_t, \quad \text{where } g_t = \nabla J(\theta_t)$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, v_{t+1}$$

**Step 2 — Unroll the recurrence** by substitution. Computing the first few terms:

- $v_1 = (1-\beta)\, g_0$
- $v_2 = \beta(1-\beta) g_0 + (1-\beta) g_1$
- $v_3 = \beta^2(1-\beta) g_0 + \beta(1-\beta) g_1 + (1-\beta) g_2$

**Step 3 — Identify the pattern.** The coefficient of gradient $g_k$ at iteration $t+1$ is $(1-\beta)\beta^{t-k}$ for $k \le t$. Hence:

$$v_{t+1} = (1-\beta)\sum_{i=0}^{t} \beta^{t-i}\, g_i$$

This is the definition of an **exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA)** of past gradients with decay rate $\beta$. Older gradients contribute geometrically smaller weights. $\blacksquare$

**(b) For a quadratic loss $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda\theta^2$, derive the convergence condition on the learning rate $\eta$ for the momentum method, and comment on how momentum relaxes this condition compared to vanilla GD. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient:** $\nabla J(\theta_t) = \lambda \theta_t$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into momentum equations:**

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\lambda\theta_t, \qquad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta v_{t+1}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $v_t$** using $v_t = (\theta_{t-1}-\theta_t)/\eta$:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \beta(\theta_t - \theta_{t-1}) - \eta(1-\beta)\lambda\theta_t$$

**Step 4 — Form the second-order linear recurrence:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \bigl[1+\beta-\eta(1-\beta)\lambda\bigr]\theta_t - \beta\,\theta_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 — Characteristic equation.** Assuming $\theta_t = c r^t$:

$$r^2 - \bigl[1+\beta-\eta(1-\beta)\lambda\bigr] r + \beta = 0$$

**Step 6 — Apply Jury stability / direct bound.** For real roots and $|\beta|<1$, stability requires both roots to satisfy $|r|<1$, which is satisfied when:

$$0 < \eta < \frac{2(1+\beta)}{\lambda(1-\beta)}$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with vanilla GD.** For $\beta = 0$, the bound reduces to $\eta < 2/\lambda$. As $\beta \to 1$, the upper bound grows — meaning **momentum tolerates larger learning rates**, accelerating convergence along shallow curvature directions without instability. $\blacksquare$

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) Explain the physical analogy of momentum-based gradient descent. How does it help overcome local minima and saddle points? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The analogy.** Gradient descent with momentum is analogous to a **heavy ball rolling down a hilly surface**. While vanilla GD is a "frictionless puck" that reverses instantly at slope reversals, momentum GD is a "heavy ball" that accumulates kinetic energy.

**Step 2 — Velocity as inertia.** The velocity $v_t$ acts as inertia: $v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta) g_t$. When consecutive gradients $g_t, g_{t+1}, \ldots$ point in similar directions, the velocity **builds up**, taking larger effective steps.

**Step 3 — Escaping local minima.** At a shallow local minimum, $\nabla J(\theta^*) \approx 0$ momentarily, so vanilla GD halts. Momentum, however, retains $v_t \neq 0$ from prior iterations and **pushes the ball past** the minimum if the curvature is insufficient to reverse its velocity.

**Step 4 — Crossing saddle points.** In high-dimensional loss landscapes, saddle points (where some directions are minima and others are maxima) are common. The momentum term maintains movement along the descending eigendirections while suppressing ascent directions, effectively tunneling through the saddle.

**Step 5 — Quantitative justification.** From the quadratic analysis, the momentum parameter $\beta$ creates a **second-order recurrence** that accelerates motion along low-curvature directions (small $\lambda$) and damps oscillations along high-curvature directions (large $\lambda$). $\blacksquare$

**(b) Describe the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) method. Write its update equations and explain why it is often preferred over classical momentum. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Update equations of NAG:**

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\nabla J\!\bigl(\theta_t - \eta\beta v_t\bigr)$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, v_{t+1}$$

**Step 2 — Lookahead interpretation.** The gradient is evaluated at the **anticipated future position** $\tilde{\theta}_t = \theta_t - \eta\beta v_t$ rather than at $\theta_t$.

**Step 3 — Why this helps.** In classical momentum, if the ball has built up too much velocity, it may overshoot the minimum and oscillate. Nesterov's lookahead allows the algorithm to **detect impending overshoot early** (because the gradient is steeper at $\tilde{\theta}_t$ near the minimum) and **apply a corrective braking force**.

**Step 4 — Convergence rate.** On smooth strongly convex functions, Nesterov achieves $O(1/t^2)$ convergence versus $O(1/t)$ for classical momentum — an **optimal first-order rate**.

**Step 5 — Practical preference.** NAG is favored when training RNNs, fine-tuning transformers, and in any setting where momentum-induced oscillations slow convergence. The computational overhead is identical to classical momentum (one extra gradient evaluation at a known point). $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-\beta)$ factor:** Many students write $v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + g_t$ without the $(1-\beta)$ multiplier. Both forms are valid but must be consistent — the effective learning rate differs by a factor of $1/(1-\beta)$.
> 2. **Confusing Polyak momentum with Nesterov:** These are distinct algorithms. Writing "Nesterov momentum" without the lookahead position loses full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the convergence derivation:** The characteristic-equation derivation of the stability bound is the heart of the 7-mark part. Numerical substitution alone earns only partial credit.
> 4. **Wrong typical values:** Saying $\beta = 0.5$ without justification. The **default** is $\mathbf{0.9}$ — quoting it shows mastery.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GD with Momentum** augments standard gradient descent with an exponentially weighted moving average of past gradients, controlled by momentum coefficient $\beta$.
- **Core update equations:**
$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\nabla J(\theta_t), \quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, v_{t+1}$$
- **Physical analogy:** A heavy ball rolling down a loss landscape — gains speed in consistent directions and dampens zig-zag oscillations.
- **Typical hyperparameters:** $\eta = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$, $\beta = \mathbf{0.9}$.
- **Effective memory window:** $\approx 1/(1-\beta)$ iterations (10 for $\beta = 0.9$).
- **Nesterov variant:** evaluates gradient at lookahead position $\theta_t - \eta\beta v_t$ for faster convergence ($O(1/t^2)$ on convex functions).
- **Stability bound on $\eta$:** for $J(\theta)=\tfrac{1}{2}\lambda\theta^2$, requires $0<\eta<\dfrac{2(1+\beta)}{\lambda(1-\beta)}$ — broader than vanilla GD's $\eta<2/\lambda$.
- **Advantages:** faster convergence, escapes shallow minima/saddles, tolerates larger $\eta$.
- **Disadvantages:** extra hyperparameter $\beta$ to tune; may overshoot sharp minima if $\beta$ too high.
- **Production usage:** default optimizer in many deep learning pipelines, often combined with adaptive scaling (Adam = Momentum + RMSProp).
- **KTU-ready mnemonic:** **"Beta-Velocity-Eta-Gradient-Theta"** — recall order of equations in the algorithm.
- **Always mention initialization:** $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$ unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nesterov Accelerated GD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nesterov Accelerated GD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nesterov Accelerated Gradient Descent (NAG / NAGD)** is a first-order optimization algorithm that refines classical **momentum-based gradient descent** by computing the gradient at a **look-ahead position** along the momentum vector, rather than at the current parameter location. It was proposed by **Yurii Nesterov (1983)** and provides a provably **$\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$** convergence rate for smooth convex functions, which is optimal among first-order methods.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Nesterov momentum is studied as an enhancement to vanilla **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** that addresses two limitations of standard momentum:
- It **reduces overshooting** near the optimum.
- It achieves **faster convergence** by using a "smart" gradient evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key NEP 2020 Outcome Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2** – *Apply optimization techniques in deep learning model training* — and exercises **Bloom's Level 4 (Analyze)** and **Level 5 (Evaluate)**.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **blindfolded skier** descending a snowy mountain slope:

- **Vanilla SGD** = The skier feels the slope beneath their **current** ski position and points downhill.
- **Standard Momentum** = The skier remembers the previous direction of travel, adds momentum inertia, and feels the slope only at the **current** position.
- **Nesterov Momentum** = The skier anticipates momentum, **leans forward** by roughly the momentum distance, and feels the slope at this **look-ahead** point — then corrects the steering accordingly.

This "look-ahead" or "peek-ahead" mechanism gives the optimizer a **foresight** advantage: if momentum is carrying it **toward a valley (optimum)**, Nesterov slows down gracefully; if momentum is about to **overshoot a hilltop**, it self-corrects before the overshoot occurs.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line intuition:** *Nesterov = "Look where you are GOING, not where you ARE."*

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Hyperparameters

The following quantities govern Nesterov Accelerated GD:

- **$\alpha$ (learning rate)** — step size along the gradient direction; typical range $\alpha \in [10^{-4}, \, 10^{-1}]$.
- **$\beta$ (momentum coefficient)** — decay rate of the velocity term; typically $\beta \in [0.9, \, 0.99]$.
- **$v_t$ (velocity vector)** — exponentially weighted moving average of past gradients.
- **$\theta_t$ (parameter vector)** — model weights at iteration $t$.
- **$\nabla L(\theta)$ (gradient)** — first-order derivative of the loss with respect to $\theta$.

---

## 1.4 Where Nesterov Fits in the KTU 2024 Optimizer Taxonomy

| Optimizer Family | Gradient Evaluation Point | Convergence (Convex) | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vanilla GD | $\theta_t$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ | High |
| SGD | $\theta_t$ (single sample) | $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{k})$ | High |
| Momentum GD | $\theta_t$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ | High |
| **Nesterov Accelerated GD** | $\theta_t - \beta v_{t-1}$ (look-ahead) | $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ | **Very High** |
| AdaGrad | $\theta_t$ (per-param scaling) | $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{k})$ | Medium |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why Nesterov Works Better

The momentum-based update framework has **two physical forces**:
1. **Gradient force** — pulls parameters toward the local minimum using $\nabla L(\theta)$.
2. **Momentum force** — accumulates past velocity $v_{t-1}$, smoothed by $\beta$.

The pivotal question is: **At which point should we evaluate the gradient?**

- **Standard Momentum:** Evaluates the gradient at the **current** location $\theta_{t-1}$. This is a **"reactive"** strategy.
- **Nesterov Momentum:** Evaluates the gradient at the **look-ahead** location $\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$. This is a **"proactive"** strategy.

### Structured Logic Steps:

- **Step 1 — Apply momentum blindly.** Project the parameters forward by $\beta v_{t-1}$ to obtain a provisional position $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$.
- **Step 2 — "Peek" at the loss surface.** Compute the gradient $\nabla L(\tilde{\theta})$ at this projected position.
- **Step 3 — Blend with momentum history.** Form the new velocity $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\tilde{\theta})$.
- **Step 4 — Take the descent step.** Update $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha v_t$.

> [!NOTE]
> The look-ahead introduces a **second-order correction** — Nesterov effectively uses the **curvature information** of the loss surface, even though it is still a first-order method (only gradients are computed).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU board exam questions on this topic are answerable using the four equations below.** Master them thoroughly.

| Symbol | Equation | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Look-ahead position | $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$ | Projected parameter location | — |
| Velocity update | $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\tilde{\theta})$ | New momentum-blended gradient | — |
| Parameter update | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha v_t$ | Descent step | — |
| Initial velocity | $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$ | Bootstrap condition | $v_0 = 0$ |
| Convergence rate (convex, smooth) | $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ | Optimal first-order bound | — |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, do not confuse the absolute-value notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ with table column separators. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX inside table cells.

---

## 2.3 Standard Momentum vs. Nesterov Momentum — Side-by-Side

| Feature | Standard (Heavy-Ball) Momentum | **Nesterov Accelerated GD** |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient point | Current $\theta_{t-1}$ | Look-ahead $\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$ |
| Velocity update | $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})$ | $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1})$ |
| Overshoot behavior | Prone to oscillation near minima | Self-correcting, smoother trajectories |
| Effective order | First-order with inertia | First-order with **second-order correction** |
| Convergence rate | $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ |
| Year proposed | Polyak, 1964 | Nesterov, 1983 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deep Neural Network Training:** Used as a building block inside modern optimizers (e.g., as the momentum term in PyTorch's `torch.optim.SGD(nesterov=True)`).
- **Convex Optimization in Signal Processing:** Applied in **LASSO regression**, **Sparse coding**, and **compressed sensing**.
- **Robotics & Control:** Solves real-time trajectory optimization problems where fast convergence is critical.
- **Computer Vision:** Used in fine-tuning large CNN backbones where gradient noise is high.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nesterov Update Equations

We begin from the **continuous-time ODE** perspective, then discretize to obtain the standard Nesterov recurrence.

### Step A — Continuous-Time Analogue

Consider the gradient flow $\dot{\theta} = -\nabla L(\theta)$ with a momentum term. Adding a momentum variable $v$, we get the second-order system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\theta}(t) &= v(t) \\
\dot{v}(t) &= -\nabla L(\theta(t) + \beta v(t))
\end{aligned}
$$

Here the gradient is sampled **ahead** of $\theta(t)$ by the offset $\beta v(t)$ — this is the geometric origin of Nesterov's look-ahead.

### Step B — Discretization via Euler's Method

Applying forward Euler discretization with step size $\alpha$ (learning rate) gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{t} &= \theta_{t-1} + \alpha \, v_{t} \\
v_{t} &= \beta \, v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The continuous update $\dot{\theta} = v$ becomes a discrete step of size $\alpha$ along $v$, and the velocity ODE becomes the momentum-blended look-ahead gradient.

### Step C — Final Update Form (Standard Nesterov Form)

By absorbing $\alpha$ into the gradient term (common convention), the canonical KTU-board form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_t &= \beta \, v_{t-1} + \nabla L\!\left(\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}\right) \\
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} - \alpha \, v_t
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The first line computes the look-ahead gradient by sampling at the provisional position; the second line uses this to descend.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A loss function has gradient $\nabla L(\theta) = 2\theta + 1$. Using Nesterov Accelerated GD with $\alpha = 0.1$, $\beta = 0.9$, $\theta_0 = 1$, $v_0 = 0$, compute $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

### Step 1 — Compute $\theta_1$

**Look-ahead position:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\theta}_1 &= \theta_0 - \beta v_0 \\
&= 1 - (0.9)(0) \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gradient at look-ahead:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_1) &= 2(1) + 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**New velocity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= \beta v_0 + \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_1) \\
&= (0.9)(0) + 3 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parameter update:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \theta_0 - \alpha v_1 \\
&= 1 - (0.1)(3) \\
&= 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\theta_2$

**Look-ahead position:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\theta}_2 &= \theta_1 - \beta v_1 \\
&= 0.7 - (0.9)(3) \\
&= 0.7 - 2.7 = -2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gradient at look-ahead:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_2) &= 2(-2.0) + 1 = -3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**New velocity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2 &= \beta v_1 + \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_2) \\
&= (0.9)(3) + (-3.0) \\
&= 2.7 - 3.0 = -0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parameter update:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_2 &= \theta_1 - \alpha v_2 \\
&= 0.7 - (0.1)(-0.3) \\
&= 0.7 + 0.03 = 0.73
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic / intuition:** Notice how $v_2$ flipped sign and shrank — Nesterov's look-ahead "saw" that the gradient at $-2.0$ was strongly negative (indicating the minimum lies in the opposite direction) and **auto-braked** the momentum to prevent overshoot. This is the central practical benefit over vanilla momentum.

---

## 3.3 Convergence Rate Derivation (Sketch)

For a smooth, convex function $L(\theta)$ with **Lipschitz gradient constant $L_{\text{lip}}$** and bounded initial distance $\Vert \theta_0 - \theta^{\star} \Vert \leq R$, Nesterov's method guarantees:

$$
L(\theta_k) - L(\theta^{\star}) \;\leq\; \frac{2 \, L_{\text{lip}} \, R^{2}}{(k+1)^{2}}
$$

This is the celebrated $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ rate — **quadratically faster** than gradient descent's $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$.

> **Conversion logic:** The proof uses a carefully designed **Lyapunov function** $V_k$ combining the iterate error and velocity error. The look-ahead term cancels a quadratic component in the descent lemma, leaving only $1/k^2$ residual.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Optimizer Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize theta0, v0=0] --> B{Choose Optimizer}
    B -->|Vanilla GD| C1[Compute grad at theta]
    B -->|Momentum| C2[Compute grad at theta_t-1]
    B -->|Nesterov NAG| C3[Compute grad at theta_t-1 - beta v_t-1]
    C1 --> D1[theta_t = theta_t-1 - alpha grad]
    C2 --> D2[v_t = beta v_t-1 + grad]
    D2 --> E2[theta_t = theta_t-1 - alpha v_t]
    C3 --> D3[Look-ahead pos tilde theta]
    D3 --> D3a[v_t = beta v_t-1 + grad tilde theta]
    D3a --> E3[theta_t = theta_t-1 - alpha v_t]
    D1 --> F[Convergence Check]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F -->|Not Converged| B
    F -->|Converged| G[Optimal theta star]
```

## 4.2 Nesterov Iteration — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Previous Parameters theta_t-1] --> SUM1
    V1[Previous Velocity v_t-1] --> MUL1[Multiply by beta]
    MUL1 --> SUM1((Subtract))
    SUM1 --> LA[Look-ahead Position tilde theta]
    LA --> GRAD[Evaluate Gradient L tilde theta]
    GRAD --> SUM2((Add))
    V1 --> MUL1
    MUL2[beta times v_t-1] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> V2[New Velocity v_t]
    V2 --> MUL3[Multiply by alpha]
    P1 --> SUM3((Subtract))
    MUL3 --> SUM3
    SUM3 --> P2[Updated Parameters theta_t]
```

## 4.3 Loss Surface Trajectory — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start at theta0]) --> M1{Standard Momentum}
    Start --> M2{Nesterov NAG}
    M1 --> P1[Path A: Reactive, oscillates near minimum]
    M2 --> P2[Path B: Look-ahead, smoother braking]
    P1 --> End1([Reaches minimum with overshoot])
    P2 --> End2([Reaches minimum cleanly])
    End1 -.vs.-> End2
```

## 4.4 Data-Flow Through One Nesterov Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1[Input: theta_t-1, v_t-1] --> StepA[Step A: Form look-ahead tilde theta]
    StepA --> StepB[Step B: Sample gradient at tilde theta]
    StepB --> StepC[Step C: Update velocity v_t]
    StepC --> StepD[Step D: Update parameters theta_t]
    StepD --> Out1[Output: theta_t, v_t for next iteration]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`
**Q: Distinguish between standard momentum-based gradient descent and Nesterov Accelerated Gradient Descent. Highlight the key conceptual difference.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** *Conceptual difference (1.5 marks)* + *Update equations comparison (1.5 marks)*

| Aspect | Standard Momentum | Nesterov Accelerated GD |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient evaluated at | $\theta_{t-1}$ (current position) | $\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$ (look-ahead) |
| Nature | Reactive correction | Proactive / predictive correction |

The **key conceptual difference** is the **point of gradient evaluation**. In standard momentum, the gradient is computed at the **current** parameter location and then momentum is added. In Nesterov's method, the momentum is first used to project the parameters to a **look-ahead position**, and the gradient is computed **there**. This allows the optimizer to **anticipate** where it is headed and **brake early** to avoid overshooting the optimum, yielding faster convergence ($\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ vs. $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**Q: State the update equations for Nesterov Accelerated Gradient Descent. What is the role of the momentum coefficient $\beta$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** *Update equations (2 marks)* + *Role of $\beta$ (1 mark)*

**Update Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_t &= \beta \, v_{t-1} + \nabla L\!\left(\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}\right) \\
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} - \alpha \, v_t
\end{aligned}
$$

with initial condition $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

**Role of $\beta$:** The momentum coefficient $\beta \in [0, 1)$ controls how much of the **past velocity** is retained in the current update. A larger $\beta$ gives more inertia (smoother trajectories but risk of overshoot), while a smaller $\beta$ makes the algorithm behave more like vanilla gradient descent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each module in the End Semester Exam contains a 14-mark question with internal choice between **(A)** and **(B)**. Solve either (a)+(b) of choice A, or (a)+(b) of choice B.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**Q (A):**
**(a)** Derive the update equations for Nesterov Accelerated Gradient Descent starting from a continuous-time second-order ODE. Explain how the look-ahead position arises naturally from this formulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the loss function $L(\theta) = \theta^{2}$. Using Nesterov Accelerated GD with $\alpha = 0.2$, $\beta = 0.5$, $\theta_0 = 4$, $v_0 = 0$, compute $\theta_1$, $\theta_2$, and $\theta_3$. Compare the trajectory with what vanilla gradient descent ($\beta = 0$) would produce. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (A)(a):

**Step 1 — Continuous-time second-order system (2 marks):**

We write the gradient flow with momentum as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\theta}(t) &= v(t) \\
\dot{v}(t) &= -\nabla L(\theta(t) + \beta v(t))
\end{aligned}
$$

The gradient is sampled at the **offset point** $\theta(t) + \beta v(t)$, i.e., ahead of the current position by an amount proportional to the velocity.

**Step 2 — Discretization (2 marks):**

Applying forward Euler discretization with step $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \alpha \, v_t \\
v_t &= \beta \, v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Canonical form and intuition (2 marks):**

Combining yields the canonical KTU form. The look-ahead $\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$ arises naturally because Euler's method propagates the momentum step **forward** before evaluating the gradient.

**Step 4 — Convergence advantage (1 mark):**

This formulation inherits the **$\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$** rate — quadratically better than gradient descent's $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$.

---

#### Model Solution to (A)(b):

Given $L(\theta) = \theta^{2}$, so $\nabla L(\theta) = 2\theta$. Parameters: $\alpha = 0.2$, $\beta = 0.5$, $\theta_0 = 4$, $v_0 = 0$.

**Iteration 1 (t = 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\theta}_1 &= \theta_0 - \beta v_0 = 4 - 0 = 4 \\
\nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_1) &= 2(4) = 8 \\
v_1 &= \beta v_0 + \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_1) = 0 + 8 = 8 \\
\theta_1 &= \theta_0 - \alpha v_1 = 4 - (0.2)(8) = 4 - 1.6 = 2.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 (t = 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\theta}_2 &= \theta_1 - \beta v_1 = 2.4 - (0.5)(8) = 2.4 - 4.0 = -1.6 \\
\nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_2) &= 2(-1.6) = -3.2 \\
v_2 &= \beta v_1 + \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_2) = (0.5)(8) + (-3.2) = 4.0 - 3.2 = 0.8 \\
\theta_2 &= \theta_1 - \alpha v_2 = 2.4 - (0.2)(0.8) = 2.4 - 0.16 = 2.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3 (t = 3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\theta}_3 &= \theta_2 - \beta v_2 = 2.24 - (0.5)(0.8) = 2.24 - 0.4 = 1.84 \\
\nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_3) &= 2(1.84) = 3.68 \\
v_3 &= \beta v_2 + \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_3) = (0.5)(0.8) + 3.68 = 0.4 + 3.68 = 4.08 \\
\theta_3 &= \theta_2 - \alpha v_3 = 2.24 - (0.2)(4.08) = 2.24 - 0.816 = 1.424
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with Vanilla GD ($\beta = 0$):**

For $\beta = 0$, $v_t = \nabla L(\theta_{t-1}) = 2\theta_{t-1}$, so $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - 0.2(2\theta_{t-1}) = 0.6 \, \theta_{t-1}$.

| Iteration | Vanilla GD $\theta_t$ | Nesterov $\theta_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 4.000 | 4.000 |
| 1 | 2.400 | 2.400 |
| 2 | 1.440 | 2.240 |
| 3 | 0.864 | 1.424 |

> **Inference:** Vanilla GD decays geometrically with factor $0.6$; Nesterov initially overshoots into negative territory but **auto-corrects via look-ahead**, settling into a smoother descent. For high-curvature functions, Nesterov converges in fewer iterations because it exploits momentum effectively.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**Q (B):**
**(a)** Compare the convergence rates of Vanilla Gradient Descent, Momentum-based GD, and Nesterov Accelerated GD for smooth convex functions. Show that Nesterov's rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ is optimal among first-order methods. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a real-world analogy why Nesterov Accelerated GD avoids the overshooting problem faced by standard momentum. Illustrate with a sketch of the parameter trajectory on a convex loss curve. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (B)(a):

**Step 1 — Vanilla GD rate (2 marks):**

For $L$-smooth, $\mu$-strongly convex $f$, gradient descent satisfies:

$$
f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\star}) \;\leq\; \left(1 - \mu/L\right)^{k} \left[f(\theta_0) - f(\theta^{\star})\right]
$$

This is a **geometric** (linear) rate. For the general convex (non-strongly-convex) case, the bound degrades to $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$.

**Step 2 — Momentum GD rate (2 marks):**

Standard (heavy-ball) momentum improves constant factors but on general convex functions it still achieves only $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$. It does **not** improve the **order** of convergence.

**Step 3 — Nesterov's $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ rate (2 marks):**

Nesterov proved:

$$
f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\star}) \;\leq\; \frac{2 \Vert \theta_0 - \theta^{\star} \Vert^{2} L_{\text{lip}}}{(k+1)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Optimality argument (1 mark):**

By the **lower-bound theorem of Nemirovski–Yudin**, any first-order oracle method on a class of smooth convex functions cannot achieve a rate better than $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$. Hence Nesterov's bound is **information-theoretically optimal**.

---

#### Model Solution to (B)(b):

**Analogy — The Braking Skier:**

A skier descending a slope has inertia. A **reactive skier** (standard momentum) steers based on the slope under their skis; near a valley bottom, they overshoot the foot of the hill and climb the other side before coming back. A **foresighted skier** (Nesterov) leans forward, **feels the up-slope ahead**, and applies the brake **before** reaching it.

**Trajectory sketch on a convex parabola (described verbally for examiner):**

- **Standard momentum trajectory:** Spirals into the minimum with damped oscillations — over-shoots, then corrects.
- **Nesterov trajectory:** Smoothly decelerates as the look-ahead detects rising slope; settles into the minimum with minimal oscillation.

**Mathematical reason:** The look-ahead term $-\beta v_{t-1}$ in $\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$ allows the optimizer to sample the gradient **at the upcoming position**. When $v_{t-1}$ points away from the minimum, the look-ahead gradient becomes large in magnitude and **opposes** the momentum — effectively applying a **braking force**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall Callout:** Students often draw the trajectory on a non-convex surface. Always use a **convex bowl** for the analogy because Nesterov's $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ guarantee applies to smooth convex functions. Using a wavy surface will be marked down. **(Common loss: 2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section 30 minutes before the exam for last-mile revision.**

- **Nesterov's defining feature:** Gradient computed at the **look-ahead** position $\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$, not at the current position.
- **Two key equations to memorize verbatim:**
  - $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1})$
  - $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha v_t$
- **Optimal convergence rate:** $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ for smooth convex functions — **doubly faster** than GD.
- **Hyperparameters:** $\alpha$ (learning rate), $\beta$ (momentum). Standard choice: $\beta \in [0.9, 0.99]$.
- **Comparison trap to avoid:** Nesterov is **not** second-order — it only uses gradients, but achieves a second-order-style rate via clever look-ahead.
- **Initial condition:** Always $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Practical use:** PyTorch flag `nesterov=True` in `torch.optim.SGD`; also embedded in many modern optimizers.
- **Origin:** Yurii Nesterov, 1983 (Soviet mathematical programming literature).
- **Limitation to mention in exams:** Theoretical $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$ guarantee assumes **smooth + convex**. For non-convex deep learning losses, it still works empirically but no formal rate guarantee exists.
- **Common exam phrases to use:** *"look-ahead correction"*, *"foresighted gradient evaluation"*, *"auto-braking via momentum projection"*, *"Nemirovski–Yudin lower bound"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students lose marks by (1) forgetting the look-ahead term and writing standard momentum, (2) not stating the initial condition $v_0 = 0$, and (3) confusing the look-ahead sign. **The look-ahead is a SUBTRACTION:** $\theta_{t-1} - \beta v_{t-1}$, **not** addition. Memorize this exact form.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AdaGrad
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Course Code:** PEEIT633
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AdaGrad

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AdaGrad (Adaptive Gradient Algorithm)** is a gradient-based optimization algorithm that adapts the **learning rate** parameter-by-parameter at every iteration by scaling it inversely proportional to the square root of the sum of all past squared gradients for that specific parameter.

Formally, AdaGrad modifies the generic gradient descent update rule by introducing a **per-parameter adaptive learning rate** derived from the historical gradient information accumulated throughout the training trajectory. The algorithm was introduced by Duchi, Hazan, and Singer in 2011 and belongs to the family of **adaptive learning rate methods**.

The core update rule is expressed as:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}} \odot g_t$$

where:
- $\theta_t$ is the parameter vector at iteration $t$
- $\eta$ is the **initial/global learning rate** (typically **0.01**)
- $G_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a diagonal matrix where each diagonal element $G_{t,i}$ is the sum of squares of past gradients w.r.t. parameter $i$
- $g_t = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ is the gradient of the objective function at step $t$
- $\epsilon$ is a smoothing term (typically **$10^{-8}$**) to prevent division by zero
- $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Smart Hiker" on a Mountain**
> 
> Imagine a hiker descending a fog-covered mountain, trying to find the lowest valley. Standard Gradient Descent takes **uniformly sized steps** regardless of terrain — reckless on steep cliffs, frustratingly slow on flat plains. **AdaGrad is like a smart hiker with a pedometer** that automatically takes **smaller, careful steps on steep slopes** (where gradients are large) and **larger, confident strides on gentle slopes** (where gradients are small). The pedometer records every step's steepness and uses that history to adjust future step sizes intelligently.

### Why AdaGrad Matters in Deep Learning

| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Adaptivity** | Learning rate adapts per parameter |
| **Sparsity Handling** | Gives larger updates to infrequent features |
| **No Manual Tuning** | Reduces need to tune individual learning rates |
| **Convergence** | Theoretically guarantees convergence for convex objectives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Philosophy:** In sparse data scenarios (e.g., NLP, recommendation systems), some features appear rarely. AdaGrad ensures that **rare parameters receive larger effective learning rates**, accelerating learning for under-represented features — a critical property in large-scale machine learning.

---

## 1.3 Position in the Optimization Landscape

AdaGrad is foundational to the family of **adaptive optimizers**, preceding and inspiring:

- **RMSProp** (fixes AdaGrad's aggressive learning rate decay)
- **Adam** (combines momentum + RMSProp-style adaptivity)
- **AdaDelta** (extension removing the need for initial learning rate)

In KTU 2024 Module 2, AdaGrad is typically taught **before** RMSProp and Adam to build the conceptual foundation of per-parameter adaptive learning rates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Algorithmic Logic (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the exhaustive operational breakdown of AdaGrad. Each step is critical for KTU board examinations.

### Step 1: Initialization
- Initialize parameter vector $\theta_0 \in \mathbb{R}^d$
- Initialize accumulator $G_0 = \mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (zero vector)
- Choose global learning rate $\eta$ (default: **0.01**)
- Choose small constant $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$ for numerical stability

### Step 2: Gradient Computation
At each iteration $t$, compute the gradient of the loss function $J(\theta)$ w.r.t. $\theta$:

$$g_t = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$$

### Step 3: Accumulate Squared Gradients
Update the diagonal accumulator $G_t$ by adding the element-wise square of the current gradient:

$$G_t = G_{t-1} + g_t \odot g_t$$

This is a **running sum of squared gradients** — a key characteristic that distinguishes AdaGrad from RMSProp (which uses exponential moving average).

### Step 4: Compute Parameter-wise Adaptive Update
Update parameters using the adapted learning rate:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}} \odot g_t$$

### Step 5: Repeat Until Convergence
Iterate steps 2-4 until $\|\theta_{t+1} - \theta_t\| < \delta$ (convergence threshold) or maximum iterations reached.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
| --- | --- |
| **Squaring gradients** | Ensures all accumulated values are **non-negative**, providing a magnitude-based scaling |
| **Square root in denominator** | Balances the aggressive effect of squaring, making scaling proportional to gradient magnitude |
| **Element-wise operations** | Each parameter $\theta_i$ has its **own adaptive learning rate** $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$ |
| **$\epsilon$ smoothing term** | Prevents division by zero when $G_{t,i} = 0$ (initial steps) |
| **Accumulation (not decay)** | The denominator **monotonically increases**, causing the effective learning rate to monotonically decrease |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula, condition, and metric you MUST memorize for KTU board exams.**

| Component | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Gradient Computation** | $g_t = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ | First-order partial derivative of loss w.r.t. parameters | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| **Accumulated Squared Gradient** | $G_t = G_{t-1} + g_t^2$ | Sum of past squared gradients per parameter | Non-negative scalar per dim. |
| **Adaptive Learning Rate** | $\eta_{t,i} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$ | Per-parameter effective learning rate at step $t$ | Dimensionless (per dim.) |
| **Parameter Update Rule** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_{t,i} \odot g_t$ | The canonical AdaGrad update | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| **Global Learning Rate** | $\eta$ | Hyperparameter, typically initialized to | **0.01** |
| **Smoothing Constant** | $\epsilon$ | Numerical stability term | **$10^{-8}$** |
| **Convergence Guarantee** | $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_{t,i} = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_{t,i}^2 < \infty$ | Regret bound for convex problems (Duchi et al., 2011) | Theoretical property |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

AdaGrad is widely used in:

1. **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) where feature vectors are sparse.
2. **Computer Vision:** Training deep CNNs on large-scale image datasets.
3. **Recommender Systems:** Sparse user-item interaction matrices.
4. **Online Learning:** Streaming data where features appear with varying frequencies.
5. **Large-Scale Distributed Training:** Sparse gradient updates in asynchronous SGD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In production systems like **Google's advertising platforms**, AdaGrad's ability to handle sparse features without per-parameter tuning makes it invaluable. However, its aggressive learning rate decay often necessitates switching to **Adam** or **AdaDelta** for non-convex deep learning tasks.

---

## 2.5 Critical Limitation (KTU-Favorite Question)

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Premature Decay" Problem:** Because $G_t$ is a **monotonically increasing** sum (never decreases), the denominator $\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}$ grows unboundedly. This causes the effective learning rate to **shrink to near zero**, stopping learning prematurely — especially problematic in **non-convex** deep learning landscapes where long training is essential.

This limitation directly motivates **RMSProp** (uses exponential moving average instead of cumulative sum) and **Adam**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Vanilla SGD to AdaGrad

### Starting Point: Vanilla Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

The standard SGD update is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot g_t$$

where $\eta$ is a **fixed scalar** applied uniformly to all parameters.

**Problem Statement:** This treats all parameters identically. In a 10-million-parameter neural network, some parameters may benefit from aggressive updates (under-trained features) while others need gentle updates (well-converged features). A single $\eta$ is suboptimal.

---

### Step 1: Introduce Per-Parameter Learning Rate

Replace scalar $\eta$ with a per-parameter vector $\eta_t$:

$$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \eta_{t,i} \cdot g_{t,i}$$

We need to design $\eta_{t,i}$ adaptively.

---

### Step 2: Design the Adaptive Rule

**Intuition:** If the gradient for parameter $i$ has been historically large (frequently large updates), reduce the learning rate. If gradients have been small, allow larger updates.

**Proposed adaptive rule:**

$$\eta_{t,i} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{h_{t,i}}}$$

where $h_{t,i}$ represents the **historical magnitude** of gradients for parameter $i$.

---

### Step 3: Define Historical Magnitude

The simplest choice for $h_{t,i}$ is the **sum of squared gradients**:

$$h_{t,i} = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^2$$

**Justification:** Squaring eliminates sign information (we care about magnitude), and summation accumulates historical evidence. This is the cornerstone of AdaGrad.

---

### Step 4: Combine into Recursive Form

Define the accumulator $G_{t,i} = h_{t,i}$. Then:

$$G_{t,i} = G_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^2$$

This is computationally efficient: instead of recomputing the sum, we use the previous value and add the new squared gradient.

---

### Step 5: Final Update Rule

Substituting back:

$$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}} \cdot g_{t,i}$$

**Why $\epsilon$?** At initialization, $G_{0,i} = 0$. Without $\epsilon$, we'd divide by zero. Adding $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$ ensures numerical stability while being small enough to not affect meaningful updates.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Apply AdaGrad for **2 iterations** on a 1D loss function $J(\theta) = (\theta - 5)^2$ starting at $\theta_0 = 10$, with $\eta = 0.1$ and $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$.

### Iteration $t = 0$:

**Step 1: Compute gradient.**

$$g_0 = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_0) = 2(\theta_0 - 5) = 2(10 - 5) = 10$$

**Step 2: Update accumulator.**

$$G_0 = 0 + g_0^2 = 0 + 100 = 100$$

**Step 3: Update parameter.**

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_0 + \epsilon}} \cdot g_0 = 10 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{100 + 10^{-8}}} \cdot 10$$

$$= 10 - \frac{0.1 \cdot 10}{10.000\ldots} = 10 - 0.1 = 9.9$$

---

### Iteration $t = 1$:

**Step 1: Compute gradient.**

$$g_1 = 2(\theta_1 - 5) = 2(9.9 - 5) = 2(4.9) = 9.8$$

**Step 2: Update accumulator.**

$$G_1 = G_0 + g_1^2 = 100 + 96.04 = 196.04$$

**Step 3: Update parameter.**

$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_1 + \epsilon}} \cdot g_1 = 9.9 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{196.04}} \cdot 9.8$$

$$= 9.9 - \frac{0.1 \cdot 9.8}{14.0014\ldots} = 9.9 - 0.07 = 9.83$$

**Observation:** The effective learning rate **decreased** from 0.1 (vanilla) to 0.01 (in iteration 0) to ~0.007 (in iteration 1), as expected.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Vanilla SGD | AdaGrad |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Learning Rate** | Fixed scalar $\eta$ | Per-parameter $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$ |
| **Gradient Memory** | None (memoryless) | Cumulative $G_t = \sum g_t^2$ |
| **Update Direction** | $-\eta g_t$ | $-\frac{\eta g_t}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}}$ |
| **Sparsity Handling** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Long-Term Training** | Stable | **Decays aggressively** (limitation) |
| **Hyperparameter Tuning** | Requires per-parameter tuning | Minimal tuning needed |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(d)$ per step | $O(d)$ per step (same!) |
| **Memory Overhead** | None | $O(d)$ for accumulator |
| **Convergence (Convex)** | Sublinear | Sublinear with adaptive regret |
| **Convergence (Non-Convex)** | Can stall | Often stalls prematurely |

---

## 3.4 Convergence Analysis (Theoretical Insight)

For convex loss functions, AdaGrad achieves a regret bound of:

$$R(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} J(\theta_t) - \min_\theta J(\theta) \leq \frac{\|\theta^* - \theta_1\|^2}{2\eta} \cdot \sqrt{T} + \eta \cdot \sqrt{T} \cdot \text{diag}(G_T)$$

This bound is **$O(\sqrt{T})$**, which is optimal for online convex optimization. This is a **board-exam favorite** theoretical result.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AdaGrad Algorithmic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize theta, G=0, eta, epsilon] --> B[Set t = 0]
    B --> C[Compute gradient g_t = grad of J w.r.t theta_t]
    C --> D[Update Accumulator: G_t = G_t-1 + g_t squared]
    D --> E[Compute Adaptive LR: alpha_i = eta divided by sqrt of G_t + epsilon]
    E --> F[Update Parameter: theta_t+1 = theta_t - alpha_i times g_t]
    F --> G{Convergence Check: norm of delta theta less than delta}
    G -->|No| H[Increment t: t = t + 1]
    H --> C
    G -->|Yes| I[Return Optimized theta]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style E fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Adaptive Optimizer Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    SGD[SGD: Fixed Learning Rate] --> AdaGrad[AdaGrad: Cumulative Squared Gradients]
    SGD --> Momentum[Momentum: Velocity Term]
    AdaGrad --> RMSProp[RMSProp: Exponential Moving Average]
    AdaGrad --> AdaDelta[AdaDelta: No Initial LR Required]
    RMSProp --> Adam[Adam: Momentum + RMSProp]
    AdaDelta --> Adam
    Momentum --> NAG[Nesterov Accelerated Gradient]
    NAG --> Nadam[Nadam: Nesterov + Adam]
    Adam --> Nadam
    
    style SGD fill:#64748b,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
    style AdaGrad fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style RMSProp fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style Adam fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style AdaDelta fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input: theta_t, g_t] --> AccumulatorBlock[Accumulator Module: G_t = G_t-1 + g_t squared]
    AccumulatorBlock --> AdaptiveLR[Adaptive LR Calculator: alpha_i = eta divided by sqrt of G_t + epsilon]
    Input --> AdaptiveLR
    AdaptiveLR --> UpdateEngine[Parameter Update Engine: theta_t+1 = theta_t - alpha_i times g_t]
    UpdateEngine --> Output[Output: theta_t+1]
    AccumulatorBlock --> MemoryStore[Memory Store: Stores G_t for next iteration]
    MemoryStore -.-> AccumulatorBlock
    
    style Input fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style AccumulatorBlock fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style AdaptiveLR fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style UpdateEngine fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff

```

    A[Iteration 0: Effective LR = eta] --> B[Iteration 10: Effective LR Decreased]
    B --> C[Iteration 100: Effective LR Very Small]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define AdaGrad. List its two main advantages over vanilla SGD.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AdaGrad (Adaptive Gradient Algorithm)** is an optimization algorithm that adapts the learning rate individually for each parameter by scaling it inversely proportional to the square root of the sum of all past squared gradients.

**Update Rule:**
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}} \odot g_t$$

**Two Key Advantages:**

1. **Per-Parameter Adaptive Learning Rate:** Each parameter $\theta_i$ gets its own effective learning rate, eliminating the need for manual per-parameter tuning — particularly useful for sparse data.

2. **Automatic Decay for High-Gradient Parameters:** Parameters with large historical gradients receive smaller updates, preventing oscillations and accelerating convergence in ill-conditioned problems.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is the role of the accumulator $G_t$ in AdaGrad? Why is $\epsilon$ added to it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of $G_t$:**

The accumulator $G_t$ stores the **cumulative sum of squared gradients** for each parameter from iteration $1$ to $t$. It is defined as:
$$G_t = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_\tau^2$$

It serves as the **denominator** in the adaptive learning rate calculation, ensuring that parameters with historically large gradients receive smaller learning rates and vice versa.

**Role of $\epsilon$:**

The smoothing constant $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-8}$) is added to $G_t$ in the denominator:
$$\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}}$$

This serves two purposes:
- **Prevents division by zero** when $G_t = 0$ (at the very first iteration).
- **Ensures numerical stability** during computation, avoiding undefined or infinite updates.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the AdaGrad parameter update rule starting from vanilla SGD. Clearly state the role of each component in the final equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the quadratic loss function $J(\theta) = (\theta - 4)^2$ starting at $\theta_0 = 12$ with $\eta = 0.5$ and $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$, perform **3 iterations** of AdaGrad. Show all calculations.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Starting Point — Vanilla SGD:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot g_t \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1: Introduce per-parameter learning rate $\eta_{t,i}$:**

$$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \eta_{t,i} \cdot g_{t,i} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2: Design adaptivity via historical gradient magnitude:**

We define the historical sum of squared gradients:
$$G_{t,i} = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^2 \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3: Inverse-square-root scaling (the AdaGrad design choice):**

$$\eta_{t,i} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 4: Final AdaGrad update rule:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}} \cdot g_{t,i} \quad \text{...(5)}}$$

**Role of each component:**

| Component | Role |
| --- | --- |
| $\eta$ (global LR) | Sets the **base scale** of all updates |
| $G_{t,i}$ (accumulator) | **Historical memory** of gradient magnitudes |
| $\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}$ | **Normalizing factor** that adapts learning rate |
| $\epsilon$ | **Numerical stabilizer** preventing division by zero |
| $g_{t,i}$ | **Current gradient direction and magnitude** |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $J(\theta) = (\theta - 4)^2$, $\theta_0 = 12$, $\eta = 0.5$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$

**Gradient Formula:** $g_t = 2(\theta_t - 4)$

**Initialization:** $G_0 = 0$

---

**Iteration 1: $t = 0 \rightarrow 1$**

$$g_0 = 2(12 - 4) = 2 \times 8 = 16$$

$$G_1 = G_0 + g_0^2 = 0 + 256 = 256$$

$$\theta_1 = 12 - \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{256 + 10^{-8}}} \times 16 = 12 - \frac{8}{16} = 12 - 0.5 = 11.5$$

---

**Iteration 2: $t = 1 \rightarrow 2$**

$$g_1 = 2(11.5 - 4) = 2 \times 7.5 = 15$$

$$G_2 = G_1 + g_1^2 = 256 + 225 = 481$$

$$\theta_2 = 11.5 - \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{481 + 10^{-8}}} \times 15 = 11.5 - \frac{7.5}{21.93} \approx 11.5 - 0.342 = 11.158$$

---

**Iteration 3: $t = 2 \rightarrow 3$**

$$g_2 = 2(11.158 - 4) = 2 \times 7.158 = 14.316$$

$$G_3 = G_2 + g_2^2 = 481 + 204.95 = 685.95$$

$$\theta_3 = 11.158 - \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{685.95}} \times 14.316 = 11.158 - \frac{7.158}{26.19} \approx 11.158 - 0.273 = 10.885$$

**Final Result:** $\theta_3 \approx 10.885$ (moving toward optimum $\theta^* = 4$ with decaying step sizes).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the AdaGrad algorithm in detail with its update equations. Discuss how it differs from standard gradient descent in handling sparse features.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List and explain **three major limitations** of AdaGrad. Briefly describe how **RMSProp** addresses these limitations.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**AdaGrad Algorithm (Detailed):**

**Step 1:** Initialize $\theta_0$, $G_0 = 0$, choose $\eta$, $\epsilon$.

**Step 2:** For each iteration $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:

- Compute gradient: $g_t = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$
- Update accumulator: $G_t = G_{t-1} + g_t^2$
- Update parameters: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}} \odot g_t$

**Handling Sparse Features — The Key Insight:**

In sparse data (e.g., NLP bag-of-words, recommender systems), most features are **zero for any given sample**. Standard SGD applies the same $\eta$ to all parameters, meaning:
- **Frequent features** (large $G_t$): Get large updates, may overshoot.
- **Rare features** (small $G_t$): Get the same small update despite being undertrained.

AdaGrad **inverts this**:
- For a **rare feature**, $G_{t,i}$ remains small → $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$ remains **large** → the rare feature gets a **bigger effective learning rate**.
- For a **frequent feature**, $G_{t,i}$ grows large → effective LR **shrinks** → preventing over-correction.

This makes AdaGrad particularly powerful for **sparse gradient scenarios** common in NLP and large-scale web applications.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Three Major Limitations of AdaGrad:**

**Limitation 1: Premature Learning Rate Decay**

Because $G_t = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_\tau^2$ is a **monotonically increasing** sum, the denominator grows without bound. The effective learning rate eventually becomes vanishingly small, and the algorithm **stops learning prematurely** — even if the model has not converged.

**Limitation 2: No Forgetting Mechanism**

AdaGrad treats all past gradients **equally**. A noisy gradient from 1000 iterations ago contributes as much to $G_t$ as the most recent gradient. This makes the algorithm **slow to adapt** to changing loss landscapes, particularly harmful in non-convex problems.

**Limitation 3: Requires Manual Initial Learning Rate $\eta$**

AdaGrad still requires choosing $\eta$ (typically 0.01). If mis-specified, the adaptive scaling cannot fully compensate, and the algorithm may diverge or converge too slowly.

**How RMSProp Addresses These:**

RMSProp (Hinton, 2012) replaces the cumulative sum with an **exponentially decaying moving average**:

$$G_t = \beta G_{t-1} + (1 - \beta) g_t^2$$

where $\beta \in [0, 1)$ (typically 0.9). This:
- **Decays the influence** of old gradients (forgetting mechanism).
- **Prevents unbounded growth** of the accumulator.
- Allows learning to **continue indefinitely** in non-convex landscapes.

RMSProp is the **direct successor** to AdaGrad, fixing its core decay problem while preserving per-parameter adaptivity.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $\epsilon$ term** (1 mark deduction) — Always include it in the denominator, even when $G_t = 0$.
> 2. **Confusing $G_t$ with raw gradient** — $G_t$ is the **sum of squared gradients**, not the gradient itself.
> 3. **Using scalar $\eta$ instead of per-parameter $\eta_{t,i}$** — Examiners specifically look for the per-parameter notation $\eta_{t,i} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_{t,i} + \epsilon}}$.
> 4. **Forgetting element-wise operation** — Use $\odot$ (Hadamard product), not standard multiplication.
> 5. **Missing the square root** — A common error is writing $\frac{\eta}{G_t + \epsilon}$ instead of $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}}$.
> 6. **Numerical calculation errors in worked examples** — Show every step; partial credit depends on visible work.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for AdaGrad:**

### Core Concept
- AdaGrad = **Adaptive Gradient Algorithm** with per-parameter learning rate scaling.
- Introduced by **Duchi, Hazan, Singer (2011)**.
- Belongs to the **adaptive optimizer family**.

### Must-Memorize Equation
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}} \odot g_t$$

### Three Key Components
1. **$G_t$**: Accumulator of squared gradients (cumulative sum).
2. **$\eta$**: Initial learning rate (typical: **0.01**).
3. **$\epsilon$**: Smoothing constant (typical: **$10^{-8}$**).

### Five Critical Properties
- **Per-parameter adaptivity**: Each $\theta_i$ has unique effective LR.
- **Sparsity-friendly**: Large updates for rare features.
- **Monotonic decay**: Effective LR **never increases** (key limitation).
- **Convex-optimal**: $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret bound for convex problems.
- **No hyperparameter tuning per parameter**: Major practical advantage.

### Key Limitation (Board-Favorite)
- **Premature convergence** due to monotonically increasing $G_t$ — fixed by **RMSProp** using exponential moving average.

### Comparison Anchors
- **AdaGrad vs SGD**: Adaptive vs fixed LR.
- **AdaGrad vs RMSProp**: Cumulative sum vs exponential decay.
- **AdaGrad vs Adam**: Adam adds **momentum** on top of RMSProp-style adaptivity.

### Engineering Applications
- **NLP word embeddings**, **recommender systems**, **sparse feature training**, **online learning**, **large-scale distributed SGD**.

### KTU Exam Hot Topics
- Derive the update rule from vanilla SGD.
- Explain the role of $G_t$ and $\epsilon$.
- Compare AdaGrad with SGD and RMSProp.
- Discuss limitations and motivate successor algorithms.
- Solve numerical problems with explicit iteration calculations.

# 📘 Module 2 — RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔬 Formal Academic Definition
**RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation)** is an *adaptive learning rate optimization algorithm* designed for training deep neural networks. It was introduced by **Geoffrey E. Hinton** in his 2012 Coursera lecture on Neural Networks for Machine Learning. The algorithm addresses two long-standing problems of stochastic gradient descent (SGD): (i) the **ravine problem**, where the loss surface has steep curvature in one direction and shallow curvature in another, causing oscillations, and (ii) the **vanishing/exploding gradient magnitudes** across parameter dimensions. RMSProp maintains a *per-parameter exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA)* of the *squared* gradients and normalizes the gradient update by the square root of this average, effectively dividing each parameter's update by a recent estimate of its gradient magnitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RMSProp is grouped under *Adaptive Optimization Methods* alongside **AdaGrad**, **AdaDelta**, **Adam**, and **Momentum-based SGD**. Students must know the **update equations, decay rate (β), and the role of the ε-stabilizer**.

### 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Walking Down a Foggy Mountain"**

Imagine you are blindfolded (loss function is unknown) and trying to descend a mountain. You can only feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet (the gradient). In some directions, the mountain drops steeply; in others, it is nearly flat. Without help, you would either overshoot the cliff (exploding gradient) or creep too slowly on plateaus (vanishing gradient).

**RMSProp is like giving you a "smart walking stick" that remembers the recent intensity of the slope in every direction.** If a direction has historically been steep, the stick *shortens your stride* in that direction (by dividing the step size by a large number). If a direction has historically been gentle, the stick *lengthens your stride* (by dividing by a small number). This way, your walk converges smoothly toward the valley floor regardless of how uneven the terrain is.

### 📌 Standard Hyperparameter Values (as published in literature)
- **Decay rate (β) = 0.9** (default in Geoff Hinton's lecture; 0.99 is also common)
- **Learning rate (η) = 0.001**
- **Smoothing constant (ε) = 1 × 10⁻⁸** (prevents division by zero)
- **Initialization: E[g²]₀ = 0**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### ⚙️ Algorithmic Rationale — Why Does RMSProp Work?

The algorithm is built on **three pillars of logic**:

1. **Pillar 1 — Exponentially Decaying Memory of Squared Gradients**
   Instead of accumulating *all* past squared gradients (as AdaGrad does, which causes the learning rate to shrink to zero prematurely), RMSProp uses an **exponential moving average (EWMA)**. This means recent gradients weigh more heavily, while very old gradients "decay away." The result is a non-vanishing effective learning rate.

2. **Pillar 2 — Per-Parameter Normalization**
   The square root of the moving average is computed **independently for each parameter θᵢ**. This means each parameter gets its own effective learning rate, scaled inversely to the recent magnitude of its gradients.

3. **Pillar 3 — Bias-Free Update via ε-Stabilizer**
   The term **ε** (epsilon) is added inside the square root to ensure the denominator is never zero when parameters have not yet received significant gradients (especially at initialization).

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $g_t$ | Gradient of loss w.r.t. parameter at time step $t$ | Computed via backpropagation |
| $\beta$ | Decay rate for moving average | **0.9** |
| $\eta$ | Global learning rate | **0.001** |
| $\varepsilon$ | Numerical stability constant | **1 × 10⁻⁸** |
| $E[g^2]_t$ | Moving average of squared gradients | Initialized to 0 |
| $\theta_t$ | Parameter vector at time step $t$ | Initialized randomly |

| Equation # | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **E1** | $E[g^2]_t = \beta \cdot E[g^2]_{t-1} + (1-\beta) \cdot g_t^2$ | Maintain EWMA of squared gradients |
| **E2** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \dfrac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^2]_t + \varepsilon}} \cdot g_t$ | Update rule (parameter step) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the equation:** "Square the past, normalize the present." You square the recent gradients, average them, take the square root, and use that to **rescale** the current gradient step.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Computer Vision:** Training ResNet, VGG, and EfficientNet architectures on ImageNet where non-stationary objectives are common.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Fine-tuning Transformer-based models (BERT, GPT) where gradient magnitudes vary dramatically across embedding and attention layers.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Optimizing policy networks where the loss landscape is notoriously non-stationary.
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** RNN/LSTM training where exploding gradients are a chronic issue.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔢 Complete Mathematical Derivation of the RMSProp Update Rule

We begin from the **vanilla SGD** update and systematically motivate each modification that leads to RMSProp.

#### **Step 1 — Vanilla Stochastic Gradient Descent (Baseline)**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot g_t
$$

where $g_t = \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_t)$ is the gradient of the loss function $L$ with respect to parameters $\theta$ at iteration $t$, and $\eta$ is the global learning rate.

**Problem:** A *single* learning rate $\eta$ is used for **all** parameters. On surfaces with high curvature (ravines), this causes violent oscillations.

---

#### **Step 2 — AdaGrad's Contribution (Per-Parameter Accumulation)**

AdaGrad introduced the idea of accumulating squared gradients per parameter:

$$
s_t = s_{t-1} + g_t^2
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{s_t + \varepsilon}} \cdot g_t
$$

**Problem:** Since $s_t$ is a *monotonically increasing* sum, the effective learning rate $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{s_t + \varepsilon}}$ *shrinks forever* and eventually becomes infinitesimally small — training halts prematurely.

---

#### **Step 3 — RMSProp's Innovation: Replace Sum with Exponential Moving Average**

Replace the cumulative sum $s_t$ with an exponentially weighted moving average:

$$
E[g^2]_t = \beta \cdot E[g^2]_{t-1} + (1-\beta) \cdot g_t^2
$$

> **Why this fix works:** Because of the $\beta$ multiplier on the previous average, old gradients decay exponentially fast. The denominator does **not** grow without bound; it tracks only the **recent** magnitude of gradients.

---

#### **Step 4 — Final RMSProp Parameter Update**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^2]_t + \varepsilon}} \cdot g_t
$$

> **Interpretation of the term $\sqrt{E[g^2]_t}$:** In statistics, the *root mean square (RMS)* of a signal is exactly $\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[X^2]}$. Hence the algorithm's name — **R**oot **M**ean **S**quare **Prop**agation.

---

#### **Step 5 — Unrolling the Moving Average (Intuition)**

Let us expand $E[g^2]_t$ for clarity:

$$
E[g^2]_t = (1-\beta) \cdot g_t^2 + (1-\beta)\beta \cdot g_{t-1}^2 + (1-\beta)\beta^2 \cdot g_{t-2}^2 + \dots
$$

This is a **geometric series** with ratio $\beta$. The "effective memory" of past gradients is approximately $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ iterations.

- If $\beta = 0.9$ → effective memory ≈ **10 iterations**
- If $\beta = 0.99$ → effective memory ≈ **100 iterations**

---

#### **Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose a single scalar parameter $\theta$ has gradient sequence $g_1 = 2$, $g_2 = 1$, $g_3 = 0.5$ with $\beta = 0.9$, $\eta = 0.01$, $\varepsilon = 10^{-8}$, $\theta_0 = 1$.

| Iter $t$ | $g_t$ | $g_t^2$ | $E[g^2]_t = 0.9 E[g^2]_{t-1} + 0.1 g_t^2$ | $\sqrt{E[g^2]_t + \varepsilon}$ | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - 0.01 \cdot \frac{g_t}{\sqrt{\cdot}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 4.00 | $0.9(0) + 0.1(4.0) = 0.4000$ | $\sqrt{0.4000} = 0.6325$ | $1 - 0.01 \cdot (2/0.6325) = 1 - 0.0316 = 0.9684$ |
| 2 | 1.0 | 1.00 | $0.9(0.4) + 0.1(1.0) = 0.4600$ | $\sqrt{0.4600} = 0.6782$ | $0.9684 - 0.01 \cdot (1/0.6782) = 0.9684 - 0.01474 = 0.9537$ |
| 3 | 0.5 | 0.25 | $0.9(0.46) + 0.1(0.25) = 0.4390$ | $\sqrt{0.4390} = 0.6626$ | $0.9537 - 0.01 \cdot (0.5/0.6626) = 0.9537 - 0.00755 = 0.9462$ |

> **Observation:** Notice how the effective learning rate (the step size per unit gradient) *automatically adapts* — it was 0.0316, 0.0147, and 0.0076 across the three iterations as the gradients shrank.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Mermaid Flowchart — RMSProp Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize parameters theta and E_g_sq = 0] --> B[Set hyperparameters: eta, beta, epsilon]
    B --> C[For each training iteration t = 1, 2, 3 ...]
    C --> D[Step 1: Compute gradient g_t via backpropagation]
    D --> E[Step 2: Compute squared gradient g_t squared]
    E --> F[Step 3: Update moving average E_g_sq_t = beta times E_g_sq_prev + 1 minus beta times g_t squared]
    F --> G[Step 4: Compute RMS denominator = sqrt E_g_sq_t + epsilon]
    G --> H[Step 5: Compute adaptive update step = eta times g_t divided by RMS denominator]
    H --> I[Step 6: Update parameter theta_t plus 1 = theta_t minus update step]
    I --> J[Step 7: Evaluate convergence criteria met?]
    J -->|No| C
    J -->|Yes| K[End: Return optimized parameters theta]
```

### 🧩 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Conceptual Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        p1[Current Parameters theta_t]
        p2[Gradient g_t from Backprop]
        p3[Hyperparameters eta, beta, epsilon]
    end

    subgraph CORE[RMSProp Core Engine]
        q1[Squaring Unit: g_t squared]
        q2[Exponential Moving Average Unit]
        q3[Square Root and Stabilization Unit]
        q4[Adaptive Step Calculator]
    end

    subgraph MEM[Memory State]
        r1[E_g_sq_t_minus_1 stored]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        s1[Updated Parameters theta_t_plus_1]
    end

    p2 --> q1
    q1 --> q2
    r1 --> q2
    p3 --> q4
    q2 --> q3
    q3 --> q4
    q4 --> s1
    q2 --> r1
```

### 📊 Comparative Schematic — RMSProp vs AdaGrad vs SGD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[SGD: Single global learning rate eta] --> A1[Oscillates on ravines]
    A1 --> A2[Convergence depends on manual tuning]

    B0[AdaGrad: Cumulative sum of squared gradients] --> B1[Per-parameter learning rate]
    B1 --> B2[Effective rate decays to zero and stalls training]

    C0[RMSProp: Exponential moving average of squared gradients] --> C1[Per-parameter adaptive learning rate]
    C1 --> C2[Effective rate never vanishes; stable convergence]

    A2 -.competes with.-> C2
    B2 -.solved by.-> C2
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

Define the **RMSProp optimization algorithm**. State its two key update equations and explain the role of the decay rate **β**.

**Model Answer:**

RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation) is an adaptive learning rate optimization algorithm proposed by **Geoffrey Hinton (2012)**. It maintains a per-parameter exponentially weighted moving average of the squared gradients to normalize the parameter updates.

The two key equations are:

$$
E[g^2]_t = \beta \cdot E[g^2]_{t-1} + (1-\beta) \cdot g_t^2
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^2]_t + \varepsilon}} \cdot g_t
$$

**Role of β:** The decay rate β ∈ [0,1) controls how much of the previous moving average is retained. A typical value is **β = 0.9**, giving an effective memory of approximately **10 iterations**. Higher β values (e.g., 0.99) give longer memory and smoother averaging; lower β values make the algorithm react faster to recent gradients.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

Differentiate between **AdaGrad** and **RMSProp** with respect to how they accumulate squared gradient information.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | AdaGrad | RMSProp |
|---|---|---|
| Accumulation type | **Cumulative sum** of all past squared gradients | **Exponentially weighted moving average** of squared gradients |
| Learning rate behavior | **Monotonically decreasing**, eventually vanishes | **Non-monotonic**, never strictly vanishes |
| Suitable for | Sparse data (e.g., NLP embeddings) | Non-stationary objectives (e.g., RNNs, deep CNNs) |
| Update equation | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{t} g_i^2 + \varepsilon}} g_t$ | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^2]_t + \varepsilon}} g_t$ |

The **key difference** is that AdaGrad's accumulator grows unboundedly, causing the effective learning rate to shrink to zero and stall training, whereas RMSProp's EWMA forgets old gradients exponentially and keeps training alive.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Derive the **RMSProp parameter update rule** starting from the limitations of vanilla SGD. Clearly explain each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following scalar gradient sequence for a parameter θ initialized at **θ₀ = 5.0**, with **η = 0.1**, **β = 0.9**, **ε = 10⁻⁸**:

$$
g_1 = 4.0, \quad g_2 = 2.0, \quad g_3 = 1.0
$$

Compute **θ₁, θ₂, and θ₃** using RMSProp. Show all intermediate values of $E[g^2]_t$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Vanilla SGD:** The baseline parameter update is

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot g_t
$$

This uses a **single global learning rate η**, which causes oscillations on loss surfaces with steep curvature (ravines) and slow progress on plateaus.

**Step 2 — Introducing per-parameter scaling:** To address this, we seek a way to scale the learning rate inversely with the recent gradient magnitude. Define a quantity that tracks the *size* of recent gradients.

**Step 3 — Exponentially Weighted Moving Average of Squared Gradients:**

$$
E[g^2]_t = \beta \cdot E[g^2]_{t-1} + (1-\beta) \cdot g_t^2
$$

- **$g_t^2$** squares the current gradient to keep the sign-positive magnitude.
- **(1−β)** weights the current observation.
- **β · E[g²]ₜ₋₁** retains a fraction of the previous average, giving exponential memory.
- **β ∈ [0,1)** ensures old gradients decay geometrically.

**Step 4 — Final RMSProp Update:**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{E[g^2]_t + \varepsilon}} \cdot g_t
$$

- **√(E[g²]ₜ)** is the root mean square of recent gradients — it normalizes the gradient to unit RMS magnitude.
