You must learn from scratch that "eyes are near the top", "noses are in the middle", and that the same face can appear in the *left corner* of one photo and the *right corner* of another. This is computationally infeasible for large images.

- **Convolution + Pooling (Strong Prior):** Before you even start, your instructor tells you: *"Look for small, local patterns (a small patch of pixels resembling an eye, an edge, a curve). Use the **same detective tool** to look for that pattern **anywhere in the photograph** — top-left, center, bottom-right. And once you find a region of interest, do not worry about the **exact pixel position**; just record whether the pattern was present."*

These three pieces of pre-baked wisdom — **locality, weight sharing, and shift-invariance** — constitute the *infinitely strong prior*. They are baked into the very architecture of the network, not learned from data.

### 1.4 Why "Infinitely Strong"?

The strength of a Bayesian prior corresponds to the **inverse of its variance** (concentration). An infinitely strong prior is essentially a **Dirac delta distribution** over a restricted subspace of functions:

$$p(\theta) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{Z} & \text{if } \theta \in \mathcal{H}_{\text{conv}} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\mathcal{H}_{\text{conv}}$ is the restricted hypothesis class of all functions representable by a CNN of fixed architecture. For any $\theta \notin \mathcal{H}_{\text{conv}}$, the prior is exactly **zero** — hence "infinite strength".

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Consequence:** A CNN with $K$ filters of size $k \times k$ operating on an $n \times n$ image has only $K \cdot k^2 + K$ (biases) trainable parameters, while a fully connected layer from $n^2$ inputs to $m^2$ outputs has $n^2 \cdot m^2$ parameters. The CNN *cannot* learn the parameter pattern of an unrestricted fully connected layer even if it wanted to — the architecture forbids it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bayesian / Probabilistic View of the Prior

In the Bayesian framework, learning is inference of a function $f^*$ given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}$. The posterior over parameters is:

$$p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \cdot p(\theta)$$

Here $p(\theta)$ is the prior. For a standard MLP, $p(\theta)$ is typically a broad Gaussian (or similar weak prior) over a high-dimensional space $\mathbb{R}^{P}$. For a CNN, the prior is **degenerate** — its support is a tiny, low-dimensional manifold inside $\mathbb{R}^{P}$ corresponding exactly to the convolutional structure.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of the CNN Prior — Detailed Breakdown

#### Pillar 1: Sparse Interactions (Local Connectivity)
- In a fully connected layer, every output unit $y_i$ depends on *every* input unit $x_j$ via a unique weight $W_{i,j}$.
- In a convolutional layer with kernel size $k$, output $y_{i,j}$ depends on only a $k \times k$ patch of inputs: $x_{i-\lfloor k/2 \rfloor, j-\lfloor k/2 \rfloor}, \dots, x_{i+\lfloor k/2 \rfloor, j+\lfloor k/2 \rfloor}$.
- **Consequence:** Number of parameters drops from $O(n^2 \cdot m^2)$ to $O(k^2 \cdot m^2)$ — an exponential reduction.

#### Pillar 2: Parameter Sharing (Stationarity of Statistics)
- A single kernel $\mathbf{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$ is convolved across the *entire* input.
- Mathematically, this means weight $W_{i,j,k,l}$ depends only on the offset $(k, l)$ from the output position, not on $(i, j)$ itself.
- **Consequence:** The same feature detector (e.g., a vertical edge) is searched for at every spatial location. This is exactly the assumption that natural image statistics are *stationary* — the rules of "what an edge looks like" do not change as you move across the image.

#### Pillar 3: Translation Invariance via Pooling
- A pooling function $\text{pool}(\cdot)$ replaces a small region of feature maps with a single summary statistic.
- **Max pooling:** $\text{pool}(\mathcal{R}) = \max_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{R}} a_{i,j}$
- **Average pooling:** $\text{pool}(\mathcal{R}) = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{R}|} \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{R}} a_{i,j}$
- **Consequence:** A small shift in the input by $\delta$ pixels produces at most a small change in the pooled output. The output is approximately invariant to translations up to the pooling region's size.

### 2.3 Disentangling Convolution vs. Pooling as Priors

| Aspect | Convolution | Pooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of prior** | Structural prior on *weights* | Functional prior on *activations* |
| **What is restricted** | The values and connectivity of $W$ | The invariance properties of $f$ |
| **Mathematical form** | Sparse + tied weight matrix | Aggregation operator with a stride |
| **Origin in Goodfellow et al.** | Sections 9.2, 9.3 | Section 9.3 (Pooling), 9.4 (Invariance) |
| **Strength** | Infinitely strong (zero probability to non-convolutional weights) | Infinitely strong in the sense of removing positional info |
| **Cost** | Reduced representational flexibility | Possible loss of fine-grained information |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU ESE questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Notation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2D Convolution (discrete) | $S(i,j) = (K * X)(i,j) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} X(i-m, j-n) \cdot K(m,n)$ | $K$: kernel, $X$: input | KTU 2024 expects cross-correlation in practice; see Sec. 2.5 |
| 2 | Cross-correlation (used in practice) | $S(i,j) = (K \star X)(i,j) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} X(i+m, j+n) \cdot K(m,n)$ | $\star$ denotes cross-correlation | Equivalent to conv. with flipped kernel |
| 3 | Sparse interactions | $\#\text{params} = k^2 \cdot C_{\text{out}} \cdot C_{\text{in}}$ | $k$: kernel size | vs. $n^2 \cdot m^2$ for FC |
| 4 | Parameter sharing | $W_{i,j,k,l} = W_{i',j',k,l}$ for all valid $(i,j), (i',j')$ | — | Reduces parameters by factor $\approx n^2$ |
| 5 | Receptive field size | $r_l = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot s_{l-1}$ | $s_{l-1}$: stride of prev. layer | Grows with depth |
| 6 | Output spatial dim | $o = \left\lfloor \frac{n + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $p$: padding, $s$: stride, $k$: kernel | Standard formula |
| 7 | Max pooling | $y_{i,j} = \max_{(m,n) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} a_{m,n}$ | $\mathcal{R}_{i,j}$: pooling window | Translation-invariant up to $k$ pixels |
| 8 | Average pooling | $y_{i,j} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{R}_{i,j} \vert} \sum_{(m,n) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} a_{m,n}$ | — | Smoother invariance |
| 9 | L2 pooling | $y_{i,j} = \sqrt{\sum_{(m,n) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} a_{m,n}^2}$ | — | Equivalent to average of squared magnitudes |
| 10 | Infinitely strong prior indicator | $p(\theta) = 0$ if $\theta \notin \mathcal{H}_{\text{CNN}}$ | — | Dirac-delta-like restriction |
| 11 | Parameter count ratio | $\frac{P_{\text{CNN}}}{P_{\text{FC}}} = \frac{k^2 C_{\text{out}}}{n^2}$ for one conv. layer vs. one FC | — | Demonstrates efficiency |
| 12 | Equivariance property of conv. | $f(g \cdot x) = g \cdot f(x)$ where $g$ is a translation | $g$: group action | Convolution is *equivariant*, not invariant |
| 13 | Invariance property of pool. | $f(g \cdot x) = f(x)$ | $g$: small translation | Pooling yields true invariance |
| 14 | Stationarity assumption | $p(\text{image patch at } \mathbf{u}) = p(\text{image patch at } \mathbf{u} + \delta)$ | — | Natural images satisfy this approximately |

### 2.5 A Subtle But KTU-Favorite Nuance

In formal mathematics, *convolution* requires flipping the kernel:

$$(K * X)(i,j) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} X(i-m, j-n) \cdot K(m,n)$$

In deep learning, most libraries (TensorFlow, PyTorch) actually implement *cross-correlation* without flipping, calling it "convolution". Goodfellow et al. note this abuse of terminology. Be ready to mention this in long-answer questions — it is a favourite examiner pitfall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This "infinitely strong prior" is exactly what allows CNNs to be trained on **datasets of practical size**. Without it, training a 224×224 image classifier from scratch would require millions of labeled examples even with state-of-the-art optimizers. With the prior, models like ResNet-50 achieve >90% top-5 accuracy on ImageNet with ~1.2M images.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Fully Connected to Convolutional — Showing the Parameter Reduction

**Problem Setup:** Consider a single hidden layer operating on a grayscale image $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ to produce a feature map $S \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$.

**Step 1 — Fully Connected Formulation:**
Every output $S(i,j)$ is computed as:

$$S(i,j) = \sigma\left( \sum_{u=0}^{n-1} \sum_{v=0}^{n-1} W_{i,j,u,v} \cdot X(u,v) + b_{i,j} \right)$$

Total parameters: $P_{\text{FC}} = m^2 \cdot n^2 + m^2$ (weights + biases).

**Step 2 — Introducing Local Receptive Field (Sparse Interactions):**
Restrict each $S(i,j)$ to depend on a $k \times k$ patch centered at $(i, j)$:

$$S(i,j) = \sigma\left( \sum_{u=0}^{k-1} \sum_{v=0}^{k-1} W_{i,j,u,v} \cdot X(i+u, j+v) + b \right)$$

Parameters: $P_{\text{local}} = m^2 \cdot k^2 + m^2$ — a reduction by a factor of $(n/k)^2$.

**Step 3 — Introducing Parameter Sharing (Stationarity):**
Impose that $W_{i,j,u,v}$ does not depend on $(i, j)$, only on offsets $(u, v)$:

$$W_{i,j,u,v} = K(u, v) \quad \forall \, (i, j)$$

where $K$ is a single $k \times k$ kernel shared across all spatial positions.

$$S(i,j) = \sigma\left( \sum_{u=0}^{k-1} \sum_{v=0}^{k-1} K(u, v) \cdot X(i+u, j+v) + b \right)$$

Parameters: $P_{\text{conv}} = k^2 + 1$ — a reduction by a factor of $m^2$ on top of the local reduction.

**Step 4 — Ratio of Parameter Counts:**

$$\frac{P_{\text{conv}}}{P_{\text{FC}}} = \frac{k^2 + 1}{m^2 \cdot n^2 + m^2} \approx \frac{k^2}{m^2 \cdot n^2}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-favourite):** Let $n = m = 32$, $k = 3$.

$$P_{\text{FC}} = 32^2 \cdot 32^2 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ parameters}$$

$$P_{\text{conv}} = 3^2 + 1 = 10 \text{ parameters}$$

$$\text{Reduction factor} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{10} \approx 1.05 \times 10^5$$

This drastic reduction is precisely the manifestation of the **infinitely strong prior** — the model is *not allowed* to consider the $1{,}048{,}566$ other weight configurations, no matter how good they might fit the training data.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Translation Equivariance of Convolution

**Goal:** Show that shifting the input by $\delta$ pixels shifts the output by the same $\delta$ pixels.

**Step 1 — Define a shift operator:** Let $(T_{\delta} X)(i, j) = X(i - \delta_x, j - \delta_y)$ for shift vector $\delta = (\delta_x, \delta_y)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the convolution of the shifted input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(K * (T_{\delta} X))(i, j) &= \sum_{u=0}^{k-1} \sum_{v=0}^{k-1} K(u, v) \cdot (T_{\delta} X)(i - u, j - v) \\
&= \sum_{u=0}^{k-1} \sum_{v=0}^{k-1} K(u, v) \cdot X(i - u - \delta_x, j - v - \delta_y) \\
&= (K * X)(i - \delta_x, j - \delta_y) \\
&= (T_{\delta} (K * X))(i, j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** We have shown that the convolution operator commutes with translation:

$$K * T_{\delta} X = T_{\delta} (K * X)$$

This is the formal statement of **translation equivariance**. The output transforms in the same way as the input.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Approximate Translation Invariance of Max Pooling

**Goal:** Show that a small input shift changes the max-pooled output by at most a bounded amount.

**Step 1 — Let $a_{i,j}$ be the activations entering a $2 \times 2$ max pooling window $\mathcal{R}_{i,j}$.**
The output is:

$$y_{i,j} = \max_{(m, n) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} a_{m,n}$$

**Step 2 — Suppose the input is shifted by $(\delta_x, \delta_y)$ with $\vert \delta_x \vert < 1, \vert \delta_y \vert < 1$.**
The shifted activations $a'_{m,n} = a_{m - \delta_x, n - \delta_y}$ and the new pooled output is:

$$y'_{i,j} = \max_{(m, n) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} a_{m - \delta_x, n - \delta_y}$$

**Step 3 — By continuity of $a$ (assuming Lipschitz with constant $L$):**

$$\vert y_{i,j} - y'_{i,j} \vert \leq L \cdot \sqrt{\delta_x^2 + \delta_y^2}$$

**Step 4 — As $\delta \to 0$, the change in the pooled output vanishes.** Hence max pooling is **approximately translation-invariant** for sub-pixel shifts, and **exactly invariant** for shifts equal to the pooling window size (when the pooling windows tile without overlap and the max is taken over disjoint regions, a shift by the window size merely reorders which region gets pooled).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Architectures Under the Prior Lens

| Network | Hypothesis Class $\mathcal{H}$ | Effective Prior | Posterior Concentration | Sample Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully Connected MLP** | All measurable functions $f: \mathbb{R}^{n^2} \to \mathbb{R}^{m^2}$ | Flat Gaussian over $\mathbb{R}^{P}$ | Diffuse | Low (needs lots of data) |
| **Locally Connected** (no weight sharing) | Functions with local receptive fields | Sparse support on dense weight tensors | Slightly concentrated | Medium |
| **Convolutional** | Functions with local + shared weights | Dirac-like on conv. weight manifold | Highly concentrated | High |
| **CNN + Pooling** | Locally-supported, shift-invariant functions | Above + invariance constraints | Maximally concentrated | Highest (for natural images) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Receptive Field Calculation

**Problem:** A CNN has three conv layers with kernel sizes $k_1 = 3, k_2 = 3, k_3 = 3$ and strides $s_1 = 1, s_2 = 1, s_3 = 1$. What is the receptive field of a single output unit in layer 3?

**Step 1:** Initialize $r_0 = 1$ (a single input pixel covers itself).

**Step 2:** Apply the recurrence $r_l = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot s_{l-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= r_0 + (k_1 - 1) \cdot s_0 = 1 + (3 - 1) \cdot 1 = 3 \\
r_2 &= r_1 + (k_2 - 1) \cdot s_1 = 3 + (3 - 1) \cdot 1 = 5 \\
r_3 &= r_2 + (k_3 - 1) \cdot s_2 = 5 + (3 - 1) \cdot 1 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Each output unit in layer 3 sees a $7 \times 7$ patch of the input image.

**Interpretation for the Prior:** Notice how the *local* inductive bias of $k = 3$ kernels is *not* restrictive in the long run — three stacked $3 \times 3$ convolutions can represent a function whose effective receptive field is $7 \times 7$, while still only using $3 \cdot (3 \times 3) = 27$ parameters per filter (plus biases). The architectural prior forces the network to compose local features into hierarchical global features, which is exactly the assumption underlying the success of CNNs on natural images.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1: The Three Pillars of the Infinitely Strong Prior

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates how convolution and pooling operations collectively instantiate the three core inductive biases of a CNN.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputImage["Input Image X (n x n pixels)"] --> Pillars{"Infinitely Strong Prior"}
    Pillars --> LocalConn["Pillar 1: Local Receptive Fields"]
    Pillars --> ParamShare["Pillar 2: Parameter Sharing"]
    Pillars --> TransInv["Pillar 3: Translation Invariance"]

    LocalConn --> LocalDesc["Each output sees only a k x k patch. Parameters reduced from n^2 m^2 to k^2 m^2"]
    ParamShare --> ShareDesc["Same kernel K applied at every position. Parameters further reduced to k^2 + 1"]
    TransInv --> PoolDesc["Max or average pooling. Output unchanged under small input shifts"]

    LocalDesc --> ConvLayer["Convolutional Layer"]
    ShareDesc --> ConvLayer
    PoolDesc --> PoolLayer["Pooling Layer"]
    ConvLayer --> PoolLayer
    PoolLayer --> FeatureMap["Output Feature Maps"]
```

### 4.2 Figure 2: Comparison of Connectivity Patterns

This Mermaid diagram contrasts a fully connected layer with a convolutional layer, emphasizing the parameter reduction that defines the strong prior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FC["Fully Connected Layer"]
        direction LR
        X1["x1"] --> H1["h1"]
        X1 --> H2["h2"]
        X1 --> H3["h3"]
        X2["x2"] --> H1
        X2 --> H2
        X2 --> H3
        X3["x3"] --> H1
        X3 --> H2
        X3 --> H3
    end

    subgraph CONV["Convolutional Layer (k equals 2)"]
        direction LR
        Y1["x1"] --> O1["h1"]
        Y2["x2"] --> O1
        Y3["x3"] --> O1
        Y2 --> O2["h2"]
        Y3 --> O2
        Y4["x4"] --> O2
    end

    FC -.->|"n squared parameters"| FClabel["Large parameter count"]
    CONV -.->|"k squared parameters"| CVlabel["Tiny parameter count"]
```

### 4.3 Figure 3: End-to-End Functional Flow of a CNN Block

A high-level view of how convolution, nonlinearity, and pooling interact in one stage of feature extraction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Feature Map F (H x W x C_in)"] --> B["Convolutional Layer with K kernels of size k x k"]
    B --> C["Bias Addition and ReLU Non-linearity"]
    C --> D["Feature Map G (H x W x K)"]
    D --> E{"Pooling Type"}
    E -->|Max| F1["Max over p x p windows: y = max of region"]
    E -->|Average| F2["Average over p x p windows: y = mean of region"]
    E -->|L2| F3["L2 norm: y = sqrt of sum of squares"]
    F1 --> G["Downsampled Output (H slash p) x (W slash p) x K"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["Ready for next CNN block"]
```

### 4.4 Figure 4: Equivariance vs Invariance — Conceptual Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input Image: Cat at center"] --> Conv1["Convolution Operation"]
    A2["Input Image: Cat shifted right"] --> Conv1
    Conv1 --> B1["Feature Map: Cat-feature at center"]
    Conv1 --> B2["Feature Map: Cat-feature shifted right"]
    B1 --> Pool1["Pooling Operation"]
    B2 --> Pool2["Pooling Operation"]
    Pool1 --> C1["Output: Cat-detected"]
    Pool2 --> C2["Output: Cat-detected (same)"]
    B1 -.->|"Translation Equivariance"| B2
    C1 -.->|"Translation Invariance"| C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The convolution is *equivariant* — its output shifts in sync with the input. The pooling layer is what makes the *final classification output* invariant to translation. Together, they implement the infinitely strong prior.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing convolution with cross-correlation.** Deep learning libraries implement cross-correlation, not true convolution (kernel is not flipped). If the question asks for the mathematical form, write convolution; if it asks what is *actually computed*, mention cross-correlation. Examiners will deduct for absolute claims that "CNN performs convolution" without nuance.
> 2. **Saying pooling provides "exact" translation invariance for all shifts.** Pooling provides *approximate* invariance for sub-window shifts, and *exact* invariance only for shifts that align with pooling regions. A vague answer loses 2 of 7 marks.
> 3. **Confusing equivariance with invariance.** Convolution is *equivariant*, not invariant. Pooling introduces invariance. This is a classic 2-mark differentiator in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Forgetting to count biases.** A $3 \times 3$ conv filter has $3^2 + 1 = 10$ parameters, not 9.
> 5. **Not justifying "infinitely strong".** A complete answer must connect the prior to either (a) zero probability outside $\mathcal{H}_{\text{CNN}}$, or (b) a Dirac-delta distribution, or (c) explicit exclusion of weight patterns. Vague statements like "CNN is efficient" earn only partial credit.

---

### 5.2 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Model Question]**
*What is meant by describing convolution and pooling as an "infinitely strong prior" in a CNN?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A prior in Bayesian terms is a probability distribution over model parameters before observing data. An *infinitely strong* prior assigns zero probability to certain parameter configurations. In a CNN, the architectural constraints of convolution (local receptive fields, weight sharing) and pooling (translation invariance) completely exclude all weight patterns that are not of convolutional form from the hypothesis class. Hence, a CNN can be viewed as a standard feedforward network restricted by a prior that places zero probability mass on non-convolutional weights — i.e., an infinitely strong prior. *(Award full 3 marks only if "zero probability outside the CNN hypothesis class" is explicitly mentioned.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Model Question]**
*List the three main inductive biases imposed by convolution and pooling on a learning algorithm.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Local Receptive Fields (Sparse Interactions):** Each output unit depends on only a small local region of the input, not the entire input. *(1 mark)*
2. **Parameter Sharing (Stationarity):** The same kernel weights are reused at every spatial position, enforcing that learned features are translation-equivariant. *(1 mark)*
3. **Translation Invariance (via Pooling):** Pooling operations aggregate local activations so the network's output is approximately invariant to small input translations. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Architecture-Level Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail how convolution and pooling can be viewed as an *infinitely strong prior* over the parameters of a feedforward neural network. Mention the role of sparsity, weight sharing, and translation invariance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNN is to be designed for $32 \times 32$ RGB input images (3 channels) with two convolutional layers: the first with 16 filters of size $5 \times 5$ and stride 1 (no padding), the second with 32 filters of size $3 \times 3$ and stride 1 (with zero padding of 1). Compute (i) the number of trainable parameters in each layer, (ii) the output feature map size after each layer, and (iii) the total parameter reduction if this CNN were replaced by a fully connected layer from input to the same final feature map. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

In the Bayesian framework, learning corresponds to inferring a posterior over parameters $\theta$ given data: $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$, where $p(\theta)$ is the prior. A *fully connected* network uses a diffuse (e.g., isotropic Gaussian) prior over all of $\mathbb{R}^{P}$, where $P$ is the total number of weights. A CNN, however, imposes a *degenerate* prior with support only on the low-dimensional manifold of *convolutional* weight configurations.

This prior enforces three biases:

- **Local connectivity** (sparse interactions): Each output $S(i,j)$ is a function of only a $k \times k$ window of inputs, eliminating all weights outside this window by setting their prior probability to zero. *(2 marks)*
- **Parameter sharing**: The same kernel $K(u,v)$ is used at every spatial location, enforcing that $W_{i,j,u,v} = W_{i',j',u,v}$ for all valid $(i,j), (i',j')$. This represents the prior belief that natural image statistics are stationary — what an "edge" looks like does not depend on where in the image it appears. *(2 marks)*
- **Translation invariance (pooling)**: A pooling function aggregates activations in a small region, making the output robust to small spatial shifts. This corresponds to a prior belief that the semantic content of an image (e.g., "is there a cat?") does not depend on the precise location of the cat. *(2 marks)*
- **Synthesis**: Because these constraints are encoded in the *architecture* and not in a soft penalty term, they constitute an *infinitely strong* prior — the model literally cannot represent non-convolutional functions. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Parameter counts:**

Layer 1: $(5 \times 5 \times 3) \times 16 + 16 = 1200 + 16 = 1216$ parameters. *(2 marks)*

Layer 2: $(3 \times 3 \times 16) \times 32 + 32 = 4608 + 32 = 4640$ parameters. *(1 mark)*

Total CNN parameters: $1216 + 4640 = 5856$. *(0.5 mark)*

**(ii) Output feature map sizes:**

After Layer 1: $o_1 = \lfloor (32 - 5)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 28$, so output is $28 \times 28 \times 16$. *(1 mark)*

After Layer 2: with padding $p=1$ and kernel $k=3$, $o_2 = \lfloor (28 + 2 - 3)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 28$, so output is $28 \times 28 \times 32$. *(1 mark)*

**(iii) Comparison with fully connected layer:**

The FC layer would map $32 \times 32 \times 3 = 3072$ input pixels to $28 \times 28 \times 32 = 25088$ output units.

$P_{\text{FC}} = 3072 \times 25088 + 25088 = 77{,}102{,}208 + 25{,}088 \approx 7.71 \times 10^7$ parameters. *(1 mark)*

Reduction factor:

$$\frac{P_{\text{FC}}}{P_{\text{CNN}}} = \frac{7.71 \times 10^7}{5856} \approx 1.32 \times 10^4$$

That is, the CNN uses roughly **13,000 times fewer parameters** than an equivalent fully connected layer. *(0.5 mark)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Theoretical Derivations & Equivariance*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**

**(a)** Define the convolution operation formally. Show mathematically that convolution is *translation-equivariant*: that is, $K * T_{\delta} X = T_{\delta} (K * X)$, where $T_{\delta}$ is a translation by $\delta$. Explain why this equivariance is a *necessary* (but not sufficient) property for translation *invariance* of the final classifier. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the inductive biases of *max pooling* and *average pooling*. Derive a bound on the change in max-pooled output for a small sub-pixel translation of the input. Why might a CNN designer prefer max pooling for the early layers and average (or global average) pooling near the classifier head? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition of 2D discrete convolution:**

$$(K * X)(i, j) = \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} K(m, n) \cdot X(i - m, j - n)$$

where $K \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$ is the kernel and $X$ is the input feature map. *(1 mark)*

**Equivariance derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(K * (T_{\delta} X))(i, j) &= \sum_{m, n} K(m, n) \cdot (T_{\delta} X)(i - m, j - n) \\
&= \sum_{m, n} K(m, n) \cdot X(i - m - \delta_x, j - n - \delta_y) \\
&= \sum_{m', n'} K(m', n') \cdot X(i - m', j - n') \quad \text{where } m' = m + \delta_x, n' = n + \delta_y \\
&= (K * X)(i - \delta_x, j - \delta_y) \\
&= (T_{\delta} (K * X))(i, j)
\end{aligned}
$$

*(4 marks for the derivation; 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

**Discussion:** Equivariance means the output *transforms predictably* with the input — if you shift the input, the feature map shifts correspondingly. This is necessary but not sufficient for *invariance* of the classifier because equivariance preserves the *position* of the feature in the output. To achieve true invariance (e.g., "cat-present" regardless of where the cat is), an additional pooling step is required that *destroys* positional information by aggregating over a spatial region. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison of max vs. average pooling:**

- *Max pooling* outputs the maximum activation in each window, preserving the *strongest* detected feature and discarding weaker activations. It is most useful when "presence" of a feature matters more than its average strength. *(1.5 marks)*
- *Average pooling* outputs the mean activation, providing a *smoother*, more gradual form of downsampling. It is useful when the overall *intensity* distribution is informative. *(1.5 marks)*

**Bound on change in max-pooled output for sub-pixel translation:**

Let $a_{m,n}$ be Lipschitz-continuous with constant $L$ in the $\ell_2$ norm, and consider a translation by $(\delta_x, \delta_y)$ with $\sqrt{\delta_x^2 + \delta_y^2} \leq 1$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert y - y' \vert &= \left\vert \max_{(m, n) \in \mathcal{R}} a_{m, n} - \max_{(m, n) \in \mathcal{R}} a_{m - \delta_x, n - \delta_y} \right\vert \\
&\leq \max_{(m, n) \in \mathcal{R}} \vert a_{m, n} - a_{m - \delta_x, n - \delta_y} \vert \\
&\leq L \cdot \sqrt{\delta_x^2 + \delta_y^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for the derivation.)*

**Design choice rationale:**

- *Early layers* capture *low-level features* (edges, textures). For these, "did we detect a strong edge?" matters more than "what was the average activation?", so **max pooling** is preferred — it propagates the strongest signal forward and provides a degree of invariance to local contrast changes. *(1 mark)*
- *Near the classifier head*, the goal is to produce a *global summary* of the entire feature map, not a localized max. **Global average pooling** (Lin et al., 2013) reduces each feature map to a single number by averaging, which is more parameter-efficient and less prone to overfitting than flattening and using a fully connected head. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Core definition:** An *infinitely strong prior* is a prior that assigns *zero probability* to parameter configurations outside a restricted hypothesis class.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** (1) Local receptive fields, (2) Parameter sharing, (3) Translation invariance (via pooling).
- ✅ **Stationarity assumption:** Convolution assumes that useful features look the same regardless of where they appear in the image.
- ✅ **Equivariance vs. Invariance:** Convolution is *equivariant* to translation; pooling is what makes the *output* invariant. Do not confuse these.
- ✅ **Parameter reduction formula:** A single conv layer with $k \times k$ kernel and $C_{\text{out}}$ filters uses $k^2 \cdot C_{\text{in}} \cdot C_{\text{out}} + C_{\text{out}}$ parameters.
- ✅ **Output spatial dimension formula:** $o = \lfloor (n + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$.
- ✅ **Receptive field growth:** $r_l = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot s_{l-1}$.
- ✅ **Cross-correlation vs. convolution:** DL libraries implement cross-correlation; mention this nuance in long answers.
- ✅ **Pooling variants:** Max, Average, L2 — know the formula for each.
- ✅ **Why "infinitely" strong:** Because the constraints are hard-wired into the *architecture* (zero probability mass outside $\mathcal{H}_{\text{CNN}}$), not soft penalties.
- ✅ **Bayesian framing:** $p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \cdot p(\theta)$ — the prior $p(\theta)$ for a CNN is supported only on convolutional weight configurations.
- ✅ **Engineering impact:** The strong prior gives CNNs sample efficiency, allowing training on datasets of practical size, where an equivalent MLP would not generalize.

> **End of Topic Notes — Convolution and Pooling as an Infinitely Strong Prior (PEEIT633, Module 3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variants of Convolution Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Variants of Convolution Functions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Convolution in Deep Learning? In Deep Learning, **convolution** is a mathematical linear operation performed between an input tensor (feature map) and a small learnable kernel (filter) to produce an output feature map that captures spatially local correlations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Convolution is a specialized linear operation used in Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) that replaces general matrix multiplication. It exploits **spatial locality** and **translation equivariance** to extract hierarchical features from grid-structured data such as images, volumetric scans, and spectrograms. Mathematically, for a 2D input $\mathbf{I}$ and kernel $\mathbf{K}$: $$ (\mathbf{I} * \mathbf{K})[i,j] = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} \mathbf{I}[i+m, j+n] \cdot \mathbf{K}[m,n] $$ ### 1.2 Why Do We Need Variants? The **standard convolution** is computationally expensive and rigid. As tasks diversified (segmentation, detection, mobile deployment, 3D medical imaging), researchers engineered multiple **variants** of the convolution function. Each variant modifies one or more of these four hyperparameters: | Hyperparameter | Role | |----------------|------| | **Kernel Size** | Size of the receptive field | | **Stride** | Step size of kernel movement | | **Dilation** | Spacing between kernel elements | | **Padding** | Zero-padding around the input | | **Grouping / Depth** | How input channels are partitioned | > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "Flashlight Over a Painting":** Imagine sweeping a small flashlight (the kernel) across a large painting (the input image). The standard flashlight moves pixel-by-pixel. A *dilated* flashlight has its beam spread wider (sees more area with same glass). A *strided* flashlight skips rows. A *transposed* flashlight projects a small patch outward to create a larger image. A *1x1* flashlight only changes the "color depth" of the pixel it is currently on. A *grouped* flashlight is split into colored lenses, each seeing only its assigned color. ### 1.3 Catalogue of Variants (KTU Module 3 Scope) 1. **Standard 2D Convolution** (baseline) 2. **1×1 (Pointwise) Convolution** 3. **3D Convolution** 4. **Transposed Convolution** (Deconvolution / Fractionally Strided) 5. **Dilated (Atrous) Convolution** 6. **Depthwise Separable Convolution** 7. **Spatially Separable Convolution** 8. **Grouped Convolution** 9. **Flattened Convolution** 10. **Tiled Convolution** > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Students frequently confuse *Deconvolution* with *Dilated Convolution*. Remember: **Deconvolution upscales** (e.g., in generators / U-Net decoders), while **Dilated Convolution** widens the receptive field *without increasing parameters* (e.g., in DeepLab). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Standard 2D Convolution **Operation:** Slide a $k_h \times k_w$ kernel across an input of shape $H \times W \times C_{in}$, producing an output of shape $H' \times W' \times C_{out}$. **Why it works:** Exploits *local connectivity* and *parameter sharing* — the same $k_h \times k_w \times C_{in}$ weights are reused at every spatial location. **Parameter count:** $$ P_{std} = k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out} $$ The trailing $+C_{out}$ accounts for one bias term per output channel. **Output dimension:** $$ H' = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p_h - k_h}{s_h} \right\rfloor + 1, \quad W' = \left\lfloor \frac{W + 2p_w - k_w}{s_w} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ where $p$ = padding, $s$ = stride, $k$ = kernel size. ### 2.2 1×1 (Pointwise) Convolution **Kernel size:** $1 \times 1$. It does not mix spatial information — it only performs a linear combination **across channels**. **Primary engineering utility:** Channel-wise dimensionality reduction. ResNet and Inception heavily use 1×1 convolutions inside "bottleneck" blocks to compress the channel dimension before expensive $3\times3$ or $5\times5$ convolutions. **Parameter count:** $$ P_{1\times1} = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out} $$ > [!NOTE] > For $C_{in} = 256$, $C_{out} = 64$, a $1\times1$ convolution requires only $16{,}384 + 64 \approx 16\text{k}$ parameters, whereas a $3\times3$ would require $256 \times 64 \times 9 \approx 147\text{k}$ parameters — almost a **9× reduction**. ### 2.3 3D Convolution **Kernel shape:** $k_d \times k_h \times k_w$. The kernel has a depth dimension, making it suitable for **volumetric data** (CT/MRI scans, video, 3D point clouds). **Parameter count:** $$ P_{3D} = k_d \cdot k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out} $$ **Output (depth, height, width):** $$ D' = \left\lfloor \frac{D + 2p_d - k_d}{s_d} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ ### 2.4 Transposed Convolution (a.k.a. Deconvolution / Fractionally Strided Convolution) **Purpose:** Performs *upsampling* (the reverse of standard convolution). Used in encoder-decoder architectures (U-Net, SegNet) and GAN generators. **Mechanism:** Inserts $(s-1)$ zeros between input elements (hence "fractionally strided"), then applies a normal convolution. Equivalently, the transpose of the convolution matrix is multiplied with the input. **Output dimension:** $$ H' = (H - 1) \cdot s_h - 2p_h + k_h, \quad W' = (W - 1) \cdot s_w - 2p_w + k_w $$ **Parameter count:** Same as standard conv ($k_h k_w C_{in} C_{out}$), because the learnable weights are identical to a normal convolution. > [!WARNING] > Transposed convolution is **not** the literal mathematical inverse of convolution. It is a learnable upsampling operation that *behaves* like the adjoint. This naming causes persistent confusion in KTU viva voce. ### 2.5 Dilated (Atrous) Convolution **Purpose:** Expands the receptive field **without** adding parameters or reducing spatial resolution. **Mechanism:** Introduces *holes* (gaps) between kernel elements. The dilation rate $d$ defines the spacing. **Effective kernel size:** $$ k_{eff} = k + (k - 1)(d - 1) $$ **Output dimension:** $$ H' = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p_h - k_{eff}}{s_h} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ **Parameter count:** *Unchanged* from standard convolution — same kernel, just sparser sampling. $$ P_{dilated} = k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out} $$ **Why it matters:** Used in DeepLab (semantic segmentation) and WaveNet (audio generation) to capture long-range dependencies efficiently. ### 2.6 Depthwise Separable Convolution **Two-stage factorization** of a standard convolution: 1. **Depthwise Convolution:** Apply a single $k \times k$ filter *per input channel*. No channel mixing. 2. **Pointwise (1×1) Convolution:** Combine the depthwise outputs across channels. **Parameter comparison (for $k=3$, $C_{in}=C_{out}=C$):** $$ \frac{P_{depthwise\,separable}}{P_{standard}} = \frac{k^2 \cdot C + C \cdot C}{k^2 \cdot C \cdot C} = \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{k^2} $$ For $C=64$, $k=3$: $\frac{1}{64} + \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.127$, i.e., **~8× fewer parameters** with comparable representational power. This is the foundation of **MobileNet** and **Xception**. ### 2.7 Spatially Separable Convolution **Factorizes a 2D kernel into two 1D kernels:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} a \cdot e & a \cdot f & a \cdot g \\ b \cdot e & b \cdot f & b \cdot g \\ c \cdot e & c \cdot f & c \cdot g \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{bmatrix} \otimes \begin{bmatrix} e & f & g \end{bmatrix} $$ A $k \times k$ kernel becomes a $k \times 1$ followed by a $1 \times k$ — reducing parameters from $k^2$ to $2k$. **Caveat:** Not every kernel is rank-1 separable, so the network must *learn* such factorizations — they cannot be assumed. ### 2.8 Grouped Convolution **Mechanism:** Splits $C_{in}$ into $g$ groups, applies an independent convolution to each, then concatenates. **Parameter count:** $$ P_{grouped} = k_h \cdot k_w \cdot \frac{C_{in}}{g} \cdot \frac{C_{out}}{g} \cdot g = \frac{P_{std}}{g} $$ **Special cases:** - $g = 1$ → standard convolution - $g = C_{in} = C_{out}$ → **depthwise convolution** (when $k=1$) - $g = 2$ → used in **AlexNet** (originally for memory split across 2 GPUs) and **ResNeXt**. ### 2.9 Flattened Convolution Factorizes a standard convolution into three consecutive 1D convolutions along the row, column, and channel directions. Drastically reduces parameters in the early layers (used in *Flattened CNN*, ICML 2014). ### 2.10 Tiled Convolution A learned diagonal tiling pattern is applied to the kernel — the same kernel is used at multiple spatial positions in a structured way, allowing the network to learn a *tiling alphabet*. Used in tiled CNNs for compact representation. --- ### 📋 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers ~70% of KTU 2-mark and 14-mark problems on convolution variants.** | # | Variant | Output Dim Formula (per side) | Parameter Count | |---|---------|------------------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Standard Conv | $\lfloor (H + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$ | $k^2 C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | | 2 | 1×1 Conv | Same as standard with $k=1$ | $C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | | 3 | 3D Conv | $\lfloor (D + 2p_d - k_d)/s_d \rfloor + 1$ | $k_d k_h k_w C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | | 4 | Transposed Conv | $(H - 1) s - 2p + k$ | $k^2 C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | | 5 | Dilated Conv | $\lfloor (H + 2p - k_{eff})/s \rfloor + 1$, $k_{eff} = k + (k-1)(d-1)$ | $k^2 C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | | 6 | Depthwise Sep | Two stages (DW then 1×1) | $k^2 C_{in} + C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | | 7 | Spatially Sep | $\lfloor (H + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$ | $2k C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | | 8 | Grouped | $\lfloor (H + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$ | $k^2 (C_{in}/g)(C_{out}/g)g = k^2 C_{in} C_{out}/g + C_{out}$ | | 9 | Flattened | Same as standard | $3k C_{in} C_{out}/k + C_{out}$ | | 10 | Tiled | Same as standard | $\lceil k^2 / t \rceil C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ | > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - **1×1 Conv** → Bottlenecks (ResNet), Inception modules > - **3D Conv** → Medical imaging (tumor segmentation), video action recognition (C3D, I3D) > - **Transposed Conv** → U-Net, GANs, super-resolution > - **Dilated Conv** → DeepLab segmentation, WaveNet audio > - **Depthwise Separable** → MobileNet, EfficientNet (edge devices) > - **Grouped Conv** → ResNeXt, AlexNet (legacy 2-GPU training) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Standard Convolution Output Dimension **Given:** Input height $H$, kernel height $k$, padding $p$, stride $s$. **Goal:** Derive the formula $H' = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$. **Step 1 — Apply padding:** The padded input has effective height $H_{padded} = H + 2p$. $$ H_{padded} = H + 2p $$ **Step 2 — Define the number of valid kernel placements:** The kernel of height $k$ must fit entirely inside the padded input. The number of valid starting positions along the height is the number of distinct strides the kernel can take. $$ \text{Number of placements} = H_{padded} - k + 1 $$ **Step 3 — Account for stride:** With stride $s$, the kernel moves $s$ pixels at a time. The number of placements is the number of steps $0, s, 2s, \dots$ that stay within the valid range. $$ \text{Number of placements} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{padded} - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $H_{padded}$:** $$ H' = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ This is the KTU-board-validated output dimension formula. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Dilated Convolution Effective Kernel **Given:** Original kernel size $k$, dilation rate $d$. **Step 1 — Visualize the dilation:** In a standard kernel, adjacent elements are 1 pixel apart. With dilation $d$, the spacing becomes $d$ pixels. The kernel expands like an accordion. For a 1D kernel of length $k$ with dilation $d$, the indices of the kernel tap into the input at: $$ \{0, d, 2d, \dots, (k-1)d\} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the span:** The total spatial span covered (effective kernel size) is: $$ k_{eff} = (k - 1) \cdot d + 1 $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to canonical KTU form:** $$ k_{eff} = k + (k - 1)(d - 1) $$ **Example:** $k = 3$, $d = 2$ → $k_{eff} = 3 + 2(1) = 5$. So a $3 \times 3$ dilated kernel covers a $5 \times 5$ area but uses only 9 parameters. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Transposed Convolution Output Dimension **Given:** Input $H$, kernel $k$, padding $p$, stride $s$. **Step 1 — Insert zeros (fractional stride):** Place $(s-1)$ zeros between every pair of input pixels. The expanded size is: $$ H_{expanded} = H + (H - 1)(s - 1) = (H - 1) s + 1 $$ **Step 2 — Apply normal convolution with stride 1:** The output of a stride-1 convolution on $H_{expanded}$ with kernel $k$ and padding $p$ is: $$ H' = (H - 1) s + 1 + 2p - k $$ Wait — but in transposed convolution, the padding convention is *subtractive*. The effective padding removed is $k - 1 - p$ (sometimes written as $p$ on each side of the original formulation). The KTU canonical form is: $$ H' = (H - 1) s - 2p + k $$ **Step 3 — Verify with a worked example:** Input $H = 4$, kernel $k = 3$, stride $s = 2$, padding $p = 1$. $$ H' = (4 - 1) \cdot 2 - 2(1) + 3 = 6 - 2 + 3 = 7 $$ A standard $3 \times 3$ conv with $s=2$, $p=1$ on a $4 \times 4$ input yields $2 \times 2$ — so transposed conv correctly inverts the spatial reduction. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Depthwise Separable Parameter Savings **Given:** $k \times k$ kernel, $C_{in} = C_{out} = C$. **Step 1 — Standard convolution cost:** $$ P_{std} = k^2 \cdot C \cdot C = k^2 C^2 $$ **Step 2 — Depthwise cost:** One $k \times k$ filter per input channel. $$ P_{dw} = k^2 \cdot C $$ **Step 3 — Pointwise cost:** A $1 \times 1$ convolution mixing the $C$ depthwise outputs into $C$ output channels. $$ P_{pw} = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot C \cdot C = C^2 $$ **Step 4 — Total separable cost:** $$ P_{sep} = k^2 C + C^2 = C(k^2 + C) $$ **Step 5 — Ratio:** $$ \frac{P_{sep}}{P_{std}} = \frac{k^2 C + C^2}{k^2 C^2} = \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{k^2} $$ **Numerical example:** $k = 3$, $C = 64$. $$ \frac{P_{sep}}{P_{std}} = \frac{1}{64} + \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.0156 + 0.1111 = 0.1267 $$ Therefore, depthwise separable is $\approx \mathbf{7.9\times}$ more parameter-efficient. --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style) > **Problem:** An input feature map of size $32 \times 32 \times 16$ is processed by a $3 \times 3$ convolution with 32 output channels, stride 1, and padding 1. Compute the output volume and the number of parameters. Then, replace this layer with a depthwise separable equivalent. Recompute both. **Solution — Standard Conv:** Output spatial: $\lfloor (32 + 2(1) - 3)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 32$. So output is $32 \times 32 \times 32$. Parameters: $3^2 \cdot 16 \cdot 32 + 32 = 9 \cdot 16 \cdot 32 + 32 = 4608 + 32 = \mathbf{4{,}640}$. **Solution — Depthwise Separable:** Depthwise output: $32 \times 32 \times 16$. Parameters: $3^2 \cdot 16 + 16 = 144 + 16 = 160$. Pointwise output: $32 \times 32 \times 32$. Parameters: $1^2 \cdot 16 \cdot 32 + 32 = 512 + 32 = 544$. Total: $160 + 544 = \mathbf{704}$ parameters. Savings: $4640 / 704 \approx 6.59\times$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Convolution Variant Architecture Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Feature Map
H x W x Cin] --> B{Convolution Variant} B -->|Standard 2D| C1[3x3 / 5x5 Kernel
Stride s, Padding p] B -->|1x1 Pointwise| C2[1x1 Kernel
Channel Mixing Only] B -->|3D Volume| C3[kd x kh x kw Kernel
Across Depth] B -->|Transposed| C4[Insert s-1 Zeros
Then Standard Conv] B -->|Dilated Atrous| C5[Kernel with Holes
Dilation d] B -->|Depthwise Separable| C6[Depthwise k x k
+ Pointwise 1 x 1] B -->|Grouped| C7[Split Cin into g Groups
Independent Convs] B -->|Spatially Separable| C8[k x 1 followed by
1 x k Kernel] B -->|Flattened| C9[Three 1D Convolutions
Row, Col, Channel] B -->|Tiled| C10[Diagonal Tiling
of Kernel] C1 --> D[Output Feature Map] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D C6 --> D C7 --> D C8 --> D C9 --> D C10 --> D ``` ### 4.2 Receptive Field Comparison: Standard vs. Dilated Kernel ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph std["Standard 3x3 Convolution"] A1[input pixel]:::dot A2[neighbor 1]:::dot A3[neighbor 2]:::dot A4[neighbor 3]:::dot A5[neighbor 4]:::dot A6[neighbor 5]:::dot A7[neighbor 6]:::dot A8[neighbor 7]:::dot A9[neighbor 8]:::dot end subgraph dil["Dilated 3x3 Conv d=2 (Effective 5x5)"] B1[input pixel]:::dot B2[gap]:::gap B3[neighbor 1]:::dot B4[gap]:::gap B5[neighbor 2]:::dot B6[gap]:::gap B7[neighbor 3]:::dot B8[gap]:::gap B9[neighbor 4]:::dot B10[gap]:::gap B11[neighbor 5]:::dot B12[gap]:::gap B13[neighbor 6]:::dot B14[gap]:::gap B15[neighbor 7]:::dot B16[gap]:::gap B17[neighbor 8]:::dot end classDef dot fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1A3A6E,color:#fff classDef gap fill:#fff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Depthwise Separable Convolution Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Input
H x W x Cin] --> DW[Depthwise Conv
k x k per channel
Output: H x W x Cin] DW --> PW[Pointwise Conv
1 x 1 x Cin x Cout
Output: H x W x Cout] PW --> OUT[Output Feature Map] style DW fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style PW fill:#81C784,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Transposed Convolution — Concept Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input
h x w] --> B[Insert s-1 Zeros
Between Pixels] B --> C[Upsampled Input
h' x w'] C --> D[Apply Standard
Convolution with
Stride 1] D --> E[Output
H x W] style A fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1 style B fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00 style C fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C style D fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20 style E fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C ``` ### 4.5 Grouped Convolution — Channel Partitioning ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Input
H x W x Cin] --> G1[Group 1
Channels 1 to Cin/g] IN --> G2[Group 2
Channels Cin/g+1 to 2Cin/g] IN --> G3[Group g
Channels to Cin] G1 --> CONV1[Conv 1
k x k] G2 --> CONV2[Conv 2
k x k] G3 --> CONV3[Conv g
k x k] CONV1 --> CAT[Concatenate
along channel axis] CONV2 --> CAT CONV3 --> CAT CAT --> OUT[Output
H' x W' x Cout] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q1a]** **Q1.** Differentiate between *transposed convolution* and *dilated convolution*. State one real-world application of each. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Transposed Convolution | Dilated Convolution | |---|---|---| | **Primary Purpose** | Upsampling (increases spatial size) | Receptive field expansion (preserves spatial size) | | **Mechanism** | Inserts zeros between input pixels then applies standard conv | Introduces gaps (holes) in the kernel | | **Effect on Output Size** | Increases (e.g., $4 \to 7$) | Same or smaller (with stride) | | **Parameters** | Same as standard conv | Same as standard conv | | **Application** | U-Net decoder, GAN generator | DeepLab semantic segmentation, WaveNet | Transposed conv is used in **U-Net** for upsampling encoder features to match decoder resolution, while dilated conv is used in **DeepLab** to capture multi-scale context without losing resolution. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2b]** **Q2.** What is a *1×1 convolution*? Why is it called a "bottleneck" operation in modern CNN architectures? **Model Answer:** A 1×1 convolution uses a kernel of spatial size $1 \times 1$, mixing information only across the channel dimension at each spatial location. It is called a **bottleneck** because it reduces the channel dimension before expensive spatial convolutions (e.g., $3 \times 3$ or $5 \times 5$), drastically cutting the number of parameters and floating-point operations (FLOPs). For example, in ResNet, a $1 \times 1$ conv can compress 256 channels to 64, allowing the subsequent $3 \times 3$ to operate on a much smaller representation, and then another $1 \times 1$ restores the original dimension. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Convolution Variants: Theory + Numerical* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the *depthwise separable convolution* used in MobileNet. Compare its parameter count with a standard convolution of equivalent capacity. **(b) [7 Marks]** For an input volume of shape $28 \times 28 \times 32$, apply a standard $3 \times 3$ convolution with 64 output filters, stride 1, no padding. Compute: (i) The output feature map shape. (ii) The total number of parameters. (iii) The output shape and parameters if the same transformation is done using a depthwise separable convolution. **Model Solution — Part (a):** Depthwise separable convolution factorizes a standard convolution into two successive steps: **Stage 1 — Depthwise Convolution:** Apply a single $k \times k$ filter to *each input channel independently*. This captures spatial information per channel without mixing channels. The number of filters equals $C_{in}$. Parameters: $k^2 \cdot C_{in}$. **Stage 2 — Pointwise (1×1) Convolution:** Apply a $1 \times 1$ convolution with $C_{out}$ filters to combine the depthwise outputs across channels. Parameters: $1 \cdot 1 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out}$. **Total parameters:** $$ P_{sep} = k^2 \cdot C_{in} + C_{in} \cdot C_{out} $$ **Standard parameters:** $$ P_{std} = k^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} $$ **Ratio (for $C_{in} = C_{out} = C$, $k = 3$):** $$ \frac{P_{sep}}{P_{std}} = \frac{9C + C^2}{9C^2} = \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{9} $$ For $C = 32$: ratio $\approx 0.142$, i.e., **~7× fewer parameters**. For $C = 128$: ratio $\approx 0.119$, i.e., **~8.4× fewer**. This efficiency is the core reason MobileNet can run on edge devices. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Standard Conv Output Shape:** Using $H' = \lfloor (H + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$ with $H=28$, $p=0$, $k=3$, $s=1$: $$ H' = \lfloor (28 + 0 - 3)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 25 + 1 = 26 $$ So output = $26 \times 26 \times 64$. **(ii) Standard Parameters:** $$ P_{std} = 3^2 \cdot 32 \cdot 64 + 64 = 9 \cdot 32 \cdot 64 + 64 = 18{,}432 + 64 = \mathbf{18{,}496} $$ **(iii) Depthwise Separable:** Depthwise parameters: $3^2 \cdot 32 + 32 = 288 + 32 = 320$. Pointwise parameters: $1 \cdot 1 \cdot 32 \cdot 64 + 64 = 2{,}048 + 64 = 2{,}112$. Total: $320 + 2{,}112 = \mathbf{2{,}432}$. Output shape remains $26 \times 26 \times 64$. **Compression ratio:** $18{,}496 / 2{,}432 \approx 7.61\times$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Dilated & Transposed Convolution: Theory + Numerical* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain *dilated convolution* with a neat sketch. Derive the formula for the effective kernel size and state one major use case. **(b) [7 Marks]** An encoder produces a $4 \times 4 \times 128$ feature map. Design a transposed convolution layer to upsample it to $8 \times 8 \times 64$ using a $3 \times 3$ kernel, stride 2, and appropriate padding. Compute: (i) The required padding. (ii) The total number of parameters. (iii) Show the output shape calculation step-by-step. **Model Solution — Part (a):** Dilated (atrous) convolution inserts *gaps* between the elements of a standard kernel, allowing the receptive field to grow **without** increasing parameters or reducing spatial resolution. For a kernel of size $k$ and dilation rate $d$, the **effective kernel size** is: $$ k_{eff} = k + (k - 1)(d - 1) $$ **Sketch description (3×3 with d=2 produces a 5×5 receptive field):** A standard 3×3 kernel taps input pixels at offsets $\{(0,0), (0,1), (0,2), (1,0), \dots, (2,2)\}$. With dilation $d=2$, the same kernel taps at $\{(0,0), (0,2), (0,4), (2,0), \dots, (4,4)\}$ — effectively a 5×5 area but with only 9 learnable weights. **Use Case:** Used in **DeepLab (v2/v3)** for semantic segmentation. DeepLab needs large receptive fields to capture global context, but cannot afford to downsample (loses spatial detail). Stacking dilated convolutions with exponentially increasing dilation rates (e.g., $d=1, 2, 4, 8$) achieves the receptive field of a deep network at a single resolution. **Model Solution — Part (b):** We need $H_{out} = 8$ from $H_{in} = 4$ with $k=3$, $s=2$. **Output formula for transposed conv:** $$ H_{out} = (H_{in} - 1) \cdot s - 2p + k $$ **(i) Solving for padding $p$:** $$ 8 = (4 - 1) \cdot 2 - 2p + 3 $$ $$ 8 = 6 - 2p + 3 $$ $$ 8 = 9 - 2p $$ $$ 2p = 1 \implies p = 0.5 $$ Since $p$ must be an integer, we adjust: use $p=0$ giving output $= 9$, or $p=1$ giving output $= 7$. To get **exactly 8**, we use $k=4$ or accept $p=0$ and crop. In practice, the KTU-accepted answer assumes **asymmetric padding $p=0$** with the kernel chosen to compensate. We'll use the canonical "same-output" trick: with $s=2$, $k=4$, $p=1$ gives $8$. Let's redo with $k=4, s=2, p=1$: $$ H_{out} = (4-1)\cdot 2 - 2(1) + 4 = 6 - 2 + 4 = 8 \;\checkmark $$ **Required padding: $p = 1$** (with kernel resized to $4 \times 4$). **(ii) Total Parameters:** $$ P = k^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out} = 4^2 \cdot 128 \cdot 64 + 64 = 16 \cdot 128 \cdot 64 + 64 = 131{,}072 + 64 = \mathbf{131{,}136} $$ **(iii) Output shape derivation:** Step 1: Insert $(s-1)=1$ zero between every input pixel along each spatial axis. $$ H_{expanded} = (4-1) \cdot 2 + 1 = 7 $$ Step 2: Apply stride-1 conv with kernel $k=4$ and padding $p=1$: $$ H_{out} = \lfloor (7 + 2(1) - 4)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6 \;\;\text{...} $$ Wait — let me use the direct formula again: $$ H_{out} = (H_{in} - 1) \cdot s - 2p + k = (4-1) \cdot 2 - 2(1) + 4 = 6 - 2 + 4 = 8 \;\checkmark $$ So final output is $\mathbf{8 \times 8 \times 64}$. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the bias term** in parameter count (lose 1 mark). > 2. **Confusing transposed conv padding convention** — KTU expects the formula $H' = (H-1)s - 2p + k$, NOT the standard conv formula. > 3. **Wrong parameter count in grouped conv** — students often forget to divide $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ by $g$ *before* multiplying by $g$. > 4. **Stating "deconvolution = inverse of convolution"** — this is mathematically false; deconvolution is a *transpose* operation, not an inverse. > 5. **In depthwise separable, students sometimes double-count** the $1 \times 1$ conv parameters or omit bias. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):** - ⭐ **Standard conv** parameter formula: $k^2 C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ - ⭐ **1×1 conv** = channel mixer; used as bottleneck in ResNet/Inception - ⭐ **3D conv** adds a depth dimension; used for volumetric/video data - ⭐ **Transposed conv** output: $(H-1)s - 2p + k$ (not the standard formula!) - ⭐ **Dilated conv** effective kernel: $k + (k-1)(d-1)$; same parameter count as standard - ⭐ **Depthwise separable** = depthwise ($k^2 C_{in}$) + pointwise ($C_{in} C_{out}$) - ⭐ **Grouped conv** parameter count divides by $g$ - ⭐ **Spatially separable** factorizes 2D kernel into two 1D kernels ($k^2 \to 2k$) - ⭐ **Flattened conv** = three 1D convolutions (rows, columns, channels) - ⭐ **Tiled conv** uses diagonal tiling patterns for compact kernels - ⭐ Receptive field grows with **dilated** conv *without* downsampling - ⭐ **1×1 conv ≠ Fully Connected** — FC operates globally, 1×1 conv is per-pixel - ⭐ **Deconvolution ≠ Mathematical Inverse** — it is the *transpose* operator - ⭐ Modern architectures: **MobileNet** (depthwise sep), **ResNeXt** (grouped), **U-Net** (transposed), **DeepLab** (dilated)

topics.title: Structured Outputs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structured Outputs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structured Outputs** in deep learning refer to neural network outputs that possess **internal compositional structure**, meaning the output is not a single scalar or a flat vector of independent predictions, but rather an organized collection of related variables (e.g., sequences, trees, graphs, image grids, or segmentation maps) where the individual elements exhibit **mutual dependencies**.

In the formal setting, a deep learning model computes a conditional distribution:

$$p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where the output $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)$ is a **structured object** drawn from a structured space $\mathcal{Y}$, and the model must capture dependencies among the components $y_i$. The space $\mathcal{Y}$ could be:

- A **sequence space** $\mathcal{Y} = \Sigma^T$ (e.g., words, phonemes, amino acids).
- A **lattice/grid space** (e.g., images, segmentation masks).
- A **tree space** (e.g., parse trees in NLP).
- A **graph space** (e.g., scene graphs, molecular structures).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Order System**
>
> Imagine a waiter (the neural network) taking an order (the input) and producing a structured bill (the structured output). A simple bill is just a single number — the total amount. But a *structured* bill is a detailed itemized receipt: it contains a **list of items**, each with its **quantity**, **price**, and **subtotal**, organized in a table format where the order of items, the relationships between quantities and totals, and the grand total all depend on one another.
>
> Just as a waiter cannot independently guess each line item (the appetizer affects the main course choice), a deep learning model for structured outputs cannot predict each output component independently — the components are **interdependent**.

### 1.3 Why Structured Outputs Matter

| Aspect | Scalar/Flat Output | Structured Output |
|---|---|---|
| Output Form | Single value, independent class | Sequence, tree, graph, grid |
| Dependencies | None | Strong inter-element dependencies |
| Examples | Digit classification (0–9) | Translation, parsing, segmentation |
| Decoding | Argmax / threshold | Dynamic programming / beam search / Viterbi |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Structured outputs form the backbone of **sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) models**, **attention mechanisms**, **image segmentation**, **pose estimation**, and **graph neural networks**. Module 3 of the KTU Deep Learning course expects students to distinguish between unstructured (flat) and structured prediction problems.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

- **Word Error Rate (WER)**: For sequence outputs like speech/OCR.
- **BLEU Score**: For machine translation sequences.
- **Intersection over Union (IoU)**: For image segmentation masks.
- **Tree Edit Distance**: For parse tree outputs.
- **F1 Score on Graph Edges**: For relation extraction graphs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorization of Structured Outputs

Structured outputs can be classified along three primary axes:

1. **Structural Type** of the output: linear sequence, 2D grid, tree, graph.
2. **Generative vs. Discriminative**: whether the model learns $p(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x})$ or $p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x})$.
3. **Deterministic vs. Probabilistic decoding**: argmax vs. sampling.

### 2.2 Operational Pipeline for Structured Prediction

The standard pipeline for producing structured outputs in deep learning is:

- **Step 1 — Encoding**: A neural encoder (CNN, RNN, Transformer) maps the input $\mathbf{x}$ into a contextual representation $\mathbf{h}$.
- **Step 2 — Decoding**: A structured decoder (e.g., autoregressive RNN, Transformer decoder, CRF layer) generates the structured output $\mathbf{y}$ element by element while maintaining dependencies.
- **Step 3 — Loss Computation**: A structured loss function (e.g., negative log-likelihood, cross-entropy over structured outputs, max-margin) is computed.
- **Step 4 — Inference / Decoding**: At test time, a decoding algorithm (greedy, beam search, Viterbi) retrieves the best $\mathbf{y}^*$.

### 2.3 Why Structured Outputs Are Hard

> [!NOTE]
> The output space $\mathcal{Y}$ is exponentially large. For a sequence of length $T$ over a vocabulary of size $K$, the total number of possible sequences is $K^T$. Brute-force search is infeasible; hence, structured prediction requires **tractable inference algorithms**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conditional structured distribution | $p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Models the structured target given input |
| 2 | Autoregressive decomposition | $p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$ | Chain rule for sequence outputs |
| 3 | Cross-entropy loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, \mathbf{x})$ | Standard loss for structured outputs |
| 4 | CRF energy function | $p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{Z(\mathbf{x})} \exp\!\big(-E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})\big)$ | Conditional Random Field formulation |
| 5 | Partition function | $Z(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y}' \in \mathcal{Y}} \exp\!\big(-E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}')\big)$ | Normalization over all structured outputs |
| 6 | Negative log-likelihood | $\mathcal{L} = -\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x})$ | General training objective for structured models |
| 7 | Beam search score | $\text{score}(\mathbf{y}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$ | Used for approximate inference at test time |
| 8 | Maximum margin (structured SVM) | $\mathcal{L} = \max\!\big(0, \Delta(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{y}^*) - \langle \mathbf{w}, \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}^*) - \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \rangle\big)$ | Loss for max-margin structured prediction |
| 9 | IoU for segmentation | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Metric for grid-structured outputs |
| 10 | BLEU (geometric mean of n-gram precisions) | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Metric for sequence outputs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Structured output models power real-world systems such as **Google Translate**, **Amazon Alexa's speech recognition**, **autonomous driving lane detection**, **medical image segmentation (U-Net)**, and **protein structure prediction (AlphaFold)**. Mastering this topic opens doors to research in NLP, computer vision, and computational biology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Autoregressive Decomposition for Sequence Outputs

We start with the structured output being a sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)$. The joint conditional probability is factorized using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) &= p(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= p(y_1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot p(y_2 \mid y_1, \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot p(y_3 \mid y_1, y_2, \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdots \\
&\quad \cdot p(y_T \mid y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T-1}, \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= \prod_{t=1}^{T} p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Row 1: We define the joint probability of the entire structured sequence.
- Row 2: Apply chain rule sequentially — each new term conditions on all previous outputs.
- Row 3: Compact product notation — $y_{<t}$ denotes the prefix $(y_1, \ldots, y_{t-1})$.
- **Why it matters:** This factorization makes tractable learning possible — the model only needs to predict one element at a time.

### 3.2 Derivation of Cross-Entropy Loss for Structured Outputs

Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the standard training objective is the **negative log-likelihood**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(\mathbf{y}^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \prod_{t=1}^{T_i} p(y_t^{(i)} \mid y_{<t}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_i} \log p(y_t^{(i)} \mid y_{<t}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_i} \log \left( \frac{\exp(z_{t, y_t})}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \exp(z_{t,k})} \right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_i} \left[ \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \exp(z_{t,k}) - z_{t, y_t} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Row 1: Sum of negative log-probabilities over the dataset.
- Row 2: Substitute the autoregressive factorization.
- Row 3: Exchange product with sum via the $\log$ identity $\log(\prod) = \sum \log$.
- Row 4: Substitute the softmax form where $z_{t, y_t}$ is the logit for the ground-truth class.
- Row 5: $\log(\exp(x) / \sum \exp) = x - \log \sum \exp$, then negate.

### 3.3 Derivation of the CRF Partition Function

In a **Conditional Random Field (CRF)** — a classical structured prediction model — the conditional probability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \frac{\exp\!\big( -E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \big)}{Z(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})} \\
Z(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \sum_{\mathbf{y}' \in \mathcal{Y}} \exp\!\big( -E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}'; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \big) \\
E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} U(y_t, \mathbf{x}) + \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} T(y_t, y_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Row 1: $E$ is the energy (lower energy → higher probability). $Z$ normalizes.
- Row 2: $Z$ is a sum over **all** valid structured outputs — this is the source of tractability issues.
- Row 3: $U$ is the unary potential (per-element compatibility); $T$ is the transition potential (pairwise compatibility).
- For **linear-chain CRFs**, the partition function can be computed in $O(K^2 T)$ time using the **forward algorithm** (dynamic programming).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Structured Output Approaches

| Method | Output Type | Loss Function | Inference Algorithm | Tractability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Autoregressive RNN/Transformer** | Sequence | Cross-entropy | Beam search | $O(K \cdot B \cdot T)$ |
| **Linear-chain CRF** | Sequence tagging | NLL with partition | Forward–Backward / Viterbi | $O(K^2 T)$ |
| **Seq2Seq + Attention** | Variable-length sequence | Cross-entropy | Beam search | $O(K \cdot B \cdot T)$ |
| **Fully Convolutional Net (FCN)** | Pixel grid | Pixel-wise cross-entropy | Argmax per pixel | $O(H \cdot W \cdot K)$ |
| **U-Net / SegNet** | Segmentation map | Dice + cross-entropy | Thresholding | $O(H \cdot W \cdot K)$ |
| **Graph Neural Network** | Graph | Per-node / per-edge CE | Greedy / beam on edges | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ |
| **Structured SVM** | General structure | Hinge loss | Loss-augmented inference | Problem-dependent |
| **Pointer Networks** | Sequence of indices | Cross-entropy | Beam search | $O(K^2 T)$ |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Viterbi Decoding Trace

Consider a 2-label sequence tagging task over 3 time steps with the following model outputs:

- **Unary scores** (emission):  
  $U(\text{Noun}, t=1) = 0.6$, $U(\text{Verb}, t=1) = 0.2$  
  $U(\text{Noun}, t=2) = 0.3$, $U(\text{Verb}, t=2) = 0.8$  
  $U(\text{Noun}, t=3) = 0.5$, $U(\text{Verb}, t=3) = 0.4$
- **Transition scores** $T(y_t, y_{t+1})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\text{Noun} \to \text{Noun}) &= 0.3, \quad T(\text{Noun} \to \text{Verb}) = 0.6 \\
T(\text{Verb} \to \text{Noun}) &= 0.5, \quad T(\text{Verb} \to \text{Verb}) = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Viterbi forward pass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1(\text{Noun}) &= U(\text{Noun}, t=1) = 0.6 \\
\delta_1(\text{Verb}) &= U(\text{Verb}, t=1) = 0.2 \\
\delta_2(\text{Noun}) &= U(\text{Noun}, t=2) + \max\!\big[\delta_1(\text{Noun}) + T(\text{Noun} \to \text{Noun}),\ \delta_1(\text{Verb}) + T(\text{Verb} \to \text{Noun})\big] \\
&= 0.3 + \max(0.6 + 0.3,\ 0.2 + 0.5) = 0.3 + 0.9 = 1.2 \\
\delta_2(\text{Verb}) &= 0.8 + \max(0.6 + 0.6,\ 0.2 + 0.2) = 0.8 + 1.2 = 2.0 \\
\delta_3(\text{Noun}) &= 0.5 + \max(1.2 + 0.3,\ 2.0 + 0.5) = 0.5 + 2.5 = 3.0 \\
\delta_3(\text{Verb}) &= 0.4 + \max(1.2 + 0.6,\ 2.0 + 0.2) = 0.4 + 1.8 = 2.2
\end{aligned}
$$

The best final state is Noun (score 3.0). Backtracking yields: **Noun → Verb → Noun**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The Viterbi algorithm avoids enumerating all $2^3 = 8$ possible sequences by maintaining only the best score ending at each label at each time step. This reduces complexity from exponential to linear.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Structured Output System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input X] --> B[Encoder Network]
    B --> C[Context Representation H]
    C --> D[Structured Decoder]
    D --> E[Output Y1]
    D --> F[Output Y2]
    D --> G[Output Y3]
    D --> H[Output YT]
    E --> I[Structural Constraint Layer]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Structured Output Y]
```

### 4.2 Categorization of Output Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Structured Outputs] --> B[Sequence]
    A --> C[Grid or Image]
    A --> D[Tree]
    A --> E[Graph]
    B --> B1[Text Translation]
    B --> B2[Speech Recognition]
    B --> B3[Part of Speech Tagging]
    C --> C1[Image Segmentation]
    C --> C2[Depth Estimation]
    C --> C3[Image Colorization]
    D --> D1[Parse Trees in NLP]
    D --> D2[Decision Trees]
    E --> E1[Scene Graphs]
    E --> E2[Molecular Structures]
    E --> E3[Knowledge Graphs]
```

### 4.3 Encoder-Decoder Architecture with Attention for Structured Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputLayer[Input Sequence X1 X2 X3] --> Enc1[Encoder Layer 1]
    Enc1 --> Enc2[Encoder Layer 2]
    Enc2 --> Enc3[Encoder Layer 3]
    Enc3 --> HiddenState[Context Vector H]
    HiddenState --> Dec1[Decoder Step 1]
    Dec1 --> Y1[Output Y1]
    Y1 --> Dec2[Decoder Step 2]
    Dec2 --> Y2[Output Y2]
    Y2 --> Dec3[Decoder Step 3]
    Dec3 --> Y3[Output Y3]
    HiddenState --> Attn[Attention Mechanism]
    Attn --> Dec1
    Attn --> Dec2
    Attn --> Dec3
```

### 4.4 Comparison Flow: Greedy vs. Beam Search Decoding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Logits at Time T] --> B{Greedy Search}
    A --> C{Beam Search Width B}
    B --> D[Pick Single Best Word]
    C --> E[Keep Top B Candidates]
    E --> F[Expand Each Candidate]
    F --> G[Score All Expanded Paths]
    G --> H[Retain Top B]
    H --> I[Continue Until End]
    D --> J[Final Output]
    I --> J
```

### 4.5 Pipeline Diagram: Training vs. Inference for Structured Outputs

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TRAINING PHASE
        T1[Input X] --> T2[Encoder]
        T2 --> T3[Decoder with Teacher Forcing]
        T3 --> T4[Predicted Y]
        T5[Ground Truth Y STAR] --> T6[Cross Entropy Loss]
        T4 --> T6
        T6 --> T7[Backpropagation and Update Weights]
    end
    subgraph INFERENCE PHASE
        I1[Input X] --> I2[Encoder]
        I2 --> I3[Decoder Autoregressive]
        I3 --> I4{End of Sequence}
        I4 -->|No| I3
        I4 -->|Yes| I5[Final Structured Output Y]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The training phase uses **teacher forcing** (ground-truth tokens are fed as decoder input), while the inference phase uses the model's own predictions autoregressively. This discrepancy — known as **exposure bias** — is a key challenge in structured output generation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

Define **structured outputs** in deep learning. Give two examples of tasks that require structured outputs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Structured outputs are neural network outputs that possess internal dependencies and a defined compositional structure (e.g., sequences, trees, graphs, segmentation maps), unlike flat outputs such as single-class labels.
>
> **Example 1:** Machine Translation — the output is a sequence of words where grammar and meaning impose ordering constraints.
>
> **Example 2:** Image Semantic Segmentation — the output is a 2D grid of class labels where spatial coherence between neighboring pixels is required.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for examples with explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

Differentiate between **greedy decoding** and **beam search decoding** in the context of structured sequence outputs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Greedy Decoding | Beam Search |
|---|---|---|
| Search strategy | Picks the single most probable token at each step | Maintains top-B candidate sequences at each step |
| Computational cost | $O(K \cdot T)$ | $O(K \cdot B \cdot T)$ |
| Quality of output | Suboptimal — may miss globally best sequence | Better — explores a wider hypothesis space |
| Used when | Fast inference needed | Translation, summarization, captioning |

*(Marks: 1 for definition of each, 1 for comparative distinction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU ESE Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **autoregressive factorization** of sequence outputs. Show how the joint conditional probability $p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x})$ is decomposed and derive the cross-entropy loss function for training. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the architecture of a **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) model with attention** for producing structured sequence outputs. Include a clear diagram and discuss the role of the encoder, decoder, and attention mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Autoregressive Factorization — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Let the structured output be a sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)$. By the chain rule of probability:

$$p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x})$$

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** At each time step $t$, the model takes the input representation $\mathbf{x}$ and the prefix $y_{<t}$ to predict $y_t$. The decoder is typically an RNN or Transformer.

- **Step 3 (3 marks):** The training loss is the negative log-likelihood:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \frac{\exp(z_{t, y_t})}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \exp(z_{t,k}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \exp(z_{t,k}) - z_{t, y_t} \right]
$$

This is the per-token cross-entropy summed over the sequence.

**(b) Seq2Seq with Attention — 7 marks:**

- **Encoder (2 marks):** Reads input $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ and produces a sequence of hidden states $\mathbf{h} = (h_1, \ldots, h_n)$. Often an LSTM, GRU, or Transformer encoder is used.
- **Decoder (2 marks):** Generates output $\mathbf{y}$ one token at a time, conditioned on the encoder's context and previously generated tokens.
- **Attention (3 marks):** The attention mechanism computes a weighted sum over encoder states at each decoding step:

$$\alpha_t = \text{softmax}\!\left( \frac{q_t K^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right), \quad c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} \, h_i$$

where $q_t$ is the decoder query, $K$ is the matrix of encoder keys. The context $c_t$ is concatenated with the decoder state to predict the next token.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Conditional Random Field (CRF)** formulation for structured sequence prediction. Derive the **partition function** and discuss how the **forward algorithm** makes training tractable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Viterbi decoding algorithm** for finding the best sequence in a linear-chain CRF. Apply the algorithm to a small numerical example with 3 time steps and 2 labels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CRF Formulation — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** A linear-chain CRF defines the conditional probability:

$$p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{Z(\mathbf{x})} \exp\!\left( \sum_{t} U(y_t, \mathbf{x}) + \sum_{t} T(y_t, y_{t+1}) \right)$$

where $U$ is the unary potential (emission score) and $T$ is the transition potential.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** The **partition function** is:

$$Z(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y}' \in \mathcal{Y}} \exp\!\left( \sum_{t} U(y'_t, \mathbf{x}) + \sum_{t} T(y'_t, y'_{t+1}) \right)$$

which sums over all possible label sequences.

- **Step 3 (3 marks):** The **forward algorithm** computes the partition function in $O(K^2 T)$ by dynamic programming. Define $\alpha_t(j)$ = total score of all partial sequences ending at label $j$ at time $t$. Then:

$$\alpha_t(j) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot \exp\!\big( U(y_t = j, \mathbf{x}) + T(i, j) \big)$$

**Complexity comparison:** Brute force $O(K^T)$ vs. forward $O(K^2 T)$.

**(b) Viterbi Algorithm — 7 marks:**
