# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test **remembering** and **understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. They are compulsory and require crisp, definition-style answers of 4–6 lines.

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Define a computational graph. Explain its role in deep learning with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A computational graph is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** where nodes represent mathematical operations or variables, and edges represent the flow of intermediate values (tensors) between these operations. In deep learning, the network's forward computation is represented as a graph, which is then traversed in **reverse topological order** during the backward pass to compute gradients via the **chain rule**. This enables **automatic differentiation (autograd)**, eliminating the need for manual gradient derivation.

**Example:** The loss function $L = (wx + b - y)^2$ is decomposed into elementary operations $u_1 = wx$, $u_2 = u_1 + b$, $u_3 = u_2 - y$, $L = u_3^2$, forming a 4-node linear DAG. The backward pass computes $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$ mechanically using the chain rule.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Differentiate between static and dynamic computational graphs. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Graph (TF1.x, Theano) | Dynamic Graph (PyTorch, TF2.x) |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Defined once, then compiled and executed in a session | Built on-the-fly during execution, line-by-line |
| Flexibility | Graph structure is fixed; control flow is limited | Native Python control flow (`if`, `while`, `for`) supported |
| **Advantage** | High performance through global graph optimization and easy deployment to mobile/embedded devices | Easier debugging and supports dynamic network architectures (e.g., variable-length RNNs, recursive networks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions test **applying**, **analysing**, and **evaluating** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. They carry **internal choice** (Or option). Sub-parts (a) and (b) are usually 7 marks each. The KTU examiner expects **fully worked solutions with diagrams, formulas, and numerical substitution**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Consider the computational graph for the function $L = \sigma(w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b)$ with target $y = 1$ and the binary cross-entropy loss $\ell = -[y \log L + (1-y)\log(1-L)]$. Given $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = -0.3, b = 0.1, x_1 = 1.0, x_2 = 2.0$ and $\eta = 0.5$:

**(i)** Draw the computational graph. **(ii)** Perform one forward pass and compute $L$ and $\ell$. **(iii)** Perform one backward pass to compute $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial w_1}, \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial w_2}, \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial b}$. **(iv)** Update the parameters using SGD. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **chain rule** as applied in computational graphs. How does the **multi-path sum rule** handle the case when a variable is used in multiple downstream operations? Provide a small example. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Forward Pass:**

$$z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (0.5)(1.0) + (-0.3)(2.0) + 0.1 = 0.5 - 0.6 + 0.1 = 0.0$$

$$L = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.0}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = 0.5$$

$$\ell = -[1 \cdot \log(0.5) + 0 \cdot \log(0.5)] = -(-0.6931) = 0.6931$$

**Step 2 — Local Gradients:**

| Node | Local Gradient |
|---|---|
| $\ell$ wrt $L$ | $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial L} = -\frac{y}{L} + \frac{1-y}{1-L} = -\frac{1}{0.5} + 0 = -2$ |
| $\sigma$ | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = L(1-L) = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.25$ |
| $z$ wrt $w_1$ | $\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_1} = x_1 = 1.0$ |
| $z$ wrt $w_2$ | $\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_2} = x_2 = 2.0$ |
| $z$ wrt $b$ | $\frac{\partial z}{\partial b} = 1$ |

**Step 3 — Backward Pass (Chain Rule):**

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial L} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = (-2)(0.25) = -0.5$$

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial w_1} = -0.5 \cdot 1.0 = -0.5$$

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial w_2} = -0.5 \cdot 2.0 = -1.0$$

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial b} = -0.5 \cdot 1 = -0.5$$

**Step 4 — SGD Update:**

$$w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.5) = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75$$

$$w_2^{\text{new}} = -0.3 - 0.5 \cdot (-1.0) = -0.3 + 0.5 = 0.20$$

$$b^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.5) = 0.1 + 0.25 = 0.35$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Chain Rule in Computational Graphs:** The chain rule states that for a composite function $L = f(g(h(x)))$, the gradient is the product of local derivatives along the unique path from $x$ to $L$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial f} \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial g} \cdot \frac{\partial g}{\partial h} \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

In a computational graph, the backward pass multiplies the **local gradient** at each node along the reverse path from $L$ to $x$.

**Multi-Path Sum Rule:** When a variable $u$ has **multiple outgoing edges** (fan-out $> 1$), the total gradient is the **sum of contributions from each path**:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial u} = \sum_{j : u \to v_j} \frac{\partial L}{\partial v_j} \cdot \frac{\partial v_j}{\partial u}$$

**Example:** For $L = u^2 + u^3$, the variable $u$ feeds into two separate branches. The gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial u} = 2u + 3u^2$$

The graph framework automatically aggregates both branches into $u$ during the backward pass.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style]`

**(a)** For the function $L = \max(wx, b) \cdot (y - 1)^2$ with $w = 1, x = 2, b = 3, y = 0$:

**(i)** Draw the computational graph with all operations. **(ii)** Compute the forward pass value of $L$. **(iii)** Perform the backward pass to find $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial b}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** in the context of deep computational graphs. Explain how techniques like **gradient clipping**, **careful initialization** (Xavier/He), and **residual connections** address this issue. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Forward Pass:**

- $u_1 = wx = 1 \cdot 2 = 2$
- $u_2 = \max(u_1, b) = \max(2, 3) = 3$
- $u_3 = y - 1 = 0 - 1 = -1$
- $u_4 = (u_3)^2 = 1$
- $L = u_2 \cdot u_4 = 3 \cdot 1 = 3$

**Step 2 — Local Gradients:**

| Node | Local Gradient |
|---|---|
| $L = u_2 \cdot u_4$ | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_2} = u_4 = 1$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_4} = u_2 = 3$ |
| $u_4 = (u_3)^2$ | $\frac{\partial u_4}{\partial u_3} = 2 u_3 = -2$ |
| $u_3 = y - 1$ | $\frac{\partial u_3}{\partial y} = 1$ |
| $u_2 = \max(u_1, b)$ | $\frac{\partial u_2}{\partial u_1} = \mathbf{1}[u_1 > b] = 0$ (since $2 < 3$), $\frac{\partial u_2}{\partial b} = \mathbf{1}[b > u_1] = 1$ |
| $u_1 = wx$ | $\frac{\partial u_1}{\partial w} = x = 2$, $\frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x} = w = 1$ |

**Step 3 — Backward Pass:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_4} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_2} = 1$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_3} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_4} \cdot \frac{\partial u_4}{\partial u_3} = 3 \cdot (-2) = -6$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_3} \cdot \frac{\partial u_3}{\partial y} = -6 \cdot 1 = -6$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_2} \cdot \frac{\partial u_2}{\partial b} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_2} \cdot \frac{\partial u_2}{\partial u_1} \cdot \frac{\partial u_1}{\partial w} = 1 \cdot 0 \cdot 2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_2} \cdot \frac{\partial u_2}{\partial u_1} \cdot \frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x} = 1 \cdot 0 \cdot 1 = 0$$

> **Interpretation:** Since the $\max$ operator selected $b$ over $wx$, the gradient with respect to $w$ and $x$ is exactly zero. This is the **"dead neuron"** phenomenon — a ReLU/max unit that is inactive contributes no gradient to its inputs.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Vanishing Gradients:** In a deep computational graph with $L$ layers, the gradient with respect to early parameters is the product of $\sim L$ local gradients, each typically in $(0, 1)$ for sigmoid/tanh activations. The product shrinks exponentially, making early-layer learning effectively impossible. Formally:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial w^{(1)}} = \prod_{k=1}^{L} \frac{\partial h^{(k)}}{\partial h^{(k-1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial h^{(L)}} \to 0 \text{ as } L \to \infty$$

**Exploding Gradients:** Conversely, if any local gradient has magnitude $> 1$, the product grows exponentially, causing unstable weight updates and numerical overflow (NaN).

**Remedies:**

1. **Gradient Clipping:** Rescale the gradient vector when its norm exceeds a threshold: $g \leftarrow g \cdot \frac{\tau}{\max(\tau, \Vert g \Vert)}$.
2. **Xavier Initialization:** Initialize weights with $\mathcal{N}(0, \tfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}})$ to keep variance stable across layers.
3. **He Initialization:** Uses $\mathcal{N}(0, \tfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}}})$ for ReLU activations.
4. **Residual Connections (ResNets):** Introduce skip connections $h^{(l+1)} = h^{(l)} + f(h^{(l)})$ so the gradient has a direct path: $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial h^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial h^{(L)}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\partial f}{\partial h^{(l)}}\right)$, preventing the product from collapsing to zero.
5. **Normalization Layers:** BatchNorm, LayerNorm, and GroupNorm rescale activations to keep gradients in a healthy range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting multi-path summation** when a variable is reused (e.g., $u$ in $u^2 + u^3$). Always trace all outgoing edges.
> 2. **Incorrect local gradients** for $\max$, $\mathrm{ReLU}$, or division operations. Memorize: $\frac{\partial \max(a,b)}{\partial a} = \mathbf{1}[a > b]$.
> 3. **Mixing up forward and backward pass directions.** Forward = left-to-right in topological order. Backward = right-to-left in reverse topological order.
> 4. **Skipping the seed gradient.** Always begin the backward pass with $\frac{\partial L}{\partial L} = 1$.
> 5. **Not stating the final SGD update equation** when the question asks for "one training step." Examiners want the actual new parameter values, not just the gradients.
> 6. **Confusing Jacobian (vector chain rule) with scalar chain rule.** Use Jacobians when input/output of an operation node is multi-dimensional.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Computational Graphs**

- **Definition:** A computational graph is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** where nodes = operations/variables and edges = data flow.
- **Two Phases:** **Forward pass** (topological order, value caching) and **Backward pass** (reverse topological order, chain rule).
- **Chain Rule (Scalar):** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial u} \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$.
- **Chain Rule (Vector/Jacobian):** $\frac{\partial \mathbf{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}}{\partial \mathbf{u}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$.
- **Multi-Path Sum Rule:** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial v_i} = \sum_{j} \frac{\partial L}{\partial v_j} \cdot \frac{\partial v_j}{\partial v_i}$ — always sum over all outgoing edges.
- **Seed Gradient:** Always start backward pass with $\frac{\partial L}{\partial L} = 1$.
- **Common Local Gradients:**
  - Square: $\frac{\partial z^2}{\partial z} = 2z$
  - Sigmoid: $\frac{\partial \sigma(z)}{\partial z} = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$
  - ReLU: $\frac{\partial \text{ReLU}(z)}{\partial z} = \mathbf{1}[z > 0]$
  - $\max(a, b)$: $\frac{\partial \max}{\partial a} = \mathbf{1}[a > b]$
- **Cost:** Both forward and backward passes are $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — the celebrated efficiency of backprop.
- **Static vs Dynamic:** Static graphs (TF1.x) optimize globally; dynamic graphs (PyTorch, TF2.x) support flexible control flow.
- **Vanishing/Exploding Gradients:** Caused by products of many small/large local gradients across deep graphs; mitigated by **He/Xavier initialization, gradient clipping, residual connections, normalization layers**.
- **SGD Update:** $w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$.
- **Frameworks:** TensorFlow, PyTorch, JAX, Theano — all use computational graphs under the hood.
- **Why it matters:** Computational graphs make **automatic differentiation (autograd)** possible, which is the engine of all modern deep learning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RNN Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RNN Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — RNN Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) Design** refers to the systematic architectural and mathematical formulation of a class of artificial neural networks that process sequential or temporal data by maintaining an internal **hidden state** which is recurrently updated at every time step. The design encompasses the choice of **input dimensionality**, **hidden state dimensionality**, **output projection**, **activation functions**, **weight sharing strategy across time**, and the corresponding **loss function** suitable for sequence-to-sequence, sequence-to-vector, or vector-to-sequence prediction tasks.

In simpler KTU-board-friendly language, an RNN is a neural network where the output of one time step is fed as input to the next. This loop is what makes it "recurrent." Designing an RNN means deciding **how this loop behaves**, how much memory the network has, what its inputs/outputs look like, and how the network is trained to handle sequences.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Story Teller" Intuition

Imagine a person reading a novel word-by-word, sentence-by-sentence:

- At any given moment, the reader's **understanding of the story so far** is the **hidden state** $h_t$.
- The reader **doesn't memorize every word** — they **compress the past** into an evolving mental summary.
- Each new word $x_t$ is read, and combined with the previous understanding $h_{t-1}$, to produce an updated understanding $h_t$.
- Depending on the task, the reader may either **predict the next word** (output $y_t$ at every step) or **summarise the whole story at the end** (output $y_T$ only).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The defining property of an RNN is **parameter sharing across time steps**. The same weight matrices $(U, W, V)$ are reused at every time step. This is what allows an RNN to generalise across sequences of variable length, unlike a vanilla feedforward network.

### 1.2 Standard Design Parameters (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

The following parameters are the **fundamental knobs** in any RNN design, and every KTU 2024 board question will reference them:

- **Input vector dimension**: $d_x$ — length of the input feature vector at each time step.
- **Hidden state dimension**: $d_h$ — the size of the recurrent memory vector. Also called the **number of hidden units**.
- **Output vector dimension**: $d_y$ — length of the prediction at each time step (or at the final step).
- **Sequence length**: $T$ — number of time steps in the input sequence.
- **Weight matrices**: $U \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$, $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$.
- **Bias vectors**: $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$, $b_y \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y}$.
- **Total trainable parameters**: $d_h \times d_x + d_h \times d_h + d_h + d_y \times d_h + d_y$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** Always state the **shape of every weight matrix** when describing an RNN. Examiners award marks specifically when the dimensions are correctly written.

### 1.3 The Five Canonical RNN Topologies (KTU Board Favourite)

| Topology | Input | Output | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **One-to-One** ($T_x = T_y = 1$) | Single vector | Single vector | Vanilla NN image classification |
| **One-to-Many** ($T_x = 1$, $T_y > 1$) | Single vector | Sequence | Image captioning |
| **Many-to-One** ($T_x > 1$, $T_y = 1$) | Sequence | Single vector | Sentiment classification |
| **Many-to-Many (Same Length)** ($T_x = T_y$) | Sequence | Sequence (per step) | Part-of-speech tagging, video frame labelling |
| **Many-to-Many (Different Length)** ($T_x \neq T_y$) | Sequence | Sequence | Machine translation (Encoder-Decoder) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core RNN Cell — Operational Breakdown

A single RNN cell, when unrolled, performs two operations at every time step $t$:

1. **Hidden state update (memory refresh):**
   $$h_t = f_h(U \, x_t + W \, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

2. **Output projection (prediction):**
   $$y_t = f_y(V \, h_t + b_y)$$

where:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ is the input at time $t$.
- $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ is the hidden state (memory) at time $t$.
- $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y}$ is the output at time $t$.
- $f_h$ is the **hidden activation function** (typically $\tanh$ or $\text{ReLU}$).
- $f_y$ is the **output activation function** (typically $\text{softmax}$ for classification, **identity** for regression).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step "Why & How" of Each Component

- **Why $W h_{t-1}$?**  
  This is the **recurrent term**. It carries forward information from the previous time step. The matrix $W$ decides **what to remember and what to forget** from the prior hidden state.

- **Why $U x_t$?**  
  This is the **input term**. It incorporates the new observation $x_t$ into the memory. The matrix $U$ learns **how to interpret the current input** in the context of the hidden representation.

- **Why $f_h$ (non-linearity)?**  
  Without a non-linear activation, stacking time steps would collapse into a single linear transformation. $\tanh$ is preferred in classical RNNs because it **centres the hidden state around zero** and prevents the activations from blowing up too quickly. **ReLU** variants are used in modern designs for faster convergence.

- **Why a separate $f_y$?**  
  The output task may differ in nature (regression vs. classification) from the hidden representation's role (memory). Decoupling them gives the designer flexibility.

- **Why parameter sharing ($U, W, V$ are fixed across $t$)?**  
  Sharing parameters across time:
  - Drastically reduces the parameter count.
  - Forces the network to learn **time-invariant patterns**.
  - Allows the model to **generalise to sequence lengths** not seen during training.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** Many students incorrectly assume that $U$, $W$, $V$ are *different* at every time step. They are **the same matrices, replicated**. This is the single most-confused concept in RNN design and frequently tested.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — RNN Design

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $h_t = f_h(U x_t + W h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Hidden state recurrence | $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (or learned) |
| $y_t = f_y(V h_t + b_y)$ | Output projection | Depends on task |
| $U \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$ | Input-to-hidden weights | Trainable |
| $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$ | Hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weights | Trainable |
| $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$ | Hidden-to-output weights | Trainable |
| $\text{BPTT: } \frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W}$ | Total gradient = sum of per-step gradients | Chain rule over time |
| $\text{Loss (classification): } L = -\sum_t y_t^{\text{true}} \log \hat{y}_t$ | Cross-entropy per step (summed) | Softmax output |
| $\text{Loss (regression): } L = \sum_t \Vert y_t - \hat{y}_t \Vert^2$ | Mean-squared error per step | Linear output |
| $\prod_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$ | Jacobian product causing vanishing/exploding gradients | Spectral radius of $W$ matters |
| Total parameters | $d_h(d_x + d_h + d_y) + d_h + d_y$ | Independent of $T$ |
| Spectral radius condition | $\rho(W) < 1$ | Stable dynamics (vanishing) |
| Spectral radius condition | $\rho(W) > 1$ | Unstable (exploding) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** When writing the Jacobian product in your exam, use the inline notation $\prod_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$ to avoid losing the vertical bar inside markdown tables. The product itself is the **mathematical origin of vanishing/exploding gradients** in vanilla RNNs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

RNN designs underpin:

- **Natural Language Processing:** language modelling, machine translation, named-entity recognition.
- **Speech Recognition:** acoustic sequence modelling (e.g., early Google Voice Search).
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** financial market prediction, weather modelling, energy load forecasting.
- **Bioinformatics:** DNA sequence classification, protein structure prediction.
- **Video Analytics:** action recognition, frame-by-frame captioning.
- **Music Generation:** composing melodies as time sequences.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Whenever you describe an RNN design in a KTU answer, **always tie it back to a real engineering application**. This demonstrates the application-level understanding that the 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based education rubric rewards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Forward Pass of an Unrolled RNN

We unroll the RNN for $T$ time steps. Let the initial hidden state be $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

**Time step $t = 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_1 &= U \, x_1 + W \, h_0 + b_h \\
h_1 &= f_h(z_1) \\
y_1 &= f_y(V \, h_1 + b_y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The network receives the first input $x_1$, combines it with the zero initial state, applies the hidden activation, and produces the first output $y_1$.

**Time step $t = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_2 &= U \, x_2 + W \, h_1 + b_h \\
h_2 &= f_h(z_2) \\
y_2 &= f_y(V \, h_2 + b_y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The same matrices $U, W, V$ are reused. The hidden state $h_1$ (which already contains a compressed representation of $x_1$) is now passed forward.

**General time step $t$ (for $1 \le t \le T$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= U \, x_t + W \, h_{t-1} + b_h \\
h_t &= f_h(z_t) \\
y_t &= f_y(V \, h_t + b_y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the canonical RNN cell equation. The same triplet of equations holds for every $t$ — only the *values* of $x_t$ and $h_{t-1}$ change.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

Assume a **per-step loss** $L_t = \mathcal{L}(y_t, y_t^{\text{true}})$, with total loss:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t
$$

We must compute the gradient of $L_{\text{total}}$ with respect to $V$, $U$, and $W$.

### 3.2.1 Gradient w.r.t. $V$ (Output weights) — Direct path

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial V} &= \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial y_t} \cdot \frac{\partial y_t}{\partial V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $y_t = f_y(V h_t + b_y)$:

$$
\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial V} = \left( \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial y_t} \odot f_y'(V h_t + b_y) \right) h_t^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L_{\text{total}}}{\partial V} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( \delta_y(t) \right) h_t^{\top}, \quad \text{where } \delta_y(t) = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial y_t} \odot f_y'(V h_t + b_y)
$$

**Conversion logic:** The gradient w.r.t. $V$ is *local* to each time step because $V$ does not feed into future hidden states. Hence we simply sum per-step contributions.

### 3.2.2 Gradient w.r.t. $W$ (Recurrent weights) — The hard part

$W$ influences not only $h_t$ but **every future hidden state** $h_{t+1}, h_{t+2}, \dots, h_T$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}
$$

The chain rule over time gives:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}
$$

Each Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(f_h'(z_i)\bigr)
$$

Hence the full gradient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W} &= \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \left( \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(f_h'(z_i)\bigr) \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W} \\
\frac{\partial L_{\text{total}}}{\partial W} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(f_h'(z_i)\bigr) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the **central BPTT identity**. The nested product of Jacobians is what makes BPTT computationally expensive (cost $\mathcal{O}(T)$ in storage and time) and what causes the **vanishing / exploding gradient problem**.

### 3.2.3 Gradient w.r.t. $U$ (Input weights)

By identical reasoning:

$$
\frac{\partial L_{\text{total}}}{\partial U} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(f_h'(z_i)\bigr) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial U}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Vanishing Gradient Condition

For a $\tanh$ activation, $f_h'(z) \le 1$ in magnitude. Assume the spectral radius $\rho(W) < 1$. The norm of the Jacobian product satisfies:

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(f_h'(z_i)\bigr) \right\Vert \le \rho(W)^{t-k}
$$

As $t - k$ grows large (i.e., for long-range dependencies), $\rho(W)^{t-k} \to 0$ exponentially fast. **Therefore, gradients for far-past time steps vanish**, and the network cannot learn long-range dependencies.

## 3.4 Derivation — Exploding Gradient Condition

Conversely, if $\rho(W) > 1$:

$$
\rho(W)^{t-k} \to \infty \quad \text{as} \quad t - k \to \infty
$$

The gradients explode, leading to numerical overflow during gradient descent. This is mitigated in practice by **gradient clipping**:

$$
\text{if } \Vert g \Vert > c, \quad g \leftarrow \frac{c \, g}{\Vert g \Vert}
$$

where $g = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}$ and $c$ is a hyperparameter (typically $c \in [1, 10]$).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Loss Functions for Different RNN Design Tasks

| Task Type | Output Activation $f_y$ | Loss Function $L$ | Why This Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sequence Classification (many-to-one) | $\text{softmax}$ | Cross-entropy | Probabilistic class assignment |
| Per-step Regression (e.g., weather) | Identity (linear) | Mean Squared Error | Continuous-valued targets |
| Per-step Classification (e.g., POS tagging) | $\text{softmax}$ | Cross-entropy (per step, summed) | Discrete labels at each step |
| Sequence Generation (e.g., text) | $\text{softmax}$ over vocabulary | Per-token cross-entropy | Vocabulary-sized probability distribution |
| Binary Sentiment (many-to-one) | $\text{sigmoid}$ | Binary cross-entropy | Single yes/no output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Gold Rule:** The **choice of output activation must match the loss function**. Softmax + cross-entropy, sigmoid + binary cross-entropy, linear + MSE. Mixing them produces a numerically unstable training loop.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unrolled RNN Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input["INPUT SEQUENCE"]
        x1["x1"]
        x2["x2"]
        x3["x3"]
        xT["xT"]
    end
    subgraph cells["RNN CELLS - SAME WEIGHTS U W V REUSED"]
        a1["h1 = fh U x1 + W h0 + bh"]
        a2["h2 = fh U x2 + W h1 + bh"]
        a3["h3 = fh U x3 + W h2 + bh"]
        aT["hT = fh U xT + W hT-1 + bh"]
    end
    subgraph output["OUTPUT SEQUENCE"]
        y1["y1 = fy V h1 + by"]
        y2["y2 = fy V h2 + by"]
        y3["y3 = fy V h3 + by"]
        yT["yT = fy V hT + by"]
    end
    h0["h0 = 0 vector"]
    x1 --> a1
    x2 --> a2
    x3 --> a3
    xT --> aT
    h0 --> a1
    a1 --> a2
    a2 --> a3
    a3 --> aT
    a1 --> y1
    a2 --> y2
    a3 --> y3
    aT --> yT
```

## 4.2 BPTT Flow — Gradient Backpropagation Through Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Total Loss L = sum L1 LT"]
    L --> gV["grad V : sum over t of delta_y t times h transpose t"]
    L --> gW["grad W : sum over t,k of long chain product"]
    L --> gU["grad U : sum over t,k of long chain product"]
    gV --> updV["Update V : V minus eta grad V"]
    gW --> clip["Gradient Clip if norm greater than c"]
    gU --> clip
    clip --> updW["Update W : W minus eta grad W clipped"]
    clip --> updU["Update U : U minus eta grad U clipped"]
    updV --> step1["Time Step t = 1"]
    updW --> step2["Time Step t = 2"]
    updU --> step3["Time Step t = T"]
```

## 4.3 RNN Design Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Define Task Type"]
    start --> q1{"Input or Output is a sequence?"}
    q1 -- "Both fixed length, single vector" --> one2one["One to One : Vanilla NN"]
    q1 -- "Single input, sequence output" --> one2many["One to Many : h0 seed, output at each step"]
    q1 -- "Sequence input, single output" --> many2one["Many to One : use hT only"]
    q1 -- "Sequence to sequence aligned" --> many2manyA["Many to Many Same Length : output at each step"]
    q1 -- "Sequence to sequence different length" --> many2manyB["Many to Many Encoder Decoder : Bridge vector"]
    one2many --> pickF["Pick fh : tanh or ReLU"]
    many2one --> pickF
    many2manyA --> pickF
    many2manyB --> pickF
    one2one --> pickF
    pickF --> pickFY["Pick fy : softmax, sigmoid, or linear"]
    pickFY --> pickLoss["Pick Loss : CE, BCE, or MSE"]
    pickLoss --> pickH["Pick Hidden Size dh via validation"]
    pickH --> pickT["Pick Sequence Length T to truncate or pad"]
    pickT --> train["Train with BPTT and gradient clipping"]
    train --> done["RNN Design Complete"]
```

## 4.4 Long-Range Dependency Problem — Visual Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["x1 the"] --> c1["h1"]
    in2["x2 cat"] --> c2["h2"]
    in3["x3 sat"] --> c3["h3"]
    in4["x4 on"] --> c4["h4"]
    in5["x5 the"] --> c5["h5"]
    in6["x6 mat"] --> c6["h6"]
    c1 --> c2 --> c3 --> c4 --> c5 --> c6
    c6 --> out6["y6 was masculine or feminine article?"]
    in7["x7 ?"] --> c6
    c1 -. "gradient signal: vanishing over distance" .-> c6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The dotted line from $h_1$ to $h_6$ shows the **vanishing gradient path**. When $x_1 = \text{"the"}$ influences the prediction at $t=6$ about gender in French, vanilla RNNs cannot reliably backpropagate the error signal. This is precisely why **LSTM and GRU** were designed — and why RNN *design* must consider long-range dependency handling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the design of a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). How is it different from a feedforward neural network in handling sequential data?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is designed to process **sequential data** by maintaining a **hidden state** $h_t$ that summarises information from all previous time steps. The core design equations are:

$$
h_t = f_h(U x_t + W h_{t-1} + b_h), \qquad y_t = f_y(V h_t + b_y)
$$

The defining design feature is the **recurrent weight matrix $W$** that connects $h_{t-1}$ to $h_t$, and the **sharing of parameters $U, W, V$ across all time steps**.

Unlike a feedforward network, which treats each input as an independent vector and has **no memory of past inputs**, an RNN propagates a compressed history through the hidden state, enabling it to model **temporal dependencies** of variable length. The total parameter count of an RNN is **independent of sequence length $T$**, while a feedforward network processing a variable-length sequence would require a different architecture for every length.

*(Keywords for full marks: hidden state, parameter sharing, recurrence, sequential, memory.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three design considerations that must be taken into account when designing an RNN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Choice of hidden state size $d_h$** — controls the memory capacity; too small underfits long dependencies, too large causes overfitting and slow training.
2. **Choice of activation functions $f_h$ and $f_y$** — typically $\tanh$ or $\text{ReLU}$ for $f_h$ (to ensure stable gradients) and $\text{softmax}$ / linear for $f_y$ depending on the prediction task.
3. **Choice of sequence length $T$ and truncation strategy** — long $T$ causes vanishing gradients; **Truncated BPTT** is used to limit the backpropagation horizon.
4. *(Any other valid point: loss function selection, gradient clipping threshold, weight initialisation, handling of variable-length sequences via masking.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Marks are deducted** if students write RNN equations without specifying the **dimensions** of $U, W, V$ and the **activation functions used**.
> - **Common mistake:** Forgetting to mention that $h_0$ is initialised (typically to a zero vector) — examiners specifically check this in 1-mark sub-questions.
> - **Vanishing vs. exploding gradient**: students frequently confuse the *cause* (spectral radius of $W$) with the *symptom* (gradient magnitude behaviour). Always state **both** explicitly.
> - **BPTT** derivation marks are lost if the **chain rule across time** is not written as a **product of Jacobians** — examiners will not accept a verbal description alone.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Design a Recurrent Neural Network for a **many-to-one sentiment classification** task on movie reviews of variable length (up to 100 words). Define the input representation, hidden state size, output activation, and loss function. Write down the forward pass equations with clearly stated dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** algorithm for this RNN, focusing specifically on the gradient of the total loss with respect to the recurrent weight matrix $W$. Explain why BPTT suffers from the **vanishing gradient problem** for long sequences. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Design Specification:**

- **Input:** Each word $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ where $d_x = 300$ (using a 300-dimensional GloVe/Word2Vec embedding). The sequence length is variable, up to $T_{\max} = 100$. Shorter sequences are **padded** with zero vectors.
- **Hidden state size:** $d_h = 128$ (chosen based on validation accuracy — a moderate balance between capacity and overfitting).
- **Output:** A single 2-class probability vector $y_T \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ (positive / negative sentiment) produced only at the **final time step** $T$.
- **Output activation:** $f_y = \text{softmax}$.
- **Loss function:** **Categorical cross-entropy** $L = -\sum_{c=1}^{2} y_c^{\text{true}} \log \hat{y}_{c}$.

**Forward Pass Equations (with dimensions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= U x_t + W h_{t-1} + b_h, & U &\in \mathbb{R}^{128 \times 300}, \quad W \in \mathbb{R}^{128 \times 128}, \quad b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{128} \\
h_t &= \tanh(z_t), & h_t &\in \mathbb{R}^{128} \\
y_T &= \text{softmax}(V h_T + b_y), & V &\in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 128}, \quad b_y \in \mathbb{R}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial state:** $h_0 = \mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^{128}$.

**Why this design:** The many-to-one topology uses only $h_T$ (which has compressed information from all 100 time steps) for the final sentiment decision, exactly as required for variable-length sequence classification.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**BPTT Derivation:**

The total loss for a many-to-one RNN is computed only at $T$:
$$
L = L_T(y_T, y_T^{\text{true}})
$$

Since $L$ depends on $W$ only through the chain $W \to h_t \to h_{t+1} \to \dots \to h_T$, we must apply the **multivariate chain rule across time**:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W}
$$

But $h_t$ itself depends on $W$ both directly *and* indirectly through $h_{t-1}$. So we use the **total derivative**:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial^{+} h_t}{\partial W} + \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{t-1}}{\partial W}
$$

Unrolling recursively:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \left( \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} \right) \frac{\partial^{+} h_k}{\partial W}
$$

Each Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ evaluates to:

$$
\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(1 - \tanh^2(z_i)\bigr)
$$

Therefore, the full gradient is:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{t} \left( \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(1 - \tanh^2(z_i)\bigr) \right) \frac{\partial^{+} h_k}{\partial W}
$$

**Why vanishing gradients appear:**

For a $\tanh$ activation, $\vert 1 - \tanh^2(z) \vert \le 1$. The norm of the Jacobian product is therefore bounded by:

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W^{\top} \, \text{diag}\bigl(1 - \tanh^2(z_i)\bigr) \right\Vert \le \rho(W)^{t-k}
$$

where $\rho(W)$ is the **spectral radius** (largest absolute eigenvalue) of $W$.

- If $\rho(W) < 1$, then $\rho(W)^{t-k} \to 0$ **exponentially fast** as $t-k$ grows. Contributions from far-past time steps (e.g., the first word of a 100-word review) become vanishingly small — the network **cannot learn long-range dependencies**.
- If $\rho(W) > 1$, the product **explodes**, causing numerical instability (handled by gradient clipping).

This is the central motivation for **LSTM/GRU** cells, which use gated mechanisms to keep $\rho \approx 1$ and preserve gradient flow across long sequences.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — **14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **five canonical RNN topologies** (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many same length, many-to-many different length). For each, give a real-world application and write the corresponding forward-pass equations specifying what $h_0$ and $y_t$ look like. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A team is designing an RNN for **real-time stock price prediction** (next-minute closing price from the last 60 minutes of OHLC data). Identify the design choices for: (i) input dimensionality, (ii) hidden state size, (iii) output activation, (iv) loss function, (v) BPTT truncation length, and (vi) gradient-handling strategy. Justify each choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The five canonical RNN topologies differ in **where inputs are fed** and **where outputs are read**.

| # | Topology | Input Span | Output Span | Real-world Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-to-One | $t=1$ only | $t=1$ only | Standard image classification (acts as a regular NN) |
| 2 | One-to-Many | $x_1$ only | $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_T$ | Image captioning — one image, many words |
| 3 | Many-to-One | $x_1, \dots, x_T$ | $y_T$ only | Sentiment analysis — review to label |
| 4 | Many-to-Many (Aligned) | $x_1, \dots, x_T$ | $y_1, \dots, y_T$ | POS tagging, video frame labelling |
| 5 | Many-to-Many (Encoder-Decoder) | $x_1, \dots, x_{T_x}$ | $y_1, \dots, y_{T_y}$ (with $T_x \ne T_y$) | Machine translation |

**Forward Pass Equations per Topology:**

- **One-to-One:** $h_1 = f_h(U x_1 + b_h)$, $y_1 = f_y(V h_1 + b_y)$.
- **One-to-Many:** $h_0 = f_h(U x_1 + b_h)$, and for $t \ge 1$: $h_t = f_h(W h_{t-1} + b_h)$, $y_t = f_y(V h_t + b_y)$. Note $x_t = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \ge 2$ — the **same seed $h_0$** generates the entire output sequence.
- **Many-to-One:** For $1 \le t \le T$: $h_t = f_h(U x_t + W h_{t-1} + b_h)$; $y_T = f_y(V h_T + b_y)$, with $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Many-to-Many (Aligned):** Standard per-step outputs $y_t = f_y(V h_t + b_y)$ at every $t$.
- **Many-to-Many (Encoder-Decoder):** Encoder produces a **context vector** $c = h_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$; decoder is initialised with $h_0^{\text{dec}} = c$ and generates outputs $y_1^{\text{dec}}, \dots, y_{T_y}^{\text{dec}}$ with $T_y$ free to differ from $T_x$.

#### Model Solution to (b)

| Design Choice | Selection | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Input dimensionality $d_x$ | $4$ (Open, High, Low, Close) per minute | OHLC data is the natural 4-feature financial representation |
| (ii) Hidden state size $d_h$ | $64$ to $128$ | Moderate — 60-step history is moderate length; too large overfits noisy market data |
| (iii) Output activation $f_y$ | **Identity (linear)** | Closing price is a continuous real number, not a probability |
| (iv) Loss function | **Mean Squared Error** | Standard for continuous regression targets |
| (v) BPTT truncation length | **$T_{\text{trunc}} = 20$ to $30$** | Full unrolling to 60 causes severe vanishing gradients and high memory cost; truncated BPTT gives a tractable horizon while capturing recent dynamics |
| (vi) Gradient handling | **Gradient clipping** at $\Vert g \Vert_{\max} = 1.0$ | Stock data has occasional price spikes (volatility) that cause gradient explosion; clipping stabilises training |

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Mark Opportunity:** In the answer to (b), students can additionally mention the use of **mini-batch training**, **early stopping on a validation set**, and **learning rate scheduling** for a complete KTU 2024-NEP-style design answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — RNN Design**

- ✅ An RNN is defined by the **two core equations**: $h_t = f_h(U x_t + W h_{t-1} + b_h)$ and $y_t = f_y(V h_t + b_y)$.
- ✅ The defining property of an RNN is **weight sharing across time steps** — $U, W, V$ are the **same matrices** at every $t$.
- ✅ **Initial state** $h_0$ is typically the **zero vector** unless otherwise specified.
- ✅ Always state the **shape of every weight matrix** explicitly in KTU answers.
- ✅ Total trainable parameters = $d_h(d_x + d_h + d_y) + d_h + d_y$ — **independent of $T$**.
- ✅ The **five canonical topologies** are one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many (aligned), many-to-many (encoder-decoder).
- ✅ **BPTT** applies the chain rule across the *time* dimension. Gradient = $\sum_{t,k}$ of a **product of Jacobians** spanning the time gap.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient** arises when the spectral radius $\rho(W) < 1$; **exploding gradient** when $\rho(W) > 1$.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradients** prevent learning **long-range dependencies**.
- ✅ **Gradient clipping** (norm-based) handles exploding gradients; **LSTM/GRU** handles vanishing gradients.
- ✅ **Truncated BPTT** is the practical workaround for both vanishing gradients and memory cost.
- ✅ **Activation function pairings** (loss–output must match): softmax + cross-entropy, sigmoid + binary cross-entropy, linear + MSE.
- ✅ For **many-to-one** tasks, only $h_T$ is used for the final output.
- ✅ For **one-to-many** tasks, the seed $h_0$ is computed from the single input $x_1$, and $x_t = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \ge 2$.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** NLP, speech, time-series, video, music, finance, bioinformatics.
- ✅ **Total parameter count is independent of $T$** — this is the architectural elegance of RNNs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoder - Decoder Sequence to Sequence Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Encoder - Decoder Sequence to Sequence Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Encoder–Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) Architecture** is a neural network framework composed of two recurrent (or attention-based) sub-networks — an **Encoder** and a **Decoder** — that maps an input sequence of variable length $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ to an output sequence of variable length $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T_y})$, where typically $T_x \neq T_y$. The Encoder compresses the input into a fixed-dimensional latent representation called the **thought vector** or **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$, and the Decoder generates the output sequence conditioned on this context.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Seq2Seq is the foundational architecture for Machine Translation, Text Summarization, Speech Recognition, and Conversational Chatbots. It is the direct predecessor of the **Transformer (Module 5)** and forms the conceptual backbone of modern generative models.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bilingual Tourist

Imagine a **Kerala tourist** who does not speak French, standing at a railway station in Paris.

1. **Listening (Encoding):** He listens to the entire French announcement. Instead of memorizing each word verbatim, he builds a **mental summary** in his head — *"Train to Lyon, platform 9, 14:30, delayed by 15 minutes."* This single internal summary is the **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$.
2. **Compressing (Bottleneck):** His summary is **compressed** — he forgets some filler words but retains the *semantic essence*.
3. **Translating (Decoding):** He then speaks the equivalent Malayalam sentence word-by-word to his friend, **conditioned entirely on that mental summary**.

The tourist is the **Encoder**; the friend is the **Decoder**; the mental summary is the **context vector** $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single context vector is the **information bottleneck**. For long sequences, this bottleneck is the *fundamental weakness* that later motivated **Bahdanau Attention (2015)** and ultimately the **Transformer (2017)**.

## 1.3 Mathematical Identity of the Building Blocks

| Component | Mathematical Role | Typical Implementation |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Encoder** | $\mathbf{c} = f_{\text{enc}}(\mathbf{x})$ | Stacked LSTM / GRU / Bi-LSTM |
| **Decoder** | $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = g_{\text{dec}}(y_{t-1}, s_t, \mathbf{c})$ | Stacked LSTM / GRU |
| **Context Vector** | $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (fixed dim) | Final hidden state of Encoder |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Architecture

The Seq2Seq pipeline executes in three structured stages:

### Stage 1 — Encoder Forward Pass
- The input tokens $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T_x}$ are converted into dense vectors via an **embedding layer** $E: \mathcal{V} \to \mathbb{R}^{d_e}$.
- Each embedding is fed sequentially into a recurrent cell (e.g., LSTM).
- The cell updates its hidden state at every time step:

$$
h_t = \text{LSTM}(E(x_t),\; h_{t-1},\; c^{\text{cell}}_{t-1})
$$

- The final hidden state $h_{T_x}$ (and the final cell state $c^{\text{cell}}_{T_x}$ in LSTM) is taken as the **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$:

$$
\mathbf{c} = h_{T_x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In **Bi-LSTM Encoders**, the context is formed by *concatenating* the final forward hidden state $\overrightarrow{h}_{T_x}$ and the final backward hidden state $\overleftarrow{h}_1$:

$$
\mathbf{c} = \left[\overrightarrow{h}_{T_x} \; ; \; \overleftarrow{h}_1\right] \in \mathbb{R}^{2d}
$$

### Stage 2 — Decoder Initialization
- The decoder's initial hidden state $s_0$ is initialized from the context: $s_0 = \phi(\mathbf{c})$, where $\phi$ is typically a learnable dense layer (or simply $s_0 = \mathbf{c}$).

### Stage 3 — Decoder Autoregressive Generation
- At each time step $t$, the decoder cell produces a new hidden state $s_t$ conditioned on:
  1. The previous decoder hidden state $s_{t-1}$.
  2. The embedding of the previously generated token $y_{t-1}$.
  3. The context vector $\mathbf{c}$.

$$
s_t = \text{LSTM}\bigl(E(y_{t-1}),\; s_{t-1},\; c^{\text{cell-dec}}_{t-1}\bigr)
$$

- The output probability distribution over the vocabulary is obtained via a **softmax** over a projection:

$$
P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \text{softmax}\bigl(W_o \, s_t + b_o\bigr)
$$

- The token is sampled: $y_t = \arg\max_v P(v \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$ (during greedy inference).

> [!NOTE]
> The decoder is **autoregressive** — each generated token depends on all previously generated tokens. This is the property that **GPT-style** models inherit.

## 2.2 Training vs Inference — Teacher Forcing

| Phase | What is fed as $y_{t-1}$? | Why? |
|-------|----------------------------|------|
| **Training** | The **ground-truth** previous token (teacher forcing) | Faster convergence, stable gradients |
| **Inference** | The **model's own** previous prediction | No ground truth available; can suffer exposure bias |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exposure Bias:** Mismatch between training and inference distributions degrades quality at test time. This motivates **Scheduled Sampling** (Bengio et al., 2015).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{c} = h_{T_x}^{(\text{encoder})}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;s_t = f_{\text{dec}}\bigl(E(y_{t-1}),\; s_{t-1},\; \mathbf{c}\bigr)\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log P\bigl(y_t^{\star} \mid y_{<t}^{\star}, \mathbf{c}\bigr)\;}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value (KTU Standard) |
|--------|---------|------------------------------|
| $T_x$ | Input sequence length | Variable (e.g., 10–500) |
| $T_y$ | Output sequence length | Variable (e.g., 10–500) |
| $d$ | Hidden state dimension | **256, 512, 1024** |
| $d_e$ | Embedding dimension | **128, 256, 512** |
| $V$ | Vocabulary size | $10^4$ – $10^5$ |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Cross-entropy loss | Minimized via Backprop-Through-Time (BPTT) |
| BPTT | Backpropagation Through Time | Unrolls RNN for $T$ steps |
| **BLEU** | Translation quality metric | Range $\mathbf{0 \leq \text{BLEU} \leq 100}$ |
| **ROUGE** | Summarization quality metric | Range $\mathbf{0 \leq \text{ROUGE} \leq 1}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Neural Machine Translation (GNMT, 2016):** 8-layer stacked LSTMs with residual connections, deployed live in Google Translate.
- **Subword Tokenization (BPE / WordPiece):** Solves the Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) problem by breaking rare words into subword units.
- **Speech Recognition:** Audio → sequence of phonemes (DeepSpeech 2, Baidu).
- **Chatbots & Dialogue Systems:** Pre-Transformer backbone of early conversational AI.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Decoder Cross-Entropy Loss

The Seq2Seq model is trained to maximize the conditional log-likelihood of the target sequence given the input. We derive the loss from first principles.

**Step 1 — Factor the joint distribution via the chain rule:**

$$
P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Seq2Seq factorization** (the decoder only conditions on $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens):

$$
P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})
$$

**Step 3 — Take the negative logarithm** to convert the maximization problem into a minimization:

$$
-\log P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the softmax parametrization** $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \dfrac{e^{w_{y_t}^{T} s_t}}{\sum_{v=1}^{V} e^{w_{v}^{T} s_t}}$:

$$
-\log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = -w_{y_t}^{T} s_t + \log \sum_{v=1}^{V} e^{w_{v}^{T} s_t}
$$

**Step 5 — Average over a mini-batch of size $N$** to obtain the empirical risk:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \log P\Bigl(y_t^{(n)} \;\Big|\; y_{<t}^{(n)}, \mathbf{c}^{(n)}\Bigr)
$$

**Final boxed loss:**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \log P\bigl(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, \mathbf{c}^{(n)}\bigr)\;}
$$

This is the standard **categorical cross-entropy** loss, which the KTU board often asks students to derive.

## 3.2 Derivation of BPTT Gradient — Loss w.r.t. Encoder Parameters

Because the loss $\mathcal{L}$ depends on the encoder parameters $\theta_{\text{enc}}$ only through the context vector $\mathbf{c} = h_{T_x}$, the gradient must traverse the entire unrolled sequence.

**Step 1 — Chain rule from $\mathcal{L}$ to $\theta_{\text{enc}}$:**

$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial s_t} \cdot \dfrac{\partial s_t}{\partial \mathbf{c}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}}$ via the unrolled hidden states** $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_{T_x}$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}} = \sum_{k=1}^{T_x} \dfrac{\partial h_{T_x}}{\partial h_k} \cdot \dfrac{\partial h_k}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}}
$$

**Step 3 — The Jacobian of the unrolled RNN is a product of transition Jacobians:**

$$
\dfrac{\partial h_{T_x}}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{T_x} \dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain the BPTT gradient:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial s_t} \cdot \dfrac{\partial s_t}{\partial \mathbf{c}} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{T_x} \left(\prod_{j=k+1}^{T_x} \dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}\right) \dfrac{\partial h_k}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product of Jacobians $\prod_{j=k+1}^{T_x} \dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$ is the source of the **vanishing / exploding gradient problem**. This is the exact reason **LSTM** and **GRU** gating mechanisms (Modules 2–3) are preferred over vanilla RNNs in Seq2Seq.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why the Basic Seq2Seq Fails on Long Sequences

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Symptom | Engineering Remedy |
|--------------|------------|---------|--------------------|
| **Bottleneck** | $\mathbf{c}$ is fixed-dim $d$ | Loss of long-range context | Increase $d$ (limited by memory) |
| **Vanishing gradient** | $\prod \partial h_j / \partial h_{j-1}$ collapses | Encoder forgets early tokens | Use LSTM/GRU gates |
| **Exposure bias** | Teacher forcing vs free-running mismatch | Error accumulation at inference | Scheduled sampling |
| **Repeated tokens** | Decoder has no global view | "the the the the" | Beam search + coverage |
| **OOV words** | Fixed vocabulary | `<unk>` tokens | BPE / WordPiece tokenization |

> [!TIP]
> Every remedy in this table is a **direct ancestor** of a concept studied later in KTU Module 5: *attention* fixes bottleneck, *residual connections* fix gradient, *subword tokenization* is universal in modern Transformers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below traces the **information flow** from raw input tokens through the encoder, context vector, and autoregressive decoder, including the teacher-forcing signal during training.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["Input Tokens x1 x2 ... xTx"] --> A1["Embedding Layer E"]
    A1 --> A2["Encoder LSTM Cell 1"]
    A2 --> A3["Encoder LSTM Cell 2"]
    A3 --> A4["Encoder LSTM Cell Tx"]
    A4 --> A5["Context Vector c in R d"]
    A5 --> A6["Decoder Init s0 phi c"]
    A6 --> A7["Decoder LSTM Cell 1"]
    A7 --> A8["Softmax Projection W o s t plus b o"]
    A8 --> A9["Argmax Sampling y1"]
    A9 --> A10["Decoder LSTM Cell 2"]
    A10 --> A11["Softmax Projection"]
    A11 --> A12["Argmax Sampling y2"]
    A12 --> A13["Output Tokens y1 y2 ... yTy"]

    TF["Ground Truth y_t star"] -. Teacher Forcing .- A7
    TF -. Teacher Forcing .- A10

    classDef enc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctr fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4 enc
    class A5 ctr
    class A6,A7,A10,A13 dec
    class A8,A9,A11,A12 out
```

## 4.2 Greedy Decoding vs Beam Search — Comparison Flow

The diagram below contrasts the two most common inference strategies. **Greedy decoding** picks the locally best token, while **beam search** keeps the top-$k$ partial hypotheses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start with s0 and c"] --> Q{"Decoding Strategy?"}

    Q -->|Greedy k=1| G1["Compute softmax over V"]
    G1 --> G2["Pick argmax v = y t"]
    G2 --> G3["Append y t to sequence"]
    G3 --> G4{"y t = EOS?"}
    G4 -->|No| G1
    G4 -->|Yes| Gout["Output Sequence"]

    Q -->|Beam Search k=B| B1["Maintain B partial hypotheses"]
    B1 --> B2["Expand each hypothesis by V tokens"]
    B2 --> B3["Score log P y t plus cumulative log P"]
    B3 --> B4["Keep top B hypotheses"]
    B4 --> B5{"All B = EOS?"}
    B5 -->|No| B2
    B5 -->|Yes| Bout["Output best-scoring sequence"]

    classDef greedy fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef beam fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef start fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class G1,G2,G3,G4,Gout greedy
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,Bout beam
    class Start,Q start
```

## 4.3 Loss Computation & Backpropagation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["True y1 star ... yTy star"] --> P1["Embedding of y t minus 1 star"]
    P1 --> P2["Decoder Forward s t"]
    P2 --> P3["Softmax P y t given y less than t c"]
    P3 --> P4["Cross Entropy Loss L"]
    P4 --> P5["Backprop Through Time BPTT"]
    P5 --> P6["Gradients w.r.t. W o b o theta dec"]
    P5 --> P7["Gradients w.r.t. theta enc via c"]
    P6 --> P8["Adam or SGD Update"]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9["Updated Parameters"]

    classDef loss fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef grad fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#8a2be2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#afeeee,stroke:#008b8b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class P0,P1,P2,P3,P4 loss
    class P5,P6,P7 grad
    class P8,P9 opt
```
