<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Encoder–Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence architecture. What is the role of the context vector?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Encoder–Decoder Seq2Seq architecture is a neural network model that maps a variable-length input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ to a variable-length output sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \ldots, y_{T_y})$. The **Encoder** recurrent network processes the input tokens one-by-one and compresses them into a fixed-dimensional **context vector** $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (typically the final hidden state $h_{T_x}$). The **Decoder** recurrent network is initialized with $\mathbf{c}$ and generates the output tokens **autoregressively**, with each token conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$ and all previously generated tokens. The context vector acts as the **only bridge** between input and output, hence the entire semantic summary of the input must flow through this single bottleneck. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain "teacher forcing" used during the training of Seq2Seq models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Teacher forcing is a training strategy in which, during the decoder's forward pass, the **ground-truth previous token** $y_{t-1}^{\star}$ is fed as input at time step $t$ instead of the model's own previous prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$. This stabilizes training, accelerates convergence, and mitigates vanishing gradients because the decoder is always conditioned on the correct history. However, it creates an **exposure bias**: the model never learns to recover from its own mistakes, motivating *scheduled sampling* as a remedy. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` (Total: 14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Encoder–Decoder Seq2Seq architecture** for Machine Translation. Clearly state the role of the context vector and show the mathematical formulation of the encoder and decoder. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block diagram** (drawn neatly, 2 marks): Show input tokens → embedding → stacked encoder RNN → context vector $\mathbf{c}$ → decoder RNN → softmax → output tokens. Use Figure 4.1 as reference.

2. **Encoder formulation** (2 marks): The encoder is an RNN that consumes the input sequence and produces hidden states $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_{T_x}$:

$$
h_t = f_{\text{enc}}(E(x_t),\; h_{t-1})
$$

The final hidden state is taken as the context: $\mathbf{c} = h_{T_x}$.

3. **Decoder formulation** (2 marks): The decoder is an autoregressive RNN initialized with $s_0 = \phi(\mathbf{c})$:

$$
s_t = f_{\text{dec}}(E(y_{t-1}),\; s_{t-1},\; \mathbf{c})
$$

$$
P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)
$$

4. **Role of context vector** (1 mark): The context vector is the **sole information channel** carrying the semantic summary of the entire input sequence to the decoder.

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the **cross-entropy loss** used to train a Seq2Seq model. Show the final expression and explain why teacher forcing makes the loss decomposable over time steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the joint conditional probability and applying the chain rule:

$$
P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})
$$

Taking negative log-likelihood and substituting the softmax parametrization:

$$
-\log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = -w_{y_t}^{T} s_t + \log\sum_{v=1}^{V} e^{w_v^{T} s_t}
$$

Averaging over a mini-batch of $N$ samples:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \log P\bigl(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, \mathbf{c}^{(n)}\bigr)
$$

**Why teacher forcing enables decomposition (2 marks):** Because the ground-truth token $y_{t-1}^{\star}$ is fed at every step, the decoder's hidden state $s_t$ depends only on the *observed* past, not on the model's own stochastic predictions. Hence the loss at time $t$ is conditionally independent of the losses at other time steps *given* the previous ground-truth tokens, allowing efficient **parallel teacher-forced training** rather than expensive sequential rollouts.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Total: 14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **limitations of the basic Encoder–Decoder Seq2Seq model** for long input sequences. How do LSTM cells and Bi-directional encoders partially address these limitations? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Bottleneck limitation** (2 marks): The single fixed-dimensional context vector $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ must encode all information from a sequence of arbitrary length $T_x$. As $T_x$ grows, the required information entropy exceeds $d$, causing the "**long-sequence forgetting problem**" — the decoder loses track of early tokens.

2. **Vanishing gradient** (2 marks): The gradient $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{\text{enc}}}$ involves a product of transition Jacobians $\prod \partial h_j / \partial h_{j-1}$, which collapses exponentially with $T_x$ in vanilla RNNs.

3. **LSTM remedy** (2 marks): LSTM cells replace the simple tanh with a **gated cell state** $c^{\text{cell}}_t$ protected by input/forget/output gates, providing a near-linear gradient path that mitigates vanishing gradients and preserves long-range memory.

4. **Bi-directional encoder remedy** (1 mark): A Bi-LSTM processes the input in both directions, so the final context $\mathbf{c} = [\overrightarrow{h}_{T_x} \,;\, \overleftarrow{h}_1]$ captures information from *both ends* of the sequence, providing a richer summary.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **greedy decoding** and **beam search** algorithms used during inference in Seq2Seq models. Compare their trade-offs with appropriate equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Greedy decoding** (2 marks): At each step, the token with the highest conditional probability is selected:

$$
\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_{v \in \mathcal{V}} P(v \mid \hat{y}_{<t}, \mathbf{c})
$$

Greedy decoding is $\mathcal{O}(T_y \cdot V)$ and simple, but **suboptimal** — a locally best token may lead to a globally poor sequence.

2. **Beam search** (3 marks): Maintains the top-$B$ partial hypotheses (beams). At each step, each beam is expanded by all $V$ tokens, producing $B \cdot V$ candidates. The top-$B$ by cumulative log-probability are retained:

$$
\text{score}(\mathbf{y}_{\leq t}) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \log P(y_k \mid y_{<k}, \mathbf{c})
$$

Stopping criterion: all $B$ beams generate $\langle \text{EOS} \rangle$. The hypothesis with the highest score is returned, optionally with a **length normalization** $\dfrac{\text{score}}{t^{\alpha}}$ to penalize short sequences.

3. **Trade-off comparison** (2 marks):
   - **Greedy:** $B=1$, fastest, lowest BLEU.
   - **Beam search ($B=5$ to $10$):** Better BLEU, $\mathcal{O}(B \cdot V \cdot T_y)$ cost.
   - **Trade-off:** Larger $B$ yields diminishing returns and may produce overly generic translations; $B \to V$ reduces to exact search (intractable).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing the context vector with the cell state:** The context vector is the *hidden state* $h_{T_x}$, not the LSTM cell state $c^{\text{cell}}_{T_x}$. These are distinct.
> 2. **Forgetting the softmax in the decoder:** Many students write only the hidden-state recurrence and forget the final output projection $W_o s_t + b_o$ followed by softmax. This alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Claiming the context vector carries "all" information:** It is a *lossy compressed summary*, not an exhaustive copy. Use the word "summary" or "compressed representation" in exams.
> 4. **Mixing up teacher forcing and scheduled sampling:** Teacher forcing = always ground truth; scheduled sampling = probability of switching between ground truth and model prediction.
> 5. **Not writing the negative sign** in the loss: KTU examiners are strict — write $-\log$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ The **Encoder–Decoder Seq2Seq** maps a variable-length input to a variable-length output via a **single fixed-dimensional context vector** $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
- ⭐ The **Encoder** is typically an LSTM/GRU/Bi-LSTM; the final hidden state is $\mathbf{c}$.
- ⭐ The **Decoder** is **autoregressive** — each output depends on $\mathbf{c}$ and all previous outputs.
- ⭐ The **decoder probability** is $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)$.
- ⭐ The **training loss** is **categorical cross-entropy**: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \log P(y_t^{\star} \mid y_{<t}^{\star}, \mathbf{c})$.
- ⭐ **Teacher forcing** feeds ground truth during training, exposing the model to a *train/test distribution mismatch* known as **exposure bias**.
- ⭐ **Greedy decoding** picks $\arg\max$ at each step; **Beam search** keeps top-$B$ hypotheses for higher BLEU.
- ⭐ The **BPTT gradient** involves a product of Jacobians — the source of vanishing/exploding gradients.
- ⭐ **LSTM/GRU** and **Bi-LSTM encoders** partially alleviate the bottleneck; **attention** (Module 5) fully solves it.
- ⭐ Real-world deployments: **GNMT (Google Translate)**, **DeepSpeech 2 (Baidu)**, and early conversational chatbots all relied on stacked LSTM Seq2Seqs.
- ⭐ KTU 2024 frequently tests: (i) the loss derivation, (ii) the role of $\mathbf{c}$, (iii) the difference between teacher forcing and inference, and (iv) beam search vs greedy decoding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Recurrent Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deep Recurrent Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Deep Recurrent Network (Deep RNN)** is a neural architecture that stacks multiple recurrent layers vertically, where each layer maintains its own hidden state, enabling the model to learn hierarchical temporal representations across multiple levels of abstraction. Unlike a standard (shallow) RNN that contains a single recurrent hidden layer, a deep recurrent network comprises **two or more recurrent layers**, allowing the network to capture both low-level temporal patterns (in lower layers) and high-level abstract sequential features (in upper layers).

Formally, for an $L$-layer deep RNN, the forward propagation at time step $t$ is governed by:

$$h_t^{(l)} = f_l\left(W^{(l)} h_{t-1}^{(l)} + U^{(l)} h_t^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}\right)$$

where $h_t^{(l)}$ is the hidden state of layer $l$ at time $t$, $W^{(l)}$ and $U^{(l)}$ are the recurrent and input weight matrices, $b^{(l)}$ is the bias, and $f_l$ is the layer-specific activation function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Deep Recurrent Networks extend shallow RNNs by introducing depth along the *layer dimension*, while preserving recurrence along the *time dimension*. This dual depth makes them powerful for tasks involving long sequences and complex temporal hierarchies.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are **reading a novel chapter by chapter**:

- A **shallow RNN** is like a reader who only remembers the *last sentence* and tries to understand the entire chapter from that single piece of memory. It can track short-term context but quickly loses the plot.
- A **Deep RNN** is like a team of readers stacked hierarchically:
  - The **bottom reader** tracks letter-by-letter or word-by-word patterns.
  - The **middle reader** notices sentence structures and grammar.
  - The **top reader** understands the abstract theme, character arcs, and plot progression across chapters.

Each layer builds a richer, more abstract understanding from the layer below it — this is the essence of hierarchical temporal learning. Combined with gating mechanisms like **LSTM** and **GRU**, deep recurrent networks can remember information over thousands of time steps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Exams:**
> - **Hidden State ($h_t$):** The memory of the network at time $t$.
> - **Cell State ($C_t$):** Long-term memory in LSTM networks.
> - **Gates:** Learnable mechanisms that control information flow (forget, input, output).
> - **BPTT:** Backpropagation Through Time — the training algorithm for RNNs.

## 1.3 Why Deep Recurrent Networks?

Standard RNNs suffer from two critical problems:

1. **Vanishing / Exploding Gradient Problem:** Gradients shrink or explode across many time steps, making long-term dependencies impossible to learn.
2. **Limited Representational Capacity:** A single hidden layer cannot capture hierarchical features in complex sequential data (e.g., speech, music, language).

Deep RNNs address capacity issues by stacking layers, while gated variants (LSTM, GRU) mitigate gradient problems through gating mechanisms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Deep Recurrent Network

A deep RNN with $L$ layers operates on an input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$ and produces an output sequence. The key components are:

- **Input Layer:** Receives the raw sequence at each time step.
- **Recurrent Hidden Layers ($l = 1, 2, \dots, L$):** Each layer maintains its own hidden state sequence.
- **Output Layer:** Generates predictions at each time step (or only at the final step for many-to-one tasks).

### Layer-wise Forward Dynamics

For each time step $t$ and each layer $l$:

$$\mathbf{a}_t^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(l)} + \mathbf{U}^{(l)} \mathbf{h}_t^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{(l)} = f_l(\mathbf{a}_t^{(l)})$$

For the input layer ($l = 0$), we substitute $\mathbf{h}_t^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}_t$.

## 2.2 LSTM: Long Short-Term Memory

The LSTM cell introduces a **cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$ and three gating mechanisms to control information flow.

### LSTM Equations

$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f) \quad \text{(Forget Gate)}$$

$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i) \quad \text{(Input Gate)}$$

$$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_C [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C) \quad \text{(Candidate Values)}$$

$$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t \quad \text{(Cell State Update)}$$

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o) \quad \text{(Output Gate)}$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t) \quad \text{(Hidden State Output)}$$

Where $\sigma$ is the **sigmoid function**, $\tanh$ is the hyperbolic tangent, and $\odot$ denotes element-wise multiplication.

## 2.3 GRU: Gated Recurrent Unit

The GRU is a simplified LSTM variant that combines the forget and input gates into a single **update gate**, and merges the cell state and hidden state.

### GRU Equations

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_z [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_z) \quad \text{(Update Gate)}$$

$$\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_r [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_r) \quad \text{(Reset Gate)}$$

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_h [\mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_h) \quad \text{(Candidate Hidden State)}$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t \quad \text{(Hidden State Update)}$$

## 2.4 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

BPTT unrolls the recurrent network across all $T$ time steps and applies the standard backpropagation algorithm on the unrolled computational graph.

The loss function for the entire sequence is typically:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$$

The gradient of the loss with respect to a recurrent weight $\mathbf{W}$ accumulates across all time steps:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$$

Due to the chain rule applied through time, the gradient at time $t$ involves products of Jacobians across all intermediate steps, which is the source of the vanishing/exploding gradient problem.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| RNN Hidden Update | $h_t = \tanh(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b)$ | Standard shallow RNN forward pass |
| Deep RNN Layer Update | $h_t^{(l)} = f_l(W^{(l)} h_{t-1}^{(l)} + U^{(l)} h_t^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$ | Stacked recurrent layer |
| LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Decides what to discard |
| LSTM Input Gate | $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Decides what new info to store |
| LSTM Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Decides what to output |
| LSTM Cell State | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Long-term memory update |
| GRU Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | Balances old vs. new state |
| GRU Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | Controls past influence |
| Total Sequence Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$ | Sum of per-timestep losses |
| BPTT Gradient | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W}$ | Accumulated gradient over time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Deep recurrent networks power real-world systems including Google Translate (sequence-to-sequence translation), Apple Siri / Amazon Alexa (speech recognition), stock market prediction models, music generation (Jukedeck), and protein structure prediction (AlphaFold uses attention, but earlier RNN variants were foundational).

## 2.6 Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Analysis

For a standard RNN, the gradient of loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ with respect to hidden state $h_k$ (where $k < t$) involves the product:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$$

Each term $\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ has eigenvalues bounded by the largest singular value of $W$. If the singular value is greater than 1, the product **explodes**; if less than 1, it **vanishes**. LSTM mitigates this with the additive cell state update $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + \dots$, which provides a near-linear gradient pathway.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass of a 2-Layer Deep RNN

Let us derive the complete forward dynamics for a **2-layer deep RNN** with input $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ and hidden sizes $n_1, n_2$ for layers 1 and 2 respectively.

### Layer 1 (Bottom Recurrent Layer)

**Step 1:** Compute the pre-activation at layer 1 using the input and previous hidden state.

$$\mathbf{a}_t^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{(1)} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(1)} + \mathbf{W}_{xh}^{(1)} \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the activation function (typically $\tanh$).

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{(1)} = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t^{(1)})$$

### Layer 2 (Top Recurrent Layer)

**Step 3:** Compute the pre-activation at layer 2 using the output of layer 1 as input.

$$\mathbf{a}_t^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(2)} + \mathbf{W}_{xh}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}_t^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the activation function.

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{(2)} = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t^{(2)})$$

### Output Layer

**Step 5:** Compute the output using the top layer's hidden state.

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy} \mathbf{h}_t^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}_y)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: BPTT Gradient for a Simple RNN

Consider a single-layer RNN with loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ at each time step. We derive the gradient of total loss with respect to the recurrent weight matrix $W$.

### Setup

The forward equations are:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{U} \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b})$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{V} \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{c})$$

### Gradient with Respect to Output Weights $\mathbf{V}$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{V}} = (\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t - \mathbf{y}_t) \mathbf{h}_t^\top$$

### Gradient with Respect to Hidden States

By the chain rule across time:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \mathbf{V}^\top (\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t - \mathbf{y}_t)$$

For earlier time steps $k < t$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}}$$

### Jacobian of the Hidden State

The Jacobian matrix at each time step is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - \mathbf{h}_i^2) \cdot \mathbf{W}$$

### Final Gradient with Respect to $\mathbf{W}$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \text{diag}(1 - \mathbf{h}_k^2) \cdot \mathbf{h}_{k-1}^\top$$

Summing over all time steps:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \text{diag}(1 - \mathbf{h}_k^2) \cdot \mathbf{h}_{k-1}^\top$$

This double summation reveals why BPTT is computationally expensive and why gradient pathologies arise.

## 3.3 Derivation: LSTM Cell State Gradient

For the LSTM, the cell state update is:

$$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$$

Taking the gradient:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{C}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(\mathbf{f}_t)$$

Unrolling across time:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{C}_T}{\partial \mathbf{C}_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \text{diag}(\mathbf{f}_i)$$

Since each $\mathbf{f}_t \in [0, 1]$ is a learnable gate, the network can keep this product near 1 (by learning $\mathbf{f}_t \approx 1$), allowing gradients to flow without vanishing — this is the **key advantage** of LSTM over plain RNN.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Simple RNN** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gates | None | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Memory Mechanism | Single hidden state | Cell state + hidden state | Single hidden state |
| Vanishing Gradient | Severe | Mitigated | Mitigated |
| Parameter Count | Low | High | Medium |
| Training Speed | Fast | Slow | Medium |
| Long-term Dependencies | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Mathematical Complexity | Simple | Complex | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deep Recurrent Network Architecture (Unrolled View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX1["x1"] --> nodeH11["h1_L1"]
    nodeX2["x2"] --> nodeH12["h2_L1"]
    nodeX3["x3"] --> nodeH13["h3_L1"]
    nodeH11 --> nodeH12
    nodeH12 --> nodeH13
    nodeH11 --> nodeH21["h1_L2"]
    nodeH12 --> nodeH22["h2_L2"]
    nodeH13 --> nodeH23["h3_L2"]
    nodeH21 --> nodeH22
    nodeH22 --> nodeH23
    nodeH21 --> nodeY1["y1"]
    nodeH22 --> nodeY2["y2"]
    nodeH23 --> nodeY3["y3"]
```

**Description:** This diagram shows a 2-layer deep RNN unrolled across 3 time steps. The horizontal arrows represent temporal recurrence within each layer, while the vertical arrows represent the feedforward connection from layer 1 to layer 2.

## 4.2 LSTM Cell Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeXt["xt and ht-1"] --> nodeFgate["Forget Gate: ft"]
    nodeXt --> nodeIgate["Input Gate: it"]
    nodeXt --> nodeOgate["Output Gate: ot"]
    nodeXt --> nodeCand["Candidate: Ct_tilde"]
    nodeFgate --> nodeMult1["Multiply: ft * Ct-1"]
    nodeCtPrev["Ct-1"] --> nodeMult1
    nodeIgate --> nodeMult2["Multiply: it * Ct_tilde"]
    nodeCand --> nodeMult2
    nodeMult1 --> nodeCsum["Cell State Sum"]
    nodeMult2 --> nodeCsum
    nodeCsum --> nodeCt["Ct"]
    nodeCt --> nodeTanh["tanh"]
    nodeOgate --> nodeMult3["Multiply: ot * tanh"]
    nodeTanh --> nodeMult3
    nodeMult3 --> nodeHt["ht"]
    nodeCt --> nodeCtNext["Ct feeds next step"]
```

**Description:** The LSTM cell receives concatenated input $[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t]$ and computes three gates plus candidate values. The forget gate modulates old cell state, the input gate modulates new candidate values, and the output gate controls what hidden state is emitted.

## 4.3 GRU Cell Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeInput["xt and ht-1"] --> nodeZgate["Update Gate: zt"]
    nodeInput --> nodeRgate["Reset Gate: rt"]
    nodeRgate --> nodeMultReset["Multiply: rt * ht-1"]
    nodeInput --> nodeCand["Candidate: ht_tilde"]
    nodeMultReset --> nodeCand
    nodeZgate --> nodeInterp["Linear Interpolation"]
    nodeInput --> nodeInterp
    nodeCand --> nodeInterp
    nodeInterp --> nodeHt["ht"]
    nodeHt --> nodeNextStep["Feeds next time step"]
```

**Description:** The GRU combines the forget and input gates into a single update gate $\mathbf{z}_t$, and uses a reset gate $\mathbf{r}_t$ to control how much past information influences the candidate hidden state.

## 4.4 BPTT Unrolled Computational Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeBPTT1["h0"] --> nodeBPTT2["h1"]
    nodeBPTT2 --> nodeBPTT3["h2"]
    nodeBPTT3 --> nodeBPTT4["h3"]
    nodeBPTT4 --> nodeBPTT5["hT"]
    nodeBPTT1 -.->|Loss gradient flow| nodeBPTT5
    nodeBPTT2 -.->|Backward pass| nodeBPTT3
    nodeBPTT3 -.->|Backward pass| nodeBPTT4
```

**Description:** Solid arrows indicate the forward pass through time, while dashed arrows represent the backward gradient flow during BPTT. The gradient must traverse from $h_T$ all the way back to $h_0$, accumulating products of Jacobians.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a Deep Recurrent Neural Network. How does it differ from a shallow RNN?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Deep Recurrent Neural Network is a recurrent architecture in which **two or more recurrent hidden layers** are stacked sequentially. The defining structural difference from a shallow RNN is that:

1. In a **shallow RNN**, there is a single recurrent hidden layer with hidden state $h_t = f(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b)$.
2. In a **Deep RNN**, each layer $l$ maintains its own hidden state $h_t^{(l)}$, and the input to layer $l$ is the output of layer $l-1$ at the same time step: $h_t^{(l)} = f_l(W^{(l)} h_{t-1}^{(l)} + U^{(l)} h_t^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$.

This hierarchical stacking enables the network to learn **multi-level temporal abstractions**, where lower layers capture fine-grained patterns and upper layers capture high-level semantic features.

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: shallow RNN equation; 1 mark: deep RNN equation and explanation of hierarchy)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? How does LSTM address it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** in RNNs occurs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), where gradients must be multiplied across many time steps. For a simple RNN, the gradient at time step $k$ involves products of Jacobians $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \cdot W$. When the spectral radius of $W$ is less than 1, these products shrink exponentially toward zero, making it impossible to learn long-range dependencies.

**LSTM addresses this** through its **additive cell state update**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$. The gradient $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$ is controlled by a learnable forget gate $f_t \in [0,1]$. The network can learn to set $f_t \approx 1$, creating a near-linear gradient highway that allows information to flow across hundreds of time steps without vanishing.

*(1 mark: explain vanishing gradient; 1 mark: mathematical source; 1 mark: LSTM solution via additive update and forget gate)*

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: LSTM Architecture and Equations

**Q: Explain the architecture of an LSTM network with a neat diagram. Derive all the forward pass equations for the LSTM cell.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Description (3 Marks):**

The Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network is a gated recurrent architecture designed to capture long-term dependencies. An LSTM cell maintains two state vectors:

- **Cell State ($C_t$):** The long-term memory that flows through the cell with minimal linear interactions.
- **Hidden State ($h_t$):** The short-term output exposed to the next time step and upper layers.

The cell uses **three multiplicative gates** to regulate information flow:

1. **Forget Gate ($f_t$):** Decides what information to discard from the cell state.
2. **Input Gate ($i_t$):** Decides what new information to store in the cell state.
3. **Output Gate ($o_t$):** Decides what parts of the cell state to output as the hidden state.

**Equations Derivation (4 Marks):**

Given input $x_t$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$, we first concatenate them:

$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{n + d}$$

**Forget Gate:** Controls how much of the previous cell state is retained.

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

**Input Gate:** Controls how much new candidate information enters.

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

**Candidate Cell State:** New values that could be added to the cell state.

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

**Cell State Update:** Combines the gated old and new information.

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Output Gate:** Controls which parts of the cell state become the output.

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

**Hidden State Output:**

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: BPTT Algorithm

**Q: Explain the Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm for training RNNs. Why is it computationally expensive for long sequences?**

**Model Solution:**

**BPTT Algorithm Steps (4 Marks):**

BPTT unrolls the recurrent network across all $T$ time steps, converting it into a deep feedforward network with shared weights. The algorithm proceeds as:

**Step 1: Forward Pass**

Compute hidden states $h_t$ and outputs $\hat{y}_t$ for all $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$ using the RNN forward equations.

**Step 2: Compute Total Loss**

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$$

**Step 3: Backward Pass (Output Layer)**

Compute the gradient of loss at each time step with respect to the output:

$$\delta_t^{(y)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t$$

**Step 4: Backpropagate Through Hidden States**

For time step $t$, the gradient flowing back from $t+1$ is:

$$\delta_t^{(h)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} + \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t} \cdot \delta_{t+1}^{(h)}$$

**Step 5: Compute Weight Gradients**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \cdot (1 - h_t^2) \cdot h_{t-1}^\top$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial U} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \cdot (1 - h_t^2) \cdot x_t^\top$$

**Computational Expense (3 Marks):**

BPTT is expensive for long sequences because:

1. **Memory:** The entire sequence of $T$ hidden states must be stored during the forward pass to be reused in the backward pass, requiring $O(T \cdot n)$ memory where $n$ is the hidden size.
2. **Sequential Computation:** The backward pass must process time steps sequentially (from $T$ to $1$), preventing parallelization across time.
3. **Vanishing/Exploding Gradients:** For long sequences, the gradient involves products of $T$ Jacobians, which can vanish or explode, making optimization numerically unstable.
4. **Quadratic Cost in Practice:** With deep stacked RNNs having $L$ layers, the cost scales as $O(L \cdot T)$ for both forward and backward passes.

**Solutions** include Truncated BPTT (limiting backpropagation to a fixed window), gradient clipping, and using gated architectures like LSTM/GRU that mitigate gradient pathologies.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: GRU Architecture and Comparison with LSTM

**Q: Explain the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) architecture. Compare GRU with LSTM in terms of parameters, memory mechanism, and performance.**

**Model Solution:**

**GRU Architecture (3 Marks):**

The GRU simplifies the LSTM by combining the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** and merging the cell state and hidden state into one vector. It has two gates:

1. **Update Gate ($z_t$):** Controls how much of the past hidden state is retained versus how much new candidate state is used.
2. **Reset Gate ($r_t$):** Controls how much of the past hidden state influences the candidate state.

**GRU Equations (2 Marks):**

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$$

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Comparison with LSTM (2 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|---|---|---|
| Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| State Vectors | 2 (cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$) | 1 (hidden state $h_t$) |
| Parameters | 4 weight matrices per layer | 3 weight matrices per layer |
| Training Speed | Slower (more parameters) | Faster (fewer parameters) |
| Long-term Memory | Separate cell state | Implicit in hidden state |
| Performance | Better on very long sequences | Comparable on moderate sequences |

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Stacked Deep RNN for Sequence Classification

**Q: Design a 3-layer deep RNN architecture for sentiment classification of movie reviews. Explain the forward pass mathematically.**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem Setup (2 Marks):**

Given a movie review as a sequence of word embeddings $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_T \in \mathbb{R}^{100}$ (with embedding dimension 100), we wish to classify it as positive or negative sentiment (binary output).

**Architecture Design (2 Marks):**

- **Layer 1:** RNN with hidden size 128, $\tanh$ activation
- **Layer 2:** RNN with hidden size 64, $\tanh$ activation
- **Layer 3:** RNN with hidden size 32, $\tanh$ activation
- **Output Layer:** Fully connected layer with sigmoid activation for binary classification

**Forward Pass Equations (3 Marks):**

**Layer 1:**

$$h_t^{(1)} = \tanh(W_{hh}^{(1)} h_{t-1}^{(1)} + W_{xh}^{(1)} x_t + b^{(1)})$$

where $W_{hh}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{128 \times 128}$, $W_{xh}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{128 \times 100}$.

**Layer 2:**

$$h_t^{(2)} = \tanh(W_{hh}^{(2)} h_{t-1}^{(2)} + W_{xh}^{(2)} h_t^{(1)} + b^{(2)})$$

where $W_{hh}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{64 \times 64}$, $W_{xh}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{64 \times 128}$.

**Layer 3:**

$$h_t^{(3)} = \tanh(W_{hh}^{(3)} h_{t-1}^{(3)} + W_{xh}^{(3)} h_t^{(2)} + b^{(3)})$$

**Classification Output (using final hidden state):**

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(W_y h_T^{(3)} + b_y)$$

**Loss Function (Binary Cross-Entropy):**

$$\mathcal{L} = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y})]$$

**Total Trainable Parameters (approximate):**
- Layer 1: $(128 \times 128 + 128 \times 100 + 128) = 29,312$
- Layer 2: $(64 \times 64 + 64 \times 128 + 64) = 12,352$
- Layer 3: $(32 \times 32 + 32 \times 64 + 32) = 3,104$
- Output: $(1 \times 32 + 1) = 33$
- **Total: ~44,801 parameters**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Deep Recurrent Networks:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the forget gate and input gate roles. Remember: forget gate *removes* old info; input gate *adds* new info.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing LSTM cell state as $C_t = i_t \odot C_{t-1} + f_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — this is WRONG. The forget gate multiplies $C_{t-1}$, and the input gate multiplies $\tilde{C}_t$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting that GRU does NOT have a separate cell state — it only has a hidden state.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating the activation functions explicitly ($\sigma$ for gates, $\tanh$ for candidates). Examiners deduct marks for missing activation specifications.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In BPTT questions, students often forget to sum gradients across all time steps. The total gradient is a sum, not just the gradient at the final step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Deep Recurrent Networks:**

- **Deep RNN Definition:** Stacked recurrent layers ($L \geq 2$), each with its own hidden state sequence.
- **LSTM has 3 gates:** Forget ($f_t$), Input ($i_t$), Output ($o_t$) — always use sigmoid for gates.
- **GRU has 2 gates:** Update ($z_t$), Reset ($r_t$) — no separate cell state.
- **Cell State Update:** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ (additive prevents vanishing gradient).
- **GRU Hidden Update:** $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$.
- **BPTT** unrolls the network across all $T$ time steps and applies backpropagation.
- **Vanishing gradient** arises from products of Jacobians with spectral radius < 1.
- **Exploding gradient** arises from products of Jacobians with spectral radius > 1 (solution: gradient clipping).
- **Bidirectional RNNs** process sequences in both forward and backward directions for richer context.
- **Deep RNN input to layer $l$** is the output of layer $l-1$, not the raw input.
- **Activation functions:** $\sigma$ for gates (output in [0,1]), $\tanh$ for hidden states and candidates (output in [-1,1]).
- **Total Sequence Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$.
- **Common variants:** Stacked RNN, Bidirectional RNN, Encoder-Decoder, Seq2Seq with Attention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursive Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recursive Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recursive Neural Network (RvNN):** A class of deep learning architecture that processes structured inputs (typically **tree-structured** or **hierarchically nested** data) by applying the *same set of learnable weights recursively* over the input structure to produce a compositional representation and a prediction at each node.

Formally, given a binary tree $T$ with leaf nodes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$, the RvNN computes hidden state vectors for each internal node $j$ using a **compositional function** $f$:

$$h_j = f(h_{j_L}, h_{j_R}, \theta)$$

where $h_{j_L}$ and $h_{j_R}$ are the hidden representations of the left and right children, and $\theta$ is the shared parameter set.

## 1.2 Recurrent vs. Recursive — The Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> Many students confuse **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** with **Recursive Neural Networks (RvNNs)**. They are fundamentally different:

| Property | Recurrent NN (RNN) | Recursive NN (RvNN) |
|---|---|---|
| Input structure | **Sequence** (linear chain) | **Tree** (hierarchical) |
| Operation | Iterative over time steps $t$ | Recursive over tree structure |
| Compositional logic | $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ | $h_j = f(h_{j_L}, h_{j_R}, x_j)$ |
| Training | **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** | **Backpropagation Through Structure (BPTS)** |
| Typical use | Time-series, language modeling | Parse trees, scene parsing, constituency parsing |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Tree of Sentences

Imagine you are trying to determine the **sentiment** of the sentence *"The movie was not very good."*

A standard RNN reads the words left-to-right like reading a flat list. But a human linguist understands the sentence as a **tree**:

```
         ROOT
        /    \
   (NP)      (VP)
  /  \      /   \
The movie  was   (ADJP)
                  / \
                not (ADJP)
                       / \
                     very good
```

A Recursive Neural Network mimics this **linguistic intuition**. It starts from the leaves (individual words), combines them into phrases (*very good*), then negations (*not (very good)*), then full clauses, and finally produces one representation for the entire sentence. At each combination, the **same neural network** is applied — just like the same "blending rule" is applied again and again across the tree.

## 1.4 Key Metrics and Architectural Constants

The following standard parameters govern RvNN design and are commonly tested in KTU examinations:

- **Input word vector dimension** $d = \mathbf{50}$ to $\mathbf{300}$ (typically initialized via word2vec/GloVe).
- **Hidden state dimension** $D = \mathbf{25}$ to $\mathbf{200}$.
- **Output classes** $C$ (e.g., $C = 2$ for binary sentiment, $C = 5$ for fine-grained).
- **Activation function** $g(\cdot)$ — typically $\tanh$ or ReLU.
- **Loss function** — Cross-entropy for classification, MSE for regression.
- **Regularization** — $L_2$ penalty on weights with $\lambda \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-2}]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How an RvNN Works

The RvNN operates in **three structured phases**:

### Phase 1: Leaf Embedding (Bottom-Up Initialization)
Each leaf node $i$ (typically a word) is mapped to a dense vector representation via a word embedding matrix $L \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$:

$$h_i = L \cdot e_i \quad \text{or} \quad h_i = W_e \cdot x_i$$

where $e_i$ is the one-hot vector and $W_e$ is the embedding matrix.

### Phase 2: Recursive Composition (Internal Nodes)
For each internal node $j$ with children $L$ and $R$:

$$h_j = g \left( W \begin{bmatrix} h_L \\ h_R \end{bmatrix} + b \right)$$

- $W \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times 2D}$ — shared weight matrix
- $b \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ — bias vector
- $g$ — nonlinear activation (usually $\tanh$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "shared weights"?** Because the tree has *variable depth and branching*, we cannot learn a unique weight per node. The same $W$ is applied at *every* composition step, making the network depth-invariant and data-efficient.

### Phase 3: Prediction (Supervised Output)
At any node $j$, a label $\hat{y}_j$ can be predicted via a softmax layer:

$$\hat{y}_j = \text{softmax}\left( W_{label} \cdot h_j + b_{label} \right)$$

## 2.2 Backpropagation Through Structure (BPTS)

> [!NOTE]
> **BPTS** is the structural analogue of backpropagation. Gradients flow from the root (where the loss is computed) **down to all leaves**, respecting the tree topology.

For a node $j$ with parent $p$, the local gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_j} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_p} \cdot \frac{\partial h_p}{\partial h_j}$$

The recursive chain gives:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_j} = \sum_{k \in \text{Children}(p)} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_j}$$

This is essentially **the chain rule applied to a tree topology** rather than a linear chain.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — these are the **highest-yield formulas** for RvNN derivations in KTU ESE.

| **Symbol / Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Dimensionality** | **Physical / Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compositional state $h_j$ | $h_j = g\left(W \cdot \begin{bmatrix}h_L \\ h_R\end{bmatrix} + b\right)$ | $D \times 1$ | Phrase/sentence representation at node $j$ |
| Softmax output $\hat{y}_j$ | $\hat{y}_j = \mathrm{softmax}(W_{s} h_j + b_{s})$ | $C \times 1$ | Class probability at node $j$ |
| Cross-entropy loss | $L = -\sum_{j \in \text{labeled}} y_j^T \log \hat{y}_j$ | scalar | Supervised objective |
| Gradient w.r.t. $W$ | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{j} \delta_j \cdot [h_L, h_R]^T$ | $D \times 2D$ | Parameter update direction |
| Local error term $\delta_j$ | $\delta_j = (W^T \delta_{parent}) \odot g'(s_j)$ | $D \times 1$ | Backpropagated signal at node $j$ |
| BPTS recursion | $\delta_j = \sum_{c \in \text{children}} (W[:,c])^T \delta_c \odot g'(s_j)$ | $D \times 1$ | Aggregates errors from all children |
| $L_2$ regularization | $L_{\text{reg}} = L + \frac{\lambda}{2} \lVert W \rVert_F^2$ | scalar | Prevents overfitting on small trees |
| Weight update (SGD) | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial L_{\text{reg}}}{\partial W}$ | $D \times 2D$ | Learning rate $\eta$ typically $10^{-3}$ |

> **Notation safeguard:** all $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ terms use the matrix/vector norm, not the markdown table pipe. Use $\lVert x \rVert$ for norms.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Recursive Neural Networks power some of the most demanding real-world NLP systems:

- **Sentiment Classification** (Socher et al., Stanford) — fine-grained sentence-level emotion detection.
- **Semantic Relatedness** — predicting similarity scores between sentence pairs (SICK dataset, $C = 1$ regression output).
- **Constituency & Dependency Parsing** — building parse trees jointly with representations.
- **Scene Graph Parsing in Computer Vision** — recursive composition of objects and relations in images.
- **Code & Program Analysis** — recursive AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) embedding for vulnerability detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Forward Pass Derivation (Complete, Step-by-Step)

Let the input be a binary parse tree with leaves $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$ (words). We have $D = 2$ for tractability and $C = 2$ (binary classification).

**Given Data:**
- Word embeddings: $x_1 = [1, 0]^T$, $x_2 = [0, 1]^T$, $x_3 = [1, 1]^T$, $x_4 = [0, 0]^T$.
- $W = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (i.e., identity-like), $b = [0, 0]^T$.
- $W_s = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $b_s = 0$.

**Step 1: Initialize leaves.** Since $h_i = x_i$ (assuming identity embedding for clarity):

$$h_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute internal node $h_5$ (child of $h_1$ and $h_2$).**

We construct the concatenated vector:

$$\begin{bmatrix} h_1 \\ h_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply the composition:

$$s_5 = W \begin{bmatrix} h_1 \\ h_2 \end{bmatrix} + b = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply $g = \tanh$:

$$h_5 = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7616 \\ 0.7616 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute internal node $h_6$ (child of $h_3$ and $h_4$).**

$$s_6 = W \begin{bmatrix} h_3 \\ h_4 \end{bmatrix} + b = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_6 = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7616 \\ 0.7616 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute the root $h_7$ (parent of $h_5$ and $h_6$).**

$$\begin{bmatrix} h_5 \\ h_6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7616 \\ 0.7616 \\ 0.7616 \\ 0.7616 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$s_7 = W \begin{bmatrix} h_5 \\ h_6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7616 \\ 0.7616 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_7 = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 0.7616 \\ 0.7616 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6419 \\ 0.6419 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute output $\hat{y}$.**

$$\hat{y} = \mathrm{softmax}(W_s h_7 + b_s) = \mathrm{softmax}\begin{bmatrix} 1.2838 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7831 \\ 0.2169 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.2 Backward Pass Derivation (BPTS)

Suppose the true label is $y = [1, 0]^T$ (positive class). Cross-entropy loss:

$$L = -y^T \log \hat{y} = -[1, 0] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \log 0.7831 \\ \log 0.2169 \end{bmatrix} = 0.2445$$

**Step 6: Compute output-layer error.**

$$\delta_s = \hat{y} - y = \begin{bmatrix} -0.2169 \\ 0.2169 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7: Backpropagate to $h_7$.**

$$\delta_{h_7} = W_s^T \delta_s = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.2169 \\ 0.2169 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

(For this trivial case, the errors cancel out — we proceed formally.)

**Step 8: Compute gradient w.r.t. $W$.**

For a node with children $h_L, h_R$ and parent receiving gradient $\delta_p$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \delta_p \cdot \begin{bmatrix} h_L \\ h_R \end{bmatrix}^T$$

At the root (where $h_7$ is the parent of $h_5, h_6$):

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{\text{root}}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.7616 & 0.7616 & 0.7616 & 0.7616 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This indicates the network is already at a stable configuration for this input — no update needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General BPTS Rule:** For an internal node $j$ with children $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k$, the gradient w.r.t. $W$ is the sum of contributions from all paths from $j$ to the root loss:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{j \in \text{internal}} \delta_j \cdot \begin{bmatrix} h_{c_1} \\ h_{c_2} \end{bmatrix}^T$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Comparison

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Node 5** | **Node 6** | **Node 7 (Root)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Children left | $h_L$ | $h_1 = [1,0]^T$ | $h_3 = [1,1]^T$ | $h_5 = [0.7616, 0.7616]^T$ |
| Children right | $h_R$ | $h_2 = [0,1]^T$ | $h_4 = [0,0]^T$ | $h_6 = [0.7616, 0.7616]^T$ |
| Pre-activation | $s_j$ | $[1, 1]^T$ | $[1, 1]^T$ | $[0.7616, 0.7616]^T$ |
| Post-activation | $h_j$ | $[0.7616, 0.7616]^T$ | $[0.7616, 0.7616]^T$ | $[0.6419, 0.6419]^T$ |
| $\tanh'(s_j)$ | $1 - h_j^2$ | $[0.4200, 0.4200]^T$ | $[0.4200, 0.4200]^T$ | $[0.5880, 0.5880]^T$ |
| Local error (toy) | $\delta_j$ | $[0, 0]^T$ | $[0, 0]^T$ | $[0, 0]^T$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursive Tree Architecture (Mermaid Schematic)

The following diagram shows the structural data flow of an RvNN applied to a parsed sentence, including the block-level functional architecture for each phase.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Input Sentence: The movie was not very good"] --> B["Tokenizer"]
    B --> C1["x1: The"]
    B --> C2["x2: movie"]
    B --> C3["x3: was"]
    B --> C4["x4: not"]
    B --> C5["x5: very"]
    B --> C6["x6: good"]

    C1 --> E1["Embedding W_e"]
    C2 --> E2["Embedding W_e"]
    C3 --> E3["Embedding W_e"]
    C4 --> E4["Embedding W_e"]
    C5 --> E5["Embedding W_e"]
    C6 --> E6["Embedding W_e"]

    E1 --> N1A["h1: The"]
    E2 --> N1B["h2: movie"]
    E3 --> N2A["h3: was"]
    E4 --> N2B["h4: not"]
    E5 --> N3A["h5: very"]
    E6 --> N3B["h6: good"]

    N1A --> NODE5["Node 5: f h1,h2 -- Wx+b -- tanh"]
    N1B --> NODE5
    N5A["h5 = tanh(W[h1;h2]+b)"] -.-> NODE5
    N3A --> NODE7["Node 7: f h5,h6 -- Wx+b -- tanh"]
    N3B --> NODE7
    N2A --> NODE8["Node 8: f h3,h4 -- Wx+b -- tanh"]
    N2B --> NODE8
    NODE5 --> ROOT["Root Node 9: f h7,h8 -- Wx+b -- tanh"]
    NODE8 --> ROOT

    ROOT --> SOFTMAX["Softmax Classifier W_s"]
    SOFTMAX --> OUT["Output: Sentiment Class"]

    style NODE5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style NODE7 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style NODE8 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style ROOT fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style SOFTMAX fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style OUT fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1["Leaf h1: dim D"] --> C1["Concat: 2D vector"]
    L2["Leaf h2: dim D"] --> C1
    C1 --> LIN["Linear Layer: W 2DxD and b D"]
    LIN --> ACT["Activation: tanh or ReLU"]
    ACT --> HNEW["New Parent Node h_new: dim D"]

    HNEW --> PRED["Optional: Predict label at this node"]
    HNEW --> RECURSE["Recurse upward as child of higher node"]

    PRED --> LOSS["Cross-Entropy Loss"]
    RECURSE --> C1

    LOSS --> BPTS["BPTS: Propagate dL/dh_new to children"]
    BPTS --> UPD["SGD Update on W, b"]

    style L1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style L2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style C1 fill:#fff4e1
    style LIN fill:#ffe1e1
    style ACT fill:#e1ffe1
    style HNEW fill:#f0e1ff
    style LOSS fill:#ffe1f0
    style BPTS fill:#f5f5f5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each gray "Node N" box represents one application of the *same* shared-weight compositional function. The architecture has a **fractal-like self-similarity** — the same block structure recurs at every level of the tree.

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic: Recurrent vs. Recursive

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph REC["RECURRENT NN -- Sequential"]
        R1["x1"] --> R2["h1"]
        R2 --> R3["h2"]
        R3 --> R4["h3"]
        R4 --> R5["h4"]
        R5 --> ROUT["y_hat"]
    end

    subgraph REQ["RECURSIVE NN -- Tree Structured"]
        Q1["x1"] --> QNODE1["hA: f h1,h2"]
        Q2["x2"] --> QNODE1
        Q3["x3"] --> QNODE2["hB: f h3,h4"]
        Q4["x4"] --> QNODE2
        QNODE1 --> QROOT["hRoot: f hA,hB"]
        QNODE2 --> QROOT
        QROOT --> QOUT["y_hat"]
    end

    style REC fill:#fff0f0
    style REQ fill:#f0fff0
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) and Recursive Neural Networks (RvNN) with respect to input structure and training algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RNN | RvNN |
|---|---|---|
| Input structure | Linear sequence $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$ | Tree/hierarchical structure with parent-child relations |
| Computation | $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ applied iteratively over time | $h_j = f(h_{j_L}, h_{j_R})$ applied recursively over tree |
| Training | Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) | Backpropagation Through Structure (BPTS) |
| Weight sharing | Across time steps | Across all internal nodes of the tree |
| Typical application | Language modeling, time-series | Sentiment analysis, parse trees, scene graphs |

*(Award 1.5 marks for the structure difference, 1 mark for training algorithm, 0.5 mark for example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three applications of Recursive Neural Networks in Natural Language Processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Sentiment Classification** — predicting polarity (positive/negative) of a sentence using its parse tree representation *(1 mark)*.
2. **Semantic Relatedness** — regression task to predict the degree of semantic similarity between two sentences (e.g., SICK dataset) *(1 mark)*.
3. **Constituency Parsing** — jointly learning parse tree structures and node representations *(1 mark)*.

*(Other valid answers: Named Entity Recognition on hierarchical structures, machine translation with syntactic trees, code AST embedding for program classification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (no mixing). Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the working of a **Recursive Neural Network**. Describe in detail the **forward pass** equations used at each internal node of the tree. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Backpropagation Through Structure (BPTS)** update rule for a binary tree. Clearly state the gradient expressions with respect to the shared weight matrix $W$ and the local error term $\delta_j$ at each internal node. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]**

**Architecture (3 marks):** Draw or describe the RvNN tree for a sample sentence (e.g., *"The cat sat"*) showing leaf nodes (word embeddings), internal nodes (composed phrase vectors), and the root node (sentence representation). The architecture consists of:

- **Embedding layer** mapping words $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to hidden vectors $h_i \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$.
- **Recursive composition units** at every internal node, sharing the same $(W, b)$.
- **Softmax output layer** at any/all nodes for prediction.

**Forward Pass Equations (4 marks):**

For an internal node $j$ with left child $L$ and right child $R$:

$$s_j = W \begin{bmatrix} h_L \\ h_R \end{bmatrix} + b \quad \text{(linear pre-activation, 1 mark)}$$

$$h_j = \tanh(s_j) \quad \text{(nonlinear activation, 1 mark)}$$

For prediction at node $j$:

$$\hat{y}_j = \mathrm{softmax}(W_s h_j + b_s) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Initialization:** For leaf nodes, $h_i = W_e x_i$ where $W_e$ is the embedding matrix *(1 mark)*.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Loss definition (1 mark):**
$$L = -\sum_{j \in \text{labeled}} y_j^T \log \hat{y}_j$$

**Step 2 — Local error at output node (1 mark):**
$$\delta_s = \hat{y}_j - y_j$$

**Step 3 — Gradient w.r.t. hidden state (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_j} = W_s^T \delta_s$$

**Step 4 — BPTS recursion (2 marks):** For an internal node $j$ with parent $p$:

$$\delta_j = \left( W[:, 1{:}D]^T \delta_p \odot (1 - h_j^2) \right) + \left( W[:, D{+}1{:}2D]^T \delta_p \odot (1 - h_j^2) \right)$$

Equivalently, summing over the parent-child paths:

$$\delta_j = \sum_{p : j \in \text{children}(p)} \left( W_{[:, \text{slot}(j,p)]}^T \delta_p \right) \odot g'(s_j)$$

**Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $W$ (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{j \in \text{internal}} \delta_j \cdot \begin{bmatrix} h_{j_L} \\ h_{j_R} \end{bmatrix}^T$$

**Step 6 — Parameter update via SGD (1 mark):**

$$W \leftarrow W - \eta \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial W} + \lambda W \right)$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate and $\lambda$ is the $L_2$ regularization coefficient.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Tree-LSTM** architecture as an extension of the standard Recursive Neural Network. List the **forget gates** for left and right children, and write the forward-pass update equations for the cell state $c_j$ and hidden state $h_j$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **three major challenges** in training Recursive Neural Networks and propose a **mitigation strategy** for each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]**

**Standard RvNN limitation (1 mark):** In a vanilla RvNN, the compositional function simply averages/blends child representations, which is too restrictive. Tree-LSTM (Tai et al., 2015) adds gating mechanisms inspired by LSTM to selectively retain or forget information from children.

**Tree-LSTM Cell Architecture (2 marks):** For a node $j$ with children $k \in \{L, R\}$:

- **Input gate:** $i_j = \sigma\left(W^{(i)} h_L + U^{(i)} h_R + b^{(i)}\right)$
- **Forget gates (one per child):**
$$f_{jL} = \sigma\left(W^{(f)} h_L + U^{(f)} h_R + b^{(f)}\right)$$
$$f_{jR} = \sigma\left(W^{(f)} h_L + U^{(f)} h_R + b^{(f)}\right)$$

- **Output gate:** $o_j = \sigma\left(W^{(o)} h_L + U^{(o)} h_R + b^{(o)}\right)$

- **Candidate cell state:**
$$\tilde{c}_j = \tanh\left(W^{(u)} h_L + U^{(u)} h_R + b^{(u)}\right)$$

**Cell and hidden state update (2 marks):**

$$c_j = i_j \odot \tilde{c}_j + f_{jL} \odot c_{jL} + f_{jR} \odot c_{jR}$$

$$h_j = o_j \odot \tanh(c_j)$$

**Key advantage (2 marks):** Tree-LSTM has **D = 4D** parameters per cell (4 gates × 2 children × D + 4 biases), giving it the capacity to learn *which* child information to retain. This significantly outperforms vanilla RvNN on tasks like sentiment classification and semantic relatedness.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]**

**Challenge 1: Vanishing / Exploding Gradients (2 marks)**
- **Problem:** As tree depth increases, BPTS gradients shrink exponentially (or blow up), making deep trees un-trainable.
- **Mitigation:** Use **Tree-LSTM gating mechanisms** to control gradient flow; apply **gradient clipping** with threshold $\tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 5$); or use **residual connections** across tree levels.

**Challenge 2: Requirement of Pre-defined Tree Structure (2 marks)**
- **Problem:** Standard RvNNs require a pre-computed parse tree, which is unavailable for many languages/domains and can be erroneous.
- **Mitigation:** Use **SPINN** (Stack-augmented Parser-Interpreter Neural Network) that combines parsing and interpretation; or use **Gumbel-Softmax** to learn the tree structure end-to-end.

**Challenge 3: Computational Cost for Large Trees (2 marks)**
- **Problem:** For deep or wide trees, BPTS requires storing all node activations, leading to $O(N \cdot D)$ memory where $N$ is the number of nodes.
- **Mitigation:** Use **truncated BPTS** with depth limit $K$; use **mini-batch tree-structured training**; or compress subtrees into single embeddings using **recursive autoencoders**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Recursive Neural Networks remain powerful for structured data, and modern variants like Tree-LSTM address many classical limitations, but careful architectural and optimization choices are essential.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in RvNN Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RvNN with RNN** — Examiners report that nearly 30% of students interchange the two. Always explicitly state: *"RvNN operates on **tree** structures, RNN operates on **sequence** structures."* (Loss: up to 2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the activation function** — In forward pass derivations, students often write $h_j = W[h_L; h_R] + b$ without the $\tanh$ or ReLU. This costs **1 full mark** in derivations.
>
> 3. **Missing the BPTS gradient summation** — When a node has multiple children paths to the root, you **must sum** the contributions: $\delta_j = \sum_{p} \ldots$. Writing only one term loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing $\delta$ dimensions** — The local error $\delta_j$ has dimension $D \times 1$, same as $h_j$. Mixing up $D$ with $2D$ or $C$ is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Not specifying the gating equations for Tree-LSTM** — When asked about Tree-LSTM, the **per-child forget gates** are mandatory. Writing a single forget gate (as in vanilla LSTM) is incorrect and costs **1.5 marks**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the regularization term** — In the final SGD update, the $\lambda W$ term is often omitted. Examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ RvNN = **tree-structured** input; RNN = **sequence** input. This is the **#1 exam fact**.
- ✅ The compositional function at every internal node is: $h_j = \tanh(W[h_L; h_R] + b)$ with **shared weights** $W$ across all nodes.
- ✅ **Tree-LSTM** has **two forget gates** (one per child), unlike vanilla LSTM which has one forget gate.
- ✅ **BPTS** is the training algorithm; gradients flow from the root down to leaves, summed across all children of each node.
- ✅ The cross-entropy loss for node-level predictions: $L = -\sum_j y_j^T \log \hat{y}_j$.
- ✅ Weight update rule: $W \leftarrow W - \eta (\partial L / \partial W + \lambda W)$ (SGD with $L_2$ regularization).
- ✅ Local error recursion: $\delta_j = W^T_{[:, \text{child slot}]} \delta_{parent} \odot (1 - h_j^2)$ for $\tanh$ activation.
- ✅ Applications: sentiment classification, semantic relatedness, scene parsing, code AST analysis.
- ✅ Challenges: vanishing gradients, requirement of pre-parsed trees, high memory cost for deep trees.
- ✅ Mitigations: Tree-LSTM gating, gradient clipping, SPINN, truncated BPTS, recursive autoencoders.
- ✅ Softmax output: $\hat{y}_j = \mathrm{softmax}(W_s h_j + b_s)$ for $C$ classes.
- ✅ The leaf embedding is $h_i = W_e x_i$, where $W_e$ is the **embedding matrix** (often pre-trained via word2vec/GloVe).

> **Final Tip:** When you see a tree diagram in an RvNN question, immediately identify (1) the leaf embeddings, (2) the internal node compositions, (3) the prediction target, and (4) the loss function. This 4-step framing will cover ~90% of the marks in any RvNN problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern RNNs - LSTM and GRU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern RNNs - LSTM and GRU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Modern RNNs?

**Modern Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** refer to advanced gated recurrent architectures—primarily **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** and **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)**—designed to overcome the limitations of vanilla RNNs, namely the **vanishing gradient problem** and the inability to capture **long-term temporal dependencies** in sequential data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Alignment)**
> A **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** network is a gated recurrent architecture that uses a **memory cell** and three multiplicative gates (forget, input, output) to selectively retain or discard information across long sequences. A **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a simplified variant that combines the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** and merges the cell state and hidden state, providing comparable performance with fewer parameters.

## 1.2 The Problem They Solve: Vanishing Gradients

In a vanilla RNN, gradients are back-propagated through time (BPTT). For long sequences, the gradient is repeatedly multiplied by the recurrent weight matrix, causing it to **shrink exponentially** (vanish) or **grow uncontrollably** (explode). This means vanilla RNNs cannot remember information from many time steps earlier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**: The vanishing gradient problem prevents vanilla RNNs from learning long-term dependencies. LSTM and GRU solve this by introducing an **additive cell state update** (gradient flows additively, not multiplicatively) and **gating mechanisms** that regulate information flow.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A "Memory Notebook" 

Imagine you are a student attending a semester-long lecture series:

- **Vanilla RNN**: You have a very small notepad. Every new sentence makes you erase something old to make room. By the end of the semester, you only remember what was said in the last few minutes.
- **LSTM**: You have a large, well-organized notebook with three colored pens:
  - 🖊️ **Red pen (Forget Gate)**: Decides which old notes to erase.
  - 🖊️ **Green pen (Input Gate)**: Decides which new information to write.
  - 🖊️ **Blue pen (Output Gate)**: Decides which notes to read aloud when asked a question.
  - This lets you keep important information from week 1 still accessible in week 12.
- **GRU**: A simpler notebook with only two pens—one to decide what to keep vs. forget, and one to decide what to write. Slightly less expressive, but faster and uses fewer pages.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for Exam Importance)

- **Gates**: Sigmoid neural network layers (output in [0,1]) that act as **information filters**.
- **Cell State ($C_t$)**: The long-term memory highway running through the network.
- **Hidden State ($h_t$)**: The short-term working memory exposed to the next time step.
- **Sigmoid ($\sigma$)**: Activation function squashing values to **[0, 1]** range.
- **Tanh**: Activation function squashing values to **[-1, 1]** range.
- **BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time)**: Training algorithm for sequential models.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for LSTM Gates**: **"FIDO"** — **F**orget, **I**nput, **D**ata (candidate), **O**utput.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LSTM Architecture — Operational Logic

LSTM introduces a **memory cell** $C_t$ that is updated through three carefully designed gates. The cell state acts as a conveyor belt where information can flow unchanged (solving vanishing gradients), modified only by gated linear interactions.

### 2.1.1 The Three Gates — Why and How

**1. Forget Gate ($f_t$) — The "What to discard" gate**
- **Why**: The network needs a mechanism to remove irrelevant past information.
- **How**: Looks at previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and current input $x_t$, outputs a number in [0,1] for each cell state dimension (0 = completely forget, 1 = completely keep).

**2. Input Gate ($i_t$) + Candidate ($\tilde{C}_t$) — The "What to store" pair**
- **Why**: The network must decide which new information is worth remembering.
- **How**: The input gate decides *which values to update*, while the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ (a tanh layer) creates *new candidate values* that could be added to the state.

**3. Output Gate ($o_t$) — The "What to expose" gate**
- **Why**: The cell state contains a lot of information; we need to filter what becomes the next hidden state.
- **How**: Sigmoid layer decides which parts of $\tanh(C_t)$ to output as $h_t$.

## 2.2 GRU Architecture — Operational Logic

GRU is a streamlined LSTM variant with **two gates** only:

**1. Update Gate ($z_t$)**: Combines LSTM's forget and input gates. Decides how much past information to pass along.
**2. Reset Gate ($r_t$)**: Decides how much past information to forget when computing the new candidate.

GRU **merges** the cell state and hidden state into a single $h_t$, reducing parameters and improving training speed.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Natural Language Processing**: Machine translation (Google Translate), sentiment analysis, speech recognition.
- **Time-Series Forecasting**: Stock market prediction, weather modeling, IoT sensor analytics.
- **Healthcare**: ECG signal classification, patient monitoring, DNA sequence analysis.
- **Robotics & Control**: Trajectory prediction for autonomous vehicles, human activity recognition.
- **Music & Audio**: Music generation, audio denoising, speech synthesis (used in Tacotron 2, WaveNet precursors).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Component** | **Equation** | **Range of Output** | **Purpose** |
|---------------|--------------|---------------------|-------------|
| Forget Gate (LSTM) | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | [0, 1] | Decide what to discard from cell state |
| Input Gate (LSTM) | $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | [0, 1] | Decide what new info to store |
| Candidate Cell (LSTM) | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | [-1, 1] | Create new candidate values |
| Cell State Update (LSTM) | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | [-1, 1] (typically) | Update long-term memory |
| Output Gate (LSTM) | $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | [0, 1] | Decide what to output from cell state |
| Hidden State (LSTM) | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | [-1, 1] | Expose filtered memory |
| Update Gate (GRU) | $z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | [0, 1] | Combine forget + input functions |
| Reset Gate (GRU) | $r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | [0, 1] | Decide how much past to forget |
| Candidate Hidden (GRU) | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$ | [-1, 1] | Propose new hidden state |
| Hidden State (GRU) | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | [-1, 1] | Linear interpolation of old + new |

**Notation Key**:
- $\sigma$ = sigmoid function = $\frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$
- $\tanh$ = hyperbolic tangent
- $\odot$ = element-wise (Hadamard) product
- $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ = concatenation of vectors
- $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ = learnable weight matrices
- $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ = learnable bias vectors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exam**: LSTM has **2 hidden states** ($C_t$ and $h_t$) and **3 gates**, while GRU has **1 hidden state** ($h_t$) and **2 gates**. The element-wise product ($\odot$) is what makes the gates act as "filters."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LSTM Forward Pass

The LSTM forward pass is implemented as a sequence of six computational steps per time step. We derive each step rigorously below.

### Step 1: Concatenation of Previous Hidden State and Current Input

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= [h_{t-1}, x_t] \\
\text{where } & h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}, \quad x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x} \\
\Rightarrow & z_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h + n_x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: We concatenate the previous hidden state vector $h_{t-1}$ (of dimension $n_h$) with the current input vector $x_t$ (of dimension $n_x$) into a single column vector $z_t$ of dimension $n_h + n_x$. This is the input to all four LSTM layers at time $t$.

### Step 2: Compute the Forget Gate

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(W_f \cdot z_t + b_f) \\
&= \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \\
\text{Dimension: } & W_f \in \mathbb{R}^{n_C \times (n_h + n_x)}, \quad b_f \in \mathbb{R}^{n_C} \\
\Rightarrow & f_t \in [0, 1]^{n_C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: The forget gate weights $W_f$ and bias $b_f$ are learned during training. The sigmoid ensures each output is between 0 (completely forget) and 1 (completely retain) for each dimension of the cell state.

### Step 3: Compute the Input Gate and Candidate Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_t &= \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: The input gate $i_t$ decides *which values will be updated*, while the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ creates *new candidate values* (in the range [-1, 1] due to tanh) that could be added to the state.

### Step 4: Update the Cell State (The Core Innovation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
\text{where } & f_t \odot C_{t-1} \text{ forgets irrelevant past} \\
& i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \text{ adds new relevant information}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: This is the **key equation** that solves the vanishing gradient problem. Note the **additive** structure (not multiplicative). The forget gate $f_t$ element-wise multiplies with the old cell state $C_{t-1}$ (selective retention), and the input gate $i_t$ element-wise multiplies with the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ (selective addition). The sum gives the new cell state.

### Step 5: Compute the Output Gate

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)
$$

**Explanation**: The output gate will determine what parts of the cell state to expose as the next hidden state.

### Step 6: Compute the New Hidden State

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) \\
\text{Since } & \tanh(C_t) \in [-1, 1]^{n_C} \text{ and } o_t \in [0, 1]^{n_C} \\
\Rightarrow & h_t \in [-1, 1]^{n_C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: The cell state is first squashed through tanh to bound it in [-1, 1], then the output gate filters it element-wise to produce the hidden state $h_t$, which is passed to the next time step.

## 3.2 Derivation of the GRU Forward Pass

### Step 1: Compute Update and Reset Gates

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z) \\
r_t &= \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: Both gates use the same concatenated input but have **separate weights and biases**. The reset gate $r_t$ will determine how much of the past to "reset" before computing the new candidate.

### Step 2: Compute the Candidate Hidden State (with Reset)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{h}_t &= \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b) \\
\text{Note: } & r_t \odot h_{t-1} \text{ selectively forgets past before combining with } x_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: Unlike LSTM, the candidate hidden state computation explicitly uses the reset gate to mask the previous hidden state *before* concatenating with the current input. This is how GRU's reset gate influences the new memory.

### Step 3: Linear Interpolation for New Hidden State

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t \\
\text{When } z_t \to 0: & \quad h_t \to h_{t-1} \text{ (copy old state)} \\
\text{When } z_t \to 1: & \quad h_t \to \tilde{h}_t \text{ (use new candidate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation**: This is an **interpolation** between the old hidden state and the new candidate. The update gate $z_t$ acts as a convex combination coefficient. When $z_t = 0$, the past is fully retained; when $z_t = 1$, the new candidate completely replaces the past.

## 3.3 Gradient Flow Analysis — Why LSTM/GRU Beat Vanilla RNN

For a vanilla RNN, the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to hidden state $h_k$ (for $k$ steps back) involves a product of Jacobian matrices:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_k} &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} \\
&= \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_h^T \cdot \text{diag}(\sigma'(\cdot))
\end{aligned}
$$

In LSTM, the gradient flows through the cell state $C_t$ via:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} &= f_t + \text{(other terms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Insight**: Since the forget gate $f_t \in [0, 1]$ can be close to 1, the gradient flows back with little decay across many time steps. This is the **multiplicative gate but with learnable, non-vanishing** behavior — fundamentally different from vanilla RNN's fixed weight matrix.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight**: When asked "Why do LSTMs solve vanishing gradients?", the answer is the **additive cell state update** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ combined with the learnable forget gate $f_t$ that can be set close to 1 to preserve gradient magnitude.

## 3.4 LSTM vs GRU — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Feature** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|-------------|----------|---------|
| Number of Gates | **3** (forget, input, output) | **2** (update, reset) |
| Hidden States | **2** ($C_t$ and $h_t$) | **1** ($h_t$) |
| Total Parameters | $4 \times (n_h + n_x) \times n_h + 4 \times n_h$ | $3 \times (n_h + n_x) \times n_h + 3 \times n_h$ |
| Memory Mechanism | Separate cell state + hidden state | Unified hidden state |
| Training Speed | Slower | **~20-30% faster** |
| Performance on Long Sequences | Generally better | Comparable for medium sequences |
| Risk of Overfitting | Higher (more parameters) | Lower (fewer parameters) |
| Year Proposed | **1997** (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber) | **2014** (Cho et al.) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LSTM Cell Architecture — Block-Level Flow

The diagram below shows the internal structure of an LSTM cell, including the three gates and the cell state highway.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Xt] --> C[Concatenate]
    B[Ht-1] --> C
    C --> D[Forget Gate f_t]
    C --> E[Input Gate i_t]
    C --> F[Candidate tilde C_t]
    C --> G[Output Gate o_t]
    D --> H[Cell State Ct]
    I[Ct-1] --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> J[tanh of Ct]
    G --> K[Hadamard Product]
    J --> K
    K --> L[New Hidden Ht]
    L --> M[Pass to next timestep]
    H --> N[Pass to next timestep as Ct-1]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- The cell state $C_t$ flows along the top, modified only by gated additions.
- All three gates ($f_t$, $i_t$, $o_t$) receive the concatenated input $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$.
- The candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ is the new information proposed to be added.

## 4.2 GRU Cell Architecture — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Xt] --> C[Concatenate]
    B[Ht-1] --> C
    C --> D[Update Gate z_t]
    C --> E[Reset Gate r_t]
    E --> F[Masked Ht-1]
    B --> F
    F --> G[Concatenate with Xt]
    A --> G
    G --> H[Candidate Hidden tilde Ht]
```
