```mermaid
    D --> I[Linear Interpolation]
    B --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[New Hidden Ht]
    J --> K[Pass to next timestep]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- The reset gate $r_t$ masks the previous hidden state before computing the candidate.
- The update gate $z_t$ blends the old hidden state with the new candidate.

## 4.3 Unrolled LSTM Through Time

This shows how LSTM cells connect across time steps, illustrating the cell state highway.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Time t-1 LSTM Cell] --> T2[Time t LSTM Cell]
    T2 --> T3[Time t+1 LSTM Cell]
    T3 --> T4[Time t+2 LSTM Cell]
    T1 -. Cell State C t-1 .-> T2
    T2 -. Cell State C t .-> T3
    T3 -. Cell State C t+1 .-> T4
    X1[X t-1] --> T1
    X2[X t] --> T2
    X3[X t+1] --> T3
    X4[X t+2] --> T4
```

**Diagram Interpretation**: Notice the **horizontal cell state line** (dashed arrows) running through the unrolled cells. This is the gradient highway that prevents vanishing gradients.

## 4.4 Information Filtering Mechanism

This shows how the forget gate $f_t$ acts as an information filter on the cell state.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Cell State Ct-1] --> B[Element-wise Multiply]
    D[Forget Gate ft values] --> B
    B --> E[Filtered Cell State]
    F[New Candidate tilde Ct] --> G[Element-wise Multiply]
    H[Input Gate it values] --> G
    G --> I[Filtered New Info]
    E --> J[Addition]
    I --> J
    J --> K[New Cell State Ct]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: LSTM Forget Gate `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the forget gate in an LSTM network. What is its mathematical form, and what does an output value of 0 versus 1 signify?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
The forget gate in an LSTM is a sigmoid neural network layer that decides what information to discard from the cell state.

**Mathematical Form (2 marks)**:
$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$
where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function, $W_f$ is the weight matrix, $b_f$ is the bias vector, $h_{t-1}$ is the previous hidden state, and $x_t$ is the current input.

**Significance (1 mark)**:
- Output of **0** = completely forget/discard that dimension of the cell state.
- Output of **1** = completely retain/keep that dimension of the cell state.

---

### Question 2: GRU vs LSTM `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three architectural differences between LSTM and GRU networks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per point)**:

1. **Number of Gates**: LSTM has **3 gates** (forget, input, output), whereas GRU has **2 gates** (update, reset).

2. **Hidden States**: LSTM maintains **two separate states** (cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$), while GRU has a **single unified hidden state** $h_t$.

3. **Parameter Count**: LSTM has **4 weight matrices** (for $f$, $i$, $C$, $o$), while GRU has **3 weight matrices** (for $z$, $r$, and candidate), making GRU more parameter-efficient and faster to train.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain the architecture of an LSTM network with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each gate with appropriate equations. (7 marks)**

**(b) How does LSTM overcome the vanishing gradient problem of vanilla RNNs? Derive the cell state update equation and explain the gradient flow. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture Overview (2 marks)**: An LSTM network consists of a chain of repeating modules (cells). Each cell has a **cell state** $C_t$ running through the top (the "conveyor belt") and three gates that regulate information flow: **forget gate** ($f_t$), **input gate** ($i_t$), and **output gate** ($o_t$).

**Forget Gate (1.5 marks)**:
$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$
Decides what information to throw away from the cell state.

**Input Gate (1.5 marks)**:
$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i), \quad \tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)
$$
Decides what new information to store in the cell state.

**Cell State Update (1 mark)**:
$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

**Output Gate (1 mark)**:
$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o), \quad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$
Decides what to output as the hidden state.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Vanishing Gradient in Vanilla RNN (2 marks)**: In vanilla RNNs, BPTT requires computing $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_h^T \cdot \text{diag}(\sigma'(\cdot))$. For long sequences, this product causes the gradient to shrink exponentially to zero, preventing learning of long-term dependencies.

**Cell State Update Derivation (2 marks)**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
C_{t-1} &= f_{t-1} \odot C_{t-2} + i_{t-1} \odot \tilde{C}_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting recursively:
$$
C_t = f_t \odot f_{t-1} \odot \cdots \odot f_{k+1} \odot C_k + \sum (\text{input contributions})
$$

**Gradient Flow Explanation (2 marks)**: The gradient of loss $L$ with respect to $C_k$ is:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} f_i + \text{(additive terms)}
$$
The forget gates $f_i$ are **learnable** and can be trained to values close to 1, allowing gradients to flow back over many time steps without vanishing. The **additive** structure (as opposed to purely multiplicative) also stabilizes gradient magnitude.

**Conclusion (1 mark)**: LSTM's combination of gated cell state and additive updates enables effective learning of long-term dependencies, making it the go-to choice for sequential tasks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Describe the GRU architecture with its mathematical equations. Explain the functions of the update gate and reset gate. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare LSTM and GRU in terms of architecture, parameters, and performance. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**GRU Architecture (2 marks)**: GRU is a simplified LSTM variant introduced by Cho et al. in 2014. It has two gates: **update gate** ($z_t$) and **reset gate** ($r_t$), and a single hidden state $h_t$ that combines the roles of LSTM's cell state and hidden state.

**Update Gate (1.5 marks)**:
$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)
$$
**Function**: Decides how much of the past information to keep vs. how much new information to incorporate. When $z_t \to 0$, the past is fully preserved; when $z_t \to 1$, the new candidate completely replaces the past.

**Reset Gate (1.5 marks)**:
$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)
$$
**Function**: Decides how much of the past to forget when computing the new candidate. It masks the previous hidden state before the candidate computation.

**Candidate and Hidden State (2 marks)**:
$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)
$$
$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

The final hidden state is a **convex combination** (linear interpolation) of the old hidden state and the new candidate, controlled by the update gate.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Architectural Comparison (2 marks)**:

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|--------|------|-----|
| Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| States | Cell state + Hidden state | Single hidden state |
| Output exposure | Output gate controls $h_t$ | $h_t$ is the only state exposed |

**Parameter Comparison (2 marks)**:
- LSTM parameters: $4 \times (n_h \cdot (n_h + n_x) + n_h)$
- GRU parameters: $3 \times (n_h \cdot (n_h + n_x) + n_h)$
- GRU has **~25% fewer parameters**, leading to **faster training** and **less memory usage**.

**Performance Comparison (2 marks)**:
- On **very long sequences** (e.g., document-level NLP), LSTM often performs slightly better due to explicit memory control.
- On **smaller datasets** or **shorter sequences**, GRU performs comparably and trains faster.
- Empirically, neither dominates universally; performance is **task-dependent**.

**When to Prefer Each (1 mark)**:
- **Prefer LSTM** when: dataset is large, sequences are very long, and you need explicit memory control (e.g., machine translation, speech recognition).
- **Prefer GRU** when: dataset is smaller, training time/memory is constrained, or you need a simpler model (e.g., mobile/edge deployment, real-time applications).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing gate symbols**: Students often swap $f_t$ (forget) and $i_t$ (input). Remember: $f$ = **f**orget, $i$ = **i**nput.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting element-wise notation**: Writing $f_t \cdot C_{t-1}$ instead of $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$. The $\odot$ (Hadamard product) is **mandatory** — gates are vectors applied element-wise, not scalars.
> 
> 3. **Missing concatenation step**: Failing to write $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ as the concatenated input to the gate layers. This shows incomplete understanding.
> 
> 4. **Saying "LSTM has 2 gates"**: Always specify **3 gates** for LSTM and **2 gates** for GRU. This is a 1-mark question in Part A.
> 
> 5. **Wrong activation function**: Using sigmoid for the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ (should be **tanh**) or tanh for the gate outputs (should be **sigmoid**).
> 
> 6. **Not explaining vanishing gradient fix**: For 7-mark questions on LSTM, merely listing equations is insufficient — you must explain **why** the additive cell state update solves vanishing gradients.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ LSTM = **3 gates** (forget $f_t$, input $i_t$, output $o_t$) + **2 states** (cell $C_t$, hidden $h_t$).
- ✅ GRU = **2 gates** (update $z_t$, reset $r_t$) + **1 state** (hidden $h_t$).
- ✅ **Gates use sigmoid** (output [0,1]); **candidates use tanh** (output [-1,1]).
- ✅ LSTM solves vanishing gradients via the **additive cell state update**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- ✅ GRU is **faster to train** and has **fewer parameters** than LSTM (~25% reduction).
- ✅ The **forget gate** is the most critical LSTM component — it was the key innovation of the 1997 paper.
- ✅ GRUs perform **linear interpolation** between old and new states using the update gate.
- ✅ LSTM was proposed by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)**; GRU by **Cho et al. (2014)**.
- ✅ For KTU exams, always include: (1) architecture diagram, (2) all gate equations, (3) explanation of vanishing gradient fix, (4) comparison with vanilla RNN.
- ✅ Common applications: machine translation, speech recognition, time-series forecasting, sentiment analysis.
- ✅ Modern transformers have largely replaced LSTM/GRU in NLP, but RNNs remain relevant for **streaming/online** and **resource-constrained** applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications: Computer Vision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications: Computer Vision # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Computer Vision (in the Deep Learning Era)? **Computer Vision (CV)** is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence that enables machines to *interpret, analyze, and extract meaningful information* from visual inputs — images, video frames, or 3D point clouds. With the advent of **Deep Learning**, CV has moved from hand-crafted feature engineering (SIFT, HOG, SURF) to *end-to-end representation learning* through **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** and transformer-based vision backbones. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEIT633 – Module 4):** > *"Computer Vision deals with tasks such as Image Classification, Object Detection, Semantic & Instance Segmentation, Image Generation, Face Recognition, Pose Estimation, and Image Captioning, all driven by Deep Neural Networks."* > [!IMPORTANT] > The unifying theme of all modern CV pipelines is the **Convolutional Backbone (Feature Extractor)** followed by a **Task-Specific Head (Classifier, Regressor, or Decoder)**. --- ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Eye-Brain" Pipeline Imagine a child learning to recognize a dog: | Human Process | Equivalent DL Component | |---|---| | Eyes capture light → retina | **Image / Video Input Tensor** (e.g., 224×224×3) | | Visual cortex extracts edges, shapes | **Convolutional Feature Maps** (Conv + Pool layers) | | Brain categorizes ("Labrador") | **Classifier Head** (Fully Connected + Softmax) | | Brain localizes ("in the garden") | **Bounding Box Regressor** (YOLO / R-CNN head) | | Brain explains the scene | **Captioning Decoder** (CNN + LSTM / Transformer) | > [!TIP] > A *single CNN* acts as the retina-cortex system, while *task-specific heads* mimic the brain's specialized regions for recognition, localization, and description. --- ## 1.3 Major CV Application Categories (KTU High-Yield Classification) 1. **Image Classification** — assign one label to the whole image. 2. **Object Detection** — classify *and* localize multiple objects via bounding boxes. 3. **Semantic Segmentation** — assign a class label to *every pixel*. 4. **Instance Segmentation** — pixel-level labels *distinguishing individual instances*. 5. **Image Generation & Style Transfer** — synthesize new images (GANs, VAEs). 6. **Face Recognition & Verification** — identity matching (Siamese Networks, FaceNet). 7. **Pose Estimation** — detect human skeleton keypoints. 8. **Image Captioning / VQA** — generate natural-language descriptions from images. 9. **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** — text-in-the-wild detection + recognition. 10. **3D / Depth Estimation** — infer 3D geometry from 2D images. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Task 1 — Image Classification (CNN Backbone Pipeline) A CNN classifier follows the **Conv → Pool → Conv → Pool → FC → Softmax** template. ### Operational Steps 1. **Input Layer** receives an image $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$. 2. **Convolutional Layers** apply learnable filters $\mathbf{W}_k$ producing feature maps: $$\mathbf{F}_k = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_k * \mathbf{X} + b_k)$$ where $*$ denotes convolution and $\sigma$ is a non-linear activation (ReLU). 3. **Pooling Layers** downsample spatial resolution, providing translation invariance. 4. **Fully Connected Layers** flatten features and map to class logits. 5. **Softmax** converts logits $\mathbf{z}$ to class probabilities: $$P(y_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$ 6. **Cross-Entropy Loss** drives training: $$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$$ ### Landmark Architectures (must-know for KTU) - **AlexNet (2012)** — 8 layers, ReLU, Dropout, GPU training. - **VGG-16/19 (2014)** — uniform 3×3 convs, deep stack. - **GoogLeNet/Inception (2014)** — parallel multi-scale filters via Inception modules. - **ResNet (2015)** — residual connections to enable 50/101/152-layer training. - **DenseNet (2017)** — dense feature reuse. - **EfficientNet (2019)** — compound scaling of depth/width/resolution. - **Vision Transformer / ViT (2020)** — patch-token self-attention for images. --- ## 2.2 Task 2 — Object Detection Two major paradigms dominate the field: ### A. Two-Stage Detectors (Region Proposal → Classification) - **R-CNN** — selective search for ~2000 region proposals, CNN features per region. - **Fast R-CNN** — share CNN features across regions via RoI Pooling. - **Faster R-CNN** — replace selective search with a **Region Proposal Network (RPN)**. - **Mask R-CNN** — adds a parallel mask branch → enables instance segmentation. ### B. One-Stage Detectors (Single Shot) - **YOLO (v1–v8)** — divides image into $S \times S$ grid, predicts boxes + class per cell. - **SSD** — multi-scale feature maps for handling varying object sizes. - **RetinaNet** — introduces **Focal Loss** to fight class imbalance. ### Key Concepts (must memorize) #### (i) Anchor Boxes Pre-defined reference boxes of multiple scales and aspect ratios tiled across feature maps. The model regresses offsets $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta w, \Delta h)$ and confidence scores relative to these anchors. #### (ii) Intersection over Union (IoU) $$IoU = \frac{\text{Area of Overlap}}{\text{Area of Union}} = \frac{|B_{pred} \cap B_{gt}|}{|B_{pred} \cup B_{gt}|}$$ - $IoU \geq 0.5$ → **True Positive** (standard PASCAL VOC threshold). - $IoU \geq 0.75$ → stricter (COCO-style). #### (iii) Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) Algorithm to keep only the best bounding box among highly overlapping candidates: 1. Discard boxes with confidence < threshold. 2. Pick the box with highest confidence. 3. Suppress all boxes with $IoU > 0.5$ with the chosen one. 4. Repeat until no boxes remain. #### (iv) Mean Average Precision (mAP) $$mAP = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$$ where Average Precision (AP) is the **area under the Precision-Recall curve** for class $c$. For multi-threshold evaluation (COCO): $mAP@[.5:.95]$ = mean AP averaged over IoU thresholds $0.5, 0.55, \ldots, 0.95$. --- ## 2.3 Task 3 — Semantic Segmentation A **Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)** replaces FC layers with $1 \times 1$ convolutions and uses **transposed convolutions** (or bilinear upsampling) to recover spatial resolution. ### Variants | Architecture | Key Idea | |---|---| | **FCN-32s/16s/8s** | Skip connections from earlier pooling layers | | **SegNet** | Uses pooling indices for upsampling (memory-efficient) | | **U-Net** | Symmetric encoder–decoder with skip connections (medical imaging) | | **DeepLab v3+** | Atrous / Dilated convolutions + ASPP module | | **PSPNet** | Pyramid Pooling Module for multi-scale context | ### Loss Function **Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy** averaged over all pixels, optionally weighted to address class imbalance: $$\mathcal{L}_{seg} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} w_c \cdot y_{i,c}\log \hat{y}_{i,c}$$ --- ## 2.4 Task 4 — Instance Segmentation **Mask R-CNN** = Faster R-CNN + parallel mask-prediction branch. - Backbone: ResNet + FPN (Feature Pyramid Network). - RoI Pooling replaced by **RoIAlign** (eliminates quantization error → better masks). - Three heads per RoI: **Classification, Bounding-Box Regression, Binary Mask** (one mask per class, $28 \times 28$ resolution). Total Loss: $$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cls} + \mathcal{L}_{box} + \mathcal{L}_{mask}$$ --- ## 2.5 Task 5 — Image Generation ### A. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) Two networks trained in opposition: - **Generator $G$**: noise $\mathbf{z} \sim p_z \rightarrow$ fake image $G(\mathbf{z})$. - **Discriminator $D$**: real vs. fake classifier. Minimax Objective: $$\min_{G}\max_{D} V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$ ### B. Variational Autoencoder (VAE) Encoder outputs distribution parameters $(\mu, \sigma^2)$; decoder reconstructs from sampled latent $\mathbf{z}$. Loss = Reconstruction + KL Divergence: $$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{z}\mid \mathbf{x})}[\log p(\mathbf{x}\mid \mathbf{z})] + D_{KL}\big(q(\mathbf{z}\mid \mathbf{x}) \Vert p(\mathbf{z})\big)$$ --- ## 2.6 Task 6 — Face Recognition Uses **Siamese Networks** or specialized models like **FaceNet / DeepFace**: - A CNN embeds a face into a **128-D Euclidean space**. - Trained with **Triplet Loss**: $$\mathcal{L}_{triplet} = \max\big(d(A,P) - d(A,N) + \alpha, \, 0\big)$$ where $A$=anchor, $P$=positive (same identity), $N$=negative, $\alpha$=margin. For verification: two faces are same if $\Vert f(A) - f(P) \Vert_2 < \tau$. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Equation | Use / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Softmax** | $\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class classification | | **Cross-Entropy** | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Classification loss | | **IoU** | $IoU = \frac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt}\vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt}\vert}$ | Detection metric | | **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Detection accuracy | | **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Detection coverage | | **AP / mAP** | $mAP = \frac{1}{C}\sum AP_c$ | Aggregated detection metric | | **Focal Loss** | $FL = -\alpha_t(1-p_t)^\gamma \log p_t$ | Class imbalance (RetinaNet) | | **YOLO Loss** | $\lambda_{coord}\sum (x-\hat{x})^2 + \lambda_{noobj}\sum (C-\hat{C})^2 + \sum CE$ | YOLO multi-part loss | | **Triplet Loss** | $\max(d(A,P)-d(A,N)+\alpha, 0)$ | Face embedding learning | | **GAN Objective** | $\min_G\max_D \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | Adversarial training | | **VAE Loss** | $\mathcal{L}_{rec} + D_{KL}(q\Vert p)$ | Generative model | | **Dice Coefficient** | $Dice = \frac{2\vert P \cap G\vert}{\vert P\vert + \vert G\vert}$ | Segmentation overlap | | **Pixel Accuracy** | $PA = \frac{\sum n_{ii}}{\sum n_{ij}}$ | Segmentation metric | | **mIoU** | $mIoU = \frac{1}{C}\sum \frac{TP}{TP+FP+FN}$ | Mean pixel overlap | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners *repeatedly* test **IoU, mAP, and YOLO/R-CNN loss formulations**. Memorize these verbatim. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — IoU Computation Between Two Boxes Suppose the **Ground Truth box** is $B_{gt} = (x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (10, 10, 50, 60)$ and the **Predicted box** is $B_{p} = (x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (20, 20, 60, 70)$ (top-left and bottom-right corners in pixels). **Step 1 — Compute intersection coordinates.** The intersection rectangle's top-left = $(\max(x_{1}^{p}, x_{1}^{gt}), \max(y_{1}^{p}, y_{1}^{gt}))$. $$\begin{aligned} x_{int,left} &= \max(20, 10) = 20 \\ y_{int,top} &= \max(20, 10) = 20 \\ x_{int,right} &= \min(60, 50) = 50 \\ y_{int,bottom} &= \min(70, 60) = 60 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute intersection area.** $$A_{int} = \max(0, x_{int,right} - x_{int,left}) \times \max(0, y_{int,bottom} - y_{int,top})$$ $$A_{int} = (50 - 20) \times (60 - 20) = 30 \times 40 = 1200 \text{ pixels}$$ **Step 3 — Compute individual areas.** $$A_{gt} = (50 - 10) \times (60 - 10) = 40 \times 50 = 2000$$ $$A_{p} = (60 - 20) \times (70 - 20) = 40 \times 50 = 2000$$ **Step 4 — Compute union area.** $$A_{union} = A_{gt} + A_{p} - A_{int} = 2000 + 2000 - 1200 = 2800$$ **Step 5 — Compute IoU.** $$IoU = \frac{A_{int}}{A_{union}} = \frac{1200}{2800} \approx 0.4286$$ > Since $IoU = 0.43 < 0.5$, this is a **False Positive** under standard PASCAL VOC threshold. To improve, regress the box offsets to push the prediction closer to ground truth. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Average Precision (AP) and mAP Consider a 3-class detection problem with per-class AP values: $AP_1 = 0.85$, $AP_2 = 0.72$, $AP_3 = 0.63$. **Step 1 — Recall the definition.** $$mAP = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$$ **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$mAP = \frac{1}{3}(0.85 + 0.72 + 0.63) = \frac{2.20}{3} \approx 0.7333$$ **Step 3 — Express as percentage.** $$mAP \approx 73.33\%$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — YOLO Loss Decomposition The YOLO v1 loss is a multi-part sum. For each grid cell: $$\mathcal{L}_{YOLO} = \lambda_{coord}\sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}\left[(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2\right]$$ $$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ $$+ \lambda_{noobj}\sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{noobj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ $$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{obj}\sum_{c \in classes} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$ **Interpretation of terms:** | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $\lambda_{coord} = 5$ | Up-weights coordinate loss to prioritize localization | | $\lambda_{noobj} = 0.5$ | Down-weights "no object" confidence (anti-dominance) | | $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ | Square-root reduces sensitivity to large boxes (small boxes penalized equally) | | $\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}$ | 1 if an object exists in cell $i$, anchor $j$ | | $C_i$ | Predicted confidence $\Pr(\text{object}) \times IoU$ | | $p_i(c)$ | Class probability distribution | > The square-root trick ensures that a 10-pixel error on a 100-pixel box and a 10-pixel error on a 20-pixel box contribute proportional gradients. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Pixel-Wise Cross-Entropy for Segmentation For a 4×4 binary segmentation map with 16 pixels, suppose: - 12 pixels are background ($y=0$). - 4 pixels are foreground ($y=1$). - Model outputs foreground probabilities $\hat{p}_i = [0.1, 0.2, 0.05, 0.9, 0.7, 0.8, 0.05, 0.1, 0.05, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.6, 0.7, 0.85, 0.9]$. **Step 1 — Build the per-pixel loss.** For each pixel $i$: $$\ell_i = -[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \hat{p}_i)]$$ **Step 2 — Compute the sum.** Because the model is reasonably confident, summing all 16 pixels yields $\sum \ell_i \approx 5.42$. The mean is: $$\mathcal{L}_{seg} = \frac{1}{16}\sum_{i=1}^{16} \ell_i \approx 0.339$$ **Step 3 — Use class weights to handle imbalance.** If we set $w_1 = 3$ for foreground (minority) and $w_0 = 1$ for background: $$\mathcal{L}_{weighted} = \frac{1}{16}\sum_{i=1}^{16} w_{y_i} \cdot \ell_i$$ This rebalances gradients so the model doesn't simply predict "all background." --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Triplet Loss Calculation (Face Recognition) Let three face embeddings (after L2-normalization) be: - Anchor $A = [0.6, 0.8]$ - Positive $P = [0.5, 0.7]$ (same person) - Negative $N = [-0.2, 0.4]$ (different person) - Margin $\alpha = 0.2$ **Step 1 — Euclidean distance to positive.** $$d(A, P) = \sqrt{(0.6-0.5)^2 + (0.8-0.7)^2} = \sqrt{0.02} \approx 0.1414$$ **Step 2 — Euclidean distance to negative.** $$d(A, N) = \sqrt{(0.6+0.2)^2 + (0.8-0.4)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.80} \approx 0.8944$$ **Step 3 — Compute triplet loss.** $$\mathcal{L}_{triplet} = \max(d(A,P) - d(A,N) + \alpha, \, 0)$$ $$\mathcal{L}_{triplet} = \max(0.1414 - 0.8944 + 0.2, \, 0) = \max(-0.5530, 0) = 0$$ Since the loss is zero, the model has *correctly* placed the positive closer than the negative with a sufficient margin. No gradient update is needed for this triplet. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Computer Vision Deep-Learning Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Image
HXWX3] --> B[Pre-processing
Resize Normalize Augment] B --> C[CNN Backbone
VGG ResNet EfficientNet] C --> D[Feature Maps
HXWXD] D --> E{Task Type} E -->|Classification| F[Global Avg Pool FC Softmax] E -->|Object Detection| G[RPN or Grid YOLO Head] E -->|Segmentation| H[Decoder U-Net FCN] E -->|Generation| I[Decoder VAE GAN] F --> J[Class Label] G --> K[Bounding Boxes Classes] H --> L[Per Pixel Mask] I --> M[Generated Image] ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Faster R-CNN Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Input Image] --> B[Shared CNN Backbone
ResNet50] B --> C[Feature Map] C --> D[Region Proposal Network RPN] D --> E[Region Proposals] C --> E E --> F[RoI Pooling Layer] F --> G[FC Layer] G --> H1[Classifier Head
Softmax] G --> H2[Bounding Box Regressor] H1 --> I1[Class Labels] H2 --> I2[Refined Boxes xywh] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — U-Net for Semantic Segmentation ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Input 256x256x3] --> B[Conv Block 1
64 channels] B --> C[MaxPool 128x128x64] C --> D[Conv Block 2
128 channels] D --> E[MaxPool 64x64x128] E --> F[Conv Block 3
256 channels] F --> G[MaxPool 32x32x256] G --> H[Bottleneck
512 channels] H --> I[UpConv 64x64x256] F --> I I --> J[Conv Block 4
256 channels] J --> K[UpConv 128x128x128] D --> K K --> L[Conv Block 5
128 channels] L --> M[UpConv 256x256x64] B --> M M --> N[Output Mask
256x256xC] ``` > Note the **symmetric skip connections** (gray arrows) that concatenate encoder features to decoder features, preserving spatial detail lost during downsampling. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — GAN Training Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Noise Vector z] --> G[Generator G] G --> F[Fake Image Gz] R[Real Image x] --> D F --> D[Discriminator D] D --> O1{Real or Fake} O1 -->|Real| L1[Log D x maximize] O1 -->|Fake| L2[Log 1 minus D Gz minimize] L2 --> G L1 --> D ``` --- ## 4.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Detection Architectures | Property | R-CNN | Fast R-CNN | Faster R-CNN | YOLO | SSD | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Stage** | Two | Two | Two | One | One | | **Region Proposal** | Selective Search | Selective Search | RPN | Grid Cells | Default Boxes | | **Speed (FPS)** | ~0.05 | ~0.5 | ~7 | 45–155 (v3–v8) | ~22–59 | | **mAP (VOC07)** | 66% | 70% | 78% | 78% (v3) | 74% | | **Strength** | First CNN detector | Shared features | End-to-end RPN | Real-time | Multi-scale | | **Weakness** | Very slow | Still slow proposal | Complex pipeline | Worse on small objects | Lower accuracy | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks: *"Compare Two-Stage vs One-Stage Detectors."* Memorize the speed-accuracy trade-off from this matrix. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between Object Detection and Semantic Segmentation. Mention one popular deep learning model for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Object Detection | Semantic Segmentation | |---|---|---| | Goal | Localize + classify objects with **bounding boxes** | Classify **every pixel** in the image | | Output | Rectangles + class labels + confidence | Per-pixel class map (same size as input) | | Model Example | YOLO, Faster R-CNN | FCN, U-Net, SegNet | | Granularity | Object-level (rectangle) | Pixel-level (mask) | | Distinguishes Instances? | ✓ Yes (different IDs) | ✗ No (all "cars" share one label) | *(1 mark for clear goal distinction, 1 mark for output difference, 1 mark for naming correct models.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)? Why is it necessary in object detection pipelines?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **NMS** is a post-processing algorithm that selects a single best bounding box from a set of highly overlapping candidates for the same object. **Why needed:** - Object detectors predict **many overlapping boxes** (e.g., YOLO predicts 98 boxes per image in v1). - Without NMS, the same object would be detected multiple times → redundant, noisy output. - NMS keeps the box with the **highest confidence** and removes others whose IoU with it exceeds a threshold (typically 0.5). **Algorithm steps (briefly):** 1. Discard boxes with confidence score < threshold. 2. Among remaining, select the box with **maximum confidence**. 3. Suppress all boxes with $IoU > 0.5$ with the selected box. 4. Repeat until no boxes remain. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark necessity, 1 mark working / algorithm.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Rule:** *Answer ONE full question from the choice.* Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **(a)** Explain the architecture of **Faster R-CNN** in detail. Describe the role of the **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** and how anchor boxes are used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **U-Net architecture** for semantic segmentation. Why are skip connections important? Mention the loss function used. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** ### Part (a) — Faster R-CNN Architecture (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015) is a two-stage object detector that replaces the slow *selective search* of Fast R-CNN with a learnable **Region Proposal Network (RPN)**, enabling near-real-time detection. **Architecture Stages (3 marks):** 1. **Shared Convolutional Backbone:** A pre-trained CNN (e.g., VGG-16, ResNet-50) processes the input image $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ to produce a shared **conv feature map** $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{H' \times W' \times D}$. 2. **Region Proposal Network (RPN):** - A small $3 \times 3$ sliding window moves over $\mathbf{F}$. - At each spatial location, $k$ **anchor boxes** of different scales and aspect ratios (e.g., 3 scales × 3 ratios = 9 anchors) are tiled. - Two parallel $1 \times 1$ conv heads predict: - **Objectness score** ($2k$ outputs) — probability of foreground vs background. - **Bounding box deltas** ($4k$ outputs) — $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta w, \Delta h)$ w.r.t. anchors. 3. **RoI Pooling Layer:** - Proposals of variable sizes are projected onto $\mathbf{F}$ and converted to fixed-size feature maps (e.g., 7×7) via max pooling. 4. **Detection Heads:** - **Classifier head** (FC + Softmax) → class probabilities over $C+1$ classes (including background). - **Box regressor head** → refined bounding box coordinates. **Role of Anchor Boxes (2 marks):** Anchors serve as **reference templates** at each spatial location. The RPN learns *offsets* to transform anchors into high-quality proposals. This parameterization makes box regression well-conditioned since anchors are close to actual objects. Anchor scales and ratios are chosen to cover the typical object-size distribution in the dataset (e.g., 128², 256², 512² with ratios 1:1, 1:2, 2:1 in the original paper). **Multi-task Loss (1 mark):** $$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cls}^{RPN} + \mathcal{L}_{reg}^{RPN} + \mathcal{L}_{cls}^{det} + \mathcal{L}_{reg}^{det}$$ Classification uses **cross-entropy**; box regression uses **smooth L1 loss**. --- ### Part (b) — U-Net Architecture (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is a symmetric **encoder-decoder** network designed for biomedical image segmentation. It outputs a pixel-wise class mask of the same spatial resolution as the input. **Architecture (4 marks):** - **Contracting Path (Encoder):** Repeated application of two $3 \times 3$ convolutions (unpadded) + ReLU, followed by $2 \times 2$ max pooling with stride 2 for downsampling. Channels double at each level: 64 → 128 → 256 → 512. - **Bottleneck:** Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with 1024 channels, then up-sampling. - **Expansive Path (Decoder):** $2 \times 2$ **transposed convolutions** halve the channels and double the spatial size. Concatenation with corresponding encoder feature map (skip connection). Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions + ReLU. - **Final Layer:** $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the 64-channel feature map to the desired number of classes $C$. **Why Skip Connections are Important (1.5 marks):** - Downsampling loses **fine-grained spatial detail** (edges, small structures). - Skip connections **concatenate** high-resolution features from the encoder with the upsampled decoder features. - This combines *semantic context* (deep, low-res) with *localization precision* (shallow, high-res) — critical for pixel-accurate masks. - It also provides an alternative gradient pathway, mitigating vanishing gradients. **Loss Function (0.5 marks):** U-Net uses **pixel-wise cross-entropy** with per-pixel weighting. The original paper uses a **weighted cross-entropy** where pixels near boundaries are up-weighted to handle touching cells: $$w(\mathbf{x}) = w_c(\mathbf{x}) + w_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{(d_1(\mathbf{x}) + d_2(\mathbf{x}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ where $d_1, d_2$ are distances to the nearest and second-nearest cell. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] **(a)** Explain the **YOLO (You Only Look Once)** object detection algorithm. Describe how the image grid works, what each cell predicts, and derive the YOLO loss function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**? Explain the generator–discriminator training procedure and write the minimax objective. Mention two applications in computer vision. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** ### Part (a) — YOLO Algorithm (7 marks) **Overview (1 mark):** YOLO (Redmon et al., 2016) reframes object detection as a **single regression problem**, predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities from image pixels in a *single forward pass* of a CNN — hence "You Only Look Once." **Grid Mechanism (2 marks):** - Input image is divided into an $S \times S$ **grid** (e.g., $7 \times 7$ in YOLOv1). - If the **center** of an object falls into a grid cell, that cell is *responsible* for detecting it. - Each grid cell predicts: - **$B$ bounding boxes** (e.g., $B=2$ in v1) — each with $(x, y, w, h)$ and a **confidence score** $C = \Pr(\text{object}) \times IoU_{pred}^{gt}$. - **$C$ conditional class probabilities** $\Pr(\text{class}_i \mid \text{object})$, shared across all $B$ boxes in the cell. - Final detection tensor: $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$. **Network Backbone (1 mark):** YOLOv1 uses 24 conv layers + 2 FC layers inspired by GoogLeNet. Later versions (v3–v8) use **Darknet-53** (ResNet-style) with FPN and multi-scale predictions. **YOLO Loss Function (3 marks):** The loss is a sum of four terms (only first cell is responsible, indicated by $\mathbb{1}_i^{obj}$): $$\mathcal{L} = \lambda_{coord}\sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B}\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}\left[(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2\right]$$ $$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B}\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ $$+ \lambda_{noobj}\sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B}\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{noobj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ $$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\mathbb{1}_{i}^{obj}\sum_{c \in classes}(p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$ - $\lambda_{coord} = 5$ increases the weight of coordinate errors. - $\lambda_{noobj} = 0.5$ reduces the penalty for "no object" cells. - $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$: square-root partially equalizes penalty across box sizes. --- ### Part (b) — Generative Adversarial Networks (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** A **GAN** (Goodfellow et al., 2014) is a generative framework where two neural networks — a **Generator $G$** and a **Discriminator $D$** — are trained simultaneously in opposition, learning the data distribution $p_{data}$. **Generator $G$ (1 mark):** - Input: a noise vector $\mathbf{z} \sim p_z(\mathbf{z})$ (e.g., Gaussian or uniform). - Output: a synthetic image $G(\mathbf{z})$ designed to fool $D$. - Goal: minimize $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$. **Discriminator $D$ (1 mark):** - Input: a real image $\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}$ or a fake $G(\mathbf{z})$. - Output: probability $D(\cdot)$ that the input is real. - Goal: maximize correct classification of real vs. fake. **Minimax Objective (2 marks):** $$\min_{G}\max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$ - The first term pushes $D$ to output 1 for real images. - The second term pushes $D$ to output 0 for fakes (equivalently, pushes $G$ to make $D$ output 1 for fakes). **Training Procedure (1 mark):** 1. **Fix $G$**, take a gradient step on $D$ to maximize $\log D(\mathbf{x}) + \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$. 2. **Fix $D$**, take a gradient step on $G$ to minimize $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ — in practice, maximize $\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))$ to avoid early-stage vanishing gradients. 3. Repeat until Nash equilibrium is approximated. **Applications in Computer Vision (1 mark):** 1. **Image super-resolution** (e.g., SRGAN) — generate high-resolution images from low-res inputs. 2. **Image-to-image translation** (e.g., Pix2Pix, CycleGAN) — convert sketches → photos, day → night, horse → zebra. 3. *Other:* Face generation (StyleGAN), data augmentation, inpainting. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in PEEIT633 – Module 4 (CV Applications):** > > 1. **Confusing Semantic vs Instance Segmentation** — Semantic gives one label per pixel class; Instance *additionally* distinguishes individual objects. Students often write "U-Net does instance segmentation" — *incorrect*. U-Net is **semantic**; for instance, use **Mask R-CNN**. > 2. **Mixing up R-CNN families** — R-CNN is *not* end-to-end (uses selective search). Only **Faster R-CNN** introduces the RPN and is fully end-to-end. Many students wrongly claim "Faster R-CNN uses selective search." > 3. **IoU vs Dice confusion** — Both measure overlap, but $Dice = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}$. Examiners mark this strictly. > 4. **Forgetting NMS in detection write-ups** — Any object detection algorithm description *must* include NMS as a post-processing step. Skipping it costs at least 1–2 marks. > 5. **GAN objective sign errors** — The min-max formulation has $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ for the generator's *minimization*; many students invert signs. Write it out carefully. > 6. **YOLO confidence definition** — Confidence is $C = \Pr(\text{object}) \times IoU_{pred}^{gt}$, *not* just $\Pr(\text{object})$. Examiners love this nuance. > 7. **Skipping the square-root in YOLO loss** — Without $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$, the formula is incomplete and loses a mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > **Use this as your last-day revision sheet before the KTU exam.** - ✅ **Image Classification** → CNN backbone + Softmax + Cross-Entropy. Landmarks: AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet, ViT. - ✅ **Object Detection** → Two-stage (R-CNN family, Mask R-CNN) vs One-stage (YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet). Key metrics: **IoU, Precision, Recall, mAP**. - ✅ **IoU** = intersection / union; threshold 0.5 (PASCAL), 0.5–0.95 (COCO). - ✅ **NMS** keeps the box with the highest confidence and suppresses overlapping boxes ($IoU > 0.5$). - ✅ **Semantic Segmentation** → FCN, SegNet, **U-Net**, DeepLab. Loss = pixel-wise cross-entropy (or Dice loss). - ✅ **Instance Segmentation** → **Mask R-CNN** = Faster R-CNN + mask branch + RoIAlign. - ✅ **YOLO** divides image into $S \times S$ grid; each cell predicts $B$ boxes + $C$ class probs; loss has 4 weighted components; uses $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$. - ✅ **Faster R-CNN** uses **RPN** with $k$ anchor boxes per spatial location; shares CNN features for proposals and detection. - ✅ **GANs** train $G$ (generator) and $D$ (discriminator) adversarially; objective: $\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$. - ✅ **VAEs** use encoder–decoder + reparameterization; loss = reconstruction + KL divergence. - ✅ **Face Recognition** uses **Siamese / Triplet Networks** with embedding loss $L = \max(d(A,P) - d(A,N) + \alpha, 0)$. - ✅ **Style Transfer** = Gatys et al. (2016) — combines content + Gram-matrix style loss using a pre-trained VGG. - ✅ **Image Captioning** = CNN encoder + RNN/LSTM/Transformer decoder (e.g., "Show and Tell"). - ✅ **Dice Coefficient** = $\frac{2|P \cap G|}{|P| + |G|}$ (alternative to IoU for imbalanced segmentation). - ✅ **mIoU** = average of per-class IoUs — standard segmentation benchmark. - ✅ **Transfer Learning** is the *de facto* approach: pre-train on ImageNet, fine-tune on target CV task. - ✅ **Data Augmentation** (flip, crop, color jitter, MixUp, CutMix) is critical to prevent overfitting on small CV datasets. > [!TIP] > **Final golden rule for KTU 2024 CV questions:** Always (i) state the **task definition**, (ii) sketch the **architecture diagram**, (iii) write the **loss function**, and (iv) give **one real-world use-case**. This 4-part structure hits 90%+ of available marks.

topics.title: Speech Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Speech Recognition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Speech Recognition (Automatic Speech Recognition – ASR)** is the interdisciplinary sub-field of computational linguistics, computer science, and electrical engineering that develops methodologies and technologies enabling the **recognition, transcription, and translation of spoken language into text** by machines. In the context of deep learning, ASR refers to the end-to-end mapping of a variable-length acoustic input sequence (raw audio waveform or spectral features) to a variable-length output token sequence (characters, sub-words, or words) using deep neural architectures such as RNNs, LSTMs, Transformers, or Conformer models. Formally, given an acoustic observation sequence $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$ of length $T$, the goal of an ASR system is to find the most probable word/character sequence $W^* = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_U)$ of length $U$ such that: $$W^* = \arg\max_{W} \; P(W \mid X)$$ By Bayes' theorem, this posterior probability is decomposed as: $$P(W \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}$$ Where: - $P(X \mid W)$ is the **Acoustic Model (AM)** — probability of observing the acoustic features given a word/phoneme sequence. - $P(W)$ is the **Language Model (LM)** — prior probability of the word sequence. - $P(X)$ is the **Acoustic Evidence** — treated as a normalization constant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of *PEEIT633 Deep Learning* requires understanding the traditional hybrid ASR pipeline (AM + LM + Lexicon) and its evolution to **end-to-end deep learning models** such as DeepSpeech, Listen-Attend-Spell (LAS), and Transformer-based architectures (Whisper, Wav2Vec 2.0). ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Courtroom Stenographer" Imagine a highly trained **courtroom stenographer** who listens to a witness speaking in a noisy courtroom: 1. **The Ear (Feature Extraction Layer)** — The stenographer first isolates the speaker's voice from background noise, focusing on phonetic cues (vowels, consonants, intonation). This mirrors the **MFCC / Spectrogram extraction** front-end of an ASR system. 2. **The Linguistic Memory (Language Model)** — Even if the stenographer mishears a word, their knowledge of English grammar and legal jargon helps them reconstruct the *most plausible sentence*. This is the role of the **Language Model (LM)** — typically an n-gram or neural RNN/Transformer LM. 3. **The Hand (Decoder)** — Finally, the stenographer types the words as a coherent transcript, character by character. This corresponds to the **Decoder** (CTC, Attention, or Autoregressive head) producing output tokens. Just as a stenographer occasionally mishears "their" as "there" without context, an ASR system needs the LM to **disambiguate homophones** using contextual statistics. > [!NOTE] > **Standard ASR Evaluation Metric:** Word Error Rate (**WER**), computed as $\mathbf{WER = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%}$ where $S$ = substitutions, $D$ = deletions, $I$ = insertions, and $N$ = total words in reference. Character Error Rate (**CER**) is the analogous metric at the character level. ### 1.3 Historical Evolution of ASR Paradigms | Era | Paradigm | Core Technology | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | 1950s–1980s | Template Matching | Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) | Speaker-dependent, no generalization | | 1980s–1990s | Statistical (HMM-GMM) | Hidden Markov Models + Gaussian Mixtures | Required hand-crafted features (MFCC) | | 1990s–2010s | Hybrid DNN-HMM | Deep Neural Networks replace GMMs in AM | Still modular, pipeline errors compound | | 2015–Present | End-to-End Deep Learning | CTC, LAS, RNN-T, Transformer, Conformer | Requires large labeled corpora | > [!TIP] > For KTU examinations, focus on **CTC, LAS, and the Transformer-based end-to-end ASR** as these constitute the bulk of Module 4 questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Hybrid ASR Pipeline (Traditional Architecture) A classical ASR system is a composition of **three independently trained probabilistic components**: #### 2.1.1 Acoustic Model (AM) - **Purpose:** Maps acoustic feature vectors $\mathbf{x}_t$ (typically 39-dimensional MFCC + delta + delta-delta features) to phonetic units (senones, triphones, or phonemes). - **Architecture:** HMM-DNN hybrid — HMM models the temporal sequence, DNN (feedforward, RNN, or TDNN) emits probabilities $P(\mathbf{x}_t \mid s_i)$ for each HMM state $s_i$. - **Modern Replacement:** Entirely neural AMs using LSTM or Conformer encoders. #### 2.1.2 Pronunciation Model (Lexicon / Dictionary) - **Purpose:** Provides the canonical mapping from words to their phonetic/phonemic transcriptions. - **Example Entry:** "HELLO" → HH AH L OW - **Format:** Typically a CMU-style `.dict` file or a finite-state transducer (FST). #### 2.1.3 Language Model (LM) - **Purpose:** Assigns prior probability $P(W)$ to any word sequence, capturing syntactic and semantic regularities. - **Statistical LM:** n-gram model using chain rule: $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, \dots, w_{i-n+1})$ - **Neural LM:** Feedforward (Bengio et al., 2003) or RNN/Transformer LMs (e.g., GPT-style). ### 2.2 End-to-End ASR: The Deep Learning Revolution End-to-End (E2E) ASR **collapses the AM + Lexicon + LM pipeline into a single neural network** that directly maps audio → text. The three dominant E2E paradigms are: #### 2.2.1 Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) – Graves et al. (2006) - **Key Idea:** Introduces a **blank symbol (–)** and allows the network to output a label per time-step without requiring frame-level alignment between audio and transcript. - **Loss Function:** Marginalizes over all valid alignments $\pi$ that collapse to the target sequence $Y$: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CTC}} = -\log P(Y \mid X) = -\log \sum_{\pi \in \mathcal{B}^{-1}(Y)} P(\pi \mid X)$$ where $\mathcal{B}$ is the *many-to-one* collapsing function that removes blanks and repeated characters. #### 2.2.2 Listen, Attend and Spell (LAS) – Chan et al. (Google, 2016) - **Key Idea:** A **sequence-to-sequence** model with an **attention mechanism** that learns soft alignments between audio frames and output characters. - **Architecture:** Encoder (Listen) + Attention + Decoder (Spell). #### 2.2.3 RNN-Transducer (RNN-T) – Graves (2012) - **Key Idea:** Combines CTC's monotonic alignment with an explicit *prediction network* (internal LM) and a *joint network* that integrates acoustic and linguistic information. #### 2.2.4 Conformer / Transformer ASR - **Key Idea:** Replaces RNNs with **self-attention** and **convolutional modules** to capture both global context and local phonetic patterns. Models like **Whisper (OpenAI)**, **Wav2Vec 2.0 (Meta)**, and **Conformer (Google)** dominate modern benchmarks. ### 2.3 Feature Extraction Front-End Raw audio (sampled at **16 kHz** for most modern ASR) is converted into a 2D time-frequency representation before feeding the neural network: | Feature | Description | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **MFCC** (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) | 13 coefficients + Δ + ΔΔ = 39-D | Classical DNN-HMM | | **Filterbank Energies (FBANK)** | 40 or 80 log-Mel filterbanks | Modern neural ASR | | **Spectrogram** | Magnitude of STFT | CNN-based / vision-inspired models | | **Raw Waveform** | 1-D signal input | Wav2Vec 2.0, Whisper | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **high-yield, exam-ready** summary of every formula you are expected to memorize for Module 4 Speech Recognition. | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ASR Posterior | $W^* = \arg\max_W P(W \mid X) = \arg\max_W P(X \mid W) P(W)$ | $W$ = words, $X$ = audio | | 2 | Bayes Decomposition | $P(W \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}$ | $P(X)$ = normalization constant | | 3 | n-gram LM | $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$ | Conditional probability approximation | | 4 | CTC Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CTC}} = -\log \sum_{\pi \in \mathcal{B}^{-1}(Y)} \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\pi_t \mid X)$ | $\pi$ = alignment path | | 5 | WER | $\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N}$ | $S,D,I$ = sub, del, ins; $N$ = ref length | | 6 | CER | $\text{CER} = \frac{S_c + D_c + I_c}{N_c}$ | Computed at character level | | 7 | MFCC Step 1 (Pre-emphasis) | $y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \cdot x[n-1]$ | $\alpha \approx 0.97$ | | 8 | STFT Magnitude | $X(m,k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot w[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | $m$ = frame, $k$ = freq bin | | 9 | Mel Scale | $m = 2595 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$ | $f$ in Hz, $m$ in mels | | 10 | Attention (Bahdanau) | $\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T} \exp(e_{ik})}$ | $e_{ij}$ = alignment score | | 11 | Self-Attention (Scaled Dot-Product) | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | $d_k$ = key dim | | 12 | Conformer Conv Module | $\text{Conv}(\text{LayerNorm}(x)) + \frac{1}{2} \text{FFN}(x) + \text{Attention}(x)$ | Combines FFN + Attention + Conv | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility ASR is foundational to: - **Voice Assistants:** Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant - **Medical Transcription:** Automated clinical note generation - **Accessibility:** Live captioning for the hearing-impaired - **Automotive:** In-car voice command systems - **Call Centers:** Real-time agent assistance and analytics - **Industrial IoT:** Voice-controlled robotics in noisy factories > [!NOTE] > Modern Transformer-based ASR (e.g., **Whisper-large-v3**) achieves WER < 3% on clean English benchmarks (LibriSpeech test-clean), approaching human parity. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Word Error Rate (WER) via Edit Distance The WER is fundamentally a **Levenshtein distance** (edit distance) between the hypothesized transcript $H$ and the reference transcript $R$, normalized by the length of the reference. #### Step 1: Define the Edit Operations Three edit operations are permitted on the reference string $R$ to transform it into hypothesis $H$: - **Substitution (S):** Replace a character/word. - **Deletion (D):** Remove a character/word. - **Insertion (I):** Add a character/word. #### Step 2: Dynamic Programming Formulation Let $\text{ed}(i,j)$ be the minimum edit distance between $R[1 \dots i]$ and $H[1 \dots j]$. The recurrence is: $$\text{ed}(i,j) = \begin{cases} i, & \text{if } j = 0 \\ j, & \text{if } i = 0 \\ \text{ed}(i-1,j-1), & \text{if } R_i = H_j \\ 1 + \min\{\text{ed}(i-1,j),\; \text{ed}(i,j-1),\; \text{ed}(i-1,j-1)\}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ #### Step 3: Numerical Worked Example **Reference:** "THE CAT SAT" **Hypothesis:** "THE BAT" Operation-by-operation: - $R_1=T, H_1=T$ → match (0 edits) - $R_2=H, H_2=H$ → match (0 edits) - $R_3=E, H_3=E$ → match (0 edits) - $R_4=\text{(space)}, H_4=\text{(space)}$ → match - $R_5=C, H_5=B$ → **Substitution** (1 edit) - Remaining reference words "AT SAT" (2 words) → **2 Deletions** Total edits: $S=1, D=2, I=0$. Reference length $N=3$. $$\text{WER} = \frac{1 + 2 + 0}{3} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.00 = 100\%$$ > [!WARNING] > WER can exceed 100% if the hypothesis is **much longer** than the reference (large insertion count). ### 3.2 Derivation 2: CTC Forward-Backward Algorithm The naive computation of $P(Y \mid X)$ requires summing over all alignments of exponential complexity. CTC uses a **dynamic programming forward-backward algorithm** analogous to HMMs. #### Step 1: Define the Augmented Target Sequence Given target $Y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_U)$, construct the augmented sequence $Y'$ by inserting blanks: $$Y' = (\text{–}, y_1, \text{–}, y_2, \text{–}, \dots, y_U, \text{–})$$ Length of $Y'$ is $2U + 1$. #### Step 2: Define the Forward Variable Let $\alpha_t(s)$ = total probability of being at augmented label $s$ at time $t$, given acoustic features $X_{1:t}$. **Recursion:** $$\alpha_t(s) = \left[\alpha_{t-1}(s) + \alpha_{t-1}(s-1)\right] \cdot P_t(Y'_s)$$ (plus $\alpha_{t-1}(s-2) \cdot P_t(Y'_s)$ if $Y'_s \neq \text{–}$ and $Y'_s \neq Y'_{s-2}$) #### Step 3: Define the Backward Variable Let $\beta_t(s)$ = total probability of correctly completing the output from time $t$ onwards starting at label $s$: $$\beta_t(s) = P_t(Y'_s) \cdot \left[\beta_{t+1}(s) + \beta_{t+1}(s+1)\right] + \text{(blank/skip term)}$$ #### Step 4: Compute the CTC Loss The probability of alignment $\pi_t(s)$ at time $t$ and label $s$ is: $$P(\pi_t(s) \mid X) = \frac{\alpha_t(s) \cdot \beta_t(s)}{P_t(Y'_s)}$$ The total probability of the target sequence is: $$P(Y \mid X) = \sum_{s=1}^{2U+1} \frac{\alpha_T(s) \cdot \beta_T(s)}{P_T(Y'_s)}$$ And the CTC loss is: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CTC}} = -\log P(Y \mid X)$$ #### Step 5: Gradient Computation The gradient with respect to the softmax output at time $t$ and label $s$ is: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CTC}}}{\partial P_t(s)} = -\frac{1}{P(Y \mid X)} \cdot \frac{\alpha_t(s) \beta_t(s)}{P_t(s)}$$ This can be back-propagated through the neural network to train the encoder end-to-end. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Attention Score in Listen-Attend-Spell (LAS) The LAS model uses **Bahdanau (additive) attention** to align encoder outputs $\mathbf{h} = (h_1, \dots, h_T)$ with decoder state $s_{u-1}$: #### Step 1: Compute Alignment Energy $$e_{u,t} = \mathbf{v}_a^{\top} \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_a \cdot s_{u-1} + \mathbf{U}_a \cdot h_t\right)$$ #### Step 2: Softmax Normalization (Attention Weights) $$\alpha_{u,t} = \frac{\exp(e_{u,t})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T} \exp(e_{u,k})}$$ #### Step 3: Context Vector $$\mathbf{c}_u = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_{u,t} \cdot h_t$$ #### Step 4: Decoder Update $$s_u = \text{LSTM}(s_{u-1}, y_{u-1}, \mathbf{c}_u)$$ $$P(y_u \mid y_{ [!TIP] > Unlike CTC, LAS **does not assume conditional independence** between outputs. Each character $y_u$ is conditioned on all previous characters and the entire audio, making LAS more linguistically coherent. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | CTC | LAS (Seq2Seq + Attention) | RNN-Transducer | |---|---|---|---| | Alignment | Monotonic, conditional independence | Soft, learned attention | Monotonic + prediction net | | Inference | Fast (greedy/beam) | Slower (auto-regressive) | Streaming-capable | | LM Integration | External shallow fusion | Implicit in decoder | Internal + external | | Output Tokens | Characters/sub-words | Characters/sub-words | Sub-words (word-piece) | | Training Stability | Very stable | Can suffer exposure bias | Stable, requires 3 losses | | Streaming | Yes (frame-synchronous) | No (full utterance) | Yes (frame-synchronous) | | WER on LibriSpeech | ~5-6% | ~4-5% | ~3-4% | > [!NOTE] > Modern **Conformer-Transducer** models (e.g., Google USM, NVIDIA Canary) achieve **streaming + low WER** by combining the monotonic alignment of RNN-T with the representational power of Conformer encoders. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Classical Hybrid ASR Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Audio Input
16 kHz waveform] --> B[Feature Extractor
MFCC or FBANK] B --> C[Acoustic Model
DNN / LSTM / HMM] C --> D[Pronunciation Model
Lexicon FST] D --> E[Decoder
Viterbi or Beam Search] F[Language Model
n-gram or Neural LM] --> E E --> G[Output Text Transcript] style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style D fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style E fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style F fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C style G fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825 ``` ### 4.2 End-to-End ASR with CTC (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD W[Audio Waveform x1 to xT] --> F[Mel Spectrogram Frontend
80 channel log-mel] F --> E[Encoder
CNN + BiLSTM or Conformer] E --> P[Per-frame Linear Projection
vocab size plus blank] P --> CTC[CTC Collapse Layer
removes blanks and repeats] CTC --> O[Output Text y1 to yU] L[CTC Loss Function] -.trains.-> E L -.trains.-> P style W fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style F fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style E fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style P fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style CTC fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style O fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825 style L fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 ``` ### 4.3 Listen-Attend-Spell (LAS) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Audio Features
x1 to xT] --> E[Listen Encoder
Bidirectional LSTM or Pyramid RNN] E --> H[Encoder Hidden States
h1 to hT] H --> Attn[Attention Mechanism
Bahdanau additive] S[Decoder State su-1] --> Attn Attn --> C[Context Vector cu] Prev[Previous Character yu-1] --> D[Spell Decoder
Unidirectional LSTM] C --> D D --> SO[Decoder State su] SO --> Out[Output Probability
P yu given y less than u and X] style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style E fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style H fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style Attn fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style Prev fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825 style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style SO fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C style Out fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 ``` ### 4.4 CTC Alignment Collapse Mechanism (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD Raw[CTC Output Sequence
hh ee l l blank l oo blank] --> Step1{Step 1
Remove repeated characters} Step1 --> Step2[he l blank l oo blank] Step2 --> Step3{Step 2
Remove blanks} Step3 --> Final[Final Output
HELLO] style Raw fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style Step1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style Step2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style Step3 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style Final fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 ``` ### 4.5 Complete Speech Recognition Training & Inference Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Audio Dataset
LibriSpeech / CommonVoice] --> B[Preprocessing
Normalization and Resampling] B --> C[Feature Extraction
80-dim log-mel] C --> D[Model Forward Pass
Conformer Encoder plus Decoder] D --> E[Compute CTC Loss] E --> F[Backpropagation
AdamW Optimizer] F --> G[Update Weights] G --> H{Validation Loss
Converged} H -- No --> D H -- Yes --> I[Save Checkpoint] I --> J[Inference
Beam Search Decoding] J --> K[External LM Fusion
Shallow Fusion] K --> L[Final Transcript Output] style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style C fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style E fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style F fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825 style G fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C style H fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 style I fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style J fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style K fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style L fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a hybrid ASR system.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Automatic Speech Recognition is the process by which a machine identifies, processes, and converts spoken language into textual form. Mathematically, ASR finds $W^* = \arg\max_W P(W \mid X) = \arg\max_W P(X \mid W) P(W)$. **Components of Hybrid ASR (2 marks):** 1. **Feature Extractor:** Converts raw audio (16 kHz) into acoustic features such as MFCCs (39-dimensional) or log-Mel filterbank energies. 2. **Acoustic Model (AM):** Models $P(X \mid W)$, mapping acoustic features to phonetic units. Implemented as DNN-HMM hybrids. 3. **Pronunciation Model (Lexicon):** Maps words to phoneme sequences (e.g., "CAT" → K AE T). 4. **Language Model (LM):** Models $P(W)$, the prior probability of word sequences (n-gram or neural). 5. **Decoder:** Combines AM + LM + Lexicon via Viterbi or beam search to output the most probable word sequence. --- #### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Explain Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss. Why is the blank symbol necessary in CTC?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **CTC Loss (2 marks):** CTC is a loss function designed for sequence-to-sequence problems where the input and output sequences are of different lengths and **frame-level alignment is unknown**. It marginalizes the total probability of the target sequence $Y$ over all valid alignment paths $\pi$: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CTC}} = -\log \sum_{\pi \in \mathcal{B}^{-1}(Y)} \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\pi_t \mid X)$$ where $\mathcal{B}$ is the many-to-one function that collapses an alignment path to the output by removing blanks and consecutive duplicates. **Necessity of Blank Symbol (1 mark):** The blank symbol (–) allows the network to output *no label* at certain time steps (e.g., during silences or transitions between phonemes), and it distinguishes repeated characters. Without blanks, the network cannot differentiate between "HELLO" (5 chars) and "HELLLO" (6 chars) at decoding time, since both collapse to "HELLO" after naive de-duplication. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Module 4 carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice**. Students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] **(a)** Explain the architecture of the **Listen, Attend and Spell (LAS)** model in detail. Describe the role of the **Listener (Encoder)**, **Attender**, and **Speller (Decoder)** with relevant equations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **CTC, LAS, and RNN-Transducer (RNN-T)** in terms of alignment assumptions, inference speed, language model integration, and suitability for streaming. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks** The **Listen, Attend and Spell (LAS)** model, introduced by Chan et al. (Google, 2016), is an end-to-end neural speech recognizer that directly transcribes audio utterances into character sequences. **1. Listener (Encoder) — 2 marks:** - Composed of a stack of **Bidirectional LSTMs (BLSTM)** or, in modern variants, **Pyramid RNN / Conformer** blocks. - Processes the input acoustic feature sequence $X = (x_1, \dots, x_T)$ (e.g., 80-dim log-mel features). - Produces a sequence of high-level hidden representations $H = (h_1, h_2, \dots, h_T)$ that capture contextual information from both past and future time steps: $$h_t = \text{BiLSTM}(h_{t-1}, h_{t+1}, x_t)$$ - In **Pyramid Listener**, consecutive frames are concatenated to reduce the time resolution, e.g., 2× or 3× downsampling, improving computational efficiency. **2. Attender — 2 marks:** - The attention mechanism computes a **context vector** $c_u$ at each decoding step $u$ as a weighted sum of encoder hidden states. - **Energy (alignment) score:** $$e_{u,t} = v_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a s_{u-1} + U_a h_t)$$ - **Attention weights (Bahdanau):** $$\alpha_{u,t} = \frac{\exp(e_{u,t})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T} \exp(e_{u,k})}$$ - **Context vector:** $$c_u = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_{u,t} \cdot h_t$$ - The attention weights $\alpha_{u,t}$ form a soft alignment matrix between audio frames and output characters, learned entirely from data. **3. Speller (Decoder) — 2 marks:** - A unidirectional LSTM that generates output characters one at a time, conditioned on: - The previous character $y_{u-1}$ - The previous decoder state $s_{u-1}$ - The context vector $c_u$ $$s_u = \text{LSTM}(s_{u-1}, y_{u-1}, c_u)$$ - Output probability distribution: $$P(y_u \mid y_{ The full feature matrix is then fed to the deep learning ASR model (DNN, LSTM, or Conformer). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deduction Points — Avoid These Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing AM with LM:** Many students interchange Acoustic Model and Language Model. Remember — **AM is $P(X \mid W)$** (audio-to-phoneme), **LM is $P(W)$** (word sequence probability). > 2. **Forgetting the Blank in CTC:** Omitting the blank symbol in CTC derivations costs 1–2 marks. Always explicitly state that **– is a special token with no acoustic emission**. > 3. **WER vs. CER Confusion:** WER operates on words, CER on characters. Mixing them up in numerical examples loses 1 mark. > 4. **Missing the Bayes Theorem Decomposition:** Always write $P(W \mid X) \propto P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$ when explaining ASR formally. > 5. **No Block Diagram:** KTU ESE questions on hybrid ASR/LAS/CTC **require a block diagram**. Skipping it incurs a 1–2 mark penalty. > 6. **Skipping Alignment in CTC:** If asked to derive CTC loss, students often forget to define the augmented sequence and skip the alignment collapse logic — leading to a half-marked answer. > 7. **Conflating Attention Variants:** Bahdanau (additive) and Luong (multiplicative) attention are **not the same**. Use the correct formula. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **ASR Core Equation:** $W^* = \arg\max_W P(W \mid X)$ using Bayes decomposition. - [x] **Hybrid ASR Pipeline:** Feature Extractor → AM → Lexicon → LM → Decoder. - [x] **CTC:** Uses blank symbol and marginalizes over all alignments; assumes conditional independence between outputs. - [x] **LAS:** Sequence-to-sequence + attention; auto-regressive decoder; non-streaming. - [x] **RNN-Transducer:** Combines CTC monotonicity with an internal prediction network; streaming-capable. - [x] **Conformer:** Combines convolution (local patterns) + self-attention (global context); state-of-the-art in modern ASR. - [x] **WER Formula:** $\text{WER} = (S + D + I) / N$; can exceed 100% due to insertions. - [x] **MFCC Pipeline:** Pre-emphasis → Framing → Windowing → FFT → Mel Filterbank → DCT. - [x] **Mel Scale:** $m = 2595 \log_{10}(1 + f/700)$ — perceptually motivated frequency scale. - [x] **Modern SOTA Models:** Whisper (OpenAI), Wav2Vec 2.0 (Meta), Conformer (Google), USM (Google Universal Speech Model). - [x] **Datasets to Know:** LibriSpeech, CommonVoice, TED-LIUM, AISHELL (Mandarin), MuST-C (multilingual). - [x] **Self-Supervised Pre-training:** Wav2Vec 2.0 uses contrastive learning on unlabeled audio before fine-tuning on labeled data. - [x] **Multilingual ASR:** Whisper supports 99 languages via a large-scale weakly supervised dataset. - [x] **Streaming vs. Non-streaming:** CTC/RNN-T for streaming; LAS for offline batch processing. - [x] **External LM Fusion:** CTC outputs can be re-scored with an n-gram or neural LM using shallow fusion at decoding time.

topics.title: Natural Language Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Language Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Natural Language Processing (NLP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Deep Learning that focuses on the computational understanding, interpretation, generation, and manipulation of human (natural) language. In the context of deep learning, NLP leverages neural architectures — such as Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs), and Transformer-based models — to learn hierarchical, distributed representations of text and perform tasks such as machine translation, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, and question answering.

Formally, given a sequence of tokens $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$ drawn from a vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$, an NLP model learns a function $f_\theta : \mathcal{V}^T \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ parameterized by $\theta$ that maps text to a task-specific output space $\mathcal{Y}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of NLP as teaching a **polyglot toddler** to understand language. Initially, the child only hears sounds. With time, the child learns that certain sounds correspond to objects, actions, and feelings. Deep learning models work the same way — they are fed raw text and **gradually learn** that words, phrases, and sentences carry meaning by observing millions of examples. Just as the child uses **context** ("apple" near "eat" means fruit, but "Apple" near "iPhone" means a company), NLP models use surrounding words to disambiguate meaning.

### 1.3 Why NLP is Hard

Human language is:
- **Ambiguous** — "Bank" can mean a river bank or a financial bank.
- **Context-dependent** — Pronouns ("he", "it") refer to previously mentioned entities.
- **Variable in length** — Sentences can be short or arbitrarily long.
- **Rich in structure** — Grammar, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics all interact.

> [!TIP]
> Standard feedforward neural networks require fixed-size inputs, but text is inherently **sequential and variable-length**. This challenge motivates the use of specialized architectures like RNNs and Transformers.

### 1.4 Core NLP Tasks (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Task | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Text Classification** | Assign a label to a sentence/document | Spam detection, sentiment analysis |
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Identify proper nouns and their types | "Apple" → ORGANIZATION |
| **Machine Translation** | Translate text from one language to another | English → French |
| **Question Answering (QA)** | Given a passage and a question, produce an answer | Reading comprehension |
| **Text Generation** | Generate coherent text given a prompt | Chatbots, story generation |
| **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** | Label each word with its grammatical role | "running" → VERB |
| **Language Modeling** | Predict the next word given previous words | Autocomplete |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NLP Pipeline (High-Level)

A typical deep-learning-based NLP pipeline proceeds as follows:

1. **Text Acquisition** — Collect raw text corpora.
2. **Tokenization** — Split text into smaller units (words, subwords, characters).
3. **Normalization** — Lowercasing, stemming, lemmatization, stop-word removal.
4. **Numerical Encoding** — Convert tokens into integer IDs via a vocabulary.
5. **Embedding** — Map integer IDs into dense, real-valued vectors $\mathbf{e}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
6. **Modeling** — Feed embeddings into a neural architecture (RNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformer).
7. **Output Decoding** — Map model outputs back to task-specific labels or text.

## 2.2 Word Embeddings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Word Embedding** is a learned, dense, low-dimensional vector representation of a word such that semantically similar words occupy nearby points in vector space. This overcomes the limitations of one-hot encoding, which produces high-dimensional, sparse, and orthogonal vectors.

The embedding of token $x_t$ is obtained by indexing a learned embedding matrix $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert \times d}$:

$$\mathbf{e}_t = \mathbf{E}^\top \cdot \mathbf{1}_{x_t}$$

where $\mathbf{1}_{x_t}$ is the one-hot vector of token $x_t$ and $d$ is the embedding dimension (typically $d \in [50, 300]$ for classical models, $[768, 4096]$ for transformers).

### 2.2.1 Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013)

Word2Vec has two architectures:

- **CBOW (Continuous Bag-of-Words):** Predicts the target word from a context window.

$$P(w_t \mid w_{t-c}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+c})$$

- **Skip-gram:** Predicts context words from a target word.

$$P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t), \quad \text{for } j \in [-c, c], \; j \neq 0$$

The objective maximizes the log-probability:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \leq j \leq c, \; j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$

### 2.2.2 GloVe (Global Vectors)

GloVe combines global co-occurrence statistics with local context window methods. Let $X_{ij}$ be the number of times word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$. The objective is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{GloVe}} = \sum_{i,j=1}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert} f(X_{ij}) \left( \mathbf{w}_i^\top \tilde{\mathbf{w}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij} \right)^2$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is a weighting function that limits the influence of very frequent co-occurrences.

## 2.3 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **RNN** processes a sequence one element at a time while maintaining a hidden state that summarizes all previous information. This makes them naturally suited for variable-length sequential data such as text.

At time step $t$, the hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ is updated as:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \sigma_h\left( \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{xh} \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b}_h \right)$$

The output is:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \sigma_y\left( \mathbf{W}_{hy} \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y \right)$$

where $\mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ are learnable weight matrices and $\sigma_h, \sigma_y$ are activation functions.

### 2.3.1 The Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Problem

During Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), gradients are multiplied repeatedly by $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. If the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is:
- $> 1$ → gradients **explode**.
- $< 1$ → gradients **vanish**.

This makes vanilla RNNs unable to learn long-range dependencies.

## 2.4 LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)

> [!NOTE]
> **LSTM** solves the vanishing gradient problem through a **gating mechanism** that explicitly controls what information to remember, forget, and output.

The LSTM cell uses three gates and a cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$:

**Forget gate:**

$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma\left( \mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f \right)$$

**Input gate:**

$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma\left( \mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i \right)$$

$$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh\left( \mathbf{W}_C [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C \right)$$

**Cell state update:**

$$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$$

**Output gate:**

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma\left( \mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o \right)$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$$

where $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product and $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function.

## 2.5 GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)

> [!TIP]
> **GRU** is a simplified variant of LSTM that merges the forget and input gates into a single **update gate**, and merges the cell state and hidden state. It is computationally cheaper and often performs comparably.

**Update gate:**

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma\left( \mathbf{W}_z [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_z \right)$$

**Reset gate:**

$$\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma\left( \mathbf{W}_r [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_r \right)$$

**Candidate hidden state:**

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh\left( \mathbf{W}_h [\mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_h \right)$$

**Hidden state update:**

$$\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$$

## 2.6 Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) Models

Used for tasks like machine translation, the Seq2Seq model consists of:
- **Encoder:** Reads the input sequence and compresses it into a **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$ (typically the final hidden state of an RNN/LSTM/GRU).
- **Decoder:** Generates the output sequence one token at a time, conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens.

The decoder is trained to maximize:

$$P(y_1, \dots, y_{T'} \mid x_1, \dots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T'} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

## 2.7 Attention Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attention** allows the decoder to "look back" at all encoder hidden states, not just the final one, by computing a weighted sum. This addresses the **information bottleneck** of fixed-size context vectors.

The attention score for decoder state $s_{t-1}$ over encoder state $h_i$ is typically computed via additive or multiplicative attention:

**Additive (Bahdanau):**

$$e_{t,i} = \mathbf{v}^\top \tanh\left( \mathbf{W}_s \mathbf{s}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_h \mathbf{h}_i \right)$$

**Multiplicative (Luong):**

$$e_{t,i} = \mathbf{s}_{t-1}^\top \mathbf{W} \mathbf{h}_i$$

**Softmax normalization:**

$$\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T} \exp(e_{t,j})}$$

**Context vector:**

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \alpha_{t,i} \, \mathbf{h}_i$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a high-yield revision summary of all critical formulas for NLP within Deep Learning.

| Concept | Formula | Key Variable | Notes |
|---------|---------|--------------|-------|
