| **Embedding Lookup** | $\mathbf{e}_t = \mathbf{E}^\top \mathbf{1}_{x_t}$ | $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert \times d}$ | One-hot to dense vector |
| **Skip-gram Objective** | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t}\sum_{j \neq 0}\log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$ | $T$ = corpus length | Maximize log-probability |
| **GloVe Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i,j} f(X_{ij})(\mathbf{w}_i^\top \tilde{\mathbf{w}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij})^2$ | $X_{ij}$ = co-occurrence count | Weighted least squares |
| **RNN Hidden State** | $\mathbf{h}_t = \sigma_h(\mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | $\mathbf{W}$ = weight matrices | Sequential model |
| **LSTM Forget Gate** | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f[\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ | $\sigma$ = sigmoid | Range: $[0,1]$ |
| **LSTM Cell Update** | $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ | $\odot$ = Hadamard product | Long-term memory |
| **LSTM Output** | $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$ | Output gate $\mathbf{o}_t$ | Short-term output |
| **GRU Update Gate** | $\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_z[\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_z)$ | Controls retention | Simpler than LSTM |
| **GRU Reset Gate** | $\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_r[\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_r)$ | Controls forgetting | Resets prior state |
| **GRU Hidden State** | $\mathbf{h}_t = (1-\mathbf{z}_t)\odot\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t\odot\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$ | Interpolation | Linear interpolation |
| **Softmax** | $P(y_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j)}$ | Probability over classes | $\sum_i P(y_i) = 1$ |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | $y_i$ = true label | Standard NLP loss |
| **Attention Score (Bahdanau)** | $e_{t,i} = \mathbf{v}^\top \tanh(\mathbf{W}_s \mathbf{s}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_h \mathbf{h}_i)$ | Learnable $\mathbf{v}$ | Additive attention |
| **Attention Weight** | $\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_j \exp(e_{t,j})}$ | $\sum_i \alpha_{t,i} = 1$ | Normalized alignment |
| **Attention Context** | $\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} \mathbf{h}_i$ | Weighted sum | Dynamic context |

### 2.9 Engineering Utility of NLP Systems

| Application Domain | Engineering Use |
|--------------------|----------------|
| Healthcare | Clinical note analysis, drug interaction detection |
| Finance | News-based sentiment trading, fraud detection in claims |
| Customer Service | Chatbots, ticket classification, automated response |
| Legal | Contract clause extraction, precedent search |
| Education | Automated essay scoring, intelligent tutoring |
| Search Engines | Semantic search, query understanding |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) in RNNs

The RNN unrolls through time, so the loss at time $t$, denoted $\mathcal{L}_t$, depends on all previous hidden states $\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2, \dots, \mathbf{h}_t$. The total loss over the sequence is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$$

We want to compute the gradient of $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$$

The middle term captures how $\mathbf{h}_t$ depends on $\mathbf{h}_k$ through intermediate steps:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{W}_{hh}^\top \cdot \text{diag}\left(\sigma'(\cdot)\right)$$

Let $\lambda_{\max}$ denote the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. Then:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \right\| \approx \left(\lambda_{\max}\right)^{t-k}$$

**Case 1:** If $\lambda_{\max} > 1$, then as $t - k \to \infty$, the gradient magnitude grows exponentially → **exploding gradients**.

**Case 2:** If $\lambda_{\max} < 1$, then as $t - k \to \infty$, the gradient magnitude shrinks exponentially → **vanishing gradients**.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Fix:** **Gradient clipping** (for explosion) and **LSTM/GRU gating** (for vanishing) are the standard remedies. LSTMs effectively keep $\lambda_{\max}$ close to 1 through their additive cell-state updates.

## 3.2 Worked Example: LSTM Forward Pass on a Toy Sentence

Consider the sentence: *"The cat sat"* with vocabulary $\mathcal{V} = \{\text{The}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}\}$ and embedding dimension $d = 2$.

**Step 1: Tokenization & Indexing**
$$\text{The} \rightarrow 1, \quad \text{cat} \rightarrow 2, \quad \text{sat} \rightarrow 3$$

**Step 2: Embedding Lookup**

Suppose the embedding matrix $\mathbf{E}$ is:

$$\mathbf{E} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & -0.3 \\ 0.0 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{e}_{\text{The}} = [0.1, \; 0.2]$$
$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{e}_{\text{cat}} = [0.5, \; -0.3]$$
$$\mathbf{x}_3 = \mathbf{e}_{\text{sat}} = [0.0, \; 0.8]$$

**Step 3: Initialize Hidden and Cell States**

$$\mathbf{h}_0 = [0, 0], \quad \mathbf{C}_0 = [0, 0]$$

**Step 4: First LSTM Cell (t = 1, input "The")**

Assume concatenated weights for simplicity:
$$\mathbf{W}_f[\mathbf{h}_0, \mathbf{x}_1] + \mathbf{b}_f = [0.2, \; -0.1]$$
$$\mathbf{f}_1 = \sigma([0.2, -0.1]) = [0.55, \; 0.475]$$

Similarly compute other gates (assume simplified values):
$$\mathbf{i}_1 = [0.6, \; 0.5], \quad \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_1 = [0.3, \; -0.2], \quad \mathbf{o}_1 = [0.4, \; 0.55]$$

Apply cell state update:
$$\mathbf{C}_1 = \mathbf{f}_1 \odot \mathbf{C}_0 + \mathbf{i}_1 \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_1 = [0,0] + [0.6 \cdot 0.3, \; 0.5 \cdot (-0.2)] = [0.18, \; -0.10]$$

Apply output gate:
$$\mathbf{h}_1 = \mathbf{o}_1 \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_1) = [0.4, \; 0.55] \odot \tanh([0.18, \; -0.10]) \approx [0.4, \; 0.55] \odot [0.178, \; -0.0997] \approx [0.0713, \; -0.0548]$$

**Step 5: Repeat for $t = 2$ and $t = 3$**

This procedure is repeated sequentially, with each cell's hidden state feeding into the next. The final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_3$ encodes the entire sentence "The cat sat" in a fixed-size vector.

**Step 6: Output Layer**

If the task is sentiment classification with vocabulary size $V_{\text{out}} = 2$:
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{\text{out}} \mathbf{h}_3 + \mathbf{b}_{\text{out}})$$

> [!NOTE]
> This example illustrates that each LSTM cell **decides** what to retain or discard based on the input and prior state, making it possible to remember long-range dependencies.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield comparison frequently tested in KTU exams.

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---------|-------------|------|-----|
| **Gates** | None | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| **Cell State** | No (only hidden) | Yes ($\mathbf{C}_t$) | No (merged into $\mathbf{h}_t$) |
| **Parameters** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Severe | Mitigated | Mitigated |
| **Training Speed** | Fast | Slow | Faster than LSTM |
| **Long-Term Memory** | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| **Typical Use** | Short sequences | Long sequences, MT | Long sequences, lightweight |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level NLP Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText[Raw Text Corpus] --> tokenize[Tokenization]
    tokenize --> normalize[Normalization and Cleaning]
    normalize --> vocab[Vocabulary Mapping]
    vocab --> embed[Word Embedding Layer]
    embed --> model[Deep Learning Model RNN LSTM GRU or Transformer]
    model --> taskHead[Task Specific Output Layer]
    taskHead --> cls[Classification Softmax]
    taskHead --> gen[Text Generation]
    taskHead --> tag[Sequence Tagging]
    taskHead --> mt[Machine Translation]

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    class rawText,tokenize,normalize,vocab,embed input
    class model,taskHead process
    class cls,gen,tag,mt output
```

## 4.2 RNN Unrolled Through Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1[Input x1] --> rnn1[RNN Cell h1]
    h0[Initial h0] --> rnn1
    rnn1 --> y1[Output y1]
    rnn1 --> rnn2[RNN Cell h2]
    x2[Input x2] --> rnn2
    rnn2 --> y2[Output y2]
    rnn2 --> rnn3[RNN Cell h3]
    x3[Input x3] --> rnn3
    rnn3 --> y3[Output y3]
    rnn3 --> rnn4[RNN Cell h4]
    x4[Input x4] --> rnn4
    rnn4 --> y4[Output y4]

    classDef cell fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    class rnn1,rnn2,rnn3,rnn4 cell
    class x1,x2,x3,x4,y1,y2,y3,y4,h0 input
```

## 4.3 LSTM Cell Internal Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inHt[Previous h t-1] --> concat1[Concatenate]
    inXt[Current Input x t] --> concat1
    concat1 --> forgetGate[Forget Gate sigmoid]
    concat1 --> inputGate[Input Gate sigmoid]
    concat1 --> candGate[Tanh Candidate Cell]
    concat1 --> outputGate[Output Gate sigmoid]
    prevCt[Previous Cell C t-1] --> multiply1[Multiply elementwise]
    forgetGate --> multiply1
    candGate --> multiply2[Multiply elementwise]
    inputGate --> multiply2
    multiply1 --> add[Add]
    multiply2 --> add
    add --> newCt[New Cell State C t]
    newCt --> tanh1[Tanh]
    outputGate --> multiply3[Multiply]
    tanh1 --> multiply3
    multiply3 --> newHt[New Hidden State h t]

    classDef gate fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    classDef state fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    class forgetGate,inputGate,candGate,outputGate gate
    class newCt,newHt,prevCt,inHt,inXt state
```

## 4.4 Seq2Seq with Attention Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Encoder
        e1[Encoder h1] --> e2[Encoder h2]
        e2 --> e3[Encoder h3]
        e3 --> e4[Encoder h4]
    end
    subgraph Attention
        e1 --> attn[Attention Layer]
        e2 --> attn
        e3 --> attn
        e4 --> attn
        attn --> ctx[Context Vector c t]
    end
    subgraph Decoder
        dPrev[Decoder s t-1] --> dec[Decoder RNN LSTM GRU]
        ctx --> dec
        dec --> dOut[Output y t]
        dOut --> dNext[Decoder s t]
        dNext --> dec
    end

    classDef enc fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    classDef attn fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    class e1,e2,e3,e4 enc
    class attn,ctx attn
    class dPrev,dec,dOut,dNext dec
```

## 4.5 Embedding Space Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AnimalCluster[Animal Cluster]
        cat[cat] --> v1[0.6, 0.7]
        dog[dog] --> v2[0.55, 0.65]
        tiger[tiger] --> v3[0.7, 0.75]
    end
    subgraph FruitCluster[Fruit Cluster]
        apple[apple] --> v4[0.1, -0.6]
        banana[banana] --> v5[0.15, -0.55]
    end
    subgraph VerbCluster[Action Cluster]
        run[run] --> v6[-0.5, 0.4]
        eat[eat] --> v7[-0.55, 0.45]
    end

    classDef animal fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
    classDef fruit fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    classDef verb fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000

    class apple,banana,v4,v5 fruit
    class run,eat,v6,v7 verb
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, semantically related words cluster together in the 2D embedding space. For instance, "cat", "dog", and "tiger" (animals) are closer to each other than to "apple" (a fruit). This property emerges automatically from Word2Vec/GloVe training.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Word Embedding. How is it different from One-Hot Encoding? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Word Embedding** is a dense, low-dimensional, distributed vector representation of words learned from a corpus, where semantically similar words have similar vectors. Formally, it maps each word $w$ to a vector $\mathbf{e}_w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ where $d$ is small (e.g., 50–300).

**Differences from One-Hot Encoding:**

| Aspect | One-Hot Encoding | Word Embedding |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| **Dimensionality** | Equals vocabulary size $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ | Small fixed $d$ (e.g., 100) |
| **Sparsity** | Sparse (mostly zeros) | Dense |
| **Semantic similarity** | Not captured (orthogonal vectors) | Captured via cosine similarity |
| **Training** | Rule-based | Learned via Word2Vec/GloVe |

(3 marks)

---

### Question 2: What is the Vanishing Gradient Problem in RNNs? How does LSTM address it? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **Vanishing Gradient Problem** occurs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) in RNNs, where gradients shrink exponentially as they are multiplied repeatedly by the recurrent weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ across many time steps. This prevents the network from learning long-range dependencies because early-time-step parameters receive negligible updates.

**How LSTM addresses it:**
- The LSTM introduces a **cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$ with an **additive** update: $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$.
- This additive structure creates a more direct path for gradient flow, mitigating vanishing.
- The **forget gate** $\mathbf{f}_t$ controls how much of the past cell state is retained, allowing the network to preserve information over many time steps.

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Explain the architecture and working of LSTM with neat diagrams. Discuss how it overcomes the limitations of vanilla RNN. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Limitations of Vanilla RNN (2 Marks):**
- Suffers from vanishing and exploding gradients during BPTT.
- Cannot capture long-range dependencies in sequences.
- Standard activation functions squash gradients to small ranges.

**2. LSTM Architecture (3 Marks):**

The LSTM cell consists of three gates and a cell state:

- **Forget Gate $\mathbf{f}_t$:** Decides what to discard from the previous cell state.

$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$$

- **Input Gate $\mathbf{i}_t$:** Decides what new information to store.

$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$$
$$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_C[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C)$$

- **Cell State Update:**

$$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$$

- **Output Gate $\mathbf{o}_t$:** Decides what to output from the cell state.

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o)$$
$$\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$$

**3. How LSTM Overcomes RNN Limitations (2 Marks):**
- The additive update of $\mathbf{C}_t$ creates a near-linear gradient path, reducing vanishing gradients.
- Explicit gating allows the network to **selectively remember** or **forget** information, enabling long-term memory.

---

#### Part (b): With a neat diagram, explain the Seq2Seq model for machine translation. How does the attention mechanism improve it? (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Seq2Seq Architecture (3 Marks):**

A Seq2Seq model has two components:
- **Encoder:** An RNN/LSTM/GRU that reads the input sentence $(x_1, \dots, x_T)$ and compresses it into a **context vector** $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T$ (final hidden state).
- **Decoder:** Another RNN/LSTM/GRU that generates the output sequence $(y_1, \dots, y_{T'})$ one token at a time, conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens.

The decoder is trained to maximize $P(y_{1:T'} \mid x_{1:T})$.

**2. Limitation of Vanilla Seq2Seq (2 Marks):**
- A single fixed-size context vector $\mathbf{c}$ must encode the **entire** input sentence.
- For long sentences, this creates an **information bottleneck**, degrading translation quality.

**3. Attention Mechanism (2 Marks):**
- Attention allows the decoder to dynamically focus on different parts of the input sequence at each decoding step.
- At decoder step $t$, attention computes alignment scores $e_{t,i}$ between decoder state $\mathbf{s}_{t-1}$ and encoder states $\mathbf{h}_i$.
- These scores are softmax-normalized into weights $\alpha_{t,i}$, and a context vector $\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} \mathbf{h}_i$ is computed.
- The decoder then uses $\mathbf{c}_t$ to generate $y_t$, accessing all encoder states rather than only the final one.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a): Explain the Word2Vec model with CBOW and Skip-gram architectures. Discuss the objective functions. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Word2Vec Overview (1 Mark):**

Word2Vec is a shallow two-layer neural network proposed by Mikolov et al. (2013) that learns word embeddings such that words appearing in similar contexts have similar vector representations.

**2. CBOW Architecture (3 Marks):**

CBOW predicts the **target (center) word** from a window of **context words**.

Given context $(w_{t-c}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+c})$, CBOW maximizes:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CBOW}} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{t-c}, \dots, w_{t+c})$$

The context words are one-hot encoded, projected to embeddings (averaged), and passed through a softmax classifier.

**3. Skip-gram Architecture (3 Marks):**

Skip-gram does the **inverse**: it predicts the surrounding context words given a target word.

The objective is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Skip-gram}} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \leq j \leq c, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$

The conditional probability is computed via softmax:

$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert} \exp(\mathbf{v}_w^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$$

> **Note:** In practice, **Negative Sampling** or **Hierarchical Softmax** is used to make training tractable for large vocabularies.

---

#### Part (b): What is the GRU? Explain its architecture and gates. Compare it with LSTM. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. GRU Definition (1 Mark):**

The **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a simplified variant of the LSTM that combines the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** and merges the cell state with the hidden state.

**2. GRU Gates and Equations (4 Marks):**

- **Update Gate $\mathbf{z}_t$:** Determines how much past information to keep.

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_z[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_z)$$

- **Reset Gate $\mathbf{r}_t$:** Determines how much past information to forget.

$$\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_r[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_r)$$

- **Candidate Hidden State:**

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_h[\mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

- **Hidden State Update (Linear Interpolation):**

$$\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$$

**3. Comparison with LSTM (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|--------|------|-----|
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell state | Separate ($\mathbf{C}_t, \mathbf{h}_t$) | Merged ($\mathbf{h}_t$ only) |
| Parameters | More | Fewer |
| Training time | Slower | Faster |
| Performance on long sequences | Excellent | Comparable |

GRU is preferred when computational efficiency is critical and the dataset is small.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LSTM gates:** Many students swap the role of input and forget gates. Remember — the **forget gate** decides what to **discard**, the **input gate** decides what to **add**, and the **output gate** decides what to **emit**.
> 2. **Forgetting activation functions:** LSTM gates use **sigmoid** ($\sigma$) for the gates themselves, but the **candidate cell state** uses **tanh**. Students often mistakenly use sigmoid everywhere.
> 3. **Omitting dimensions in matrices:** When writing equations, KTU examiners expect clear dimensionality — e.g., state that $\mathbf{W}_f \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times (d_h + d_x)}$.
> 4. **Confusing CBOW and Skip-gram:** CBOW predicts target from context; Skip-gram predicts context from target. Many students reverse this.
> 5. **Skipping the vanishing gradient explanation:** When asked "why LSTM?", students must explicitly link to the **additive cell-state update**, not just say "LSTM is better."
> 6. **Missing diagrams:** KTU awards 2–3 marks for neat, labeled diagrams in 7-mark questions. Always include block diagrams for LSTM cells and Seq2Seq models.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — NLP in Deep Learning**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **NLP** = Computational processing of natural language using deep learning.
- **Tokenization → Embedding → Sequence Model → Output** is the canonical pipeline.
- **Word Embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe) replace sparse one-hot vectors with dense semantic vectors.

### 🔑 Key Architectures
- **RNN:** Sequential, but suffers from vanishing/exploding gradients.
- **LSTM:** Three gates (forget, input, output) + cell state; handles long-range dependencies.
- **GRU:** Two gates (update, reset); simpler and faster than LSTM.
- **Seq2Seq:** Encoder-decoder for variable-length input/output (e.g., machine translation).
- **Attention:** Allows decoder to dynamically focus on relevant encoder states.

### 🔑 High-Yield Formulas
- LSTM cell update: $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$
- GRU hidden state: $\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t)\odot\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$
- Attention weight: $\alpha_{t,i} = \dfrac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_j \exp(e_{t,j})}$

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Swapping LSTM gate definitions.
- ❌ Using sigmoid instead of tanh in candidate cell state.
- ❌ Confusing CBOW with Skip-gram direction.
- ❌ Omitting diagrams in 7-mark answers.

### 🔑 Exam Strategy
- Always **define → explain → illustrate with diagram → write equations → give example**.
- For 14-mark questions, split clearly into sub-parts (e.g., 7 + 7).
- Mention **advantages AND limitations** for full marks.
- Use **bullet points and labeled diagrams** — KTU examiners reward clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Research Areas: Autoencoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Research Areas: Autoencoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autoencoder (AE)** is a specialized class of artificial neural networks designed for unsupervised representation learning. It is trained to copy its input to its output through a **bottleneck architecture** that forces the network to learn a compressed, distributed encoding of the data. Formally, an autoencoder consists of two coupled functions:

- An **Encoder** function $\phi: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{H}$ that maps the input space $\mathcal{X}$ to a latent (hidden) representation space $\mathcal{H}$.
- A **Decoder** function $\psi: \mathcal{H} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ that reconstructs the input from the latent representation.

The training objective minimizes a **reconstruction loss** $L(x, \psi(\phi(x)))$ between the original input $x$ and the reconstructed output $\hat{x} = \psi(\phi(x))$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEIT633 Module 4):** Autoencoders are categorized as a research area in deep learning, emphasizing their role in unsupervised feature extraction, generative modeling, and dimensionality reduction. Mastery of variants (Vanilla, Sparse, Denoising, Variational) is mandatory for ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **literary translator** who receives a 500-page English novel and must produce a 5-page summary in English that captures the essence of the story. Later, a different person reads this 5-page summary and tries to reconstruct the original novel as closely as possible. The "summary" is the **latent representation** (the bottleneck), the original novel is the **input**, and the reconstructed novel is the **output**.

> [!NOTE]
> The genius of the autoencoder lies in the fact that the network *learns by itself* what information is most important to preserve. Unlike PCA (Principal Component Analysis), autoencoders can capture **non-linear relationships** in the data because of the multi-layer neural network architecture.

## 1.3 Why Autoencoders Matter

Autoencoders are central to modern deep learning research because they:

- **Learn unlabeled representations** — no class labels required during training.
- Enable **dimensionality reduction** superior to PCA in non-linear regimes.
- Form the backbone of **generative models** (e.g., VAEs, diffusion models).
- Power **anomaly detection** systems in industry (fraud, manufacturing defects).
- Enable **denoising** of corrupted signals (medical imaging, speech).

## 1.4 The Core Tension: Undercomplete vs. Overcomplete

| Property | Undercomplete AE | Overcomplete AE |
|---|---|---|
| **Latent dim** $h < x$ | Yes (compression forced) | No (regularization needed) |
| **Risk** | Trivial identity mapping | Identity mapping if unregularized |
| **Solution** | Bottleneck constraint | Sparsity / Denoising / Contractive penalties |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student misconception: *"An autoencoder just learns the identity function."* This is **only true** if the bottleneck is too wide or the network is too deep without regularization. Proper architectural constraints (narrow bottleneck, sparse penalty, or noise injection) prevent this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Standard Autoencoder

The autoencoder operates in **three logical stages**:

### Stage 1 — Encoding Phase
- The input vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is passed through the encoder network.
- Each layer applies a linear transformation followed by a non-linear activation:

$$h^{(l)} = \sigma(W^{(l)} h^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$$

- The final encoder layer produces the latent code $z = h^{(L)} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$, where typically $p < d$.

### Stage 2 — Bottleneck / Latent Representation
- The code $z$ resides in a lower-dimensional manifold, capturing the **most salient features** of $x$.
- This is where **information compression** happens.

### Stage 3 — Decoding Phase
- The decoder mirrors the encoder (often symmetrically):

$$\hat{x} = \sigma'(W'^{(L)} h'^{(L-1)} + b'^{(L)})$$

- The output $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the **reconstruction**.

### Stage 4 — Loss Computation & Backpropagation
- The reconstruction error is computed and gradients are backpropagated through both networks simultaneously.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Encoder map | $z = \phi(x) = \sigma(W_e x + b_e)$ | Maps input to latent code |
| Decoder map | $\hat{x} = \psi(z) = \sigma'(W_d z + b_d)$ | Reconstructs input from code |
| **MSE Loss** | $L_{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert\vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert\vert^{2}$ | Used for continuous data |
| **BCE Loss** | $L_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1-x_i)\log(1-\hat{x}_i)\right]$ | Used for binary / pixel data in [0,1] |
| Sparsity penalty | $\Omega(h) = \sum_{j} \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$ | Forces latent units to be inactive |
| Denoising loss | $L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert\vert x_i - \psi(\phi(\tilde{x}_i)) \vert\vert^{2}$ | Reconstruct clean from corrupted $\tilde{x}$ |
| Contractive loss | $L = L_{MSE} + \lambda \sum_{i}\Vert \nabla_x h_i \Vert^{2}$ | Penalizes Jacobian of encoder |
| **VAE ELBO** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z\mid x)}[\log p(x\mid z)] - D_{KL}(q(z\mid x) \Vert p(z))$ | Evidence Lower Bound |
| KL Divergence (Gaussian) | $D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\left(1 + \log \sigma_j^{2} - \mu_j^{2} - \sigma_j^{2}\right)$ | Regularizer for VAE |
| Reparameterization | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ | Enables backprop through sampling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical notation alert:** The table above uses `\vert\vert` (double vertical bar) to denote the L2 norm to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax with single `|` characters.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why a bottleneck?** Forces the network to discard redundant information and learn only the most discriminative features. Without compression, the network would simply memorize the input.
- **Why non-linear activations?** Linear encoder + linear decoder = equivalent to PCA. Non-linearities allow the network to learn curved, complex manifolds.
- **Why the sigmoid output for BCE?** Maps reconstruction values to $[0, 1]$, matching the probabilistic interpretation of binary cross-entropy.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Autoencoder Variant |
|---|---|---|
| **Computer Vision** | Image denoising, super-resolution | Denoising / Convolutional AE |
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor detection, MRI reconstruction | VAE |
| **Cybersecurity** | Anomaly detection in network traffic | Vanilla AE (reconstruction error threshold) |
| **NLP** | Document embedding, semantic hashing | Sparse AE |
| **Recommender Systems** | Collaborative filtering (AutoRec) | Autoencoder with rating matrix |
| **Generative AI** | Image synthesis, drug discovery | VAE, Beta-VAE |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reconstruction Loss (Mean Squared Error)

The **MSE loss** for an autoencoder is derived from the assumption that the reconstruction error follows a Gaussian distribution.

### Step 1: Likelihood Assumption
Assume the reconstruction $\hat{x}$ is generated from a Gaussian centered at $\psi(\phi(x))$ with variance $\sigma^{2}$:

$$p(x \mid z) = \mathcal{N}(x; \psi(z), \sigma^{2} I)$$

### Step 2: Log-Likelihood for n Samples
Take the natural log of the likelihood for a single sample:

$$\log p(x \mid z) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \Vert x - \psi(\phi(x)) \Vert^{2} - \frac{d}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^{2})$$

### Step 3: Negate to Form Loss
Since maximizing log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the negative log-likelihood, we drop constants (independent of parameters) to obtain:

$$L_{MSE}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^{2}$$

This is the **standard MSE reconstruction loss** used in continuous-valued autoencoders.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) ELBO

The VAE is a **probabilistic autoencoder**. Instead of encoding $x$ to a single point $z$, it encodes it to a **distribution** $q(z \mid x)$.

### Step 1: Variational Lower Bound
We want to maximize $\log p(x)$, the marginal likelihood of the data. Introduce the variational distribution $q(z \mid x)$:

$$\log p(x) = \log \int p(x, z) \, dz$$

### Step 2: Apply Jensen's Inequality
Using the concavity of $\log$:

$$\log p(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(z \mid x)}[\log p(x \mid z)] - D_{KL}(q(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))$$

This is the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**.

### Step 3: Decompose the KL Divergence
Assuming $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ (standard Gaussian prior) and $q(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu(x), \sigma^{2}(x) I)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{KL}(q(z \mid x) \Vert p(z)) &= \int q(z \mid x) \log \frac{q(z \mid x)}{p(z)} \, dz \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{p} \left(1 + \log \sigma_j^{2} - \mu_j^{2} - \sigma_j^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Final ELBO
Combining the reconstruction term (as a Gaussian) and the KL regularizer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{ELBO} &= \mathbb{E}_{q(z \mid x)}[\log p(x \mid z)] - D_{KL}(q(z \mid x) \Vert p(z)) \\
&\approx -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\left(\mu_j^{2} + \sigma_j^{2} - \log \sigma_j^{2} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This **two-term loss** is the heart of every VAE training.

## 3.3 The Reparameterization Trick Derivation

The challenge: we cannot backpropagate through a **random sampling** operation. Solution: move the randomness to an auxiliary variable $\epsilon$.

### Step 1: Original Sampling
$$z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2} I)$$

### Step 2: Reformulate
Let $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

### Step 3: Gradient Flow
Now the gradient with respect to the parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$ is differentiable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial z}{\partial \mu} &= 1 \\
\frac{\partial z}{\partial \sigma} &= \epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

This allows **end-to-end backpropagation** through the stochastic layer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Autoencoder Variants

| Variant | Latent Constraint | Key Penalty Term | Primary Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vanilla AE** | Dimension bottleneck $(p < d)$ | None (just reconstruction) | Dimensionality reduction |
| **Sparse AE** | Sparsity on activations | $\lambda \sum_j \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$ | Feature selection, interpretability |
| **Denoising AE** | Forced robustness | Reconstruction of clean $x$ from noisy $\tilde{x}$ | Image/signal denoising |
| **Contractive AE** | Local invariance | $\lambda \Vert \nabla_x h \Vert_{F}^{2}$ | Robust feature learning |
| **VAE** | Probabilistic bottleneck | KL divergence to prior $\mathcal{N}(0,I)$ | Generative modeling |
| **Convolutional AE** | Spatial hierarchy | MSE on feature maps | Image processing |
| **Adversarial AE** | Adversarial prior | GAN-style discriminator loss | Disentangled representations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When answering ESE questions on autoencoder variants, students frequently confuse the **sparsity penalty** (KL term) with the **VAE KL term** (divergence to prior). They serve different purposes — sparsity forces individual units to be inactive, while VAE KL forces the latent distribution to match a Gaussian.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Basic Autoencoder Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input X dim d] --> B[Encoder Layer 1]
    B --> C[Encoder Layer 2]
    C --> D[Latent Code Z dim p]
    D --> E[Decoder Layer 1]
    E --> F[Decoder Layer 2]
    F --> G[Reconstruction Xhat dim d]
    G --> H{Loss Computation}
    A --> H
    H --> I[Backpropagation to Encoder and Decoder]
```

## 4.2 Variational Autoencoder (VAE) Probabilistic Pipeline


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the role of the bottleneck layer in an autoencoder. Why is it essential for learning meaningful representations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bottleneck layer is the compressed latent representation $z \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ where $p < d$ (the input dimension). It is essential because:

1. **Information compression (1 mark):** It forces the encoder to discard redundant or noisy features and retain only the most informative aspects of the input.
2. **Non-linear feature learning (1 mark):** The bottleneck learns a lower-dimensional manifold that captures the intrinsic structure of high-dimensional data, similar to PCA but capable of non-linear mappings.
3. **Regularization (1 mark):** By constraining capacity, the bottleneck acts as an inductive bias, preventing the network from learning the trivial identity function $f(x) = x$.

Without the bottleneck, an autoencoder with sufficient capacity would simply memorize the input, defeating its purpose as a representation learner.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between a Vanilla Autoencoder and a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) in terms of latent space and training objective.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vanilla AE | Variational AE |
|---|---|---|
| **Latent space** (1.5 marks) | Deterministic point $z = \phi(x)$ | Probabilistic distribution $q(z\mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu(x), \sigma^{2}(x))$ |
| **Training objective** (1.5 marks) | Minimize $L = \Vert x - \psi(\phi(x)) \Vert^{2}$ (reconstruction only) | Maximize ELBO: reconstruction term + KL divergence to prior $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ |

The VAE enforces a structured, continuous latent space that enables **generation** of new samples by sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and decoding, whereas vanilla AEs cannot reliably generate new data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture and training procedure of a Denoising Autoencoder. Discuss how it differs from a standard autoencoder and why denoising acts as a regularizer.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for a Variational Autoencoder. Show explicitly how the KL divergence term arises for a Gaussian latent prior.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture (2 marks):**
A Denoising Autoencoder (DAE) has the same two-part architecture as a standard AE (encoder + decoder) but is trained on **corrupted inputs** with the objective of reconstructing the **clean original**.

**Training Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Given a clean sample $x$, generate a corrupted version $\tilde{x}$ by adding noise (e.g., Gaussian, masking, salt-and-pepper):

$$\tilde{x} = x + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2} I) \quad \text{or} \quad \tilde{x} = x \odot m, \quad m \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$$

2. Encode: $z = \phi(\tilde{x})$
3. Decode: $\hat{x} = \psi(z)$
4. Loss: $L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert x_i - \psi(\phi(\tilde{x}_i)) \Vert^{2}$

**Why Denoising Regularizes (2 marks):**
- The network cannot memorize $\tilde{x} \rightarrow \tilde{x}$ because the input is stochastic. It must learn the **manifold structure** of the clean data to map noisy variants back to clean reconstructions.
- This forces the encoder to learn **robust features** invariant to small perturbations, equivalent to a form of **data augmentation** at the input level.
- Mathematically, the DAE approximates the score function $\nabla_x \log p_{\text{data}}(x)$, linking it to score-based generative models.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Goal: maximize $\log p(x)$ (log-marginal likelihood).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Introduce variational posterior $q(z \mid x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log p(x) &= \int q(z \mid x) \log p(x) \, dz \\
&= \int q(z \mid x) \log \frac{p(x, z)}{p(z \mid x)} \, dz \\
&= \int q(z \mid x) \log \frac{p(x, z) \, q(z \mid x)}{p(z \mid x) \, q(z \mid x)} \, dz \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{q(z \mid x)}[\log p(x \mid z)] - D_{KL}(q(z \mid x) \Vert p(z)) + D_{KL}(q(z \mid x) \Vert p(z \mid x))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The last KL term is non-negative. Drop it to get the lower bound (ELBO):

$$\log p(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(z \mid x)}[\log p(x \mid z)] - D_{KL}(q(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** For Gaussian latent $q(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu(x), \sigma^{2}(x) I)$ and standard normal prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\left(1 + \log \sigma_j^{2} - \mu_j^{2} - \sigma_j^{2}\right)$$

The final training objective to maximize is:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\left(\mu_j^{2} + \sigma_j^{2} - \log \sigma_j^{2} - 1\right)$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the concept of a Sparse Autoencoder. Explain how the KL-divergence-based sparsity penalty is derived and why it encourages interpretable features.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast autoencoders with Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Under what conditions does an autoencoder reduce to PCA? Explain with mathematical justification.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Concept of Sparse AE (2 marks):**
A Sparse Autoencoder imposes a **sparsity constraint** on the activations of the hidden layer, forcing most neurons to be inactive (zero) for any given input. This is done by penalizing the average activation $\hat{\rho}_j$ of each hidden unit $j$ to be close to a small target $\rho$ (e.g., 0.05).

**Derivation of KL Sparsity Penalty (3 marks):**
The average activation of unit $j$ over the training set is:

$$\hat{\rho}_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_j(x_i)$$

We want $\hat{\rho}_j \approx \rho$. Using Kullback-Leibler divergence between two Bernoulli random variables with success probabilities $\rho$ and $\hat{\rho}_j$:

$$\text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j) = \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1-\rho) \log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j}$$

This is **zero** when $\hat{\rho}_j = \rho$ and **positive** otherwise. The total sparsity penalty added to the reconstruction loss is:

$$L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{MSE}} + \beta \sum_{j=1}^{p} \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$$

**Why it encourages interpretable features (2 marks):**
- Most hidden units are silent for any given input, making the representation **disentangled**.
- Each active unit tends to specialize in detecting a specific feature (edge, texture, concept), making the model **interpretable**.
- Sparsity acts as an **information bottleneck**, forcing the network to use the fewest possible units to encode each input — analogous to sparse coding in neuroscience.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PCA | Autoencoder |
|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | Linear projection | Non-linear (with activations) |
| **Components** | Orthogonal eigenvectors | Arbitrary latent directions |
| **Loss** | Reconstruction (variance) | MSE / BCE / custom |
| **Capacity** | Limited to linear manifold | Can model curved manifolds |
| **Stacking** | Single projection | Deep encoder/decoder |

**When does AE reduce to PCA? (2 marks):**
An autoencoder reduces to PCA **if and only if**:
1. The encoder and decoder use **linear activations** (identity).
2. The loss function is **MSE**.
3. The latent dimension $p \leq d$.

**Mathematical Justification (2 marks):**
With linear encoder $z = W_e x$ and linear decoder $\hat{x} = W_d z = W_d W_e x$, the optimal solution that minimizes $\Vert x - W_d W_e x \Vert^{2}$ subject to orthogonality constraints recovers the **eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix** — which is exactly the principal components of PCA. Thus PCA is a **special linear case** of the autoencoder family.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing reconstruction error with classification accuracy (–1 mark):** Autoencoders do *not* use labels. Students who compute "accuracy" lose marks.
> 2. **Missing the KL term in VAE derivations (–2 marks):** The ELBO has TWO terms; presenting only the reconstruction loss is incomplete.
> 3. **Forgetting the reparameterization trick (–1.5 marks):** VAE backpropagation is impossible without it. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Stating "autoencoder = PCA" (–1 mark):** Only true under linear, single-layer, MSE conditions. Always state the conditions explicitly.
> 5. **Not mentioning the bottleneck role in sparse AEs (–1 mark):** Sparsity regularizes *overcomplete* networks, where the bottleneck alone is insufficient.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [x] **Autoencoder = Encoder + Bottleneck + Decoder** trained to reconstruct input.
- [x] **Undercomplete AE** uses dimension bottleneck $(p < d)$ to force compression.
- [x] **Loss function:** MSE for continuous, BCE for binary/pixel data.
- [x] **Linear AE with MSE = PCA.** Non-linear activations unlock complex manifold learning.
- [x] **Sparse AE:** Adds KL penalty $\text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$ to enforce activation sparsity.
- [x] **Denoising AE:** Trains on corrupted $\tilde{x}$, reconstructs clean $x$ — acts as regularizer + score function approximator.
- [x] **Contractive AE:** Penalizes $\Vert \nabla_x h \Vert_{F}^{2}$ for local smoothness of encoder.
- [x] **VAE latent:** $q(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$, not a point.
- [x] **VAE loss = Reconstruction + KL divergence** (ELBO).
- [x] **Reparameterization trick:** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ enables backprop.
- [x] **Applications:** Denoising, anomaly detection, generative modeling, dimensionality reduction.
- [x] **Generative capability:** Only probabilistic AEs (VAE, AAE) can generate new samples reliably.
- [x] **Industrial use cases:** Medical imaging, fraud detection, recommender systems, NLP embeddings.
