topics.title: Representation Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Representation Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Representation Learning (Feature Learning)** is a set of techniques in machine learning that allow a system to automatically discover the optimal representations (features) required for tasks such as classification, regression, or generation, directly from raw input data (e.g., pixels, audio waveforms, or text tokens), without relying on hand-crafted, manually engineered features.

Mathematically, a *representation* is a function $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ that maps an input $x \in \mathcal{X}$ from the raw data space into a feature (latent) space $\mathcal{Z} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, parameterized by $\theta$, such that the downstream task (e.g., classification) becomes significantly easier to solve in $\mathcal{Z}$ than in $\mathcal{X}$.

According to the canonical *Deep Learning* textbook (Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville, 2016), representation learning is positioned as the central problem of deep learning: replacing the traditional pipeline of `raw data → manual feature engineering → classifier` with an end-to-end pipeline of `raw data → learned features + classifier`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A representation is *good* if it makes the underlying explanatory factors of variation in the data **more explicit** and the irrelevant nuisance factors **more invariant**, thereby easing downstream discrimination.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Coffee Sommelier" vs. the "Barista"**
>
> Imagine teaching a machine to recognize coffee beans.
>
> - **Hand-crafted features (Old ML):** A human engineer manually measures color, weight, and bean length, then feeds these numbers to a classifier. This is like a barista who *memorizes a checklist* of bean attributes for every single variety.
> - **Representation Learning (Deep Learning):** The system is shown millions of coffee bean images and *autonomously discovers* that the meaningful factors are roast level, origin altitude, and bean geometry — encoding them as a compact numerical vector. This is the *coffee sommelier*, who has internalized an intuitive "feature space" through experience.
>
> The sommelier's vector $\mathbf{z}$ is the **representation**.

## 1.3 Why Representation Learning Matters

| Aspect | Hand-crafted Features | Learned Representations |
|---|---|---|
| Scalability | Poor — requires domain experts | Excellent — scales with data |
| Performance ceiling | Limited by human ingenuity | Scales with model capacity |
| Domain transfer | Brittle | Naturally transferable |
| Information preservation | Often lossy | Tunable via loss function |

## 1.4 Categories of Representation Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Be ready to differentiate these three paradigms as they appear in **Part A (3-mark)** questions frequently.

1. **Supervised Representation Learning** — Representations learned using label information (e.g., training a CNN classifier; the penultimate-layer activations are the representations).
2. **Unsupervised Representation Learning** — Representations learned from unlabeled data (e.g., **PCA**, **K-Means**, **Autoencoders**).
3. **Self-Supervised Representation Learning (SSRL)** — A label is *automatically generated from the data itself* (e.g., predicting a masked word in a sentence, or predicting the rotation angle of an image). This is the foundation of modern **foundation models** like BERT and SimCLR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Axioms of a Good Representation (Bengio et al., 2013)

According to the foundational paper *"Representation Learning: A Review and New Perspectives"* (Bengio, Courville, Vincent, 2013), a learned representation should ideally satisfy the following properties. These are **direct KTU syllabus items**.

1. **Smoothness / Local Continuity** — Semantically similar inputs $x_1 \approx x_2$ should map to nearby points in $\mathcal{Z}$. Formally: if $d_{\mathcal{X}}(x_1, x_2)$ is small, then $d_{\mathcal{Z}}(f(x_1), f(x_2))$ should also be small.
2. **Multiple Explanatory Factors** — The representation should disentangle the independent generative factors of variation.
3. **Hierarchical Organization of Explanatory Factors** — Low-level features (edges, phonemes) combine into high-level concepts (objects, words).
4. **Semi-Supervised Friendly** — The same representation should be useful for many downstream tasks, not just one.
5. **Manifold Hypothesis** — High-dimensional data ($x \in \mathbb{R}^p$, $p$ large) actually lies near a low-dimensional manifold $\mathcal{M} \subset \mathbb{R}^p$. The representation should compress onto this manifold.

## 2.2 Symbolic vs. Distributed Representations

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic 7-mark exam question** topic.

| Feature | Symbolic (One-Hot) | Distributed (Dense) |
|---|---|---|
| Dimension | Equals vocabulary size $N$ | Small (50–1024) |
| Sparsity | Extreme (one "1", rest "0") | Dense (all non-zero) |
| Semantic geometry | None — all vectors orthogonal | Similar concepts are close |
| Generalization | Poor (no interpolation) | Excellent (linear interpolation works) |
| Example | $\text{cat} = [0,0,1,0,\ldots,0]$ | $\text{cat} \approx [0.21, -0.55, 0.83, \ldots]$ |

**Why distributed works:** It exploits *statistical regularities*. The features in $\mathbf{z}$ can be reused across many examples, giving an **exponential gain** in representational efficiency — a concept formalized in Bengio's "curse of dimensionality" argument.

## 2.3 Mathematical Framework

Let the input be $x \in \mathbb{R}^p$ and the representation be $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with $d \ll p$.

$$z = f_\theta(x), \quad \hat{y} = g_\phi(z)$$

The **end-to-end objective** is:

$$\min_{\theta, \phi} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \; \mathcal{L}\bigl(g_\phi(f_\theta(x)),\; y\bigr) + \lambda \, \Omega(\theta)$$

where:
- $\mathcal{L}$ is the task loss (e.g., cross-entropy).
- $\Omega(\theta)$ is a **representation regularizer** (e.g., sparsity, orthogonality, disentanglement penalty).
- $\lambda$ is the regularization weight.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most important revision artifact for the exam. Memorize the equations and their boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Encoder map** | $z = f_\theta(x) = \sigma(Wx + b)$ | $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times p}$, $\sigma$ is non-linearity |
| 2 | **Reconstruction** | $\hat{x} = g_\phi(z) = \sigma'(W'z + b')$ | Autoencoder output |
| 3 | **Reconstruction loss (MSE)** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert_2^2$ | Mean-squared error |
| 4 | **Sparsity penalty (KL)** | $\Omega_{sparse} = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \mathrm{KL}\!\left(\rho \, \Vert \, \hat{\rho}_j\right)$ | $\rho$ is target activation, $\hat{\rho}_j$ is empirical |
| 5 | **Contrastive loss** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cont}} = -\log \frac{\exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_{k=1}^{2B}\mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]} \exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)}$ | NT-Xent, used in SimCLR, $\tau$ is temperature |
| 6 | **InfoNCE bound** | $I(z; c) \geq \log(N) - \mathcal{L}_{\text{NCE}}$ | Mutual information lower bound |
| 7 | **PCA reconstruction** | $\min_{U: U^\top U = I_d} \Vert X - XUU^\top \Vert_F^2$ | $U$ contains top-$d$ eigenvectors |
| 8 | **Word2Vec SG loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\log \frac{\exp(v_c^\top v_o)}{\sum_{w \in V}\exp(v_c^\top v_w)}$ | $v_c, v_o$ are center / outside word vectors |
| 9 | **Transfer-learning objective** | $\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{S}(\theta) + \mu \, \mathcal{L}_{T}(\theta)$ | $S$ = source, $T$ = target, $\mu$ is mixing weight |
| 10 | **Disentanglement (TC)** | $\mathrm{TC} = \mathrm{KL}\!\bigl(q(z) \Vert \prod_j q(z_j)\bigr)$ | Total Correlation, $\beta$-VAE family |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision:** Self-supervised representations (DINO, MAE) reduce labelled data requirements by **10×–100×**.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Word2Vec, GloVe, and contextual embeddings (BERT) revolutionized transfer learning.
- **Speech:** wav2vec 2.0 learns phonetic representations from raw audio.
- **Healthcare & Industry:** Sparse representations from autoencoders detect anomalies in ECG, IoT sensor streams, and manufacturing defects where labelled data is scarce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PCA as Linear Representation Learning

**Problem Setup:** Given data $X = [x_1, \ldots, x_N] \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times N}$ (centred so $\bar{x} = 0$), find the linear encoder $z_i = W^\top x_i$ with $W \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times d}$, $W^\top W = I_d$, that preserves maximum variance.

**Step 1 — Define the objective.**
The projection is $\hat{X} = W W^\top X$, and the reconstruction error is:

$$J(W) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - W W^\top x_i \Vert_2^2 = \frac{1}{N} \Vert X - W W^\top X \Vert_F^2$$

**Step 2 — Use the trace identity.**
For any matrix $A$ with orthonormal columns, $\Vert A - AA^\top A \Vert_F^2 = \mathrm{tr}(A^\top A) - \mathrm{tr}(A^\top A A^\top A)$. Applying this:

$$J(W) = \mathrm{tr}(X^\top X) - \mathrm{tr}(W^\top X X^\top W)$$

**Step 3 — Maximize the preserved variance.**
Since $\mathrm{tr}(X^\top X)$ is a constant, minimizing $J(W)$ is equivalent to:

$$\max_W \; \mathrm{tr}\!\left(W^\top X X^\top W\right) \quad \text{subject to} \quad W^\top W = I_d$$

**Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(W, \Lambda) = \mathrm{tr}(W^\top X X^\top W) - \mathrm{tr}\!\left(\Lambda^\top (W^\top W - I_d)\right)$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. $W$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = 2 X X^\top W - 2 W \Lambda = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; X X^\top W = W \Lambda$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**
$W$ is the matrix whose columns are the **top $d$ eigenvectors of the covariance matrix** $S = \frac{1}{N} X X^\top$, and $\Lambda$ is the diagonal matrix of the corresponding eigenvalues (variances).

**Final KTU-ready result:**

$$z_i = W^\top x_i, \quad W = [v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_d], \quad S v_k = \lambda_k v_k$$

> **Significance:** PCA is the *simplest* linear representation learner; modern deep methods are its non-linear descendants.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reconstruction Loss of a Linear Autoencoder

**Setup:** Single-layer linear autoencoder: $z = W_1 x$, $\hat{x} = W_2 z$ with $W_1, W_2$ unconstrained.

**Step 1 — Substitute.**
$\hat{x} = W_2 W_1 x$, so the effective map is $\hat{x} = P x$ with $P = W_2 W_1$.

**Step 2 — Squared error loss.**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \Vert x_i - P x_i \Vert_2^2 = \frac{1}{N} \Vert X - P X \Vert_F^2$$

**Step 3 — Optimality condition via calculus.**
Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P} = 0$ yields $P X X^\top = X X^\top$, i.e., $P$ is a projection onto the column space of $X$.

**Step 4 — Optimal $P$ is the PCA projector.**

$$P^* = U_d U_d^\top$$

where $U_d$ contains the top $d$ eigenvectors of $X X^\top$. Thus **a linear autoencoder with bottleneck $d$ is mathematically equivalent to PCA**, but PCA gives an explicit, closed-form, orthonormal solution, whereas the autoencoder discovers the same span via gradient descent.

> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly state "autoencoders are non-linear PCA." A *linear* autoencoder with bottleneck is exactly PCA; only *non-linear* encoders extend beyond PCA.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Contrastive Loss Gradient (NT-Xent)

The NT-Xent loss used in SimCLR is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{i,j} = -\log \frac{\exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_{k=1}^{2B} \mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]} \exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)}$$

with $\mathrm{sim}(u, v) = u^\top v / \Vert u \Vert \Vert v \Vert$.

**Step 1 — Expand the log.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{i,j} = -\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_j)/\tau + \log \sum_{k \neq i} \exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)$$

**Step 2 — Gradient w.r.t. $z_i$.**
Using $\frac{\partial}{\partial z_i} \mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k) = \frac{1}{\tau}\bigl(\bar{z}_k - \mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k)\bar{z}_i\bigr)$ where $\bar{z} = z / \Vert z \Vert$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{i,j}}{\partial z_i} = -\frac{1}{\tau}\Bigl[ \bar{z}_j - \mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_j)\bar{z}_i \Bigr] + \frac{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)\bigl(\bar{z}_k - \mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k)\bar{z}_i\bigr)}{\tau \sum_{k \neq i}\exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
The gradient has **two opposing forces**:
- An **attractive** force pulling $z_i$ toward its positive pair $z_j$ (the numerator term).
- A **repulsive** force pushing $z_i$ away from all $2B - 2$ negative samples (the denominator term).

**Geometrically**, this carves out a hyperspherical embedding space where semantically similar samples cluster together — a learned **representation manifold**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Skip-Gram Word2Vec (Negative Sampling)

**Setup:** Predict context words $w_o$ given a center word $w_c$ using embedding vectors $v_c, v_o \in \mathbb{R}^d$. Sample $K$ negative words $w_k$ from a unigram distribution $P_n(w) \propto U(w)^{3/4}$.

$$\mathcal{L} = -\log \sigma(v_c^\top v_o) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log \sigma(-v_c^\top v_k)$$

**Step 1 — Gradient for the positive sample.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v_c} = \bigl(\sigma(v_c^\top v_o) - 1\bigr) v_o$$

**Step 2 — Gradient for a negative sample.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v_c} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma(v_c^\top v_k) v_k$$

**Step 3 — Combined update rule.**

$$v_c \leftarrow v_c - \eta \left[ \bigl(\sigma(v_c^\top v_o) - 1\bigr) v_o + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma(v_c^\top v_k) v_k \right]$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic result.**
At convergence, the embedding inner product satisfies:

$$v_c^\top v_o \approx \log\!\left(\frac{P(w_o \mid w_c)}{P(w_o)}\right) - \log K$$

This recovers a shifted **Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)** matrix, proving that **Word2Vec implicitly factorizes the word-context PMI matrix** — a celebrated theoretical result (Levy & Goldberg, 2014).

> **KTU Importance:** This derivation is a *favorite 14-mark question* because it links representation theory to a concrete algorithm.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Representation Learning Method

| Scenario | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Small labelled data, large unlabelled | Self-supervised (e.g., SimCLR) | Leverages free labels from data |
| Sequential / text data | Contrastive sentence embeddings (SimCSE) | Captures semantic similarity |
| Need to detect anomalies | Sparse / denoising autoencoder | Robust to noise |
| Need interpretable factors | $\beta$-VAE, FactorVAE | Enforces disentanglement |
| Need to transfer across tasks | Pre-trained foundation model | Reusable features |
| Tabular, low-dim data | PCA, Kernel PCA | Closed-form, fast, interpretable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Representation Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Input X] --> B[Preprocessing and Augmentation]
    B --> C[Encoder Network f theta]
    C --> D[Latent Representation Z]
    D --> E[Task Head g phi]
    E --> F[Prediction y hat]
    F --> G[Loss L]
    G -->|Backpropagation| C
    G -->|Backpropagation| E
    H[Representation Regularizer Omega] -->|Constraint| D
```

**Reading the diagram:** The encoder $f_\theta$ compresses raw input $X$ into a latent vector $Z$. The task head $g_\phi$ produces the prediction $\hat{y}$. The total loss combines task loss $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ with a representation regularizer $\Omega(Z)$, and gradients are back-propagated through the entire graph.

## 4.2 Autoencoder as a Representation Learner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Image X] --> E1[Encoder Layer 1]
    E1 --> E2[Encoder Layer 2]
    E2 --> E3[Encoder Layer 3]
    E3 --> Z[Bottleneck Latent Z]
    Z --> D1[Decoder Layer 1]
    D1 --> D2[Decoder Layer 2]
    D2 --> D3[Decoder Layer 3]
    D3 --> XHAT[Reconstruction X hat]
    XHAT --> LOSS[Reconstruction Loss MSE]
    Z --> DOWN[Downstream Classifier]
    DOWN --> Y[Class Label Y]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The encoder compresses the input into a low-dimensional latent code $Z$. The decoder tries to reconstruct the input. The latent $Z$ — *not the reconstruction* — is the representation that is fed into downstream classifiers. The bottleneck forces $Z$ to discard nuisance information and retain only the most semantically meaningful factors.

## 4.3 Self-Supervised Representation Learning (Pretext + Downstream)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unlabelled Image X] --> P[Pretext Task]
    P -->|e.g. rotation prediction| PL[Pseudo Label Y pseudo]
    PL --> T1[Train Encoder f theta on pretext]
    T1 --> FR[Learned Representation Z]
    FR --> FRZ[Freeze or fine tune]
    FRZ --> T2[Train Classifier on small labelled set]
    T2 --> OUT[Final Model]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Self-supervised learning first creates a free "pretext" task from the data (e.g., rotation, jigsaw, masked patches). After training on the pretext, the encoder produces reusable features $Z$ that are then fine-tuned on a small labelled dataset for the target task.

## 4.4 Transfer Learning Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Source Domain Large Labelled Dataset] --> SRCNET[Source Model CNN]
    SRCNET -->|Extract| F1[Pre trained Encoder E]
    F1 --> FT1[Option A Freeze E and train new head]
    F1 --> FT2[Option B Fine tune E end to end]
    FT1 --> TGTNET[Target Model on small target dataset]
    FT2 --> TGTNET
    DST[Target Domain Small Labelled Dataset] --> TGTNET
    TGTNET --> PRED[Predictions on Target Task]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The encoder $E$ is first trained on a large source domain. It is then either *frozen* (feature extraction) or *fine-tuned* (parameter adjustment) on a small target dataset, transferring the learned representation across domains.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define representation learning. How does it differ from traditional feature engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

Representation learning is a class of machine learning techniques that *automatically* discover the optimal features (representations) needed for a task directly from raw data, without requiring manual feature engineering.

**Key differences:**

| Aspect | Traditional Feature Engineering | Representation Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Feature design | Hand-crafted by domain expert | Learned automatically by model |
| Adaptability | Rigid, task-specific | Flexible, transferable |
| Data efficiency | Works with less data | Scales with more data |
| Example | SIFT, HOG for images | CNN penultimate-layer activations |

A classic example is replacing hand-crafted Gabor filters in early computer vision with learned convolutional filters trained end-to-end via back-propagation.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What are the desirable properties of a good representation? List any four.**

**Model Answer (4 properties, each carrying 0.75 marks):**

1. **Smoothness** — Similar inputs map to similar points in feature space.
2. **Disentanglement / Multiple Explanatory Factors** — Independent generative factors are separated in different dimensions.
3. **Hierarchical Composition** — Low-level features compose into high-level concepts.
4. **Invariance** — Insensitive to nuisance transformations (e.g., illumination, translation).
5. **Manifold Compliance** — Maps high-dimensional data onto its low-dimensional manifold.
6. **Transferability** — Useful across multiple downstream tasks (semi-supervised friendliness).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question is **14 marks** and is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. *Internal choice* is mandatory — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **distributed representations**. Discuss how they overcome the curse of dimensionality compared to one-hot (symbolic) representations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **autoencoder** as a representation learner. Derive the reconstruction loss and discuss how the bottleneck enforces feature learning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Distributed Representations

**Definition (1 mark):** A *distributed representation* is one in which each concept is represented by a pattern of activations across many elements of a vector, and each element participates in representing many concepts. Formally, an input $x$ is encoded as $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$ where each $z_j$ can be non-zero and the vector has semantic geometry.

**Comparison with one-hot (3 marks):**

- One-hot: $z \in \{0, 1\}^N$ where $N = \mid V \mid$ (vocabulary size). Only one entry is 1.
- Distributed: $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with $d \ll N$, and **all entries are real-valued**.

**Curse of dimensionality resolution (2 marks):**
A one-hot representation distinguishes $N$ symbols using $N$ parameters. A distributed one with $d$ dimensions and $k$ simultaneously active features can represent $\binom{d}{k}$ distinct concepts — *exponential* in $d$. This is Bengio's central argument in *"Representation Learning: A Review and New Perspectives"* (2013).

**Example (1 mark):**
- One-hot: $\text{cat} = [0,0,1,0,0,0,\ldots,0]$
- Distributed (e.g., Word2Vec): $\text{cat} \approx [0.21, -0.55, 0.83, 0.12, -0.34, \ldots]$

Semantic relations like $v_{\text{king}} - v_{\text{man}} + v_{\text{woman}} \approx v_{\text{queen}}$ emerge from this geometry.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Autoencoder Architecture and Derivation

**Block diagram (2 marks):** See the **Autoencoder Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2** — input → encoder layers → bottleneck latent $Z$ → decoder layers → reconstruction $\hat{x}$.

**Architecture (1 mark):** An autoencoder is a neural network trained to copy its input to its output via a low-dimensional bottleneck. It has two parts: encoder $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ and decoder $g_\phi : \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$.

**Derivation of reconstruction loss (3 marks):**
The mean-squared error (MSE) loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}}(\theta, \phi) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - g_\phi(f_\theta(x_i)) \Vert_2^2$$

This is minimized by stochastic gradient descent. The *bottleneck constraint* (dimensionality of $Z$ being smaller than that of $X$) prevents the network from learning a trivial identity map. Instead, the network is forced to learn a **compressed code** that retains only the most informative factors of variation in $X$ — the definition of a useful representation.

**Why bottleneck matters (1 mark):** Without the bottleneck, an autoencoder with sufficient capacity simply memorizes the identity function. The bottleneck acts as an **information-theoretic constraint** that forces the model to prioritize the factors of variation that maximize reconstruction fidelity, effectively performing non-linear PCA in the over-complete regime.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Discuss **self-supervised representation learning** in detail. How does it differ from supervised and unsupervised paradigms? Provide two real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **transfer learning** and **domain adaptation** as applications of representation learning. Discuss the role of the pre-trained encoder and the fine-tuning strategy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Self-Supervised Representation Learning

**Definition (1 mark):** Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a paradigm where supervisory signals are *automatically generated from the data itself*, by formulating a "pretext" task that does not require human annotation.

**Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Paradigm | Supervision Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Supervised | Human labels | ImageNet classification |
| Unsupervised | None (cluster / density) | K-Means, PCA |
| Self-Supervised | Pseudo-labels from data | Masked word prediction (BERT), rotation prediction (RotNet) |

**Pretext task examples (2 marks):**
- **Context prediction:** Given a patch, predict its neighbors (Doersch et al., 2015).
- **Rotation prediction:** Rotate by $\{0°, 90°, 180°, 270°\}$ and predict the angle.
- **Masked modelling:** Mask 15% of tokens and predict them (BERT); mask image patches and reconstruct them (MAE).
- **Contrastive:** Pull augmented views of the same image together, push different images apart (SimCLR, MoCo).

**Real-world applications (2 marks):**
1. **BERT / GPT for NLP** — Learns contextual word representations from massive unlabelled text, then fine-tuned for QA, sentiment, NER.
2. **MAE / DINO for Vision** — Learns visual features from ImageNet without labels, then fine-tuned for detection, segmentation with 10× less labelled data.
3. **wav2vec 2.0 for Speech** — Learns phonetic representations from raw audio, fine-tuned for ASR with minimal transcribed data.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Transfer Learning and Domain Adaptation

**Definitions (1.5 marks):**
- **Transfer Learning** is the reuse of a model trained on a *source task* (typically with abundant data) as a starting point for a *target task* (with limited data).
- **Domain Adaptation** is a special case where the task is the same but the input distribution shifts (e.g., synthetic to real images).

**Role of the pre-trained encoder (2 marks):** The early layers of deep networks learn *generic* features (edges, textures, motifs), which are universally useful. The pre-trained encoder provides a high-quality initialization in the inductive bias landscape, dramatically reducing the data and compute needed for the target task.

**Fine-tuning strategies (2.5 marks):**
1. **Feature Extraction (Freeze):** Freeze encoder weights $E$, train only a new classification head $h$.
2. **Fine-Tuning:** Unfreeze the encoder and update all weights with a small learning rate.
3. **Discriminative Learning Rates:** Use a smaller learning rate for early layers, larger for later layers.
4. **Adapter Modules:** Insert small trainable modules inside a frozen encoder (parameter-efficient transfer).
5. **LoRA / Prompt Tuning:** Train a tiny set of low-rank parameters instead of the full encoder.

**Mathematical objective (1 mark):**

$$\min_{\theta_E, \theta_h} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}\!\bigl(h_{\theta_h}(E_{\theta_E}(x)), y\bigr)$$

where $E_{\theta_E}$ is initialized from the source pre-trained weights and either frozen or fine-tuned.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "linear autoencoder" with "non-linear PCA".** A *linear* autoencoder is *exactly* PCA. Only non-linear activations (ReLU, sigmoid) extend it beyond PCA. Students often write the reverse and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the bottleneck dimension.** When asked why an autoencoder learns features, the answer must mention the *bottleneck constraint*, not just "it reconstructs the input."
> 3. **Missing the "learned vs. hand-crafted" distinction.** When defining representation learning, always explicitly contrast it with manual feature engineering — a 1-mark trigger examiners watch for.
> 4. **Ignoring the manifold hypothesis.** Bengio's 2013 paper explicitly lists 4–5 axioms of good representations. A complete answer must cite at least 3.
> 5. **Writing "autoencoders are unsupervised"** — autoencoders are *self-supervised* (the input is its own target). The wording matters for marks.
> 6. **In Part B sub-questions**, students often write 4 pages for the 7-mark part and 1 page for the other — examiners allocate marks per sub-part, so imbalance is the most common reason for partial scores.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Representation Learning (Module 4):**

- [x] **Definition:** Representation Learning = automatically discovering features from raw data, replacing manual feature engineering.
- [x] **Three paradigms:** Supervised, Unsupervised, **Self-Supervised** (the modern winner).
- [x] **Axioms of a good representation** (cite at least 3 of 5 in exams): smoothness, multiple explanatory factors, hierarchical, semi-supervised, manifold.
- [x] **Symbolic vs Distributed:** Symbolic is one-hot, $O(N)$ parameters, no semantic geometry. Distributed is dense, $O(d)$ with $d \ll N$, captures semantic similarity.
- [x] **Exponential gain** of distributed representations over one-hot comes from $k$-active features in $d$ dimensions giving $\binom{d}{k}$ combinations.
- [x] **Autoencoder** = encoder $f_\theta$ + decoder $g_\phi$ + bottleneck $Z$. Loss = MSE reconstruction. Bottleneck prevents identity learning.
- [x] **Linear autoencoder == PCA** (closed-form eigendecomposition vs gradient descent).
- [x] **Self-supervised pretext tasks:** masked modelling, rotation prediction, contrastive (SimCLR), jigsaw.
- [x] **Contrastive loss (NT-Xent):** attractive + repulsive gradient forces; uses temperature $\tau$.
- [x] **InfoNCE** is a lower bound on mutual information $I(z; c)$.
- [x] **Word2Vec (Skip-Gram)** implicitly factorizes the **word-context PMI matrix** — Levy & Goldberg (2014).
- [x] **Transfer learning** has two strategies: **freeze** (feature extraction) and **fine-tune** (full network update). Pre-trained encoder provides inductive bias.
- [x] **Domain adaptation** = same task, different input distribution. **Multi-task learning** = one model, multiple related tasks.
- [x] **Disentanglement:** Total Correlation (TC) penalty in $\beta$-VAE family enforces factor separation.
- [x] **Manifold hypothesis:** high-dim data lies on a low-dim manifold; good representations compress onto it.
- [x] **Exam ready equations** to memorize: encoder $z = \sigma(Wx + b)$; MSE loss; contrastive loss; PCA objective $\max_W \mathrm{tr}(W^\top X X^\top W)$ s.t. $W^\top W = I_d$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boltzmann Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT633 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Course Code:** PEEIT633
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boltzmann Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Boltzmann Machine (BM)** is a type of stochastic, generative, energy-based neural network model that learns a probability distribution over its input data through unsupervised learning. It derives its name from the **Boltzmann distribution** (also known as the Gibbs distribution) in statistical mechanics, which governs the probability of a system being in a particular state as a function of its energy and temperature.

Mathematically, a Boltzmann Machine is a network of symmetrically connected, stochastic binary units (neurons) that are either **ON (state = 1)** or **OFF (state = 0)**. The network consists of two types of units:
- **Visible units ($\mathbf{v}$)**: These receive the input data and represent the observable features.
- **Hidden units ($\mathbf{h}$)**: These capture latent dependencies and higher-order correlations within the data that are not directly observed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Boltzmann Machine is fundamentally characterized by its **Energy Function** $E(\mathbf{x})$ and a **Probability Distribution** $p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}}{Z}$, where $T$ is the temperature parameter and $Z$ is the partition function (normalizing constant).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Dance Floor of Opinions

Imagine a large hall filled with people, each holding a small flag that is either **raised (1)** or **lowered (0)**. The room is a "Boltzmann party." Each person (unit) is influenced by their immediate neighbors — they tend to agree with friends (positive weights) and disagree with rivals (negative weights).

Now, a DJ (the **temperature parameter $T$**) controls the chaos:
- **Low Temperature ($T \to 0$)**: People are rigid and stuck in low-energy, high-agreement states. The crowd becomes orderly and predictable.
- **High Temperature ($T \to \infty$)**: People randomly flip their flags regardless of neighbors. The crowd is chaotic and noisy.

Over time, the crowd settles into patterns (states) that the whole network finds "comfortable" (low energy). The Boltzmann Machine does the same: it learns which joint configurations of visible and hidden units are statistically most likely, and it can then generate new samples from the learned distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of the Boltzmann Machine as a system that learns a "preference map" over possible configurations. Configurations the network likes (low energy) become probable, and configurations it dislikes (high energy) become rare.

### 1.3 Historical Context & Motivation

Boltzmann Machines were introduced by **Geoffrey Hinton, David Ackley, and Terrence Sejnowski** in **1985** as a learning algorithm for neural networks based on statistical mechanics. They were among the first models capable of:
- Learning **internal representations** (hidden units).
- Handling **incomplete data** (missing inputs).
- Performing **generative modeling** (creating new samples).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Boltzmann Machines Matter in Deep Learning:** BMs are the ancestors of **Deep Belief Networks (DBNs)** and **Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs)**, which revolutionized deep learning in 2006 by enabling efficient pre-training of deep networks — the breakthrough that triggered the modern deep learning era.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Visible Units** | Input/output units that interface with the environment/data. |
| **Hidden Units** | Internal feature detectors that learn latent patterns. |
| **Energy Function $E(\mathbf{x})$** | Scalar function assigning low values to "preferred" states. |
| **Boltzmann Distribution** | $p(\mathbf{x}) \propto e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}$; probability of state $\mathbf{x}$. |
| **Partition Function $Z$** | $\sum_{\mathbf{x}} e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}$; ensures probabilities sum to 1. |
| **Stochastic Unit** | Binary unit with probabilistic activation based on a sigmoid function. |
| **Symmetric Weights** | Connection weight $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$ (no self-loops: $w_{ii} = 0$). |
| **Contrastive Divergence (CD)** | Approximate, fast learning algorithm for BMs. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a General Boltzmann Machine

A general (unrestricted) Boltzmann Machine has a fully connected graph structure:
- Every visible unit is connected to every other visible unit, every hidden unit, and so on.
- The connection matrix $\mathbf{W}$ is **symmetric**: $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$.
- No self-connections: $w_{ii} = 0$.

This unrestricted connectivity makes exact inference and learning **computationally intractable** for large networks, because computing the partition function $Z$ requires summing over all $2^{N}$ possible states (where $N$ is the total number of units).

### 2.2 The Energy Function — The Heart of the Model

For a state vector $\mathbf{x} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ where each $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (or $\{-1, +1\}$ depending on the convention), the **energy function** is:

$$
E(\mathbf{x}) = -\sum_{i < j} w_{ij} x_i x_j - \sum_{i} b_i x_i
$$

Where:
- $w_{ij}$ is the symmetric weight between units $i$ and $j$.
- $b_i$ is the bias of unit $i$.
- The first term represents pairwise interactions.
- The second term represents individual unit biases (analogous to external fields in physics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Negative Signs?** Following the convention of statistical mechanics, configurations with **lower energy are more probable**. The negative sign in front of interactions and biases ensures that aligned units with positive weights lower the energy (preferred state), making them more probable under the Boltzmann distribution.

### 2.3 The Boltzmann Distribution

The probability of the network being in state $\mathbf{x}$ is given by the **Boltzmann (Gibbs) distribution**:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}}{Z} = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}}{\sum_{\mathbf{y}} e^{-E(\mathbf{y})/T}}
$$

Where:
- $T$ is the **temperature** (typically set to $T = 1$ in standard ML usage).
- $Z$ is the **partition function** ensuring $\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Physics:** This is identical to the probability of a physical system being in a state with energy $E$ at temperature $T$, as described by the Boltzmann distribution in statistical mechanics.

### 2.4 Activation Probability of a Single Unit

Given the states of all other units, the probability that unit $i$ will be ON (state = 1) is:

$$
p(x_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i}) = \sigma\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + \frac{b_i}{T}\right) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\left(\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + b_i\right)/T}}
$$

Where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the **logistic sigmoid function**:

$$
\sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Interpretation:** This is the famous "logistic activation" of a Boltzmann unit. Each unit independently decides its state based on the weighted sum of inputs from its neighbors, mapped through a sigmoid. As $T \to 0$, this becomes a deterministic step function; as $T \to \infty$, it approaches 0.5 (random coin flip).

### 2.5 Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) — The Tractable Special Case

A **Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM)** restricts the general BM such that:
- **No connections within the visible layer** (no $v_i \leftrightarrow v_j$ links).
- **No connections within the hidden layer** (no $h_j \leftrightarrow h_k$ links).
- Only **bipartite connections** between visible and hidden units exist.

This restriction enables efficient **block Gibbs sampling** and exact computation of conditional probabilities:

$$
p(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{i} w_{ij} v_i + b_j\right)
$$

$$
p(v_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{j} w_{ij} h_j + a_i\right)
$$

Where $a_i$ and $b_j$ are the biases for visible and hidden units respectively.

### 2.6 The Learning Rule

The general Boltzmann Machine learning rule maximizes the **log-likelihood** of the training data. The gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to a weight $w_{ij}$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \log p(\mathbf{v})}{\partial w_{ij}} = \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}}
$$

Where:
- $\langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{data}}$ is the expected value of $x_i x_j$ when the visible units are clamped to a training sample and the network reaches thermal equilibrium (the **positive phase**).
- $\langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}}$ is the expected value when the network runs freely with no external input (the **negative phase**).

The weight update is:

$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \left( \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}} \right)
$$

Where $\eta$ is the learning rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The learning rule has a beautiful intuition: **increase weights for correlations seen in the data, decrease weights for correlations generated by the model itself.** This is essentially a "wake-sleep" style contrast between reality and the model's dreams.

### 2.7 Contrastive Divergence (CD-$k$) — Practical Approximation

Computing $\langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}}$ requires running the network to thermal equilibrium, which is prohibitively slow. **Contrastive Divergence (CD)**, proposed by **Hinton (2002)**, approximates the negative phase using only $k$ steps of Gibbs sampling (typically $k = 1$).

The CD-1 update rule is:

$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \left( \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{recon}} \right)
$$

Where:
- $\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}}$ is the correlation computed after one upward pass (data $\to$ hidden).
- $\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{recon}}$ is the correlation computed after one reconstruction (hidden $\to$ visible $\to$ hidden).

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Function** | $E(\mathbf{x}) = -\sum_{i<j} w_{ij} x_i x_j - \sum_i b_i x_i$ | Defines "preference" over states; foundation of all BM computations. |
| **Boltzmann Distribution** | $p(\mathbf{x}) = e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T} / Z$ | Probabilistic generative model; sample generation in AI. |
| **Partition Function** | $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{x}} e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}$ | Normalization; intractable for large networks ($2^N$ states). |
| **Unit Activation Probability** | $p(x_i = 1) = \sigma\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + \frac{b_i}{T}\right)$ | Stochastic neuron firing; equivalent to logistic regression with peer input. |
| **Sigmoid Function** | $\sigma(u) = 1 / (1 + e^{-u})$ | Activation mapping from energy difference to probability. |
| **Log-Likelihood Gradient** | $\partial \log p / \partial w_{ij} = \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}}$ | Drives learning; positive phase minus negative phase. |
| **General BM Weight Update** | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \left( \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}} \right)$ | Maximum likelihood learning step. |
| **RBM Conditional (Visible $\to$ Hidden)** | $p(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(\sum_i w_{ij} v_i + b_j\right)$ | Fast inference; enables efficient block sampling. |
| **RBM Conditional (Hidden $\to$ Visible)** | $p(v_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma\!\left(\sum_j w_{ij} h_j + a_i\right)$ | Reconstruction; enables Gibbs chain. |
| **Contrastive Divergence (CD-1)** | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \left( \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{recon}} \right)$ | Fast approximate learning; biases parameters in correct direction. |
| **Free Energy of Visible Units (RBM)** | $F(\mathbf{v}) = -\sum_i a_i v_i - \sum_j \log\!\left(1 + e^{b_j + \sum_i w_{ij} v_i}\right)$ | Tractable energy after marginalizing hidden units; used in evaluation. |
| **Temperature Limit (Cold)** | $T \to 0$: deterministic, low-energy states | Used in inference for prediction (similarity to Hopfield networks). |
| **Temperature Limit (Hot)** | $T \to \infty$: uniform random sampling | Used in initial exploration of state space. |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Boltzmann Machines and their descendants power several modern engineering applications:

- **Dimensionality Reduction:** RBMs can learn compact feature representations similar to PCA but for non-linear data.
- **Collaborative Filtering:** The Netflix Prize competition was famously won using RBM-based recommender systems.
- **Pre-training Deep Networks:** Stacking RBMs creates **Deep Belief Networks (DBNs)**, used to pre-train deep neural networks in the pre-2012 era.
- **Anomaly Detection:** The energy function can flag low-probability (high-energy) inputs as outliers — useful in fraud detection, industrial fault monitoring, and cybersecurity.
- **Generative Modeling:** Sampling from the Boltzmann distribution can create new data points similar to training data (precursor to modern GANs and VAEs).
- **Pattern Completion:** Given partial input, a BM can fill in missing features (like associative memory).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Energy to Boltzmann Probability

**Goal:** Show that the Boltzmann distribution is the natural consequence of maximizing entropy subject to a fixed average energy.

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a system of $N$ binary units in a thermal bath at temperature $T$. Each unit can be in state $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$. The system can be in $2^N$ possible configurations $\mathbf{x} \in \{0,1\}^N$. The probability of being in state $\mathbf{x}$ is $p(\mathbf{x})$, with constraints:

$$
\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) = 1
$$

$$
\langle E \rangle = \sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) \, E(\mathbf{x}) = \bar{E} \quad \text{(fixed average energy)}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximize Entropy:** The Shannon entropy of the distribution is:

$$
H[p] = -\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) \log p(\mathbf{x})
$$

To find the distribution that maximizes $H[p]$ subject to the two constraints, we form the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) \log p(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda_0 \!\left(\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) - 1\right) - \lambda_1 \!\left(\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) E(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{E}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Take Derivative:** Differentiate with respect to $p(\mathbf{x})$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p(\mathbf{x})} = -\log p(\mathbf{x}) - 1 - \lambda_0 - \lambda_1 E(\mathbf{x}) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p(\mathbf{x})$:**

$$
\log p(\mathbf{x}) = -1 - \lambda_0 - \lambda_1 E(\mathbf{x})
$$

$$
p(\mathbf{x}) = e^{-1 - \lambda_0} \cdot e^{-\lambda_1 E(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify Constants:** Applying the normalization constraint $\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p(\mathbf{x}) = 1$ gives:

$$
e^{-1 - \lambda_0} = \frac{1}{\sum_{\mathbf{x}} e^{-\lambda_1 E(\mathbf{x})}} = \frac{1}{Z(\lambda_1)}
$$

Setting $\lambda_1 = 1/T$ (the inverse temperature, where $T$ is a free parameter) yields the celebrated **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}}{\sum_{\mathbf{y}} e^{-E(\mathbf{y})/T}} = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}}{Z}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Boltzmann distribution is the **maximum-entropy distribution** consistent with a given average energy. In information theory, this makes it the "least biased" probabilistic model, which is a beautiful bridge between physics and machine learning.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Activation Probability of a Single Unit

**Goal:** Derive $p(x_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})$ from the Boltzmann distribution.

**Step 1 — Conditional Probability:** The probability that unit $i$ takes value 1, given the states of all other units, is:

$$
p(x_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i}) = \frac{p(x_i = 1, \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})}{p(x_i = 1, \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i}) + p(x_i = 0, \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Boltzmann Form:** Using $p(\mathbf{x}) \propto e^{-E(\mathbf{x})/T}$ and separating the energy into terms involving $x_i$ and the rest:

$$
E(\mathbf{x}) = -x_i \!\left(\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + b_i\right) + E'(\mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})
$$

Where $E'(\mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})$ is the energy term not involving $x_i$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Ratio:** 

$$
\frac{p(x_i = 1, \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})}{p(x_i = 0, \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})} = \frac{e^{-E(x_i=1)/T}}{e^{-E(x_i=0)/T}} = \frac{e^{\left(\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + b_i\right)/T} \cdot e^{-E'/T}}{e^{-E'/T}} = e^{\left(\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + b_i\right)/T}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p(x_i = 1)$:** Let $u_i = \sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + b_i$. Then:

$$
p(x_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i}) = \frac{e^{u_i/T}}{1 + e^{u_i/T}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u_i/T}} = \sigma\!\left(\frac{u_i}{T}\right)
$$

This confirms the **sigmoid activation rule**:

$$
p(x_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i}) = \sigma\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} x_j + \frac{b_i}{T}\right)
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Log-Likelihood Gradient (BM Learning Rule)

**Goal:** Derive the weight update rule $\Delta w_{ij} \propto \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}}$.

**Step 1 — Log-Likelihood of a Training Sample:** The probability of observing visible vector $\mathbf{v}$ is (marginalizing over hidden units $\mathbf{h}$):

$$
p(\mathbf{v}) = \sum_{\mathbf{h}} p(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})/T}}{Z}
$$

The log-likelihood is:

$$
\log p(\mathbf{v}) = \log \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})/T} - \log Z
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $w_{ij}$:** 

$$
\frac{\partial \log p(\mathbf{v})}{\partial w_{ij}} = \frac{1}{p(\mathbf{v})} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial w_{ij}} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})/T}}{Z} \cdot \frac{Z}{Z} + \text{similar for } -\log Z
$$

After algebraic manipulation (using the fact that $E$ is linear in each $w_{ij}$):

$$
\frac{\partial \log p(\mathbf{v})}{\partial w_{ij}} = -\frac{1}{T}\!\left(\langle x_i x_j \rangle_{p(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})} - \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{p(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Two Phases:**
- The first expectation $\langle x_i x_j \rangle_{p(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})}$ is taken with the visible units **clamped to data** and hidden units sampled — this is the **positive phase** (denoted "data" because it depends on the training sample).
- The second expectation $\langle x_i x_j \rangle_{p(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$ is taken with the **network running freely** — this is the **negative phase** (denoted "model" because it represents the model's internal statistics).

**Step 4 — Gradient Ascent Update:** Using $T = 1$ and performing gradient ascent:

$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \left( \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle x_i x_j \rangle_{\text{model}} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Interpretation of the Learning Rule:**
> - **Positive Phase:** When data shows units $i$ and $j$ are often ON together, increase $w_{ij}$ (the model should reproduce this correlation).
> - **Negative Phase:** When the model itself (without data) shows units $i$ and $j$ ON together, decrease $w_{ij}$ (the model has a "hallucination" of this correlation that is not supported by data).
> - The **net effect** is to make the model's correlation statistics match the data's correlation statistics — a beautiful instance of moment matching.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of BM Variants

| Property | General Boltzmann Machine | Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) | Deep Boltzmann Machine (DBM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Connectivity** | Fully connected (intra-layer allowed) | Bipartite (visible-hidden only) | Multi-layer bipartite stack |
| **Inference** | Intractable | Tractable (block Gibbs) | Approximate (variational) |
| **Learning** | Intractable in general | Contrastive Divergence (CD-$k$) | Variational + persistent CD |
| **Sampling** | Gibbs sampling (slow) | Fast block Gibbs | Layer-wise Gibbs |
| **Partition Function** | Exponential ($2^N$) | Tractable closed form (per layer) | Intractable |
| **Applications** | Theoretical, small problems | Recommender systems, pre-training | Pre-training deep nets, image modeling |
| **Training Time** | Very slow (needs equilibrium) | Fast (CD-1 works well in practice) | Slow (multi-layer dependencies) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Boltzmann Machine Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[V1 Visible Unit 1] --- B[V2 Visible Unit 2]
    A --- C[V3 Visible Unit 3]
    A --- D[H1 Hidden Unit 1]
    A --- E[H2 Hidden Unit 2]
    B --- C
    B --- D
    B --- E
    C --- D
    C --- E
    D --- E
    F[Input Data Clamp] --> A
    F --> B
    F --> C
    G[Output Sampling] --> A
    G --> B
    G --> C
    G --> D
    G --> E
    H[Bias b1] -.-> A
    I[Bias b2] -.-> B
    J[Bias b3] -.-> C
    K[Bias c1] -.-> D
    L[Bias c2] -.-> E
    M[Weight wij Symmetric] -.-> A
    M -.-> B
    M -.-> C
    M -.-> D
    M -.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid lines represent symmetric weights $w_{ij}$ between all pairs of units. Dotted lines represent bias connections. Note the **fully connected** nature — every unit is connected to every other unit, including within the visible and hidden layers. This is what makes inference intractable.

---

### 4.2 Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[V1] --- C[H1]
    A --- D[H2]
    A --- E[H3]
    B[V2] --- C
    B --- D
    B --- E
    N[V3] --- C
    N --- D
    N --- E
    P[V4] --- C
    P --- D
    P --- E
    F[Visible Bias a1] -.-> A
    Q[Visible Bias a2] -.-> B
    R[Visible Bias a3] -.-> N
    S[Visible Bias a4] -.-> P
    G[Hidden Bias b1] -.-> C
    H[Hidden Bias b2] -.-> D
    I[Hidden Bias b3] -.-> E
    J[Input Data Vector] --> A
    J --> B
    J --> N
    J --> P
    K[Sampled Hidden Representation] --> C
    K --> D
    K --> E
    L[Reconstructed Visible Vector] --> A
    L --> B
    L --> N
    L --> P
    M[Block Gibbs Sampling] --> K
    M --> L
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Difference:** In the RBM, there are **no connections within the visible layer** (V1–V4 are independent given hidden) and **no connections within the hidden layer** (H1–H3 are independent given visible). This is the **bipartite restriction** that enables efficient block Gibbs sampling: all hidden units can be updated in parallel given the visible vector, and vice versa.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow: Training an RBM with CD-1

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Initialize Weights and Biases] --> B[Read Training Sample v0]
    B --> C[Positive Phase: Sample h0 from p(h to the power of v0)]
    C --> D[Compute Positive Statistics v0 times h0]
    D --> E[Negative Phase: Reconstruct v1 from h0 using p(v to the power of h0)]
    E --> F[Re-sample h1 from p(h to the power of v1)]
    F --> G[Compute Negative Statistics v1 times h1]
    G --> H[Update Weights: delta w equals eta times v0 h0 minus v1 h1]
    H --> I[Update Visible Biases: delta a equals eta times v0 minus v1]
    I --> J[Update Hidden Biases: delta b equals eta times h0 minus h1]
    J --> K{More Samples?}
    K -->|Yes| B
    K -->|No| L[End: Trained RBM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Insight:** This is the **Contrastive Divergence (CD-1)** algorithm visualized. Note the two phases:
> 1. **Positive Phase (Wake):** Reality drives the model upward (visible $\to$ hidden).
> 2. **Negative Phase (Sleep):** The model dreams downward (hidden $\to$ visible $\to$ hidden), and we measure how its dreams differ from reality.
> The weights are updated to make the model's dreams better match reality.

---

### 4.4 Energy-Based Model Workflow (KTU Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Define Energy Function E of x] --> B[Compute Boltzmann Probability p of x equals exp of negative E divided by T divided by Z]
    B --> C{Choose Architecture}
    C -->|Small Network| D[General BM: Compute Z by Summation over 2 power of N States]
    C -->|Large Network| E[RBM: Exploit Bipartite Structure for Block Gibbs]
    C -->|Very Large Network| F[DBM: Multi-Layer Stacking with Variational Inference]
    D --> G[Run Gibbs Sampling to Equilibrium for Both Phases]
    E --> H[Use Contrastive Divergence CD-k for Fast Approximate Learning]
    F --> I[Use Persistent Contrastive Divergence PCD]
    G --> J[Apply Learning Rule delta w equals eta times data minus model]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Evaluate Using Free Energy F of v]
    K --> L[Application: Generative Modeling, Recommender Systems, Anomaly Detection, Pre-Training]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** The Boltzmann Machine family shares a common mathematical skeleton (energy $\to$ probability $\to$ learning via moment matching) but differs in tractability. The choice of architecture depends on the **scale of the problem** and the **available computational resources**.
